### general-relativity.globally-hyperbolic-lorentzian-manifolds

- **title**: Globally hyperbolic Lorentzian manifolds
- **unit**: `13.09.01`
- **prerequisites**: `general-relativity.tensors-smooth-manifolds`, `general-relativity.geodesics-parallel-transport`, `general-relativity.schwarzschild-solution`
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Hawking and Ellis, *The Large Scale Structure of Space-Time* (Cambridge, 1973), Ch. 6; Wald, *General Relativity* (1984), Ch. 8; Bernal and Sánchez, *Comm. Math. Phys.* 257 (2005) 43; Gérard, *Microlocal Analysis of Quantum Fields on Curved Spacetimes* (EMS, 2019), Ch. 5
  - intermediate: O'Neill, *Semi-Riemannian Geometry* (Academic, 1983), Ch. 14; Bär-Ginoux-Pfäffle, *Wave Equations on Lorentzian Manifolds and Quantization* (EMS, 2007), Ch. 1
  - beginner: Penrose, *The Road to Reality* (Cape, 2004), Ch. 17 (selected sections on causal structure)
- **notes**: Lorentzian manifold $(M, g)$ with signature $(-, +, +, +)$ and time orientation; causal classification of tangent vectors (timelike/null/spacelike); chronological and causal future/past sets $I^\pm(p), J^\pm(p)$; strong causality. Geroch 1970 *J. Math. Phys.* 11 437: global hyperbolicity ($J^+(p) \cap J^-(q)$ compact for all $p, q$, together with strong causality) is equivalent to the existence of a Cauchy hypersurface — a topological codimension-1 hypersurface $\Sigma \subset M$ met exactly once by every inextendible timelike curve. Bernal-Sánchez 2003 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 243 461 and 2005 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 257 43 smooth refinement: there exists a smooth spacelike Cauchy hypersurface, and $M$ is diffeomorphic to $\mathbb{R} \times \Sigma$ with metric splitting $g = -\beta^2 dt^2 + h_t$ in adapted coordinates, where $\beta : M \to \mathbb{R}_{>0}$ is the lapse function and $h_t$ a smoothly $t$-varying Riemannian metric on $\Sigma$. Worked examples: Minkowski $(\mathbb{R}^{1,3}, \eta)$ is globally hyperbolic with $t = \text{const}$ Cauchy hyperplanes; Schwarzschild exterior is globally hyperbolic; FLRW is globally hyperbolic. Non-examples: anti-de Sitter spacetime fails (timelike conformal boundary, information leaks in finite proper time); Reissner-Nordström and Kerr interiors past Cauchy horizons fail strong causality; Gödel spacetime contains closed timelike curves. Originators: Leray 1953 (causal-domain notion for hyperbolic systems); Geroch 1970 (existence-of-Cauchy-surface theorem); Hawking-Ellis 1973 (textbook consolidation); Bernal-Sánchez 2003 + 2005 (smooth splitting). Foundational for well-posedness of the Klein-Gordon Cauchy problem on curved spacetime and the microlocal / Hadamard-state programme (Radzikowski 1996; Gérard 2019). Lean status: `none` — Mathlib has Riemannian metric and smooth-manifold infrastructure but no Lorentzian signature, no causal structure, no Cauchy-surface formalism.

### general-relativity.klein-gordon-equation-globally-hyperbolic

- **title**: Klein-Gordon equation on a globally hyperbolic spacetime
- **unit**: `13.09.02`
- **prerequisites**: `general-relativity.globally-hyperbolic-lorentzian-manifolds`, `general-relativity.tensors-smooth-manifolds`, `microlocal-analysis.wave-front-set`
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Gérard, *Microlocal Analysis of Quantum Fields on Curved Spacetimes* (EMS, 2019), Ch. 5-6; Bär, Ginoux & Pfäffle, *Wave Equations on Lorentzian Manifolds and Quantization* (EMS, 2007), Ch. 3; Wald, *Quantum Field Theory in Curved Spacetime and Black Hole Thermodynamics* (Chicago, 1994), Ch. 3
  - intermediate: Bär-Ginoux-Pfäffle (EMS, 2007), Ch. 3; Dimock, *Trans. AMS* 269 (1980) 133; Gérard (EMS, 2019), §5.3
  - beginner: Wald, *General Relativity* (1984), Ch. 10; Hawking and Ellis, *The Large Scale Structure of Space-Time* (1973), §7.4 (selected passages on the wave equation)
- **notes**: Klein-Gordon equation $(\Box_g + m^2 + V)u = f$ on a globally hyperbolic spacetime $(M, g)$ with d'Alembertian $\Box_g = -|g|^{-1/2}\partial_\mu(|g|^{1/2} g^{\mu\nu}\partial_\nu)$ in signature $(-,+,+,+)$. Theorem (Leray 1953 / Choquet-Bruhat 1952 / Bär-Ginoux-Pfäffle 2007): given $(u_0, u_1) \in C_c^\infty(\Sigma) \oplus C_c^\infty(\Sigma)$ on a smooth spacelike Cauchy hypersurface $\Sigma$, the Cauchy problem has a unique smooth global solution $u \in C^\infty(M)$ depending continuously on the data, with $\mathrm{supp}\,u \subset J(\mathrm{supp}(u_0) \cup \mathrm{supp}(u_1))$ (finite propagation speed). Unique retarded and advanced fundamental solutions $E^\pm : C_c^\infty(M) \to C^\infty(M)$ with $\mathrm{supp}(E^\pm f) \subset J^\pm(\mathrm{supp}\,f)$; Pauli-Jordan commutator $E = E^- - E^+$ as the causal propagator. Energy estimate over a Cauchy slice: $\|u(t)\|_{H^1(\Sigma_t)} + \|\partial_t u(t)\|_{L^2(\Sigma_t)}$ controlled by initial data and source via Grönwall. Wave-front set of $E$ on Minkowski: $\mathrm{WF}(E)$ supported on pairs of points joined by a null geodesic with cotangent the parallel transport of itself — prepares the Radzikowski wave-front-set criterion for Hadamard states (13.09.03). Worked example: free massless Klein-Gordon on Minkowski $\mathbb{R}^{1,3}$, retarded propagator $E^+(x) = (2\pi)^{-1}\theta(x^0)\delta(x^\mu x_\mu)$; massive case picks up the Bessel-function tail $\theta(x^0)\theta(x^\mu x_\mu)\, (m/4\pi\sqrt{x^\mu x_\mu})\, J_1(m\sqrt{x^\mu x_\mu})$. Originator citations: Leray 1953 (mimeographed IAS lecture notes); Choquet-Bruhat 1952 *Acta Math.* 88 141 (local well-posedness of hyperbolic systems on Lorentzian manifolds); Lichnerowicz 1961 *Publ. IHÉS* 10 5 (propagateur construction on a globally hyperbolic background); Dimock 1980 *Trans. AMS* 269 133 (Dirac and Klein-Gordon Cauchy problem on a curved background, algebraic quantisation); Hörmander 1985 *The Analysis of Linear Partial Differential Operators III* (Springer) for the microlocal apparatus; Bär-Ginoux-Pfäffle 2007 EMS Lectures (definitive textbook treatment with full proofs and a free arXiv preprint at 0806.1036); Wald 1994 *Quantum Field Theory in Curved Spacetime* (Chicago) for the QFT-side framing; Gérard 2019 EMS Ch. 5-6 for the modern microlocal consolidation. Foundational input to algebraic QFT on curved spacetimes: the causal propagator $E$ is the integral kernel of the symplectic form on Cauchy data, the CCR commutator $[\hat\phi(f), \hat\phi(g)] = i\hbar\, E(f, g)\mathbf{1}$, and the wave-front-set characterisation of Hadamard states. Lean status: `none` — Mathlib lacks Lorentzian-metric infrastructure, the d'Alembertian on a pseudo-Riemannian manifold, and any treatment of hyperbolic Cauchy problems on a curved background.

### general-relativity.hadamard-states-wave-front-set-criterion

- **title**: Hadamard states via the wave-front-set criterion (Radzikowski)
- **unit**: `13.09.03`
- **prerequisites**: `general-relativity.globally-hyperbolic-lorentzian-manifolds`, `general-relativity.klein-gordon-equation-globally-hyperbolic`, `quantum-mechanics.ccr-algebra-weyl-algebra-quasi-free-states`, `microlocal-analysis.propagation-of-singularities`
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Gérard, *Microlocal Analysis of Quantum Fields on Curved Spacetimes* (EMS, 2019), Ch. 7; Radzikowski, *Comm. Math. Phys.* 179 (1996) 529; Brunetti, Fredenhagen & Köhler, *Comm. Math. Phys.* 180 (1996) 633; Hollands and Wald, *Comm. Math. Phys.* 223 (2001) 289
  - intermediate: Kay & Wald, *Phys. Rep.* 207 (1991) 49; Gérard (EMS, 2019), Ch. 7; Bär, Ginoux & Pfäffle, *Wave Equations on Lorentzian Manifolds and Quantization* (EMS, 2007), Ch. 4
  - beginner: Wald, *Quantum Field Theory in Curved Spacetime and Black Hole Thermodynamics* (Chicago, 1994), Ch. 4
- **notes**: A quasi-free state $\omega$ on the CCR algebra of the Klein-Gordon field on a globally hyperbolic spacetime $(M, g)$ is **Hadamard** if its two-point function $\omega_2(x, y) := \omega(\phi(x)\phi(y))$ differs from the Hadamard parametrix $H(x, y) = (8\pi^2)^{-1}(U(x,y)/\sigma_\epsilon(x,y)) + V(x,y)\log\sigma_\epsilon(x,y) + W(x,y)$ by a smooth function, where $\sigma$ is Synge's world function and $U, V$ are the Hadamard coefficients fixed by transport equations along null geodesics. Radzikowski 1996 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 179 529 proved this is equivalent to the **microlocal spectrum condition**: $\mathrm{WF}(\omega_2) = \{(x, k; y, -k') \in T^*(M \times M) \setminus 0 : (x, k) \sim_{\mathrm{bichar}} (y, k'),\ k \in \overline{V}^+_x\}$, where $\sim_{\mathrm{bichar}}$ is the null-bicharacteristic relation (the two points lie on a common null geodesic with the covectors parallel-transported) and $\overline{V}^+_x$ is the closed future light cone in $T^*_x M$. Proof passes through Duistermaat-Hörmander parametrix theory for the distinguished Hadamard parametrix plus propagation of singularities along the Hamiltonian flow of $g^{\mu\nu}\xi_\mu\xi_\nu$. Existence: Fulling-Narcowich-Wald 1981 *Ann. Phys.* 136 243 for ultrastatic spacetimes; Junker 1996 *Rev. Math. Phys.* 8 1091 and the FNW deformation argument extend to general globally hyperbolic spacetimes. Worked example: Minkowski vacuum two-point function $\omega_2^{\mathrm{vac}}(x, y) = \int (2\pi)^{-3}(2\omega_k)^{-1} e^{-ik\cdot(x-y)}\, d^3k$ has wave-front set on the future mass shell paired with the past mass shell, restricting to the future light cone in the massless limit — the Hadamard condition in the simplest case. Renormalisation application: Hadamard subtraction $T_{\mu\nu}^{\mathrm{ren}}(x) = \lim_{y \to x}[\partial^x_\alpha \partial^y_\beta(\omega_2(x, y) - H(x, y))]$ (Wald 1977 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 54 1) supplies the renormalised stress-energy tensor on a curved background; BFK 1996 extended this to all Wick polynomials and time-ordered products (Hollands-Wald 2001). Application to Hawking radiation: the Hartle-Hawking state on Kruskal is Hadamard, and the thermal spectrum of outgoing modes is read off from the analytic structure of its two-point function. Originator citations: Hadamard 1923 *Lectures on Cauchy's Problem* (parametrix construction); DeWitt-Brehme 1960 *Ann. Phys.* 9 220 (coincidence-limit subtraction); Fulling, Sweeny & Wald 1978 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 63 257 (Hadamard form on a globally hyperbolic background); Kay & Wald 1991 *Phys. Rep.* 207 49 (definition of admissible quasi-free Hadamard states); Radzikowski 1996 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 179 529 (wave-front-set characterisation); Brunetti, Fredenhagen & Köhler 1996 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 180 633 (microlocal spectrum condition for higher $n$-point functions); Junker 1996 *Rev. Math. Phys.* 8 1091 (existence on general globally hyperbolic spacetimes); Hollands & Wald 2001 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 223 289 (extension to all Wick polynomials); Gérard 2019 EMS Ch. 7 (modern textbook consolidation). Lean status: `none` — Mathlib lacks Lorentzian-metric infrastructure, the wave-front-set machinery on a manifold, the CCR / Weyl-algebra layer, and the entire Hadamard-parametrix apparatus.

### general-relativity.existence-of-hadamard-states-fnw-deformation

- **title**: Existence of Hadamard states via the FNW deformation argument
- **unit**: `13.09.04`
- **prerequisites**: `general-relativity.globally-hyperbolic-lorentzian-manifolds`, `general-relativity.klein-gordon-equation-globally-hyperbolic`, `general-relativity.hadamard-states-wave-front-set-criterion`
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Gérard, *Microlocal Analysis of Quantum Fields on Curved Spacetimes* (EMS, 2019), Ch. 8; Fulling, Narcowich & Wald, *Ann. Phys.* 136 (1981) 243; Junker, *Rev. Math. Phys.* 8 (1996) 1091; Gérard & Wrochna, *Comm. Math. Phys.* 325 (2014) 713
  - intermediate: Bär, Ginoux & Pfäffle, *Wave Equations on Lorentzian Manifolds and Quantization* (EMS, 2007), Ch. 4; Gérard (EMS, 2019), Ch. 8; Sahlmann & Verch, *Rev. Math. Phys.* 13 (2001) 1203
  - beginner: Wald, *Quantum Field Theory in Curved Spacetime and Black Hole Thermodynamics* (Chicago, 1994), Ch. 4
- **notes**: For every globally hyperbolic Lorentzian manifold $(M, g)$ and every Klein-Gordon operator $P = \Box_g + m^2 + V$ on it, the CCR algebra of the quantised free scalar field admits at least one Hadamard quasi-free state. Fulling-Narcowich-Wald 1981 *Ann. Phys.* 136 243 supplied the original construction via deformation: by Bernal-Sánchez splitting $M \cong \mathbb{R} \times \Sigma$, deform $g$ along a smooth one-parameter family $g_s$, $s \in [0, 1]$, of globally hyperbolic metrics with $g_0$ ultrastatic ($g_0 = -dt^2 + h_0$) and $g_1 = g$, with $g_s$ agreeing with $g_0$ outside a compact spacetime region for $s$ near 0 and with $g$ for $s$ near 1. The ground state of the ultrastatic Klein-Gordon field is explicitly Hadamard via the mode-decomposition formula $\omega_0(x, y) = \langle f(x), (2\sqrt{-\Delta_{h_0} + m^2})^{-1} e^{-i(t_x - t_y)\sqrt{-\Delta_{h_0} + m^2}} f(y)\rangle$ on $L^2(\Sigma, d\mathrm{vol}_{h_0})$. Pull back the ground state along the symplectic isomorphism of Cauchy-data phase spaces $(\mathcal{S}_{g_s}, \sigma_{g_s})$ induced by $E_{g_s}$. The Hadamard wave-front-set condition $\mathrm{WF}(\omega_2) = \{(x, k; y, -k') : (x, k) \sim_{\mathrm{bichar}} (y, k'),\ k \in \overline{V}^+_x\}$ is preserved along the deformation because: (i) the principal symbol $p_s(x, \xi) = g_s^{\mu\nu}(x)\xi_\mu\xi_\nu$ varies smoothly in $s$; (ii) the bicharacteristic flow on $T^*M \setminus 0$ varies smoothly with the metric; (iii) Hörmander's propagation-of-singularities theorem applied to the deformed equation forces the wave-front set of the pulled-back state to remain on the future-pointing half of the bicharacteristic relation of $g_s$ at each $s$. The pulled-back state on $(M, g_1)$ is therefore Hadamard. Junker 1996 *Rev. Math. Phys.* 8 1091 gave the modern pseudo-differential / adiabatic-vacuum sharpening of the FNW construction; Brunetti-Fredenhagen-Köhler 1996 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 180 633 extended the microlocal-spectrum condition to all $n$-point functions; Sahlmann-Verch 2001 *Rev. Math. Phys.* 13 1203 codified the existence theory in the locally-covariant-functor language. Most explicit modern construction: Gérard-Wrochna 2014 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 325 713 / 2017 *Ann. Henri Poincaré* 18 2715 builds Hadamard states directly on a Cauchy surface $\Sigma$ from a pseudo-differential approximation $\epsilon$ of $\sqrt{D}$ where $D = -\Delta_h + m^2 + V$, with explicit Hadamard-form initial data; bypasses the deformation entirely. Originator citations: Fulling, Narcowich & Wald 1981 (deformation existence); Junker 1996 (pseudo-differential / adiabatic-vacuum existence); Brunetti, Fredenhagen & Köhler 1996 (extension to $n$-point functions); Sahlmann & Verch 2001 (locally-covariant existence); Gérard & Wrochna 2014/2017 (direct pseudo-differential construction); Gérard 2019 EMS Ch. 8 (textbook consolidation). Lean status: `none` — same gap chain as 13.09.03; depends on Lorentzian-metric, wave-front-set, CCR-algebra and Hadamard-parametrix infrastructure absent from Mathlib.

### general-relativity.wick-polynomials-in-curved-spacetime

- **title**: Wick polynomials in curved spacetime via Hadamard parametrix subtraction
- **unit**: `13.09.06`
- **prerequisites**: `general-relativity.hadamard-states-wave-front-set-criterion`, `general-relativity.existence-of-hadamard-states-fnw-deformation`
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Hollands and Wald, *Comm. Math. Phys.* 223 (2001) 289; *Comm. Math. Phys.* 231 (2002) 309; *Comm. Math. Phys.* 237 (2003) 123; Brunetti, Fredenhagen & Köhler, *Comm. Math. Phys.* 180 (1996) 633; Gérard, *Microlocal Analysis of Quantum Fields on Curved Spacetimes* (EMS, 2019), Ch. 9
  - intermediate: Hollands & Wald, *Phys. Rep.* 574 (2015) 1 (arXiv:1401.2026); Gérard (EMS, 2019), Ch. 9; Wald, *Quantum Field Theory in Curved Spacetime and Black Hole Thermodynamics* (Chicago, 1994), Ch. 4
  - beginner: Wald, *Quantum Field Theory in Curved Spacetime and Black Hole Thermodynamics* (Chicago, 1994), Ch. 4
- **notes**: Renormalised Wick powers $:\!\phi^n(x)\!:_H$ of the free scalar Klein-Gordon field on a globally hyperbolic spacetime $(M, g)$, defined by point-splitting and subtraction of the Hadamard parametrix $H(x, y) = (8\pi^2)^{-1}(U(x,y)/\sigma_\epsilon(x,y)) + V(x,y)\log\sigma_\epsilon(x,y)$. Wald 1977 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 54 1 showed that naive normal ordering against a Minkowski-style two-point function fails to define a covariant Wick power on a curved background; the Hadamard parametrix supplies the local, state-independent, geometry-determined subtraction that does work. Definition: $:\!\phi^2(x)\!:_H := \lim_{y \to x}[\omega_2(x, y) - H(x, y)]$ where $\omega$ is any Hadamard state; the coincidence limit exists because $\omega_2 - H$ is smooth, by the Radzikowski wave-front-set criterion. Brunetti-Fredenhagen-Köhler 1996 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 180 633 systematised the construction for all Wick monomials $\phi^n$; Hollands-Wald 2001/2002/2003 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 223/231/237 axiomatised the construction: any assignment $\phi^n \mapsto :\!\phi^n\!:_H$ satisfying (i) locality, (ii) general covariance under isometric embeddings, (iii) scaling behaviour, (iv) Leibniz rule under derivatives, (v) smooth dependence on the metric, is unique up to a finite-dimensional family of local covariant counterterms expressible as polynomials in the curvature invariants. Stress-energy tensor: the renormalised $T_{\mu\nu}^{\mathrm{ren}}(x)$ is defined by the same point-splitting procedure applied to the symmetric bi-tensor $T_{\mu\nu}(x, y) = \nabla^x_\mu \nabla^y_\nu(\omega_2(x, y) - H(x, y))$ at coincidence (Christensen 1976 *Phys. Rev. D* 14 2490; Wald 1977 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 54 1; Wald 1978 *Phys. Rev. D* 17 1477). Trace anomaly: a classically conformally invariant scalar field acquires a trace $\langle T^\mu_\mu\rangle = a\,C_{abcd}C^{abcd} + b\,(R_{ab}R^{ab} - \tfrac{1}{3}R^2) + c\,\Box R$ after renormalisation, with the conformal-anomaly coefficients $(a, b, c)$ fixed by the matter content (Capper-Duff 1974 *Nuovo Cim. A* 23 173; Duff 1977 *Nucl. Phys. B* 125 334; Duff 1994 *Class. Quant. Grav.* 11 1387 for the modern review). Applications: Hawking radiation $\langle T_{\mu\nu}\rangle$ near the Schwarzschild horizon (Christensen-Fulling 1977); semiclassical Einstein equation $G_{\mu\nu} = 8\pi G\,\langle T_{\mu\nu}\rangle^{\mathrm{ren}}$ as the back-reaction self-consistency equation (Wald 1977); cosmological constant from vacuum-energy subtraction. Worked example: Minkowski case — Hadamard subtraction reproduces standard normal ordering with the Minkowski vacuum two-point function as the unique state-independent reference. Originator citations: Wald 1977 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 54 1 (renormalised stress-energy via Hadamard subtraction); Wald 1978 *Phys. Rev. D* 17 1477 (axiomatisation); Christensen 1976 *Phys. Rev. D* 14 2490 (covariant point-splitting); Capper-Duff 1974 *Nuovo Cim. A* 23 173 (trace anomaly); Brunetti-Fredenhagen-Köhler 1996 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 180 633 (Wick polynomials via microlocal spectrum condition); Hollands-Wald 2001 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 223 289 (local Wick polynomials and time-ordered products); Hollands-Wald 2002 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 231 309 (existence of time-ordered products); Hollands-Wald 2003 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 237 123 (renormalisation freedom classification); Hollands-Wald 2015 *Phys. Rep.* 574 1 (modern review). Lean status: `none` — same gap chain as 13.09.03/04/05, plus the additional Hadamard-parametrix apparatus (Synge world function, parallel-propagator, transport-equation Hadamard coefficients) and the locally covariant functor framework of Brunetti-Fredenhagen-Verch 2003 absent from Mathlib.

### general-relativity.bunch-davies-state-on-de-sitter

- **title**: Bunch-Davies state on de Sitter spacetime
- **unit**: `13.09.08`
- **prerequisites**: `general-relativity.hadamard-states-wave-front-set-criterion`, `general-relativity.existence-of-hadamard-states-fnw-deformation`, `general-relativity.flrw-cosmology-friedmann`
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Bunch & Davies, *Proc. R. Soc. A* 360 (1978) 117; Allen, *Phys. Rev. D* 32 (1985) 3136; Mottola, *Phys. Rev. D* 31 (1985) 754; Gibbons & Hawking, *Phys. Rev. D* 15 (1977) 2738; Birrell & Davies, *Quantum Fields in Curved Space* (CUP, 1982), §5.4; Mukhanov & Winitzki, *Quantum Effects in Gravity* (CUP, 2007), Ch. 8; Gérard, *Microlocal Analysis of Quantum Fields on Curved Spacetimes* (EMS, 2019), Ch. 10
  - intermediate: Birrell & Davies, *Quantum Fields in Curved Space* (CUP, 1982), §5.4; Mukhanov & Winitzki, *Quantum Effects in Gravity* (CUP, 2007), Ch. 8; Gérard (EMS, 2019), Ch. 10
  - beginner: Mukhanov & Winitzki, *Quantum Effects in Gravity* (CUP, 2007), Ch. 8 (physicist-side framing of the de-Sitter vacuum question); Wald, *Quantum Field Theory in Curved Spacetime and Black Hole Thermodynamics* (Chicago, 1994), Ch. 4
- **notes**: The Bunch-Davies state is the unique de-Sitter-invariant Hadamard quasi-free state of the Klein-Gordon field on de Sitter spacetime $dS_4$, the maximally symmetric Lorentzian manifold with positive cosmological constant and Ricci scalar $R = 12H^2$ and isometry group $\mathrm{SO}(4, 1)$. In the flat slicing $ds^2 = -dt^2 + e^{2Ht}(dx^2 + dy^2 + dz^2)$ with conformal time $\eta = -e^{-Ht}/H$, the positive-frequency mode functions of the Klein-Gordon equation $(\Box + m^2 + \xi R)\phi = 0$ with conformal coupling $\xi = 1/6$ are $u_k(\eta) = (1/\sqrt{2k})(1 + i/(k\eta))e^{-ik\eta}$; the Bunch-Davies vacuum is the state annihilated by the corresponding annihilation operators. Two-point function in closed form: $W(x, x') = (H^2/16\pi^2)\Gamma(\Delta_+)\Gamma(\Delta_-) {}_2F_1(\Delta_+, \Delta_-; 2; (1 + Z(x, x'))/2)$ where $Z(x, x')$ is the de-Sitter geodesic-distance invariant and $\Delta_\pm = 3/2 \pm \sqrt{9/4 - m^2/H^2 - 12\xi}$. For massless conformal coupling $\xi = 1/6$, $m = 0$ the two-point function reduces to the flat-space Wightman function via the conformal transformation between $dS_4$ and the Einstein static universe. Originators: Chernikov-Tagirov 1968 *Ann. IHP A* 9 109 (first quantisation of the scalar field on de Sitter); Bunch-Davies 1978 *Proc. Roy. Soc. A* 360 117 (the de-Sitter-invariant Hadamard state with explicit hypergeometric two-point function). Gibbons-Hawking 1977 *Phys. Rev. D* 15 2738 showed that a static observer in a single de Sitter static patch detects thermal radiation at the Gibbons-Hawking temperature $T_{dS} = H/(2\pi k_B)$ — the analog of the Hawking temperature, with the KMS condition holding with respect to the static-patch boost as time-translation. Allen-Mottola 1985 *Phys. Rev. D* 32 3136 / *Phys. Rev. D* 31 754 classified a one-parameter family of de-Sitter-invariant quasi-free states ($\alpha$-vacua); the Bunch-Davies state is the unique member that is Hadamard. The Hadamard wave-front-set condition picks it out as the only physically admissible de-Sitter-symmetric state: the wave-front set lies on the future-pointing half of the bicharacteristic relation of the de Sitter Klein-Gordon operator (Radzikowski criterion of 13.09.03), and the $\alpha \neq 0$ vacua have wave-front-set components on both halves, hence fail to be Hadamard. Inflationary cosmology application: the inflaton field in single-field slow-roll inflation is approximately Bunch-Davies on the inflationary de-Sitter-like background, giving the scale-invariant power spectrum $\Delta_\phi^2(k) = (H/2\pi)^2$ that seeds the CMB temperature anisotropies — observed by Planck 2018 *A&A* 641 A6 in agreement with slow-roll corrections. The Bunch-Davies state is the natural example of a symmetry-respecting refinement of the FNW existence theorem (13.09.04): instead of an arbitrary ultrastatic reference, the de Sitter group $\mathrm{SO}(4, 1)$ supplies the symmetry-selected reference. Originator citations: Chernikov-Tagirov 1968 *Ann. IHP A* 9 109 (originating de-Sitter mode-sum); Bunch-Davies 1978 *Proc. Roy. Soc. A* 360 117 (the de-Sitter-invariant Hadamard state); Gibbons-Hawking 1977 *Phys. Rev. D* 15 2738 (thermal property at $T = H/(2\pi)$); Allen 1985 *Phys. Rev. D* 32 3136 / Mottola 1985 *Phys. Rev. D* 31 754 ($\alpha$-vacua classification); Birrell-Davies 1982 *Quantum Fields in Curved Space* CUP §5.4 (textbook treatment); Mukhanov-Winitzki 2007 *Quantum Effects in Gravity* CUP Ch. 8 (pedagogic / cosmological treatment); Hollands-Wald 2005 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 257 (uniqueness theorem); Gérard 2019 EMS Ch. 10 (modern textbook consolidation). Lean status: `none` — same gap chain as 13.09.03/04, plus the additional de-Sitter-specific apparatus (hypergeometric mode functions on the conformal-time slicing, the geodesic-distance invariant $Z$, the $\mathrm{SO}(4, 1)$ isometry-action on the CCR algebra) absent from Mathlib.

### general-relativity.unruh-effect-bisognano-wichmann

- **title**: Unruh effect via the Bisognano-Wichmann theorem
- **unit**: `13.09.09`
- **prerequisites**: `general-relativity.globally-hyperbolic-lorentzian-manifolds`, `general-relativity.klein-gordon-equation-globally-hyperbolic`, `general-relativity.hadamard-states-wave-front-set-criterion`, `stat-mech.wightman-axioms`
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Bisognano & Wichmann, *J. Math. Phys.* 16 (1975) 985 and 17 (1976) 303; Sewell, *Ann. Phys.* 141 (1982) 201; Bratteli & Robinson, *Operator Algebras and Quantum Statistical Mechanics* vol. 1 (Springer, 2nd ed. 1987), §2.5; Haag, *Local Quantum Physics* (Springer, 2nd ed. 1996), §V.4; Gérard, *Microlocal Analysis of Quantum Fields on Curved Spacetimes* (EMS, 2019), Ch. 10
  - intermediate: Wald, *Quantum Field Theory in Curved Spacetime and Black Hole Thermodynamics* (Chicago, 1994), Ch. 5; Haag, *Local Quantum Physics* (Springer, 2nd ed. 1996), §V.4; Gérard (EMS, 2019), Ch. 10
  - beginner: Wald, *Quantum Field Theory in Curved Spacetime and Black Hole Thermodynamics* (Chicago, 1994), Ch. 5; Crispino, Higuchi & Matsas, *Rev. Mod. Phys.* 80 (2008) 787
- **notes**: The Unruh effect is the statement that a uniformly accelerated observer in the Minkowski vacuum perceives a thermal bath at the Unruh temperature $T = a/(2\pi)$ (natural units), where $a$ is the proper acceleration. The rigorous derivation routes through the Bisognano-Wichmann theorem (1975/1976 *J. Math. Phys.* 16 985 / 17 303): for a Wightman field, the Tomita-Takesaki modular operator $\Delta$ of the von Neumann algebra $\mathcal{R}(W_R)$ of the right Rindler wedge $W_R = \{x^1 > |x^0|\}$ in the vacuum state $\Omega$ is the analytically continued Lorentz boost, $\Delta^{it} = U(\Lambda_1(-2\pi t))$, and the modular conjugation is $J = \Theta\,U(R_1(\pi))$ (TCP times $\pi$-rotation). Because the modular automorphism group $\sigma_t = \mathrm{Ad}(\Delta^{it})$ makes the vacuum a $\beta = 1$ KMS state (Tomita-Takesaki + the Haag-Hugenholtz-Winnink 1967 KMS characterisation of thermal equilibrium), the wedge-restricted vacuum is KMS at $\beta = 2\pi$ for the boost; on an $a$-accelerated orbit ($x^1{}^2 - x^0{}^2 = a^{-2}$, proper time $\tau = s/a$) this is $T = a/(2\pi)$. Sewell 1982 *Ann. Phys.* 141 201 packaged this as the rigorous, detector-independent Unruh effect and gave the general bifurcate-Killing-horizon version; Kay-Wald 1991 *Phys. Rep.* 207 49 sharpened it into a uniqueness-and-thermality theorem covering Schwarzschild (Hartle-Hawking state, $T_H = \kappa/(2\pi)$) and de Sitter (Bunch-Davies / Gibbons-Hawking) as special cases. The Reeh-Schlieder theorem (1961 *Nuovo Cimento* 22 1051) supplies the cyclic-separating vacuum that makes the modular construction available. Borchers 1992 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 143 315 reduced the modular-boost commutation relations to energy positivity alone (half-sided modular inclusions), seeding the modular-localisation reconstruction programme. Equivalent Euclidean derivation: conical-smoothness of the Euclidean Rindler section at the horizon $\rho = 0$ requires the Euclidean angle $\theta = a\tau_E$ to be $2\pi$-periodic, giving $\beta = 2\pi/a$ — the same $2\pi$ as the modular continuation $\Delta = U(\Lambda_1(2\pi i))$. Originator citations: Unruh 1976 *Phys. Rev. D* 14 870 (heuristic detector derivation); Fulling 1973 *Phys. Rev. D* 7 2850 (Rindler quantisation, inequivalent vacuum); Bisognano & Wichmann 1975/1976 *J. Math. Phys.* 16 985 / 17 303 (modular-boost identification, wedge duality); Tomita 1967 / Takesaki 1970 *LNM* 128 (modular theory); Haag-Hugenholtz-Winnink 1967 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 5 215 (KMS condition); Sewell 1982 *Ann. Phys.* 141 201 (rigorous Unruh); Kay-Wald 1991 *Phys. Rep.* 207 49 (bifurcate-horizon generalisation); Borchers 1992 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 143 315 (commutation theorem from energy positivity); Gérard 2019 EMS Ch. 10 (textbook consolidation). Lean status: `none` — Mathlib lacks the Tomita-Takesaki modular apparatus ($\Delta$, $J$, modular automorphism group, the $J\mathcal{M}J = \mathcal{M}'$ theorem), the KMS predicate and the modular-KMS theorem, the Wightman-field / Reeh-Schlieder framework, and the Rindler-wedge geometry with its boost analytic continuation.

### general-relativity.hartle-hawking-unruh-states-schwarzschild

- **title**: Hartle-Hawking and Unruh states on Schwarzschild
- **unit**: `13.09.10`
- **prerequisites**: `general-relativity.globally-hyperbolic-lorentzian-manifolds`, `general-relativity.hadamard-states-wave-front-set-criterion`, `general-relativity.black-holes`, `general-relativity.unruh-effect-bisognano-wichmann`
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Hartle & Hawking, *Phys. Rev. D* 13 (1976) 2188; Israel, *Phys. Lett. A* 57 (1976) 107; Kay & Wald, *Phys. Rep.* 207 (1991) 49; Wald, *Quantum Field Theory in Curved Spacetime and Black Hole Thermodynamics* (Chicago, 1994), Ch. 5 and 8; Gérard, *Microlocal Analysis of Quantum Fields on Curved Spacetimes* (EMS, 2019), Ch. 10
  - intermediate: Wald, *Quantum Field Theory in Curved Spacetime and Black Hole Thermodynamics* (Chicago, 1994), Ch. 5 and 8; Kay & Wald, *Phys. Rep.* 207 (1991) 49; Gérard (EMS, 2019), Ch. 10
  - beginner: Wald, *Quantum Field Theory in Curved Spacetime and Black Hole Thermodynamics* (Chicago, 1994), Ch. 8; Birrell & Davies, *Quantum Fields in Curved Space* (Cambridge, 1982), Ch. 8
- **notes**: The Schwarzschild spacetime of mass $M$ admits three physically distinguished quasi-free states for a quantised scalar field, separated by their Hadamard regularity across the bifurcate Killing horizon of the Kruskal extension. The Boulware state (Boulware 1975 *Phys. Rev. D* 11 1404) is the vacuum of the static Killing field $\partial_t$; it is regular far from the hole but its stress tensor diverges in an orthonormal frame as one approaches either horizon, so it is non-Hadamard there and represents the exterior of a static star, not a black hole. The Hartle-Hawking state (Hartle & Hawking 1976 *Phys. Rev. D* 13 2188; Israel 1976 *Phys. Lett. A* 57 107 thermofield/Kruskal-doubling construction) is the unique state invariant under $\partial_t$ that is Hadamard on the whole Kruskal manifold, including both horizons; it is a KMS state at the Hawking temperature $T_H = \kappa/2\pi = 1/8\pi M$, with surface gravity $\kappa = 1/4M$, and describes a black hole in equilibrium with a thermal bath. The Unruh state (Unruh 1976 *Phys. Rev. D* 14 870) is Hadamard on the future horizon but not the past one; it models gravitational collapse and exhibits a steady outgoing thermal flux at infinity at $T_H$, the Hawking radiation. The discriminator is the Hadamard wave-front-set condition (13.09.03) restricted to a neighbourhood of the horizon. The equilibrium $\beta = 8\pi M$ follows from conical smoothness of the Euclidean Schwarzschild section: near $r = 2M$ the $(t_E, r)$ part of the metric is a plane in polar coordinates with angle $\kappa t_E$, smooth only if $t_E$ has period $2\pi/\kappa = 8\pi M$. This is the curved-space instance of the Bisognano-Wichmann / Kay-Wald mechanism (13.09.09): the horizon-generating Killing field is the modular flow of the exterior-wedge algebra in the Hartle-Hawking state, and the modular KMS property gives the Hawking temperature. Lean status: `none` — Mathlib lacks the Kruskal-extension Lorentzian geometry, the Hadamard / wave-front-set state framework on curved spacetimes, the bifurcate-Killing-horizon and surface-gravity definitions, and the Tomita-Takesaki modular apparatus needed to state the KMS characterisation.

### general-relativity.quantum-energy-inequalities-fewster

- **title**: Quantum energy inequalities (Fewster)
- **unit**: `13.09.11`
- **prerequisites**: `general-relativity.hadamard-states-wave-front-set-criterion`, `general-relativity.unruh-effect-bisognano-wichmann`, `general-relativity.hartle-hawking-unruh-states-schwarzschild`
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Ford, *Proc. Roy. Soc. A* 364 (1978) 227; Ford & Roman, *Phys. Rev. D* 51 (1995) 4277; Fewster & Eveson, *Phys. Rev. D* 58 (1998) 084010; Fewster, *Class. Quantum Grav.* 17 (2000) 1897; Fewster & Smith, *Ann. Henri Poincaré* 9 (2008) 425; Fewster, *Lectures on quantum energy inequalities* (arXiv:1208.5399, 2012)
  - intermediate: Fewster & Eveson, *Phys. Rev. D* 58 (1998) 084010; Fewster, *Class. Quantum Grav.* 17 (2000) 1897; Flanagan, *Phys. Rev. D* 56 (1997) 4922; Fewster, *Lectures on quantum energy inequalities* (arXiv:1208.5399, 2012)
  - beginner: Ford & Roman, *Sci. Am.* 282 (2000) 46 (negative energy and quantum interest); Fewster, *Lectures on quantum energy inequalities* (arXiv:1208.5399, 2012), §1
- **notes**: A quantum energy inequality (QEI) is a state-independent lower bound on the energy density of a quantum field weighted (smeared) along a worldline or against a sampling function. The pointwise weak energy condition $\langle T_{00}\rangle_\omega(x) \geq 0$ holds for all classical matter but fails for quantum fields: the renormalised energy density of the free scalar field is unbounded below at a point, and explicit Hadamard states (e.g. squeezed/two-mode states, or the Casimir vacuum between plates) realise arbitrarily negative $\langle :\!T_{00}\!:\rangle_\omega(x)$. Epstein-Glaser-Jaffe 1965 *Nuovo Cim.* 36 1016 first proved the energy density is not a positive operator. The QEI replaces the pointwise condition by a weighted average: for a real sampling function $g$ with $\int g^2 = 1$ and any Hadamard state $\omega$, $\int \langle :\!T_{00}\!:\rangle_\omega(\gamma(\tau))\,g(\tau)^2\,d\tau \geq -C[g]$, where the bound $C[g]$ depends on the sampling function but not on the state $\omega$. Ford 1978 *Proc. Roy. Soc. A* 364 227 introduced the first such inequality (motivated by the second law: a sustained negative flux would let a beam violate it); Ford-Roman 1995 *Phys. Rev. D* 51 4277 derived the worldline averaged-weak-energy "quantum inequality" $|\Delta E| \cdot \Delta\tau \lesssim 1$ form. Fewster-Eveson 1998 *Phys. Rev. D* 58 084010 gave the clean microlocal/Fourier derivation for the free scalar field: in the four-dimensional Minkowski vacuum the massless field obeys $\int \langle :\!T_{00}\!:\rangle_\omega(t,\mathbf{0})\,g(t)^2\,dt \geq -\frac{1}{16\pi^2}\int_{-\infty}^\infty \big(g''(t)\big)^2\,dt$ (equivalently $-\frac{1}{16\pi^2}\int (\widehat{g}'' )$-type momentum-space bound), with the sharp constant obtained from the positivity of the point-split two-point function and the Hadamard subtraction. Flanagan 1997 *Phys. Rev. D* 56 4922 obtained the optimal (sharp) constant in the $1{+}1$-dimensional case by a variational argument, $\int \langle T_{00}\rangle g^2 \geq -\frac{1}{24\pi}\int (g')^2/g^2$ type bound saturated by an explicit state. Fewster 2000 *Class. Quantum Grav.* 17 1897 generalised the derivation to arbitrary globally hyperbolic curved spacetimes via the Hadamard parametrix, producing a *difference* QEI bounding $\langle T_{00}\rangle_\omega - \langle T_{00}\rangle_{\omega_0}$ against a reference Hadamard state $\omega_0$; the bound is finite because the difference of two Hadamard two-point functions is smooth (Radzikowski microlocal spectrum condition, 13.09.03). Fewster-Smith 2008 *Ann. Henri Poincaré* 9 425 upgraded these to *absolute* QEIs, eliminating the reference state, using the locally covariant framework. Physical consequences: QEIs constrain exotic spacetimes — they forbid macroscopic traversable wormholes and warp drives sustained by quantum matter unless the negative-energy region is implausibly thin (Ford-Roman 1996 *Phys. Rev. D* 53 5496; Pfenning-Ford 1997), and they restrict violations of the classical singularity theorems (Fewster-Galloway 2011 *Class. Quantum Grav.* 28 125009 prove a Hawking-type singularity theorem under a smeared energy condition implied by a QEI). Originator citations: Epstein, Glaser & Jaffe 1965 *Nuovo Cim.* 36 1016 (unboundedness below); Ford 1978 *Proc. Roy. Soc. A* 364 227 (first quantum inequality); Ford-Roman 1995 *Phys. Rev. D* 51 4277 (worldline form); Flanagan 1997 *Phys. Rev. D* 56 4922 (sharp 2d constant); Fewster-Eveson 1998 *Phys. Rev. D* 58 084010 (microlocal derivation, 4d scalar); Fewster 2000 *Class. Quantum Grav.* 17 1897 (curved-spacetime difference QEI); Fewster-Smith 2008 *Ann. Henri Poincaré* 9 425 (absolute QEIs); Fewster 2012 arXiv:1208.5399 (lecture-note synthesis). Lean status: `none` — Mathlib lacks the renormalised stress-energy tensor of a quantum field, the Hadamard parametrix and point-splitting subtraction, the wave-front-set / microlocal-spectrum-condition apparatus on a Lorentzian manifold, and the operator-positivity arguments on the bosonic Fock space that the Fewster-Eveson derivation rests on.

### general-relativity.time-ordered-products-hollands-wald

- **title**: Time-ordered products and Hollands-Wald renormalisation on curved spacetimes
- **unit**: `13.09.07`
- **prerequisites**: `general-relativity.wick-polynomials-in-curved-spacetime`, `general-relativity.hadamard-states-wave-front-set-criterion`
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Hollands & Wald, *Comm. Math. Phys.* 223 (2001) 289; *Comm. Math. Phys.* 231 (2002) 309; *Comm. Math. Phys.* 237 (2003) 123; Brunetti & Fredenhagen, *Comm. Math. Phys.* 208 (2000) 623; Epstein & Glaser, *Ann. IHP* A 19 (1973) 211; Brunetti, Fredenhagen & Verch, *Comm. Math. Phys.* 237 (2003) 31; Gérard, *Microlocal Analysis of Quantum Fields on Curved Spacetimes* (EMS, 2019), Ch. 9
  - intermediate: Hollands & Wald, *Gen. Rel. Grav.* 42 (2010) 2009 / *Phys. Rep.* 574 (2015) 1 (arXiv:1401.2026); Gérard (EMS, 2019), Ch. 9; Rejzner, *Perturbative Algebraic Quantum Field Theory* (Springer, 2016), Ch. 6
  - beginner: Wald, *Quantum Field Theory in Curved Spacetime and Black Hole Thermodynamics* (Chicago, 1994), Ch. 4
- **notes**: Time-ordered products $T(\phi^{n_1}(x_1) \cdots \phi^{n_k}(x_k))$ of Wick polynomials at $k$ distinct spacetime points on a globally hyperbolic spacetime $(M, g)$, defined inductively as a hierarchy of operator-valued distributions on $M^k$ extending across the time-ordered diagonal. Epstein-Glaser 1973 *Ann. IHP* A 19 211 gave the original flat-space construction by causal perturbation theory: starting from $T_1(\phi(x)) = \phi(x)$, the time-ordered product $T_k$ at order $k$ is defined off the total diagonal $\Delta_k \subset M^k$ by Bogoliubov's recursion (writing $T_k$ as a sum of products of lower-order $T_j$'s in causally ordered subsets), then extended across $\Delta_k$ subject to the Stueckelberg-Petermann symmetry, causality, unitarity, and Poincaré-invariance axioms. The extension across the diagonal involves a finite renormalisation freedom in the form of local counterterms supported on $\Delta_k$, controlled by the scaling degree of the corresponding distribution. Brunetti-Fredenhagen 2000 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 208 623 adapted the construction to general globally hyperbolic spacetimes by replacing the Poincaré-invariance axiom with local covariance, using Hörmander's scaling-degree-of-a-distribution-at-a-submanifold theorem and the BFK 1996 microlocal-spectrum-condition machinery to control the extension. Hollands-Wald 2001 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 223 289 axiomatised the resulting time-ordered products with eight axioms: (i) locality / causality (factorisation when $\{x_1, \ldots, x_j\}$ and $\{x_{j+1}, \ldots, x_k\}$ are causally separated), (ii) general covariance under isometric embeddings of globally hyperbolic spacetimes, (iii) microlocal spectrum condition on the wave-front set of $T_k$ on $M^k$, (iv) scaling under metric / mass rescaling, (v) Leibniz rule under field derivatives, (vi) unitarity, (vii) smooth dependence on the metric, (viii) the principle of perturbative agreement (consistency of renormalisation choices across variations of the interaction Lagrangian). Hollands-Wald 2002 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 231 309 proved the existence theorem: time-ordered products satisfying axioms (i)–(viii) exist on every globally hyperbolic spacetime via the inductive Epstein-Glaser construction adapted to the curved-spacetime setting with Hadamard-parametrix scaling-degree control. Hollands-Wald 2003 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 237 123 classified the renormalisation freedom: at each loop order, the local covariant counterterms compatible with the axioms form a finite-dimensional vector space generated by curvature monomials of the appropriate scaling dimension (dimension-4 in four spacetime dimensions for the leading $\phi^4$-interaction counterterms, with basis $\{R^2, R_{\mu\nu}R^{\mu\nu}, R_{\mu\nu\rho\sigma}R^{\mu\nu\rho\sigma}, \Box R, m^4, m^2 R, m^2 V, V^2\}$ modulo the Bianchi-identity reductions). The classification is the curved-spacetime analogue of the BPHZ classification on flat space (Bogoliubov-Parasiuk 1957 *Acta Math.* 97 227; Hepp 1966 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 2 301; Zimmermann 1969 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 15 208), with curvature-monomial freedom replacing polynomial-coupling freedom. Worked example: one-loop bubble $T(\phi^4(x)\phi^4(y))$ in $\phi^4$-theory on a curved 4d background — the singular Hadamard contribution $H(x,y)^4$ from four Wick contractions is subtracted by an extension across the diagonal whose ambiguity is precisely the dimension-4 local covariant counterterm $\int(c_1 R^2 + c_2 R_{\mu\nu}R^{\mu\nu} + c_3 R_{\mu\nu\rho\sigma}R^{\mu\nu\rho\sigma})\, d\mathrm{vol}_g$ (the curvature analogue of the flat-space $\lambda^2$-coupling counterterm). Connections: the gauge-invariance question is solved by the Master Ward Identity of Dütsch-Fredenhagen 2003 *Rev. Math. Phys.* 15 1291 / Brennecke-Dütsch 2008, the curved-spacetime locally covariant version of the BRST identities; the locally covariant functor framework of Brunetti-Fredenhagen-Verch 2003 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 237 31 supplies the natural categorical setting. Originator citations: Stueckelberg & Petermann 1953 *Helv. Phys. Acta* 26 499 (originating the renormalisation-group viewpoint); Bogoliubov & Parasiuk 1957 *Acta Math.* 97 227 (the BP construction); Hepp 1966 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 2 301 (the H-proof of finiteness); Zimmermann 1969 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 15 208 (the forest formula); Epstein & Glaser 1973 *Ann. IHP* A 19 211 (the flat-space causal perturbation theory time-ordered products); Brunetti & Fredenhagen 2000 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 208 623 (microlocal Epstein-Glaser on a curved background); Hollands & Wald 2001 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 223 289 (locally covariant axiomatisation); Hollands & Wald 2002 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 231 309 (existence theorem); Hollands & Wald 2003 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 237 123 (renormalisation freedom classification); Brunetti, Fredenhagen & Verch 2003 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 237 31 (locally covariant functor framework); Hollands & Wald 2010 *Gen. Rel. Grav.* 42 2009 (review article); Hollands & Wald 2015 *Phys. Rep.* 574 1 (modern review, arXiv:1401.2026); Gérard 2019 EMS Ch. 9 (textbook consolidation). Lean status: `none` — same gap chain as 13.09.06 (Synge world function, parallel-propagator bi-tensor framework, Hadamard transport equations, BFV locally covariant functor framework), plus the additional Hörmander scaling-degree-at-a-submanifold theorem and the inductive Epstein-Glaser extension-across-the-diagonal machinery, both absent from Mathlib.

### general-relativity.hadamard-states-pseudo-differential-calculus

- **title**: Hadamard states by pseudo-differential calculus (Gérard-Wrochna)
- **unit**: `13.09.05`
- **prerequisites**: `general-relativity.existence-of-hadamard-states-fnw-deformation`, `microlocal-analysis.pseudo-differential-operators`
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Gérard, *Microlocal Analysis of Quantum Fields on Curved Spacetimes* (EMS, 2019), Ch. 7-8; Gérard & Wrochna, *Comm. Math. Phys.* 325 (2014) 713; Gérard & Wrochna, *Ann. Henri Poincaré* 18 (2017) 2715; Junker & Schrohe, *Ann. Henri Poincaré* 3 (2002) 1113; Seeley, *Proc. Symp. Pure Math.* 10 (1967) 288
  - intermediate: Gérard (EMS, 2019), Ch. 7; Gérard-Wrochna 2014 §3-§4; Bär-Ginoux-Pfäffle (EMS, 2007), Ch. 3-4 (free PDF arXiv:0806.1036)
  - beginner: Wald, *Quantum Field Theory in Curved Spacetime and Black Hole Thermodynamics* (Chicago, 1994), Ch. 4 (physicist-side framing of the state-selection question)
- **notes**: Direct construction of Hadamard states on a globally hyperbolic spacetime $(M, g)$ via pseudo-differential calculus on a Cauchy hypersurface $\Sigma$. Strategy (Gérard-Wrochna 2014 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 325 713): apply the Bernal-Sánchez splitting $M \cong \mathbb{R} \times \Sigma$ with metric $g = -\beta^2 dt^2 + h_t$, take the time-zero slice $\Sigma$ with induced Riemannian metric $h := h_0$, and form the spatial Klein-Gordon operator $D_h := -\Delta_h + m^2 + V$ on $L^2(\Sigma, d\mathrm{vol}_h)$. Assume $D_h \geq c > 0$ for some constant $c$. By Seeley 1967 *Proc. Symp. Pure Math.* 10, the positive self-adjoint square root $\sqrt{D_h}$ exists by the spectral theorem and admits a pseudo-differential representative: there is a $\Psi^1$-pseudo-differential operator $\epsilon \in \Psi^1(\Sigma)$ with principal symbol $\sigma_1(\epsilon)(x, \xi) = \sqrt{p_h(x, \xi)}$ where $p_h(x, \xi) = h^{ij}(x)\xi_i \xi_j$ is the principal symbol of $-\Delta_h$, and with $\epsilon^2 - D_h \in \Psi^{-\infty}(\Sigma)$ smoothing (parametrix). From the pair $(\epsilon, \sigma_h)$ of operator and Cauchy-data symplectic form, construct the quasi-free covariance $\eta(\Phi_1, \Phi_2) = \frac{1}{2}\int_\Sigma (u_0^{(1)} \epsilon u_0^{(2)} + u_1^{(1)} \epsilon^{-1} u_1^{(2)})\, d\mathrm{vol}_h$ on Cauchy data $\Phi_j = (u_0^{(j)}, u_1^{(j)})$, giving the two-point function $\omega_2 = \eta + \frac{i}{2}\sigma_h$. **Theorem (Gérard-Wrochna 2014):** the resulting state $\omega$ is Hadamard, i.e. $\mathrm{WF}(\omega_2)$ on $M \times M$ equals the future-pointing half of the bicharacteristic relation of $g$ (the Radzikowski criterion). **Proof mechanism:** the principal-symbol identity $\sigma_1(\epsilon) = \sqrt{p_h}$ encodes the future-pointing positive-frequency selection on $T^*\Sigma$; pushing forward via the Cauchy evolution along the Klein-Gordon equation, propagation of singularities (Hörmander, applied to $P = \Box_g + m^2 + V$) transports the wave-front-set support from $T^*\Sigma$ to its future light-cone projection on $T^*M$. The smoothing-error term $\epsilon^2 - D_h \in \Psi^{-\infty}(\Sigma)$ produces only smooth corrections to the two-point function, so the Hadamard wave-front-set support is exact. Comparison with FNW (13.09.04): no auxiliary ultrastatic spacetime, no smooth one-parameter family of metrics; everything happens on the target $(M, g)$ with $\Sigma$ as working data. Trade-off: the construction requires the full pseudo-differential calculus on $\Sigma$ and the Seeley complex-power machinery, while FNW uses only the explicit ultrastatic-ground-state spectral construction. The Gérard-Wrochna 2017 *Ann. Henri Poincaré* 18 2715 refinement extends the construction to asymptotically static spacetimes and establishes the Hadamard property of in/out states for scattering theory on a curved background. Symbol-class characterisation of the microlocal vacuum: the choice of positive square root $\sqrt{p_h}$ (versus the negative root $-\sqrt{p_h}$) picks out the future-pointing half of the bicharacteristic relation, distinguishing the particle wave-front-set component from the antiparticle component; the entire freedom in Hadamard-state construction is parametrised by lower-order pseudo-differential corrections to $\epsilon$, all giving states that differ by smooth functions. Worked examples: Minkowski case recovers the standard Poincaré vacuum (which on de Sitter becomes the Bunch-Davies state in the appropriate conformal frame); applied to FLRW spacetimes the construction gives explicit Hadamard initial data underlying the adiabatic vacua of Junker-Schrohe 2002 *Ann. Henri Poincaré* 3 1113; applied to the Schwarzschild exterior with bifurcate Killing horizon, gives the Hadamard wave-front-set structure underlying the Hartle-Hawking state. The framework supplies a natural family of approximate Hadamard states with controlled smoothing-error bounds, useful for explicit cosmological-perturbation and Hawking-radiation calculations. Originator citations: Seeley 1967 *Proc. Symp. Pure Math.* 10 288 (complex powers of an elliptic operator; the pseudo-differential square-root machinery); Junker 1996 *Rev. Math. Phys.* 8 1091 (precursor adiabatic-vacuum / pseudo-differential construction); Junker-Schrohe 2002 *Ann. Henri Poincaré* 3 1113 (adiabatic-vacuum refinement for FLRW); Gérard-Wrochna 2014 *Comm. Math. Phys.* 325 713 (direct construction by pseudo-differential calculus); Gérard-Wrochna 2017 *Ann. Henri Poincaré* 18 2715 (in/out-state Hadamard property on asymptotically static spacetimes); Gérard-Stoskopf 2021 *Lett. Math. Phys.* 111 95 (curved-spacetime extensions); Hörmander vol. III §18 §26 (the microlocal-analysis machinery); Gérard 2019 EMS Ch. 7 (modern textbook consolidation). Lean status: `none` — same gap chain as 13.09.03 and 13.09.04, plus the additional pseudo-differential symbol calculus on a Riemannian manifold absent from Mathlib.

### quantum-mechanics.born-approximation-lippmann-schwinger

- **title**: Born approximation and the Lippmann-Schwinger equation
- **unit**: `12.08.02`
- **prerequisites**: `quantum-mechanics.hilbert-space-formalism`, `quantum.spherical-harmonics-legendre-polynomials`, `quantum-mechanics.time-independent-perturbation-theory`, `quantum.time-dependent-perturbation-theory`
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Landau & Lifshitz, Quantum Mechanics, Vol. 3 (Pergamon, 1977), Ch. XVII §§125-127; Born 1926 *Zeits. f. Physik* 37, 38; Lippmann & Schwinger 1950 *Phys. Rev.* 79, 469
  - intermediate: Sakurai & Napolitano, Modern Quantum Mechanics, 2e (2017), Ch. 6.1-6.3; Newton, Scattering Theory of Waves and Particles, 2e (1982), Ch. 7-9
  - beginner: Griffiths & Schroeter, Introduction to Quantum Mechanics, 3e (2018), Ch. 11 intro
- **notes**: The stationary scattering state has the asymptotic form $\psi \sim e^{ikz} + f(\theta) e^{ikr}/r$, with differential cross-section $d\sigma/d\Omega = |f(\theta)|^2$. The Lippmann-Schwinger equation $\psi = \phi + \hat G_0^{+} V \psi$ recasts the Schrödinger equation as an integral equation with the outgoing-wave free Green's function $G_0^{+}(\mathbf r, \mathbf r') = -(m/2\pi\hbar^2) e^{ik|\mathbf r - \mathbf r'|}/|\mathbf r - \mathbf r'|$ fixed by the $+i\epsilon$ (Sommerfeld radiation) boundary condition. Iterating once gives the first Born amplitude $f_{\text{Born}}(\mathbf q) = -(m/2\pi\hbar^2)\int V(\mathbf r) e^{i\mathbf q\cdot\mathbf r}\,d^3r = -(m/2\pi\hbar^2)\tilde V(\mathbf q)$, the Fourier transform of the potential at momentum transfer $\mathbf q = \mathbf k - \mathbf k'$, $q = 2k\sin(\theta/2)$. Worked example: the Yukawa potential $V = V_0 e^{-\mu r}/r$ gives $f = -(2mV_0/\hbar^2)/(q^2 + \mu^2)$, whose $\mu\to 0$ Coulomb limit reproduces the Rutherford cross-section $d\sigma/d\Omega = (2mZ_1Z_2 e^2/\hbar^2 q^2)^2 \cdot (\hbar^2/2m)^2 \sim \sin^{-4}(\theta/2)$. Validity criterion $|V| \ll \hbar^2/ma^2$ (low energy) or the high-energy relaxation $(mV_0 a/\hbar^2 k)\ln(ka)\ll 1$. The Born series $f = \sum_n f^{(n)}$ is the Neumann series of the integral equation, convergent when the spectral radius of $\hat G_0^{+} V$ is below one; the optical theorem $\sigma_{\text{tot}} = (4\pi/k)\,\mathrm{Im}\,f(0)$ is exact (unitarity) and fails order-by-order in the truncated Born series — the leading real first Born amplitude has zero forward imaginary part, recovered only at second order. Originator chain: Born 1926 *Zeits. f. Physik* 37 (the perturbative amplitude) and 38 (probability interpretation); Lippmann & Schwinger 1950 *Phys. Rev.* 79 469 (the integral-equation / Green's-function form); Gell-Mann & Goldberger 1953 *Phys. Rev.* 91 398 (formal time-dependent derivation, Møller operators). Lean status `none`: Mathlib lacks the resolvent of the free Schrödinger operator on $L^2(\mathbb{R}^3)$, the limiting-absorption principle that supplies the $+i\epsilon$ outgoing Green's function, the Fredholm/Neumann-series theory for the Lippmann-Schwinger operator on weighted $L^2$ spaces, and the Agmon-Kato-Kuroda estimates needed to prove Born-series convergence.


### quantum-mechanics.partial-wave-expansion-phase-shifts

- **title**: Partial-wave expansion and phase shifts
- **unit**: `12.08.03`
- **prerequisites**: `quantum-mechanics.hilbert-space-formalism`, `quantum.spherical-harmonics-legendre-polynomials`, `quantum-mechanics.time-independent-perturbation-theory`, `quantum-mechanics.born-approximation-lippmann-schwinger`
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Landau & Lifshitz, Quantum Mechanics, Vol. 3 (Pergamon, 1977), Ch. XVII §§122-124; Faxén & Holtsmark 1927 *Zeits. f. Physik* 45, 307; Levinson 1949 *Kgl. Danske Vid. Selsk.* 25
  - intermediate: Sakurai & Napolitano, Modern Quantum Mechanics, 2e (2017), Ch. 6.4-6.6; Newton, Scattering Theory of Waves and Particles, 2e (1982), Ch. 11
  - beginner: Griffiths & Schroeter, Introduction to Quantum Mechanics, 3e (2018), Ch. 11.3
- **notes**: For a central potential the stationary scattering state expands in Legendre polynomials, $\psi = \sum_\ell (2\ell+1) i^\ell R_\ell(r) P_\ell(\cos\theta)$, decoupling the Schrödinger equation into radial equations for each angular momentum $\ell$. Outside the range of the potential the radial function is a combination of free spherical waves whose asymptotic form $R_\ell(r) \sim \sin(kr - \ell\pi/2 + \delta_\ell)/kr$ defines the **phase shift** $\delta_\ell$ — the entire effect of the potential on partial wave $\ell$ at energy $E = \hbar^2 k^2/2m$. The scattering amplitude is $f(\theta) = (1/2ik)\sum_\ell (2\ell+1)(e^{2i\delta_\ell}-1)P_\ell(\cos\theta)$, and integrating $|f|^2$ over solid angle gives the total cross-section $\sigma = (4\pi/k^2)\sum_\ell (2\ell+1)\sin^2\delta_\ell$ via Legendre orthogonality. The optical theorem $\sigma = (4\pi/k)\,\mathrm{Im}\,f(0)$ is a one-line consequence with $P_\ell(1)=1$. Low-energy limit: only the s-wave survives, $\delta_0 \approx -ka$ with $a$ the **scattering length** $a = -\lim_{k\to 0}\delta_0/k$, and $\sigma \to 4\pi a^2$. Hard-sphere worked example: $\delta_\ell = -\arctan[j_\ell(ka)/n_\ell(ka)]$, giving $\delta_0 = -ka$ and the low-energy $\sigma = 4\pi a^2$ (four times the geometric cross-section). Levinson's theorem $\delta_\ell(0) = n_\ell \pi$ ties the zero-energy phase shift to the number $n_\ell$ of bound states of angular momentum $\ell$. A resonance is a rapid increase of $\delta_\ell$ through $\pi/2$, locally $\delta_\ell \approx \arctan[(\Gamma/2)/(E_r - E)]$, the Breit-Wigner form. This is the angular-momentum (low-energy) complement of the Born/momentum-transfer (high-energy) amplitude of `quantum-mechanics.born-approximation-lippmann-schwinger` (12.08.02). Originator chain: Rayleigh 1894 *Theory of Sound* (partial waves for acoustic scattering); Faxén & Holtsmark 1927 *Zeits. f. Physik* 45 (quantum partial-wave analysis); Levinson 1949 *Kgl. Danske Vid. Selsk.* 25 (the bound-state theorem); Breit & Wigner 1936 *Phys. Rev.* 49, 519 (the resonance line shape). Lean status `none`: Mathlib lacks spherical Bessel functions, the asymptotics of the free radial Schrödinger operator, and the analytic structure of the partial-wave S-matrix needed for Levinson's theorem.


### quantum-mechanics.inelastic-collisions-distorted-wave-born

- **title**: Inelastic collisions and the distorted-wave Born approximation
- **unit**: `12.08.04`
- **prerequisites**: `quantum-mechanics.born-approximation-lippmann-schwinger`, `quantum-mechanics.partial-wave-expansion-phase-shifts`, `quantum.time-dependent-perturbation-theory`
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Landau & Lifshitz, Quantum Mechanics, Vol. 3 (Pergamon, 1977), Ch. XVIII §§131-136; Bethe 1930 *Annalen der Physik* 5, 325; Mott & Massey, *The Theory of Atomic Collisions*, 3e (1965)
  - intermediate: Sakurai & Napolitano, Modern Quantum Mechanics, 2e (2017), Ch. 6.7-6.8; Newton, Scattering Theory of Waves and Particles, 2e (1982), Ch. 16-17
  - beginner: Griffiths & Schroeter, Introduction to Quantum Mechanics, 3e (2018), Ch. 11.4
- **notes**: When the target carries internal degrees of freedom, scattering can leave it in an excited state: the collision is **inelastic**, and energy conservation $E_i + \varepsilon_n = E_f + \varepsilon_{n'}$ ties the final relative momentum $k_f$ to the excitation energy. The asymptotic state is a sum over open **channels** $|n\rangle \otimes (\text{relative motion})$. The exact amplitude for the transition $i \to f$ is the **transition matrix element** $T_{fi} = \langle \phi_f | V | \psi_i^{(+)}\rangle$ between asymptotic channel states $\phi_f$ and the full scattering state $\psi_i^{(+)}$. The two-potential (Gell-Mann–Goldberger) decomposition $V = U + W$ with $U$ an elastic distorting potential gives the exact rearrangement $T_{fi} = \langle \phi_f | U | \chi_i^{(+)}\rangle\delta_{fi} + \langle \chi_f^{(-)} | W | \psi_i^{(+)}\rangle$; replacing $\psi_i^{(+)} \to \chi_i^{(+)}$ yields the **distorted-wave Born approximation** $T_{fi}^{\text{DWBA}} = \langle \chi_f^{(-)} | W | \chi_i^{(+)}\rangle$, first order in the residual interaction $W$ but exact in the elastic distortion. The plane-wave (first Born) inelastic amplitude factorises into a kinematic Coulomb factor and a **form factor** $F_{n'n}(\mathbf q) = \langle n' | \sum_j e^{i\mathbf q\cdot\mathbf r_j}|n\rangle$. Worked example: $1s\to 2p$ excitation of hydrogen by electron impact. Fast collisions: **Bethe theory** — the cross-section behaves as $\sigma \sim (A_n/v^2)\ln(B_n v^2)$, with $A_n$ fixed by the **generalised oscillator strength** $f_n(q) = (2m\,\varepsilon_n/\hbar^2 q^2)|F_{n0}(\mathbf q)|^2$, which obeys the **Bethe sum rule** $\sum_n f_n(q) = Z$ (Thomas-Reiche-Kuhn at $q\to 0$). Integrated over collisions this gives the stopping power $-dE/dx = (4\pi N Z e^4/m v^2)\ln(2mv^2/I)$ with mean excitation energy $I$. **Wigner threshold laws**: just above an inelastic threshold $\sigma_{n\to n'} \propto k_f^{2\ell'+1}$ for short-range and a modified law for Coulomb final states. The **generalised optical theorem** expresses S-matrix unitarity across all open channels, summing elastic and reaction cross-sections to the total. The close-coupling equations $(\nabla^2 + k_{n'}^2)F_{n'} = \sum_n U_{n'n} F_n$ truncate the channel sum. Originator chain: Bethe 1930 *Annalen der Physik* 5 325 (the log term, sum rule, stopping power); Mott & Massey 1933/1965 *Theory of Atomic Collisions* (the multichannel framework and DWBA); Gell-Mann & Goldberger 1953 *Phys. Rev.* 91 398 (the two-potential formula); Wigner 1948 *Phys. Rev.* 73 1002 (threshold laws). This is the multichannel extension of `quantum-mechanics.born-approximation-lippmann-schwinger` (12.08.02) and `quantum-mechanics.partial-wave-expansion-phase-shifts` (12.08.03). Lean status `none`: Mathlib lacks the multichannel (matrix-valued) resolvent, the channel-Green's-function decomposition, the form-factor Fourier-transform machinery on the hydrogen eigenbasis, and the Wigner threshold-law analytic-continuation arguments.

### quantum-mechanics.identical-particles

- **title**: Identical particles and many-body quantum mechanics
- **unit**: `12.09.01`
- **prerequisites**: `quantum-mechanics.angular-momentum-su2`, `quantum-mechanics.hilbert-space-formalism`
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Fetter and Walecka, *Quantum Theory of Many-Particle Systems* (2003); Abrikosov, Gorkov, Dzyaloshinski, *Methods of Quantum Field Theory in Statistical Physics* (1975)
  - intermediate: Sakurai, *Modern Quantum Mechanics* (2020), Ch. 7
  - beginner: Griffiths, *Introduction to Quantum Mechanics* (2018), Ch. 5
- **notes**: Symmetrization and antisymmetrization postulates; Slater determinants; Pauli exclusion principle; second quantization; Hartree-Fock method; Bose-Einstein condensation; BCS superconductivity.


### quantum-mechanics.exchange-interaction-helium

- **title**: Exchange interaction and the helium atom
- **unit**: `12.09.02`
- **prerequisites**: `quantum-mechanics.hilbert-space-formalism`, `quantum-mechanics.operators-observables-hermiticity`, `quantum-mechanics.angular-momentum-su2`, `quantum-mechanics.time-independent-perturbation-theory`, `quantum-mechanics.fermionic-fock-space-pauli-anticommutators`
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Landau, L. D. & Lifshitz, E. M., *Quantum Mechanics: Non-Relativistic Theory*, 3rd ed. (Pergamon, 1977), Ch. IX §§62–66; Bethe, H. A. & Salpeter, E. E., *Quantum Mechanics of One- and Two-Electron Atoms* (Springer, 1957), §§32–33
  - intermediate: Landau & Lifshitz, *Quantum Mechanics*, Ch. IX §§62–63; Bransden & Joachain, *Physics of Atoms and Molecules*, 2nd ed. (Pearson, 2003), Ch. 7
  - beginner: Griffiths, *Introduction to Quantum Mechanics*, 2nd ed. (Pearson, 2005), §5.1–5.2
- **notes**: The antisymmetrisation postulate for two electrons; symmetric-spatial ⊗ spin-singlet versus antisymmetric-spatial ⊗ spin-triplet; the two-electron Slater determinant; first-order degenerate perturbation theory for the helium ground and excited states; the Coulomb integral $J$ and exchange integral $K$ with $E_\pm = J \pm K$; ortho/para-helium; the Dirac spin Hamiltonian $\hat H = -2K\,\mathbf S_1\cdot\mathbf S_2$ from the projector identity $\mathbf S_1\cdot\mathbf S_2 = \tfrac12(\mathbf S^2 - \mathbf S_1^2 - \mathbf S_2^2)$ with singlet/triplet eigenvalues $-\tfrac34, +\tfrac14$; Heitler-London covalent bond; the Heisenberg model and the exchange origin of ferromagnetism. Conceptually upstream of the Hartree-Fock exchange operator (12.09.03).


### quantum-mechanics.hartree-fock-self-consistent-field

- **title**: Hartree-Fock self-consistent field method
- **unit**: `12.09.03`
- **prerequisites**: `quantum-mechanics.hilbert-space-formalism`, `quantum-mechanics.operators-observables-hermiticity`, `quantum-mechanics.time-independent-perturbation-theory`, `quantum-mechanics.fermionic-fock-space-pauli-anticommutators`
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Landau, L. D. & Lifshitz, E. M., *Quantum Mechanics: Non-Relativistic Theory*, 3rd ed. (Pergamon, 1977), Ch. X §§69–70; Szabo, A. & Ostlund, N. S., *Modern Quantum Chemistry* (Dover, 1996), Chs. 2–3
  - intermediate: Szabo & Ostlund, *Modern Quantum Chemistry* (Dover, 1996), Ch. 3; Slater, J. C., *Quantum Theory of Atomic Structure*, Vol. I (McGraw-Hill, 1960)
  - beginner: Griffiths, *Introduction to Quantum Mechanics*, 2nd ed. (Pearson, 2005), Ch. 7 (variational principle) and Ch. 5 (multi-electron atoms)
- **notes**: The variational principle on a single Slater determinant; Hartree direct potential and the non-local exchange operator; the Hartree-Fock integro-differential eigenvalue equations; Koopmans' theorem; self-consistent-field iteration; Roothaan-Hall matrix equations; correlation energy as the defect of the single-determinant ansatz.


### quantum-mechanics.multi-electron-atomic-structure-ls-coupling

- **title**: Multi-electron atomic structure and LS coupling
- **unit**: `12.09.04`
- **prerequisites**: `quantum-mechanics.angular-momentum-su2`, `quantum.addition-angular-momenta-clebsch-gordan`, `quantum-mechanics.time-independent-perturbation-theory`, `quantum-mechanics.exchange-interaction-helium`, `quantum-mechanics.fermionic-fock-space-pauli-anticommutators`
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Landau, L. D. & Lifshitz, E. M., *Quantum Mechanics: Non-Relativistic Theory*, 3rd ed. (Pergamon, 1977), Ch. X §§71–75 (the periodic system, atomic levels, the Zeeman effect); Condon, E. U. & Shortley, G. H., *The Theory of Atomic Spectra* (Cambridge, 1935), Chs. VI–VIII
  - intermediate: Bransden, B. H. & Joachain, C. J., *Physics of Atoms and Molecules*, 2nd ed. (Pearson, 2003), Ch. 7; Cowan, R. D., *The Theory of Atomic Structure and Spectra* (UC Press, 1981), Chs. 4–5
  - beginner: Griffiths, *Introduction to Quantum Mechanics*, 2nd ed. (Pearson, 2005), §5.2 (the periodic table); Eisberg & Resnick, *Quantum Physics*, 2nd ed. (Wiley, 1985), Ch. 10
- **notes**: The central-field approximation as the zeroth-order single-electron picture; electron configuration $1s^2 2s^2 2p^k$ notation; the total operators $\hat{\mathbf L} = \sum_i \hat{\mathbf l}_i$ and $\hat{\mathbf S} = \sum_i \hat{\mathbf s}_i$; the Russell-Saunders term symbol ${}^{2S+1}L_J$; enumeration of the Pauli-allowed terms of $p^2$ (${}^1S, {}^1D, {}^3P$) and of $d^n$; Hund's three rules; derivation of Hund's first two rules from the exchange-energy argument (maximise $S$, then $L$, to maximise the exchange lowering through the integral $K$); the Landé interval rule $E(J)-E(J-1)=AJ$ from the spin-orbit operator $\zeta\,\hat{\mathbf L}\cdot\hat{\mathbf S}$; ground-term worked examples (carbon ${}^3P_0$, nitrogen ${}^4S_{3/2}$, oxygen ${}^3P_2$); the jj-coupling crossover in heavy atoms. Originator chain: Russell & Saunders 1925 *Astrophys. J.* 61 (the LS term scheme); Hund 1925–1927 *Zeits. f. Physik* 33, 36, 40 (the empirical spectral rules); Landé 1923 *Zeits. f. Physik* 15 (the interval rule and the $g$-factor); Slater 1929 *Phys. Rev.* 34 (the determinantal multiplet theory). Conceptually downstream of the two-electron exchange interaction (12.09.02). Lean status: `none` — Mathlib lacks the antisymmetrised $N$-electron configuration space, the term-symbol enumeration as a representation-theoretic branching, and the spin-orbit interval rule.


### quantum-mechanics.born-oppenheimer-diatomic-molecule

- **title**: Diatomic molecule and the Born-Oppenheimer approximation
- **unit**: `12.09.05`
- **prerequisites**: `quantum-mechanics.hilbert-space-formalism`, `quantum-mechanics.hydrogen-atom-bound-states`, `quantum-mechanics.angular-momentum-su2`, `quantum-mechanics.hartree-fock-self-consistent-field`
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Landau, L. D. & Lifshitz, E. M., *Quantum Mechanics: Non-Relativistic Theory*, 3rd ed. (Pergamon, 1977), Ch. XI §§77–82; Born, M. & Huang, K., *Dynamical Theory of Crystal Lattices* (Oxford, 1954), Appendices VII–VIII
  - intermediate: Bransden, B. H. & Joachain, C. J., *Physics of Atoms and Molecules*, 2nd ed. (Pearson, 2003), Ch. 9; Atkins, P. & Friedman, R., *Molecular Quantum Mechanics*, 5th ed. (Oxford, 2011), Ch. 10
  - beginner: Griffiths, D. J., *Introduction to Quantum Mechanics*, 2nd ed. (Pearson, 2005), §7.3 (the hydrogen molecule ion); Atkins, P., *Physical Chemistry*, Ch. 12 (molecular spectra)
- **notes**: Separation of electronic and nuclear motion via the mass disparity; the BO small parameter $\kappa = (m_e/M_N)^{1/4}$ organising the adiabatic expansion; the BO ansatz $\Psi(\mathbf r,\mathbf R)=\chi(\mathbf R)\phi(\mathbf r;\mathbf R)$; the clamped-nucleus electronic Schrödinger equation; the effective nuclear potential $E_{\mathrm{el}}(\mathbf R)$ and equilibrium bond length; rovibrational levels (harmonic, Morse anharmonicity, rigid rotor); breakdown at conical intersections and the non-adiabatic derivative couplings; the diagonal Born-Oppenheimer correction.

### chemistry.stoichiometry-gas-laws

- **title**: Stoichiometry and gas laws
- **unit**: `14.03.01`
- **prerequisites**: (none)
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Atkins Ch. 1 + statistical-mechanics perspective on the ideal gas law
  - intermediate: Atkins & de Paula, *Physical Chemistry*, 12e (2023), Ch. 1
  - beginner: Tro, *Chemistry: A Molecular Approach*, 6e (2023), Ch. 4-5
- **notes**: Stoichiometry (mole ratios, limiting reagent, theoretical yield) and the ideal gas law PV = nRT with Dalton's law of partial pressures. Intermediate: limiting-reagent theorem with extent-of-reaction proof, van der Waals corrections. Master: kinetic theory derivation of PV = nRT from molecular collisions, Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution and characteristic speeds, equipartition theorem, virial expansion with statistical-mechanical B(T) expression and Mayer cluster-integral proof, cubic equations of state (Redlich-Kwong, Peng-Robinson), Graham's law of effusion, mean free path, Joule-Thomson coefficient and inversion temperature, principle of corresponding states, generalised compressibility charts. Originators: Richter, Dalton, Avogadro, Boyle, Charles, Clausius, Maxwell, Boltzmann, van der Waals, Andrews, Graham, Joule-Thomson, Kamerlingh Onnes. Cycle 4 Track B deepening from 3512w to 8004w.

### physical-chemistry.mo-theory-homonuclear-diatomics

- **title**: Molecular orbital theory for homonuclear diatomics
- **unit**: `14.05.02`
- **prerequisites**: `linear-algebra.eigenvalue-eigenvector`, `rep-theory.group-representation`, `rep-theory.character`
- **tier_anchors**:

### chemistry.functional-groups-nomenclature

- **title**: Functional groups and nomenclature
- **unit**: `15.02.01`
- **prerequisites**: (none)
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: IUPAC — Nomenclature of Organic Chemistry: IUPAC Recommendations and Preferred Names 2013 (Blue Book); Favre & Powell — Nomenclature of Organic Chemistry: IUPAC Recommendations and Preferred Names 2013 (RSC, 2014)
  - intermediate: Clayden, Greeves & Warren — Organic Chemistry 2nd ed. Ch. 2–3 (Functional groups, nomenclature); Bruice — Organic Chemistry, nomenclature chapters
  - beginner: Clayden, Greeves & Warren — Organic Chemistry 2nd ed. Ch. 2 (Functional groups)
- **notes**: (to be filled during production)


### chemistry.acids-bases-organic

- **title**: Acids and bases in organic chemistry
- **unit**: `15.03.01`
- **prerequisites**: `chemistry.functional-groups-nomenclature`
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: March's Advanced Organic Chemistry 7th ed. Ch. 8; Anslyn & Dougherty — Modern Physical Organic Chemistry Ch. 1 (thermodynamics of acids and bases)
  - intermediate: Clayden, Greeves & Warren — Organic Chemistry 2nd ed. Ch. 8; Bruice — Organic Chemistry, acid-base chapters
  - beginner: Clayden, Greeves & Warren — Organic Chemistry 2nd ed. Ch. 8 (Acids and bases)
- **notes**: Deepened Cycle-A Track-B unit. Master tier covers four substantive H2 sections: Hammett equation with sigma+/sigma- and Yukawa-Tsuno extensions; acid-base catalysis with Brønsted law and enzymatic serine-protease examples; quantitative pKa prediction via Taft sigma-I/sigma-R separation and DFT thermodynamic cycles; borderline acid-base phenomena including carbon acids (pKa > 20), kinetic vs thermodynamic acidity, KIE on proton transfer, and superacids (Hammett H_0 function). Key originators: Brønsted 1923, Hammett 1937, Taft 1952, Olah 1972. Bridge paragraph connects pKa-comparison to leaving-group ability in [15.04.02] and enolate formation in [15.07.01].

### chemistry.symmetry-group-theory-chemistry

- **title**: Symmetry and group theory in chemistry
- **unit**: `16.02.01`
- **prerequisites**: `chemistry.periodic-trends-quantified`
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Cotton — Chemical Applications of Group Theory, 3rd ed.; Bishop — Group Theory and Chemistry
  - intermediate: Housecroft & Sharpe — Inorganic Chemistry, Ch. 4; Miessler, Fischer & Tarr — Inorganic Chemistry, Ch. 4
  - beginner: Kettle — Symmetry and Structure; Crash Course Chemistry — Molecular Geometry
- **notes**: Point groups, character tables, and irreducible representations applied to molecular symmetry. Covers SALC construction via projection operators, vibrational-mode decomposition, spectroscopic selection rules (Laporte, mutual exclusion), the Jahn-Teller theorem, crystallographic restriction, correlation tables, and factor-group analysis. Proves the decomposition formula for reducible representations, the crystallographic restriction theorem, and the direct-product identity for the totally symmetric irrep. Originator chain: Hessel 1830, Bethe 1929, Wigner 1931, Jahn-Teller 1937, Cotton 1963. Shipped at ~8100w with `lean_status: none`.

  - beginner: Atkins / Shriver — Chemistry: A Very Short Introduction (Beginner sections); Crash Course Chemistry — Transition Metals

  - intermediate: Housecroft & Sharpe — Inorganic Chemistry, Ch. 20; Crabtree — Organometallic Chemistry of the Transition Metals, Ch. 1–4
  - beginner: Crash Course Chemistry — Organometallics; Crabtree Ch. 1 introductory material

### chemistry.bioinorganic-metalloenzymes

- **title**: Bioinorganic chemistry
- **unit**: `16.06.01`
- **prerequisites**: `chemistry.coordination-chemistry`, `chemistry.enzyme-mechanism`
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Lippard & Berg — Principles of Bioinorganic Chemistry; Berg, Tymoczko & Stryer — Biochemistry Ch. 7; Bertini, Gray, Stiefel & Valentine — Biological Inorganic Chemistry
  - intermediate: Lippard, S. J. & Berg, J. M. — Principles of Bioinorganic Chemistry; Frausto da Silva & Williams — The Biological Chemistry of the Elements
  - beginner: Crash Course Biology — Enzymes; Lippard & Berg introductory chapters
- **notes**: Metalloenzymes and metalloproteins across the periodic table. Covers haemoglobin cooperative binding (Hill equation, MWC allosteric model, Perutz stereochemical mechanism, Bohr effect, 2,3-BPG), iron-sulphur clusters and Marcus electron-transfer theory, photosynthetic water oxidation (Kok S-state cycle, Mn4CaO5 structure), zinc enzymes (carbonic anhydrase, carboxypeptidase, alcohol dehydrogenase, Lewis acid catalysis), metal ion homeostasis (metallochaperones, IRP/IRE system, Irving-Williams selectivity), vitamin B12 organometallic biochemistry (adenosylcobalamin radical rearrangements, methylcobalamin methyl transfer), and cytochrome P450 hydroxylation (Compound I, oxygen-rebound mechanism). Key theorem: Hill equation proof for cooperative binding. Prerequisites: coordination chemistry [16.04.01]. Deepened in Cycle 4 Track B to 8000+ words with 7 substantive Master H2 sections.


### chemistry.solid-state-chemistry

- **title**: Solid-state chemistry
- **unit**: `16.07.01`
- **prerequisites**: (none)
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: West, A. R. — Solid State Chemistry; Kittel — Introduction to Solid State Physics, Ch. 1–7
  - intermediate: Housecroft & Sharpe — Inorganic Chemistry, Ch. 6; West — Solid State Chemistry and its Applications
  - beginner: Crash Course Chemistry — Network Solids; Khan Academy — Solids
- **notes**: Covers crystal structures (NaCl, CsCl, ZnS, diamond, CaF2) and the 14 Bravais lattices; radius-ratio rules for structure prediction; Madelung constants and Born-Lande lattice energies; the Born-Haber cycle; Kapustinskii approximation; Bloch theorem and band theory (nearly-free electron model, tight-binding model, reciprocal lattice, Brillouin zone); classification of metals, semiconductors, and insulators by band gap; carrier concentration, effective mass, and density of states; semiconductor doping (n-type, p-type); optical properties and direct vs indirect gaps; point defects (vacancies, interstitials, Frenkel, Schottky) and defect thermodynamics; ionic conductivity and fast ion conductors; non-stoichiometry; colour centres (F-centres); covalent network solids (diamond, graphite, graphene, silicates); metastable polymorphs and phase transitions. Key theorems: packing efficiency of NaCl structure (proved), Bloch theorem, Born-Lande equation. Originator chain: Bragg 1913 (X-ray diffraction), Madelung 1909 (Coulomb sum), Born-Lande 1918 (lattice energy), Bravais 1850 (lattice classification), Bloch 1928 (band theory), Wilson 1931 (semiconductor classification). Status: deepened to 8001w in Cycle 4 Track B with 5 substantive Master H2 sections.

  - intermediate: Alberts et al., *Molecular Biology of the Cell* (6th ed., Garland 2014), Ch. 15 *Cell Signaling*; Lodish et al., *Molecular Cell Biology* (8th ed., W. H. Freeman 2016), Ch. 15

### biology.rtk-mapk-cascade

- **title**: Receptor tyrosine kinases and the MAPK signaling cascade
- **unit**: `17.07.02`
- **prerequisites**: `biology.cell-signaling-gpcrs`
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Lemmon & Schlessinger — *Cell signaling by receptor tyrosine kinases* (Cell 2010); Huang & Ferrell — *Ultrasensitivity in the MAPK cascade* (PNAS 1996); Goldbeter & Koshland — *Amplified sensitivity from covalent modification* (PNAS 1981); Kholodenko — *Negative feedback and ultrasensitivity in MAPK cascades* (Eur. J. Biochem. 2000); Ferrell & Machleder — *Biochemical basis of an all-or-none cell fate switch* (Science 1998)
  - intermediate: Alberts et al., *Molecular Biology of the Cell* (6th ed., Garland 2014), Ch. 15; Lodish et al., *Molecular Cell Biology* (8th ed., W. H. Freeman 2016), Ch. 16; Lehninger *Principles of Biochemistry* (7th ed., Macmillan 2017), Ch. 12
  - beginner: Khan Academy (intracellular signal transduction); Crash Course Biology — Signal Transduction; Amoeba Sisters — Cell Signaling
- **notes**: Receptor tyrosine kinases, ligand-induced dimerization and trans-autophosphorylation, GRB2-SOS adapter recruitment via SH2 phospho-tyrosine recognition, Ras GTP loading, and the three-tier Raf-MEK-ERK MAPK cascade. Quantitative content: Hill-function kinetics at each tier, Huang-Ferrell composition theorem for cascade ultrasensitivity, Goldbeter-Koshland zero-order ultrasensitivity, distributive multisite phosphorylation. Master tier covers four named sub-sections: stacked-Hill mathematics of ultrasensitivity; bistability via positive feedback (saddle-node bifurcations, oscillations from delayed negative feedback per Kholodenko); spatial dynamics including scaffold proteins, KSR, and reaction-diffusion gradients of active ERK; and cross-system connections to oncology via RTK/Ras/Raf mutations and clinical inhibitors (EGFR-TKIs, vemurafenib, trametinib, sotorasib).

Physiol.* 117, 500-544) — the four foundational papers culminating in the quantitative description; Neher & Sakmann 1976 (*Nature* 260, 799-802) — patch-clamp recording of single-channel currents; Sakmann & Neher 1995 *Single-Channel Recording* (2nd ed., Plenum); Izhikevich, *Dynamical Systems in Neuroscience* (MIT Press 2007) — bifurcation analysis of HH and reduced models

### biology.embryology-morphogenesis

- **title**: Embryology and morphogenesis
- **unit**: `18.11.01`
- **prerequisites**: `biology.cell-cycle-mitosis`
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Gilbert advanced sections; primary literature — Spemann & Mangold 1924, Nusslein-Volhard & Wieschaus 1980, Driever & Nusslein-Volhard 1988, Turing 1952, Davidson et al. 2002, Gierer & Meinhardt 1972, Takahashi & Yamanaka 2006, Thiery 2002
  - intermediate: Gilbert Developmental Biology 12th ed. Ch. 1-12; Wolpert Principles of Development 6th ed.
  - beginner: Campbell Biology 12th ed. Ch. 46-47; Crash Course Biology development episodes; Khan Academy embryology modules
- **notes**: Embryogenesis from fertilisation through organogenesis. Beginner covers stages (cleavage, gastrulation, neurulation), morphogen gradients (Bicoid), and Hox genes. Intermediate formalises the French flag model and exponential-gradient threshold theorem with proof. Master tier covers four advanced systems: gene regulatory networks (Davidson sea urchin GRN), Turing reaction-diffusion patterns (Gierer-Meinhardt model, wavelength selection), epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT transcription factors, morphomechanics), and stem cell niches/pluripotency (Oct4/Sox2/Nanog, iPSC reprogramming). Key theorems: Bicoid threshold model, Turing instability condition, wavelength selection proposition. Prose-first BIO unit; lean_status: none.
