A mirror pair is an identification of the A-model of one with the B-model of the other, implemented near the large-volume / large-complex-structure limit by the **mirror map** $q(t) = \exp(\Pi_1/\Pi_0) = t(1 + O(t))$ of 04.12.13. **Headline prediction (Candelas-de la Ossa-Green-Parkes 1991):** the genus-0 Gromov-Witten instanton numbers $n_d$ of the **quintic threefold** $X \subset \mathbb{P}^4$ (which has $h^{1,1} = 1$, $h^{2,1} = 101$) are extracted from the order-4 Picard-Fuchs periods of the one-parameter mirror $\check X$ (which has $h^{1,1} = 101$, $h^{2,1} = 1$); the quantum Yukawa coupling $K(q) = 5 + \sum_{d \geq 1} n_d d^3 q^d/(1 - q^d)$ yields the instanton table $n_1 = 2875$ (lines), $n_2 = 609250$ (conics), $n_3 = 317206375$. **Key theorem (genus-0 mirror theorem):** proved independently by **Givental 1996** (equivariant localisation, $J$-function vs $I$-function) and **Lian-Liu-Yau 1997** (mirror principle / Euler data); the period prediction equals the genus-0 Gromov-Witten generating series after the mirror map. **Structural pointers:** **Kontsevich 1994 homological mirror symmetry** — $D^b\mathrm{Coh}(\check X) \simeq D^b\mathcal{F}(X)$ (derived category of coherent sheaves $\simeq$ derived Fukaya category), the categorical lift; **Strominger-Yau-Zaslow 1996** — dual special-Lagrangian torus fibrations over a common integral-affine base, with fibrewise T-duality interchanging A- and B-model data (cf. 04.12.10). The **Gross-Siebert programme** (04.12.07-13) makes the exchange algebraic: both mirrors and their equivalence descend from a single integral-affine manifold with singularities $(B, \mathscr{P}, \varphi)$, with wall/slab scattering data (04.12.11) encoding the Gromov-Witten counts; the Batyrev reflexive-polytope duality (04.11.16) is the toric special case. Full-proof-set propositions: Hodge-exchange forces $\chi(\check X) = -\chi(X)$; a rigid Calabi-Yau ($h^{2,1} = 0$) has no smooth Kähler mirror; the mirror map is a local coordinate. Originator chain: Candelas-de la Ossa-Green-Parkes 1991 (prediction); Kontsevich 1994 (HMS); Strominger-Yau-Zaslow 1996 (SYZ); Givental 1996, Lian-Liu-Yau 1997 (genus-0 proof); Cox-Katz 1999, Hori et al. 2003 (monographs); Gross-Siebert 2011, Gross 2011 (tropical/algebraic mechanism). Lean status: `none`; the substantive mathlib_gap records the full slate of absent foundations — quantum cohomology and the Dubrovin connection (A-model), variation of Hodge structure and the Yukawa coupling (B-model), the Frobenius-manifold isomorphism and Hodge-exchange (statement), and the triangulated/$A_\infty$-category and special-Lagrangian-fibration apparatus (HMS and SYZ pointers).

### chemistry.molecular-spectroscopy-fundamentals

- **title**: UV-Vis, IR, and NMR — fundamentals of molecular spectroscopy
- **unit**: `14.12.01`
- **prerequisites**: `chemistry.hydrogen-atom-quantum-chemistry` (14.04.01); `chemistry.mo-theory-homonuclear-diatomics` (14.05.02); `chemistry.hybridization-valence-bond` (14.02.02)
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Hollas 2004 *Modern Spectroscopy* 4e (Wiley); Levine 1975 *Molecular Spectroscopy* (Wiley); Bernath 2020 *Spectra of Atoms and Molecules* 4e (Oxford); Atkins-de Paula 2022 *Physical Chemistry* 12e Ch. 12-14; Cohen-Tannoudji-Diu-Laloe 1977 *Quantum Mechanics* Vol. II; originators: Bouguer 1729 *Essai d'optique* + Lambert 1760 *Photometria* + Beer 1852 *Ann. Phys. Chem.* 86 (Beer-Lambert law); Raman 1928 *Indian J. Phys.* 2 (Raman scattering, Nobel 1930); Bloch 1946 *Phys. Rev.* 70 + Purcell-Torrey-Pound 1946 *Phys. Rev.* 69 (NMR, Nobel 1952); Rabi 1938 *Phys. Rev.* 53 (molecular-beam magnetic resonance, Nobel 1944); Franck 1925 *Trans. Faraday Soc.* 21 + Condon 1926 *Phys. Rev.* 28 (Franck-Condon principle); Ernst-Anderson 1966 *Rev. Sci. Instrum.* 37 (FT-NMR, Nobel 1991); Aue-Bartholdi-Ernst 1976 *J. Chem. Phys.* 64 (2D-NMR); Wuthrich 2002 Nobel Lecture (biomolecular NMR); Zewail 1999 Nobel Lecture (femtochemistry)
  - intermediate: Atkins-de Paula 2022 Ch. 12-14; Pavia et al. 2015 *Introduction to Spectroscopy* 5e Ch. 2-5
  - beginner: Tro 2023 *Chemistry: A Molecular Approach* 6e Ch. 7 spectroscopy callouts; Crash Course Chemistry — spectroscopy episodes
- **notes**: Foundations of molecular spectroscopy unifying UV-Vis, IR/Raman, and NMR techniques around a single quantum-mechanical principle: a photon is absorbed when its energy matches an allowed gap between two stationary states of the molecule, with rate governed by the transition dipole moment $\boldsymbol{\mu}_{fi} = \langle f|\hat{\boldsymbol{\mu}}|i\rangle$ via Fermi's golden rule (see [12.07.02]). **UV-Vis spectroscopy** probes electronic transitions (2-6 eV photons); the Beer-Lambert law $A = \varepsilon c \ell$ — derivable from a first-order linear ODE on intensity in the linear-response regime — relates absorbance to concentration with the molar absorptivity $\varepsilon$ identified with the transition rate. Vibronic-band shape is governed by the Franck-Condon principle, with intensity proportional to the squared vibrational-overlap integral $|\langle\chi_f|\chi_i\rangle|^2$; for harmonic potentials with displacement $\Delta x$ the intensity distribution follows a Poisson law $P(v_f) = e^{-S} S^{v_f}/v_f!$ in the Huang-Rhys factor $S = (\Delta x/x_0)^2/2$. Chromophore concept transferable across molecules; HOMO-LUMO gaps scale as $1/L^2$ for conjugated polyenes (free-electron-in-a-box approximation); transition-metal d-d transitions in the visible due to crystal-field splitting $\Delta_o$ (see [16.04.02]); charge-transfer bands much more intense than d-d because not parity-forbidden. **Vibrational spectroscopy** probes normal modes of the mass-weighted Hessian; a molecule with $N$ atoms has $3N - 6$ (non-linear) or $3N - 5$ (linear) modes. Harmonic-oscillator energies $E_n = \hbar\omega(n+1/2)$ with selection rule $\Delta n = \pm 1$; anharmonic Morse-potential corrections $E_n = \hbar\omega_e(n+1/2) - \hbar\omega_e x_e(n+1/2)^2$ make overtones weakly allowed. IR-active modes require $\partial\boldsymbol{\mu}/\partial Q \neq 0$; Raman-active modes require $\partial\boldsymbol{\alpha}/\partial Q \neq 0$. **Mutual-exclusion principle**: in centrosymmetric molecules, no mode is both IR- and Raman-active because dipole is $u$-parity and polarisability is $g$-parity. Group-theoretic selection rule via direct-product decomposition $\Gamma_f \otimes \Gamma_\mu \otimes \Gamma_i$ contains the trivial representation $\Gamma_1$; for $C_{2v}$ formaldehyde, $\pi \to \pi^*$ ($B_1 \otimes B_1 = A_1$, $z$-polarised, allowed); $n \to \pi^*$ ($B_2 \otimes B_1 = A_2$, none of $\mu_x,\mu_y,\mu_z$, forbidden, weakly observed via vibronic coupling). Group-frequency analysis: C=O at 1715 cm$^{-1}$ in ketones, 1735 in esters, 1690 in conjugated, 1640 in amides — chemical environment modulates bond strength and frequency. **NMR spectroscopy** probes nuclear-spin states in external field $B_0$ via Larmor frequency $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$. Two key chemical observables: chemical shift $\delta$ in ppm relative to TMS reflecting local electronic shielding $\sigma$ via $B_{\text{eff}} = B_0(1-\sigma)$; J-coupling Hamiltonian $\hat{H}_J = 2\pi J\hat{\mathbf{I}}_1\cdot\hat{\mathbf{I}}_2$ mediated by Fermi-contact interaction through bonding electrons, with Karplus relation $^3J(\phi) = A\cos^2\phi + B\cos\phi + C$ linking vicinal couplings to dihedral angles. **Bloch equations** $d\mathbf{M}/dt = \gamma\mathbf{M}\times\mathbf{B} - (M_x\hat{x}+M_y\hat{y})/T_2 - (M_z-M_0)\hat{z}/T_1$ govern macroscopic-magnetisation dynamics: Larmor precession plus phenomenological relaxation, yielding Lorentzian lineshape of half-width $1/T_2$. FT-NMR revolution (Ernst 1966) increased sensitivity by $\sqrt{N}$ Felgett advantage, enabling routine $^{13}$C NMR despite 1.1% natural abundance and reduced gyromagnetic ratio. 2D pulse sequences (COSY, HSQC, HMBC, NOESY, TOCSY) generate molecular-connectivity maps via Fourier transform of two evolution times; Wuthrich 2002 extended to biological-macromolecule structure determination. **Modern frontiers** include hyperpolarisation (DNP, parahydrogen-induced polarisation, optical pumping) amplifying NMR sensitivity by $10^2$-$10^4$; single-molecule fluorescence and super-resolution microscopy (Moerner-Hell-Betzig 2014 Nobel); ultrafast pump-probe spectroscopy reaching femtosecond timescales (Zewail 1999 Nobel) and attosecond pulses (Krausz-Agostini-L'Huillier 2023 Nobel); 2D-IR (Hochstrasser, Tokmakoff) and 2D electronic spectroscopy applying the 2D-NMR concept to optical regime. Connections: [12.07.02] time-dependent perturbation theory + Fermi golden rule (transition-rate basis); [16.04.02] CFSE + spectrochemical series (UV-Vis of transition-metal complexes via d-d transitions); [15.11.01] NMR organic chemistry (structure-elucidation companion). Lean status: `none`, with substantive mathlib_gap describing three independent formalisation targets — transition dipole moments as matrix elements of position operator in bra-ket inner-product API; Beer-Lambert law in measure-theoretic form derivable from Bochner integral over photon-absorption probabilities; Bloch equations as first-order linear ODE on magnetisation vector $\mathbf{M}(t) \in \mathbb{R}^3$ with existence-uniqueness from Picard-Lindelöf already in Mathlib. The (3) ODE-foundation route is the most accessible formalisation target; (1) and (2) need new analytic foundations.

### number-theory.hensels-lemma

- **title**: Hensel's lemma
- **unit**: `21.02.04`
- **prerequisites**: `foundations.real-numbers` (00.01.01); `foundations.absolute-value` (00.01.02 — $p$-adic absolute value as ultrametric refinement); `analysis.cauchy-bolzano-weierstrass` (02.03.02 — Newton iteration as Cauchy sequence in $\mathbb{Z}_p$); `linalg.field` (01.01.01 — residue field $\mathbb{F}_p$); `algebra.algebraic-field-extension` (01.02.12 — finite extensions of $\mathbb{Q}_p$)
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Hensel 1897 *Jahresber. Deutsch. Math.-Verein.* 6, 83-88 (originator); Hensel 1908 *Theorie der algebraischen Zahlen* (Teubner Leipzig — first systematic treatment); Krasner 1944 *J. Math. Pures Appl.* 23 (Krasner's lemma); Serre 1973 *A Course in Arithmetic* (Springer GTM 7) Ch. II §2; Cassels 1986 *Local Fields* (LMS Student Texts 3) Ch. 4; Neukirch 1999 *Algebraic Number Theory* (Grundlehren 322) Ch. II §§4-6; Manin-Panchishkin 2005 *Introduction to Modern Number Theory* (Springer EMS 49) Ch. 1 §3; Bosch-Güntzer-Remmert 1984 *Non-Archimedean Analysis* (Grundlehren 261) §3.4; Eisenbud 1995 *Commutative Algebra* (GTM 150) §7.4
  - intermediate: Serre 1973 Ch. II §2; Cassels 1986 Ch. 4 §4; Neukirch 1999 Ch. II §4
  - beginner: Gouvêa 1997 *p-adic Numbers* (Springer Universitext, 2nd ed.) Ch. 3; Koblitz 1984 *p-adic Numbers, p-adic Analysis, and Zeta-Functions* (GTM 58, 2nd ed.) Ch. I §6
- **notes**: Hensel's lemma is the $p$-adic incarnation of Newton's method and the structural property that distinguishes the $p$-adic integers $\mathbb{Z}_p$ from the ordinary integers $\mathbb{Z}$. **Newton-iteration form (Serre Ch. II §2)**: for $f \in \mathbb{Z}_p[X]$ and a seed $a_0 \in \mathbb{Z}_p$ with $|f(a_0)|_p < |f'(a_0)|_p^2$, the Newton sequence $a_{n+1} = a_n - f(a_n)/f'(a_n)$ is Cauchy in $\mathbb{Z}_p$ and converges to a unique root $a \in \mathbb{Z}_p$ of $f$ in the disc $|a - a_0|_p < |f'(a_0)|_p$. **Factorisation-lifting form**: if $\bar f \in \mathbb{F}_p[X]$ factors as $\bar g_0 \bar h_0$ with $\gcd(\bar g_0, \bar h_0) = 1$, then $f$ factors uniquely as $gh \in \mathbb{Z}_p[X]$ lifting the residual factorisation. **Quadratic convergence**: the Taylor-remainder ultrametric estimate $|f(a_{n+1})|_p \leq c |f(a_n)|_p$ with $c = |f(a_0)|_p / |f'(a_0)|_p^2 < 1$ gives $|f(a_n)|_p \leq c^{2^n - 1} |f(a_0)|_p$, doubling the precision per step. **Teichmüller lift corollary**: applied to $f(X) = X^{p-1} - 1$, Hensel's lemma produces $(p-1)$ distinct $(p-1)$-th roots of unity in $\mathbb{Z}_p$, forming the **Teichmüller subgroup** $\mu_{p-1}(\mathbb{Z}_p) \subset \mathbb{Z}_p^\times$. The exact sequence $1 \to 1 + p\mathbb{Z}_p \to \mathbb{Z}_p^\times \to \mathbb{F}_p^\times \to 1$ splits via the Teichmüller character $\omega : \mathbb{F}_p^\times \to \mu_{p-1}$, yielding the structure theorem $\mathbb{Z}_p^\times \cong \mu_{p-1} \times (1 + p\mathbb{Z}_p)$ for odd $p$; for $p = 5$, the four fourth roots of unity are $\{1, 7, 18, 24\} \pmod {25}$ Hensel-lifted from $\{1, 2, 3, 4\} \pmod 5$. **Worked example**: square root of $-1$ in $\mathbb{Z}_5$ from seed $a_0 = 2$, lifting through $7 \pmod{25}$, $57 \pmod{125}$, $182 \pmod{625}$, $2057 \pmod{3125}$, each $a_n^2 \equiv -1 \pmod{5^{n+1}}$. **Henselian local ring (Eisenbud §7.4)**: a local ring $(R, \mathfrak{m})$ where every monic polynomial with a coprime residual factorisation lifts; equivalent to "every simple residual root lifts" and to "the valuation extends uniquely to algebraic extensions". Completeness implies Henselian; the converse fails (Henselisation is the minimal Henselian subextension of the completion). **Krasner's lemma (Krasner 1944)**: if $\alpha, \beta$ are algebraic over $\mathbb{Q}_p$ with $|\beta - \alpha|_p < \min_{i \geq 2} |\alpha - \alpha_i|_p$ over conjugates $\alpha_i$ of $\alpha$, then $\mathbb{Q}_p(\alpha) \subseteq \mathbb{Q}_p(\beta)$. Combined with Hensel, Krasner gives the classification of finite extensions of $\mathbb{Q}_p$ as (unramified part lifted from $\mathbb{F}_p$ via Hensel) $\circ$ (totally ramified part generated by an Eisenstein polynomial), and the continuity-of-roots theorem: nearby polynomials generate the same extension. **Originator chain**: Hensel 1897 (originator note); Hensel 1908 (monograph); Hasse 1923 (local-global principle for quadratic forms, Hasse-Minkowski); Krasner 1937/1944 (Krasner's lemma); Serre 1962 *Corps Locaux* (modern presentation); Grothendieck EGA + SGA (Henselian local rings as étale-local algebra). **Forward-connects to** [21.02] $p$-adic numbers chapter; [21.05.01] $\ell$-adic Galois representations (decomposition group structure rests on local-field machinery); [21.07.01] $\mathbb{Z}_p$-extensions and Iwasawa theory (Weierstrass preparation for $\mathbb{Z}_p[[T]]$ as $\Lambda$-coefficient Hensel); [21.10.01] Langlands programme (local-global compatibility). Lean status: `none` — Mathlib has `PadicInt.hensel` for the Newton-iteration form, but the factorisation-lifting form, Krasner's lemma, the Eisenstein characterisation of totally ramified extensions, the Teichmüller-lift structure theorem $\mathbb{Z}_p^\times \cong \mu_{p-1} \times (1 + p\mathbb{Z}_p)$, and the abstract definition of Henselian valuation rings are all formalisation targets.

### number-theory.riemann-zeta

- **title**: Riemann zeta function $\zeta(s)$
- **unit**: `21.03.01`
- **prerequisites**: `complex-analysis.analytic-continuation` (06.01.04); `complex-analysis.gamma-function` (06.01.15); `analysis.infinite-series-convergence` (02.03.03)
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Riemann 1859 *Monatsber. Berliner Akad.* (originator: definition for complex $s$, analytic continuation, functional equation, Hadamard product, explicit formula, Riemann hypothesis); Edwards 1974 *Riemann's Zeta Function* (Academic Press, line-by-line reading); Titchmarsh 1986 *Theory of the Riemann Zeta-Function* (2nd ed., revised Heath-Brown, Oxford); Davenport 2000 *Multiplicative Number Theory* (GTM 74, 3rd ed.); Iwaniec-Kowalski 2004 *Analytic Number Theory* (AMS Colloquium 53); Hadamard 1896 + de la Vallée Poussin 1896 (PNT via $\zeta \neq 0$ on $\operatorname{Re}(s) = 1$); Bombieri 2000 Clay Millennium Problem statement; Conrey 2003 *Notices AMS* survey
  - intermediate: Davenport 2000 Ch. 8; Iwaniec-Kowalski 2004 Ch. 5
  - beginner: Derbyshire 2003 *Prime Obsession* Ch. 5-7 informal; Euler 1735 Basel opening with $\zeta(2) = \pi^2/6$
- **notes**: The Riemann zeta function is the foundational analytic object of analytic number theory. Definition by Dirichlet series $\zeta(s) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} 1/n^s$ on the half-plane $\operatorname{Re}(s) > 1$; equivalent Euler product $\zeta(s) = \prod_p (1 - p^{-s})^{-1}$ over primes (Euler 1737, encoding unique factorisation in $\mathbb{Z}$); analytic continuation to a meromorphic function on $\mathbb{C}$ with a single simple pole at $s = 1$ of residue $1$. The signature theorem is the **functional equation** in symmetric form $\xi(s) = \xi(1-s)$ where the completed function $\xi(s) = \frac{1}{2}s(s-1) \pi^{-s/2} \Gamma(s/2) \zeta(s)$ is entire of order $1$; equivalently in asymmetric form $\zeta(s) = 2^s \pi^{s-1} \sin(\pi s/2) \Gamma(1-s) \zeta(1-s)$. Two proofs (both Riemann 1859): (i) contour deformation via the Hankel integral $\zeta(s) = -\Gamma(1-s) (2\pi i)^{-1} \int_C (-z)^{s-1}/(e^z - 1) dz$, providing analytic continuation in one step; (ii) theta-function symmetry via the Mellin transform $\pi^{-s/2} \Gamma(s/2) \zeta(s) = \int_0^{\infty} \psi(x) x^{s/2-1} dx$ where $\psi(x) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} e^{-\pi n^2 x}$ and the Jacobi transformation $\theta(1/x) = x^{1/2} \theta(x)$ (a Poisson summation identity on the Gaussian) produces $s \leftrightarrow 1-s$ symmetry of the integrand. **Trivial zeros** $\zeta(-2k) = 0$ for $k \geq 1$, forced by the entirety of $\xi$ cancelling the simple poles of $\Gamma(s/2)$ at negative even integers. **Special values** $\zeta(2k) = (-1)^{k+1} (2\pi)^{2k} B_{2k} / (2(2k)!)$ for positive integer $k$, including $\zeta(2) = \pi^2/6$ (Euler 1735 Basel problem) and $\zeta(4) = \pi^4/90$; $\zeta(0) = -1/2$, $\zeta(-1) = -1/12$, $\zeta(1-2n) = -B_{2n}/(2n)$. **Hadamard product** $\xi(s) = e^{a + bs} \prod_\rho (1 - s/\rho) e^{s/\rho}$ over non-trivial zeros (Riemann 1859 stated, Hadamard 1893 proved). **Explicit formula** (Riemann-von Mangoldt) $\psi(x) = x - \sum_\rho x^\rho/\rho - \log(2\pi) - \frac{1}{2}\log(1 - x^{-2})$ connecting Chebyshev's $\psi$ to non-trivial zeros. **Prime number theorem** (Hadamard 1896 + de la Vallée Poussin 1896 independently), equivalent to $\zeta(s) \neq 0$ on $\operatorname{Re}(s) = 1$. **Riemann hypothesis** (Riemann 1859, statement only): all non-trivial zeros lie on the critical line $\operatorname{Re}(s) = 1/2$. Open since 1859; Hilbert problem 8; Clay Millennium Prize (Bombieri 2000). Partial results: Hardy 1914 (infinitely many zeros on the line), Selberg 1942 (positive proportion), Levinson 1974 ($\geq 1/3$), Conrey 1989 ($\geq 2/5$). Random-matrix heuristic (Montgomery 1973, Odlyzko 1987-): local zero statistics match GUE eigenvalue statistics. Function-field analogue proved by Weil 1948. Chapter-opening unit of Section 21.03 L-functions; forward-connects to Dirichlet $L$-functions (21.03.02), Dedekind/Hecke/Artin $L$-functions (21.03.03), modular forms (21.04.01), Hecke operators (21.04.02), and the Langlands programme (21.10.01). Originator chain: Euler 1735 *Basel* ($\zeta(2) = \pi^2/6$); Euler 1737 *Var. obs.* (Euler product); Riemann 1859 *Monatsber.* (complex-variable treatment, analytic continuation, functional equation, Hadamard product, explicit formula, RH); Hadamard 1893 (factorisation theorem); Mangoldt 1895 (explicit-formula rigour); Hadamard 1896 + de la Vallée Poussin 1896 (PNT); Hardy 1914 (infinitely many critical-line zeros); Selberg 1942 (positive proportion); Weil 1948 (function-field analogue); Montgomery 1973 + Odlyzko 1987 (random-matrix heuristic). Lean status: `partial`, module `Codex.NumberTheory.LFunctions.RiemannZeta` declares `riemannZeta : ℂ → ℂ`, `riemannZeta_eq_dirichlet_series`, `riemannZeta_residue_at_one`, `riemannZeta_meromorphic`, `zeta_euler_product`, `zeta_nonzero_re_gt_one`, `completedZeta`, `completedZeta_functional_equation`, `zeta_functional_equation`, `zeta_trivial_zeros`, `criticalStrip`, `criticalLine`, `RiemannHypothesis` — all `sorry`-stubbed pending Mathlib infrastructure on Dirichlet-series meromorphic continuation, Poisson summation on $\mathbb{Z} \subset \mathbb{R}$, and Mellin-transform $\Gamma$-twist pairing.

### number-theory.modular-forms-sl2-z

- **title**: Modular Forms on $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$
- **unit**: `21.04.01`
- **prerequisites**: linalg.linear-transformation-rank-nullity; complex-analysis.holomorphic-function; complex-analysis.mobius-transformations
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Hecke 1936-37 *Math. Ann.* 112 + 114 (originator: definition with multiplier, $T_n$ operators, Euler-product factorisation, structure theorem $M_*(\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})) = \mathbb{C}[E_4, E_6]$); Diamond-Shurman 2005 (Springer GTM 228) §§1.1-1.5 + §§3.5-3.7; Serre 1973 *A Course in Arithmetic* Ch. VII §§1-4 (valence formula, structure theorem, dimension formula); Manin-Panchishkin 2005 (Springer EMS 49, 2nd ed.) Ch. 6 §§1-3 (encyclopaedic survey); Shimura 1971 (Princeton/Iwanami) §§2.1-2.7 (arithmetic theory, Petersson inner product); Miyake 1989 Ch. 2; Koblitz 1993 (GTM 97) Ch. III; Lang 1976 (Grundlehren 222) Ch. III + Ch. X; Bump 1997 (Cambridge Studies 55) Ch. 1 (automorphic-representation framing); Petersson 1939 (originator of the inner product + Ramanujan-Petersson conjecture); Deligne 1974 *Publ. Math. IHES* 43 (Ramanujan-Petersson via Weil conjectures); Atkin-Lehner 1970 *Math. Ann.* 185 (new-form theory at level $N$); Borcherds 1992 *Invent. Math.* 109 (monstrous moonshine connecting $j$-invariant to Monster representation theory)
  - intermediate: Diamond-Shurman §§1.1-1.2; Serre Ch. VII §§2-3
  - beginner: Diamond-Shurman §1.1 informal opening; Serre Ch. VII §1 introduction
- **notes**: Foundational definitional unit for Section 21.04 modular forms. A **modular form of weight $k$ for $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$** is a holomorphic function $f : \mathbb{H} \to \mathbb{C}$ on the upper half-plane satisfying the transformation law $f((a\tau + b)/(c\tau + d)) = (c\tau + d)^k f(\tau)$ for every $\begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix} \in \mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$, together with holomorphy at the cusp $\infty$ (equivalently, the Fourier expansion $f(\tau) = \sum_{n \geq 0} a_n q^n$ in $q = e^{2\pi i\tau}$ has no negative-index terms). A **cusp form** has additional vanishing $a_0 = 0$. The space $M_k$ of weight-$k$ modular forms is finite-dimensional, with dimension formula $\dim M_k = \lfloor k/12\rfloor + 1$ for $k$ even and $k \not\equiv 2 \pmod{12}$, $\lfloor k/12\rfloor$ for $k \equiv 2 \pmod{12}$, and $0$ for $k$ odd. Foundational examples: **Eisenstein series** $E_k = 1 - (2k/B_k) \sum_{n \geq 1} \sigma_{k-1}(n) q^n$ for even $k \geq 4$ (lattice sum $G_k(\tau) = \sum_{(m,n) \neq (0,0)} (m\tau + n)^{-k}$ normalised by $2\zeta(k)$); **modular discriminant** $\Delta = (E_4^3 - E_6^2)/1728 = q\prod_{n \geq 1}(1 - q^n)^{24}$ of weight 12, the smallest non-zero cusp form, with Fourier coefficients $\tau(n)$ the Ramanujan tau function; **Klein $j$-invariant** $j = E_4^3/\Delta = 1/q + 744 + 196884 q + \cdots$, the generator of the field of meromorphic modular functions on $X(1)$. Signature theorem is the **structure theorem** $M_*(\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})) = \bigoplus_{k \geq 0} M_k \cong \mathbb{C}[E_4, E_6]$ as graded $\mathbb{C}$-algebras with $\deg E_4 = 4, \deg E_6 = 6$ (Hecke 1937; Serre VII §3 Theorem 4). The proof uses the **valence formula** $\nu_\infty(f) + \tfrac{1}{2}\nu_i(f) + \tfrac{1}{3}\nu_\rho(f) + \sum_{P} \nu_P(f) = k/12$ derived from the residue theorem on the standard fundamental domain $F = \{|\tau| \geq 1, |\mathrm{Re}\,\tau| \leq 1/2\}$. The **Petersson inner product** $\langle f, g\rangle = \int_F f\bar{g} y^k y^{-2} dx\, dy$ makes $S_k$ into a finite-dimensional Hilbert space on which the Hecke algebra acts self-adjointly; simultaneous Hecke eigenforms give an orthogonal basis with multiplicative Fourier coefficients and attached $L$-function $L(f, s) = \sum a_n/n^s$ with Euler product. The **Ramanujan-Petersson conjecture** $|a_p| \leq 2 p^{(k-1)/2}$ (Petersson 1939) is proved by Deligne 1974 *Publ. Math. IHES* 43 via the Weil conjectures for Kuga-Sato varieties. Forward-connects to Hecke operators and Hecke algebra (21.04.02), Eichler-Shimura correspondence (21.04.03), $\ell$-adic Galois representations (21.05.01), modularity theorem and BSD (21.06.01), Langlands programme (21.10.01); backward-connects to Möbius transformations (06.01.08), holomorphic function (06.01.01), Riemann surfaces (06.03.01), Riemann zeta function (21.03.01). Lean status: `partial`, module `Codex.NumberTheory.ModularForms.SL2Z` declares `UpperHalfPlane`, `ModularGroup`, `mobius`, `automorphyFactor`, `ModularForm k`, `CuspForm k`, `eisensteinSeries k`, `modularDiscriminant`, `gradedRingOfModularForms`, plus theorems `modular_ring_polynomial_in_E4_E6`, `discriminant_via_E4_E6`, `dim_formula_even`, `dim_formula_odd`, `eisenstein_constant_term_one` — all `sorry`-stubbed pending Mathlib infrastructure on lattice-sum convergence, holomorphy of Eisenstein series, the valence formula via residue calculus on the fundamental domain, and the Hecke-Serre polynomial-ring identification.

### number-theory.theta-series-quadratic-forms

- **title**: Theta Series of Quadratic Forms and Sums of Squares
- **unit**: `21.04.04`
- **prerequisites**: sibling `number-theory.modular-forms-sl2-z` (21.04.01 — the finite-dimensional spaces $M_k$ into which a theta series is projected); sibling `number-theory.hecke-operators` (21.04.02 — Eisenstein/cusp eigenspace decomposition that separates the genus part from the class part); sibling `number-theory.hasse-minkowski` (21.02.08 — genus and local data of a positive-definite form $Q$, the local densities entering Siegel's mass formula); `number-theory.quadratic-residues-legendre-symbol` (21.01.06 — the character $\chi_{-4}$ governing the two-square case)
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Jacobi 1829 *Fundamenta Nova* (originator: the two- and four-square formulas via theta-function identities); Serre 1973 *A Course in Arithmetic* (GTM 7) Ch. VII §§6-7 ($\theta(z) = \sum q^{n^2}$, Poisson-summation modularity, weight-$1/2$ on $\Gamma_0(4)$, theta series of a quadratic form, the sum-of-squares payoff, four-square theorem); Shimura 1973 *Ann. of Math.* 97 (modular forms of half-integral weight, the theta multiplier, the Shimura correspondence); Siegel 1935 *Ann. of Math.* 36 (mass formula, genus average of representation numbers as a product of local densities); Iwaniec 1997 *Topics in Classical Automorphic Forms* (AMS GSM 17) Ch. 10-11; Ogg 1969 *Modular Forms and Dirichlet Series* Ch. VI; Miyake 1989 *Modular Forms* Ch. 4 §4.9; Conway-Sloane 1999 *Sphere Packings, Lattices and Groups* (Grundlehren 290, 3rd ed.) Ch. 2-7 (theta series of lattices, $E_8 \mapsto E_4$, isospectral non-isometric pairs); Lagrange 1770 (four-square theorem $r_4(n) > 0$); Hardy-Wright 2008 Ch. XX (elementary sums of squares)
  - intermediate: Serre 1973 Ch. VII §6; Iwaniec 1997 Ch. 10-11
  - beginner: Serre 1973 Ch. VII §6 informal opening; Hardy-Wright 2008 Ch. XX
- **notes**: The **theta series** $\theta(z) = \sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} q^{n^2} = 1 + 2q + 2q^4 + 2q^9 + \cdots$ ($q = e^{2\pi i z}$) is the number-theoretic Jacobi theta function (distinct from the Riemann/Jacobian theta of 06.06.05). **Modular transformation**: Poisson summation applied to the Gaussian $e^{-\pi t x^2}$ (self-Fourier-dual up to $\sqrt t$) gives the inversion $\vartheta(1/t) = \sqrt{t}\,\vartheta(t)$, equivalently $\theta(-1/(4z)) = \sqrt{-2iz}\,\theta(z)$, making $\theta$ a **modular form of weight $1/2$ on $\Gamma_0(4)$** with the **theta multiplier** $\nu_\theta(\gamma) = (\tfrac{c}{d})\varepsilon_d^{-1}$ (eighth root of unity; the half-integer weight is forced because $\theta$ transforms by the square root of $cz+d$, the natural level being $4$ since only $z \mapsto z+2$ fixes $\theta$). The **theta series of a positive-definite integral quadratic form** $Q$ of rank $m$ is $\theta_Q(z) = \sum_{x \in \mathbb{Z}^m} q^{Q(x)} = \sum_n r_Q(n) q^n$, a modular form of **weight $m/2$** on $\Gamma_0(N)$ with character $\chi_D$, whose $n$-th Fourier coefficient is the **representation number** $r_Q(n) = \#\{x : Q(x) = n\}$. **Strategy for exact formulas**: project $\theta_Q$ inside the finite-dimensional $M_{m/2}(\Gamma_0(N),\chi_D)$ as $\theta_Q = E_Q + f_Q$ (Eisenstein part + cusp part); the Eisenstein part depends only on the **genus** of $Q$ and its coefficients are products of local densities given by **Siegel's mass formula** (1935); the cusp part detects the finer class within the genus. **Signature results (sums of squares)**: $\theta^k \in M_{k/2}(\Gamma_0(4))$, and where the cusp space vanishes the formula is exact — **Jacobi's four-square theorem** $r_4(n) = 8\sum_{d\mid n, 4\nmid d} d$ (from $\theta^4 = \phi_2 \in M_2(\Gamma_0(4))$, a $2$-dimensional all-Eisenstein space with $S_2(\Gamma_0(4)) = 0$), the **two-square** $r_2(n) = 4(d_1(n) - d_3(n))$ from $\theta^2 = E_{1,\chi_{-4}}$, and the **eight-square** $r_8(n) = 16\sum_{d\mid n}(-1)^{n-d}d^3$. **Lagrange's four-square theorem** $r_4(n) > 0$ is the corollary (the divisor $d=1$ always contributes). Beginner framing: counting representations as a sum of squares becomes reading off coefficients of a modular form. **Half-integer weight** lives in **Shimura's 1973** theory with the Shimura correspondence to integral weight; **even unimodular lattices** ($m \equiv 0 \bmod 8$) give level-$1$ theta series inside $\mathbb{C}[E_4,E_6]$ — $\theta_{E_8} = E_4$, the rank-$16$ pair $E_8\oplus E_8$ and $D_{16}^+$ share $\theta = E_4^2$ (Milnor isospectral non-isometric example, since $\dim M_8 = 1$). The Mellin transform of $\theta$ is the completed Riemann zeta, the theta inversion being the source of the zeta functional equation (21.03.01). Closes Serre's Part I (quadratic forms) to Part II (modular forms) arc. Forward-connects to Hecke operators (21.04.02); backward/lateral to modular forms on $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$ (21.04.01), Hasse-Minkowski / quadratic forms over $\mathbb{Q}$ (21.02.08), quadratic residues (21.01.06); contrasts Riemann/Jacobian theta function (06.06.05). Originator chain: Lagrange 1770 (four-square positivity); Jacobi 1829 (exact two- and four-square formulas via theta identities); Siegel 1935 (mass formula); Shimura 1973 (half-integral weight). Lean status: `none`. Mathlib has the Jacobi theta and its Poisson-summation functional equation but lacks the general $\theta_Q$, its weight-$m/2$ modularity on $\Gamma_0(4N)$ with the theta multiplier, the Eisenstein-cusp projection, Siegel's mass formula, and the resulting exact representation-number formulas (Jacobi four/two/eight-square) and Lagrange's theorem as the corollary $r_4(n) > 0$.

### number-theory.dirichlet-l-functions

- **title**: Dirichlet $L$-functions $L(s, \chi)$
- **unit**: `21.03.02`
- **prerequisites**: `algebra.group` (01.02.01 — the multiplicative group $(\mathbb{Z}/m)^\times$); `algebra.subgroup-coset-quotient` (01.02.02 — quotient and finite-abelian-group machinery underlying $(\mathbb{Z}/m)^\times$); `complex-analysis.complex-numbers-euler` (02.09.01 — codomain $\mathbb{C}^\times$ and Mellin / contour-integral background); sibling `number-theory.riemann-zeta` (21.03.01 — principal-character $L$-function inherits the analytic continuation of $\zeta$)
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Dirichlet 1837 *Abh. Königl. Preuss. Akad.* 45-81 (originator paper: Dirichlet characters, $L$-series $L(s, \chi) = \sum \chi(n)/n^s$, non-vanishing $L(1, \chi) \neq 0$, primes in arithmetic progressions); Dirichlet 1839/40 *J. reine angew. Math.* 19, 21 (analytic class-number formula); Davenport 2000 *Multiplicative Number Theory* (GTM 74, 3rd ed., revised Montgomery); Iwaniec-Kowalski 2004 *Analytic Number Theory* (AMS Colloquium 53) Ch. 4-5; Apostol 1976 *Introduction to Analytic Number Theory* (Springer UTM) Ch. 6-7; Selberg 1949 *Canad. J. Math.* 2 (elementary proof of PNT in arithmetic progressions); Tate 1950 Princeton PhD thesis (Cassels-Fröhlich 1967, Ch. XV — adelic / Fourier-analytic reformulation); Bump 1997 *Automorphic Forms and Representations* (Cambridge Studies 55) Ch. 1, 3; Hecke 1918, 1920 *Math. Z.* 1, 6 (Hecke $L$-functions of number fields, functional equation via theta inversion); Artin 1923/1930 *Abh. Math. Sem. Univ. Hamburg* 3, 8 (Artin $L$-functions of Galois representations); Page 1935 *Proc. Lond. Math. Soc.* 39 + Siegel 1935 *Acta Arith.* 1 (exceptional Siegel zero); Linnik 1944 *Mat. Sb.* 15 (least prime in arithmetic progression); Manin-Panchishkin 2005 *Introduction to Modern Number Theory* (Springer EMS 49) Ch. 6 of Part II
  - intermediate: Apostol 1976 Ch. 6-7; Davenport 2000 Ch. 1, 4, 6
  - beginner: Dirichlet 1837 informal opening — counting primes in $4k + 1, 4k + 3$ progressions through a $\pm 1$-character sieve; Leibniz 1674 / Madhava ca. 1400 identity $1 - 1/3 + 1/5 - \cdots = \pi/4$ as a special value of $L(1, \chi_1)$
- **notes**: The Dirichlet $L$-function $L(s, \chi) = \sum_{n \geq 1} \chi(n)/n^s$ on the half-plane $\mathrm{Re}(s) > 1$ is the originator and prototype of analytic number theory. Dirichlet 1837 *Abh. Königl. Preuss. Akad.* introduces the **Dirichlet character** $\chi : (\mathbb{Z}/m)^\times \to \mathbb{C}^\times$ as a homomorphism of the finite multiplicative group of units, extended to $\mathbb{Z}$ by zero on integers sharing a factor with $m$, completely multiplicative and $m$-periodic. The **Euler product** $L(s, \chi) = \prod_p (1 - \chi(p) p^{-s})^{-1}$ for $\mathrm{Re}(s) > 1$ encodes complete multiplicativity. **Principal character $\chi_0$**: $L(s, \chi_0) = \zeta(s) \prod_{p \mid m} (1 - p^{-s})$, inheriting the simple pole at $s = 1$ from $\zeta$ with residue $\varphi(m)/m$. **Non-principal characters**: $L(s, \chi)$ is entire on $\mathbb{C}$ (Hecke 1918). **Signature theorem (non-vanishing at $s = 1$, Dirichlet 1837)**: $L(1, \chi) \neq 0$ for every non-principal $\chi$; proof splits into the complex-character case (Landau's theorem on Dirichlet series with non-negative coefficients applied to $\prod_\chi L(s, \chi)$) and the real-character case (class-number formula $L(1, \chi) = 2\pi h(D)/(w_D \sqrt{|D|})$ for $D < 0$ or $L(1, \chi) = 2\pi h(D) \log\varepsilon / \sqrt{|D|}$ for $D > 0$, both strictly positive). **Dirichlet's theorem on primes in arithmetic progressions** (Dirichlet 1837): for every modulus $m$ and residue $a$ coprime to $m$, the arithmetic progression $a, a + m, a + 2m, \ldots$ contains infinitely many primes; quantitative PNT (de la Vallée Poussin 1896) gives $\pi(x; m, a) \sim x/(\varphi(m) \log x)$. The proof reduces via orthogonality of characters and the logarithmic Euler product to the divergence $\log L(s, \chi_0) \to +\infty$ at $s = 1^+$ — the non-vanishing $L(1, \chi) \neq 0$ being the only analytic input. **Functional equation** (Hecke 1918 *Math. Z.* 1): for primitive $\chi$ of conductor $m$ with parity $\mathfrak{a} = (1 - \chi(-1))/2$, the completed $L$-function $\Lambda(s, \chi) = (m/\pi)^{(s+\mathfrak{a})/2} \Gamma((s+\mathfrak{a})/2) L(s, \chi)$ satisfies $\Lambda(s, \chi) = W(\chi) \Lambda(1 - s, \overline{\chi})$ with root number $W(\chi) = \tau(\chi) / (i^\mathfrak{a} \sqrt{m})$ and Gauss sum $\tau(\chi) = \sum_a \chi(a) e^{2\pi i a/m}$, $|\tau(\chi)| = \sqrt{m}$. Proof via Mellin transform of the Dirichlet theta function and Poisson summation. **Exceptional Siegel zero** (Page 1935, Siegel 1935): real $\chi$ has at most one real zero $\beta < 1$ within $1 - O(1/\log m)$ of $1$; ineffective Siegel bound $1 - \beta \gg_\varepsilon m^{-\varepsilon}$. **Linnik's theorem** (Linnik 1944): least prime $p \equiv a \bmod m$ is $\ll m^L$, current $L \leq 5$ (Xylouris 2011), conjecturally $L = 1 + \varepsilon$ under GRH. **Modern reformulations**: Hecke 1918 generalises to characters of the idele class group of number fields, giving **Hecke $L$-functions**; Artin 1923/1930 generalises further to $L$-functions of Galois representations, with one-dimensional Artin = Dirichlet by class field theory and Artin reciprocity (Artin 1927); Tate 1950 PhD thesis recasts everything as global zeta integrals over $\mathbb{A}_\mathbb{Q}^\times$ against Schwartz-Bruhat functions — the $\mathrm{GL}_1/\mathbb{Q}$ prototype of the Langlands programme (Bump 1997). The Dirichlet $L$-function is the foundational unit of Section 21.03; sibling $\zeta$ (21.03.01), forward Dedekind/Hecke/Artin $L$ (21.03.03), forward modular forms (21.04.01) and Hecke operators (21.04.02), and lateral character of a finite group (07.01.03). Originator chain: Euler 1737 ($\zeta$ Euler product); Dirichlet 1837 (characters + $L$-series + non-vanishing + arithmetic progression theorem); Dirichlet 1839/40 (class-number formula); Riemann 1859 (functional equation for $\zeta$, analytic-continuation method generalised by Hecke); Hadamard-de la Vallée Poussin 1896 (PNT and zero-free region); Hecke 1918, 1920 (Hecke $L$ + functional equation); Artin 1923, 1930 (Artin $L$); Page 1935, Siegel 1935 (Siegel zero); Linnik 1944 (least prime); Selberg 1949 (elementary proof of PNT in arithmetic progressions); Tate 1950 (adelic Tate thesis). Lean status: `partial`, module `Codex.NumberTheory.LFunctions.DirichletL` declares `structure DirichletCharacter`, `def DirichletL`, `theorem L_nonvanish_at_one`, `theorem dirichlet_progression`, plus orthogonality lemmas and the witness `L_one_chi_one_mod_four = π/4` (Leibniz series), all `sorry`-stubbed pending Mathlib infrastructure on Dirichlet-series meromorphic continuation, Landau theorem on positive-coefficient series, and the class-number formula for real characters.
### number-theory.dirichlet-density

- **title**: Dirichlet density and primes in arithmetic progressions
- **unit**: `21.03.04`
- **prerequisites**: sibling `number-theory.dirichlet-l-functions` (21.03.02 — Dirichlet characters and the non-vanishing $L(1, \chi) \neq 0$ supply the only analytic input); sibling `number-theory.riemann-zeta` (21.03.01 — $\log \zeta(s) \sim \log(1/(s-1))$ at $s = 1^+$ is the singular comparison; the prime $p^{-s}$-sum is the leading term); `analysis.infinite-series-convergence` (02.03.03 — Dirichlet-series convergence for $\sum p^{-s}$ on $\mathrm{Re}(s) > 1$)
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Serre 1973 *A Course in Arithmetic* (GTM 7) Ch. VI §§3-4 (originator anchor — Dirichlet density, the logarithmic singularity of $\log \zeta(s)$, the density-$1/\varphi(m)$ theorem); Dirichlet 1837 *Abh. Königl. Preuss. Akad.* 45-81 (originator of primes-in-AP, statement in density-counting form); Chebotarev 1922 *Math. Ann.* 95 (Chebotarev density theorem, Galois generalisation); Hecke 1918 *Math. Z.* 1 (Hecke $L$-functions, the number-field generalisation that drives Chebotarev); Tate 1950 Princeton PhD thesis (Cassels-Fröhlich 1967, Ch. XV — adelic / Mellin reformulation); Lang 1994 *Algebraic Number Theory* (GTM 110) Ch. VIII (Chebotarev density theorem proof); Neukirch 1999 *Algebraic Number Theory* (Grundlehren 322) Ch. VII §13 (Chebotarev + density formula in modern form); Iwaniec-Kowalski 2004 *Analytic Number Theory* (AMS Colloquium 53) Ch. 5 §5.6 (quantitative density, the PNT in AP); Davenport 2000 *Multiplicative Number Theory* (GTM 74) Ch. 7 (density-form proofs); Bump 1997 *Automorphic Forms and Representations* (Cambridge Studies 55) Ch. 1 §1.6, Ch. 3 (Tate-thesis density viewpoint, idele-class-group Hecke characters)
  - intermediate: Serre 1973 Ch. VI §§3-4; Apostol 1976 *Introduction to Analytic Number Theory* Ch. 7 (Dirichlet density-style argument inside the proof of Dirichlet's theorem)
  - beginner: Serre 1973 Ch. VI §1 informal opening — counting primes by residue class via a Dirichlet-series weight
- **notes**: The **Dirichlet density** of a set $A$ of primes is the limit $d(A) = \lim_{s \to 1^+} \frac{\sum_{p \in A} p^{-s}}{\sum_p p^{-s}}$ when it exists, comparing the $p^{-s}$-weighted partial sum over $A$ with the corresponding sum over all primes. Equivalently, since $\sum_p p^{-s} = \log(1/(s-1)) + O(1)$ as $s \to 1^+$, $d(A) = \lim_{s \to 1^+} \frac{\sum_{p \in A} p^{-s}}{\log(1/(s-1))}$ when the limit exists. **Comparison with natural density**: if the natural density $\delta(A) = \lim_{x \to \infty} |\{p \in A : p \leq x\}|/\pi(x)$ exists, then $d(A) = \delta(A)$ by Abel summation against the prime counting function; the converse fails. **Signature theorem (Dirichlet's theorem in density form, Serre Ch. VI Theorem 2)**: for every modulus $m$ and residue $a$ with $\gcd(a, m) = 1$, the set $P_{a, m} = \{p : p \equiv a \pmod m\}$ has Dirichlet density $d(P_{a, m}) = 1/\varphi(m)$. **Proof**: orthogonality of Dirichlet characters expresses the indicator of the residue class as a character average $\mathbf{1}_{p \equiv a} = \varphi(m)^{-1} \sum_\chi \overline{\chi(a)} \chi(p)$; the principal-character term contributes $\varphi(m)^{-1} \log(1/(s-1))$ while every non-principal term is bounded via the non-vanishing $L(1, \chi) \neq 0$ (Dirichlet 1837). **Chebotarev density theorem (Chebotarev 1922)**: for a finite Galois extension $L/K$ with $G = \mathrm{Gal}(L/K)$ and any conjugacy class $C \subseteq G$, the set $\{\mathfrak{p} : \mathrm{Frob}_\mathfrak{p} \in C\}$ has Dirichlet density $|C|/|G|$. Dirichlet's theorem is the $K = \mathbb{Q}$, $L = \mathbb{Q}(\zeta_m)$ case. The proof uses Hecke $L$-functions of one-dimensional characters of $G$ via Artin reciprocity, plus Brauer induction to reduce higher-dimensional cases to one-dimensional. **Tate-thesis viewpoint** (Tate 1950): $\sum_{p \in A} p^{-s}$ is a $\mathrm{GL}_1$-zeta integral against the indicator of a set of finite places, and Dirichlet density is the residue at $s = 1$ of that integral divided by the residue of the full $\zeta$. **Originator chain**: Dirichlet 1837 (primes-in-AP); Dirichlet 1839/40 (class-number formula); Chebotarev 1922 *Math. Ann.* 95 (Galois generalisation); Hecke 1918, 1920 (Hecke $L$-functions); Artin 1927/1930 (Artin reciprocity); Tate 1950 (adelic reformulation). Lean status: `none`. Mathlib lacks the analytic asymptotic $\sum_p p^{-s} = \log(1/(s-1)) + O(1)$ as $s \to 1^+$, Dirichlet density as a notion on subsets of $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z} \setminus \{(0)\}$, and the orthogonality-average derivation of $d(P_{a, m}) = 1/\varphi(m)$ — predicated on a Dirichlet-character / Hecke / Artin $L$-function infrastructure not yet present in Mathlib, plus the Chebotarev density theorem itself.

### tropical.log-gromov-witten-invariants

- **title**: Log Gromov-Witten Invariants (pointer)
- **unit**: `04.12.15`
- **prerequisites**: `tropical.nishinou-siebert-correspondence` (04.12.06 — uses log GW theory in its proof); `tropical.toric-degeneration-calabi-yau` (04.12.07); `tropical.gross-siebert-reconstruction` (04.12.09 — consumes log GW as scattering input)
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Gross-Siebert 2013 *Logarithmic Gromov-Witten invariants*, J. Amer. Math. Soc. 26 (2), 451-510 (originator paper, basic monoid construction, perfect obstruction theory, degeneration formula); Abramovich-Chen 2014 *Stable logarithmic maps to Deligne-Faltings pairs II*, Asian J. Math. 18 (3), 465-488 (parallel construction, minimal log structure, Deligne-Faltings pairs); Chen 2014 *Stable logarithmic maps to Deligne-Faltings pairs I*, Ann. of Math. 180 (2), 455-521 (foundations of the Abramovich-Chen moduli theory); Abramovich-Chen-Marcus-Wise 2017 *Compos. Math.* 153 (8), 1633-1666 (equivalence of the two constructions); Kato 1989 *Logarithmic structures of Fontaine-Illusie* (foundational log-geometry framework: fs log structures, log smooth morphisms, log differentials, chart theory); Gross-Pandharipande-Siebert 2010 *Publ. Math. IHÉS* 122, 65-168 (BPS invariants from log GW; tropical vertex algebra); Mandel-Ruddat 2020 *Algebraic Geometry* 7 (5), 591-624 (canonical scattering diagrams from log GW; wall functions as tropical-curve generating series); Nishinou-Siebert 2006 *Duke* 135 (the tropical-correspondence application); Behrend-Fantechi 1997 *Invent. Math.* 128 (virtual fundamental class, perfect obstruction theory machinery); Mikhalkin 2005 *J. AMS* 18 (the $n = 2$ predecessor of the tropical correspondence); Gross 2011 *Tropical Geometry and Mirror Symmetry* (CBMS 114) Lecture 6 (textbook synthesis)
  - intermediate: Abramovich-Chen 2014 §1-§3; Gross-Siebert 2013 §0-§4; Kato 1989 §1-§2; Behrend-Fantechi 1997 §1
  - beginner: Gross 2011 CBMS Lecture 6 informal opening; Abramovich 2014 *Bull. AMS* 51 *Logarithmic geometry and moduli* informal §1
- **notes**: Pointer unit recording the foundational log Gromov-Witten package consumed by [04.12.06] Nishinou-Siebert correspondence and [04.12.09] Gross-Siebert reconstruction. A **log Gromov-Witten invariant** is an integer count of stable log maps from a log smooth curve to a log smooth target, weighted by the virtual fundamental class on the moduli stack with perfect obstruction theory. Key data: a fine saturated (fs) log scheme $(X, M_X)$ in the sense of Kato 1989; a log smooth morphism $f : (C, M_C) \to (X, M_X)$ from a log smooth pointed curve; contact-order data $\mathbf{c} = (c_1, \ldots, c_m)$ at the marked points recording tangency to the boundary divisor; the moduli stack $\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{g, m}^{\log}(X / S, \beta, \mathbf{c})$ of stable log maps of fixed genus, marking, class, and contact data, with its perfect obstruction theory and virtual fundamental class $[\overline{\mathcal{M}}^{\log}]^{\mathrm{vir}}$ of expected dimension $(1 - g)(n - 3) + \langle c_1(T X^{\log}), \beta\rangle + m$. The log GW invariant is the integral $\langle \tau_{a_1}(\gamma_1) \cdots \tau_{a_m}(\gamma_m)\rangle^{\log}_{g, \beta, \mathbf{c}} = \int_{[\overline{\mathcal{M}}^{\log}]^{\mathrm{vir}}} \prod \psi_i^{a_i} \mathrm{ev}_i^*(\gamma_i)$. Foundational theorems: (1) the **Abramovich-Chen vs Gross-Siebert equivalence** (Abramovich-Chen-Marcus-Wise 2017), proving that the two parallel constructions of the moduli stack agree as Deligne-Mumford stacks with matching obstruction theories, so the resulting log GW invariants are well-defined; (2) the **degeneration formula** (Gross-Siebert 2013, Theorem 0.3): for a flat family $\pi : \mathcal{X} \to \mathbb{A}^1$ with smooth generic fibre and log smooth central fibre, $N_{g, \beta}(\mathcal{X}_t) = \sum_\tau N^{\log, \tau}_{g, \beta}(\mathcal{X}_0)$, the sum over decoration types $\tau$ of dual graphs with contact-order labels; (3) the **Nishinou-Siebert application** (Nishinou-Siebert 2006, [04.12.06]): for a toric degeneration of a smooth toric variety, the central-fibre log GW counts equal a multiplicity-weighted tropical-curve count on the dual intersection complex $B(\Xi)$. Master-tier sections: §1 foundational log-scheme setup after Kato 1989 (fs log structures, log smooth morphisms, log differentials, chart theory, basic monoid construction); §2 the two parallel constructions of Abramovich-Chen vs Gross-Siebert with the 2017 equivalence theorem; §3 the Nishinou-Siebert correspondence as a tropical computation via the degeneration formula. Forward-connects to [04.12.06] Nishinou-Siebert, [04.12.09] Gross-Siebert reconstruction, [04.12.10] SYZ, [04.12.13] period integral, plus a future Gromov-Witten foundations unit (not yet shipped; the Behrend-Fantechi virtual-class machinery is sketched here in lieu). Lean status `partial` with module `Codex.AlgGeom.Tropical.LogGromovWitten`: declares `FsLogStructure`, `LogScheme`, `LogSmoothMap`, `LogSmoothCurve`, `StableLogMap`, `ModuliStableLogMaps`, `virtualFundamentalClass`, `logGWInvariant`, plus theorems `logGW_well_defined`, `degeneration_formula`, `nishinou_siebert_application` — all `sorry`-stubbed pending Mathlib infrastructure on log geometry, moduli of stable log maps, perfect obstruction theory in the log category, and the virtual fundamental class.

### tropical.log-structures-log-smooth

- **title**: Logarithmic structures and log smooth morphisms
- **unit**: `04.12.14`
- **prerequisites**: `tropical.toric-degeneration-calabi-yau` (04.12.07 — the consumer that invokes log smoothness and log Calabi-Yau but defines neither); `04.08.02` canonical sheaf (the log canonical sheaf $K_X + D$ is its log-twisted analogue); `toric-geometry` fan and $X_\Sigma$ (04.11.04 — the toric log structure is built from the cone monoid $S_\sigma = \sigma^\vee \cap M$)
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Kato 1989 *Logarithmic structures of Fontaine-Illusie* (Algebraic Analysis, Geometry, and Number Theory, JHU Press, 191-224 — originator: the log structure $\alpha : M_X \to \mathcal{O}_X$, charts, fine saturated log schemes, log smooth/log etale via the infinitesimal-lifting and chart criteria, log differentials $\Omega^1_{X/S}(\log)$); Fontaine-Illusie 1980s (unpublished originating idea, $p$-adic Hodge theory / semistable reduction); Ogus 2018 *Lectures on Logarithmic Algebraic Geometry* (Cambridge Studies 178 — canonical modern textbook); F. Kato 1996 *Tohoku Math. J.* 48 (log smooth deformation theory — unobstructedness of normal-crossings smoothings); Kato-Nakayama 1999 *Kodai Math. J.* 22 (the Kato-Nakayama space $X^{\log}$, Betti realisation, the torus-fibration bridge to SYZ); Gross-Siebert 2006 *J. Differential Geom.* 72 §1-§2 and Gross-Siebert 2010 *J. Algebraic Geom.* 19 (divisorial, central-fibre, and intrinsic Kato-fan log structures on toric degenerations); Gross 2011 *Tropical Geometry and Mirror Symmetry* (CBMS 114) Lecture 3 (textbook exposition oriented to mirror symmetry); Abramovich 2014 *Bull. AMS* 51 (modern survey)
  - intermediate: Kato 1989 §1-§3; Ogus 2018 Ch. II-IV; Gross 2011 CBMS Lecture 3 §3.1-§3.2
  - beginner: Gross 2011 CBMS Lecture 3 informal opening; Abramovich 2014 *Bull. AMS* 51 §1 (remembering the boundary as bookkeeping)
- **notes**: Foundational unit defining the Kato apparatus invoked but never constructed across [04.12.07], [04.12.08], [04.12.09], and [04.12.15]. A **log structure** on a scheme $X$ is a sheaf of commutative monoids $M_X$ with a multiplicative map $\alpha : M_X \to (\mathcal{O}_X, \cdot)$ whose restriction over $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ is an isomorphism (Kato 1989, after Fontaine-Illusie); the **ghost sheaf** $\overline{M}_X = M_X / \mathcal{O}_X^*$ records the combinatorial/tropical vanishing data, with rank jumping along the stratification (rank $k$ where $k$ branches of a normal-crossings divisor meet). Two motivating examples: the **divisorial log structure** $M_{(X,D)} = \{g \in \mathcal{O}_X : g$ invertible on $X \setminus D\}$ of a normal-crossings divisor $D$, with ghost stalk $\mathbb{N}^k$; and the **toric log structure** from the monoid $S_\sigma = \sigma^\vee \cap M$ of a cone [04.11.04]. Charts subordinate to a monoid $P$, and the integral/fine/saturated hierarchy, give **fine saturated (fs) log schemes**, the class aligning ghost monoids with lattice cones. The **standard log point** ($\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{C}$ with $\mathbb{C}^* \oplus \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{C}$) is the base for the central fibre. **Log smooth** and **log etale** morphisms are characterised by **Kato's chart criterion** (the unit's key theorem, Kato 1989 Thm 3.5): $f$ is log smooth iff étale-locally it factors through a chart $\theta : Q \to P$ with $\ker\theta^{\mathrm{gp}}$ and the torsion of $\operatorname{coker}\theta^{\mathrm{gp}}$ finite of invertible order, and $X \to S \times_{\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}[Q]} \operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}[P]$ classically smooth — log smoothness is smoothness relative to a monomial model. The headline: a normal-crossings degeneration $\mathfrak{X} \to \operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{C}[\![t]\!]$ is log smooth (chart $\mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}^k$, $1 \mapsto (1,\dots,1)$) even though it is not smooth — the whole point. **Log differentials** $\Omega^1_{X/S}(\log D)$ (frame $dz_1/z_1, dz_2, \dots, dz_n$) and the **log canonical sheaf** $\omega_X^{\log} = \omega_X(D)$ realise the adjunction divisor $K_X + D$ [04.08.02]; a **log Calabi-Yau** pair has $K_X + D \sim 0$, and the central fibre of a toric degeneration [04.12.07] with its divisorial log structure is log Calabi-Yau even when singular — the Gross-Siebert starting point. Master sections: log smooth deformation theory (F. Kato 1996 — unobstructedness; Friedman d-semistability as a log condition), intrinsic Kato-fan log structures (Gross-Siebert 2010), and the log de Rham complex feeding the period/mirror-map output. Full-proof set: ghost sheaf $= \mathbb{N}^{I(\bar x)}$ for divisorial log structures, log smoothness of the standard semistable family $\{x_1\cdots x_r = t\}$ via the chart criterion, and log differentials detecting the log canonical. Forward-connects to [04.12.15] log Gromov-Witten (its stable log maps are log smooth over the standard log point) and the wider Gross-Siebert programme. Lean status: `none`. Mathlib has commutative-monoid and scheme infrastructure but no log structure: no sheaf-of-monoids-with-structure-map, no fine/saturated hierarchy, no chart formalism, no ghost sheaf, no log smooth/log etale morphism via the lifting criterion; formalisation requires first the theory of fine saturated monoids, then the log-scheme category, Kato's chart criterion, and the sheaf of log differentials with its residue sequence.

### number-theory.ramanujan-tau-function

- **title**: Ramanujan $\tau$-function and Ramanujan conjectures
- **unit**: `21.04.05`
- **prerequisites**: sibling `number-theory.modular-forms-sl2-z` (21.04.01 — defines the ambient space $S_{12}(\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z}))$ and the modular discriminant $\Delta$); sibling `number-theory.hecke-operators` (21.04.02 — Hecke-algebra relations from which multiplicativity and the recursion for $\tau$ descend); sibling `number-theory.riemann-zeta` (21.03.01 — prototype Dirichlet series with Euler product and functional equation, of which $L(s, \Delta)$ is the modular-form $\mathrm{GL}_2$ analogue)
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Ramanujan 1916 *Trans. Cambridge Phil. Soc.* 22, 159-184 (originator: $\Delta = q \prod (1 - q^n)^{24} = \sum \tau(n) q^n$, multiplicativity, recursion, $|\tau(p)| \leq 2 p^{11/2}$ conjectures); Mordell 1917 *Proc. Cambridge Phil. Soc.* 19, 117-124 (first proof of multiplicativity and recursion via the attached Dirichlet series); Hecke 1937 *Math. Ann.* 114 (Hecke-operator framework deriving both as automatic from $\Delta$ being a one-dimensional eigenform); Deligne 1971 *Sém. Bourbaki* 355 (the $\ell$-adic Galois representation $\rho_\Delta$ via étale cohomology of the Kuga-Sato eleven-fold); Deligne 1974 *Publ. Math. IHÉS* 43 (Ramanujan-Petersson bound $|\tau(p)| \leq 2 p^{11/2}$ via Weil II); Petersson 1939 *Math. Ann.* 116 (Ramanujan-Petersson conjecture stated for arbitrary weight); Swinnerton-Dyer 1973 *Lecture Notes in Math.* 350 (exceptional primes for $\Delta$, mod-691 congruence); Lehmer 1947 *Duke Math. J.* 14 (Lehmer's non-vanishing conjecture); Serre 1973 *A Course in Arithmetic* (GTM 7) Ch. VII §§4-5; Diamond-Shurman 2005 (GTM 228) Ch. 1 §1.2 + Ch. 5 §§5.2-5.3; Manin-Panchishkin 2005 (Springer EMS 49, 2nd ed.) Ch. 6 §§3-4 + Ch. 7; Shimura 1971 (Princeton/Iwanami) Ch. 3
  - intermediate: Serre 1973 Ch. VII §§4-5; Diamond-Shurman 2005 §1.2 + §§5.2-5.3; Manin-Panchishkin 2005 Ch. 6 §3
  - beginner: Serre 1973 Ch. VII §4 (the discriminant $\Delta$ and its first Fourier coefficients); Diamond-Shurman 2005 §1.2 informal opening on $\Delta$ and $\tau$
- **notes**: The **modular discriminant** $\Delta(z) = q \prod_{n \geq 1} (1 - q^n)^{24}$ is the unique normalised cusp form of weight 12 on $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$ ($\dim S_{12} = 1$); equivalently $\Delta = \eta^{24}$ where $\eta$ is the Dedekind eta function, equivalently $\Delta = (E_4^3 - E_6^2)/1728$. The **Ramanujan $\tau$-function** $\tau : \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 1} \to \mathbb{Z}$ is the integer sequence of Fourier coefficients, $\Delta = \sum_n \tau(n) q^n$, with $\tau(1) = 1, \tau(2) = -24, \tau(3) = 252, \tau(4) = -1472, \tau(5) = 4830, \tau(6) = -6048, \tau(7) = -16744, \ldots$. **Ramanujan three conjectures (1916)**: (i) **multiplicativity** $\tau(mn) = \tau(m) \tau(n)$ for $\gcd(m, n) = 1$; (ii) **prime-power recursion** $\tau(p^{k+1}) = \tau(p) \tau(p^k) - p^{11} \tau(p^{k-1})$; (iii) **Ramanujan-Petersson bound** $|\tau(p)| \leq 2 p^{11/2}$. **Mordell 1917** proved (i) and (ii) directly via the Dirichlet series; **Hecke 1937** recovered them automatically from the Hecke-algebra relations applied to $\Delta$ as a one-dimensional Hecke eigenform; **Deligne 1971** constructed the two-dimensional $\ell$-adic Galois representation $\rho_\Delta : G_\mathbb{Q} \to \mathrm{GL}_2(\overline{\mathbb{Q}_\ell})$ on the $\Delta$-isotypic component of $H^{11}_\text{ét}$ of the Kuga-Sato eleven-fold, with $\mathrm{tr}\,\rho_\Delta(\mathrm{Frob}_p) = \tau(p)$ and $\det \rho_\Delta(\mathrm{Frob}_p) = p^{11}$; **Deligne 1974** proved (iii) via Weil II (purity of Frobenius eigenvalues on étale cohomology), settling Ramanujan strongest conjecture fifty-eight years after the original paper. The attached **Dirichlet series** $L(s, \Delta) = \sum_{n \geq 1} \tau(n) n^{-s}$ on $\mathrm{Re}(s) > 13/2$ admits the **Euler product** $\prod_p (1 - \tau(p) p^{-s} + p^{11 - 2s})^{-1}$ from the multiplicativity and recursion; the completed $L$-function $\Lambda(s, \Delta) = (2\pi)^{-s} \Gamma(s) L(s, \Delta)$ extends to an entire function on $\mathbb{C}$ satisfying the **functional equation** $\Lambda(s, \Delta) = \Lambda(12 - s, \Delta)$, derived from the Mellin transform of $\Delta(i y)$ split at $y = 1$ together with the weight-12 transformation $\Delta(-1/z) = z^{12} \Delta(z)$. **Lehmer non-vanishing conjecture** (1947): $\tau(n) \neq 0$ for every $n \geq 1$ — verified to $n \leq 10^{16}$ (2024) but open in general. **Ramanujan congruences** (Ramanujan 1916, Serre 1968, Swinnerton-Dyer 1973): $\tau(p) \equiv 1 + p^{11} \pmod{691}$, $\tau(p) \equiv p + p^{10} \pmod{23}$, plus congruences mod $\ell \in \{2, 3, 5, 7\}$; the exceptional primes for $\Delta$ are exactly $\{2, 3, 5, 7, 23, 691\}$, with the mod-691 congruence explained by the reducible residual representation $\bar\rho_{\Delta, 691} \cong \mathbf{1} \oplus \chi_\text{cyc}^{11}$. **Sato-Tate distribution** (Barnet-Lamb-Geraghty-Harris-Taylor 2011 *Publ. RIMS* 47): the normalised values $\tau(p) / (2 p^{11/2}) \in [-1, 1]$ are equidistributed against the semicircle measure $\frac{2}{\pi} \sqrt{1 - x^2} dx$; covered in unit [21.06.02]. **Originator chain**: Jacobi 1829 (triple product identity giving $\eta^{24}$); Ramanujan 1916 (three conjectures); Mordell 1917 (multiplicativity + recursion proofs); Hecke 1937 (Hecke-operator framework); Petersson 1939 (general Ramanujan-Petersson conjecture); Rankin 1939 + Selberg 1940 (partial bounds via Rankin-Selberg); Eichler 1954 (weight-2 case via Eichler-Shimura); Deligne 1971 ($\rho_\Delta$ via Kuga-Sato); Deligne 1974 (Weil II); Serre 1968 + Swinnerton-Dyer 1973 (exceptional primes); Barnet-Lamb-Geraghty-Harris-Taylor 2011 (Sato-Tate for general weight). Forward-connects to [21.04.03] Eichler-Shimura correspondence (sibling, weight-2 analogue), [21.05.01] $\ell$-adic Galois representations (sibling, $\rho_\Delta$ as the second example after the Tate module), [21.06.01] modularity theorem + BSD (sibling, modularity extends the $\rho_\Delta$ construction to elliptic curves), [21.06.02] Sato-Tate (successor, equidistribution refinement of Ramanujan-Petersson); backward-connects to [21.04.01] modular forms on $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$, [21.04.02] Hecke operators and Hecke algebra, [21.03.01] Riemann zeta (sibling Dirichlet series). Lean status: `none`, Mathlib lacks named `modularDiscriminant : ModularForm SL2Z 12` and `ramanujanTau` of natural numbers to integers, the dimension theorem $\dim S_{12} = 1$, the multiplicativity and recursion theorems, the Euler product and functional equation of $L(s, \Delta)$, Deligne 1971 attachment $\rho_\Delta$ via the Kuga-Sato eleven-fold, and Deligne 1974 Ramanujan-Petersson bound via Weil II. The full formalisation roadmap requires Kuga-Sato varieties, étale cohomology of smooth proper varieties over $\mathbb{Q}$, the purity statement for Frobenius eigenvalues, and the Eichler-Shimura congruence relation in higher weight.

### number-theory.hecke-operators

- **title**: Hecke Operators and Hecke Algebra
- **unit**: `21.04.02`
- **prerequisites**: sibling `number-theory.modular-forms-sl2z` (21.04.01 — defines the ambient space $M_k(\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z}))$ on which the Hecke operators act); `complex-analysis.complex-numbers-euler` (02.09.01 — Fourier-series / Dirichlet-series machinery)
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Hecke 1936 *Math. Ann.* 112, 664-699 and Hecke 1937 *Math. Ann.* 114, 1-28 (originator pair: Hecke operators $T_n$, multiplicativity, the Hecke algebra $\mathbb{T}$, Euler product for the attached $L$-function, functional equation); Petersson 1939 *Math. Ann.* 116 (Petersson inner product on cusp forms, Hecke self-adjointness, spectral decomposition of $S_k(\Gamma)$); Atkin-Lehner 1970 *Math. Ann.* 185 (new and old forms at level $\Gamma_0(N)$, Atkin-Lehner involutions $w_q$, multiplicity-one theorem for newforms); Eichler 1954 *Arch. Math.* 5 + Shimura 1958 *Tohoku Math. J.* 10 (cohomological realisation of Hecke operators on $H^1(X_0(N))$); Deligne 1971 *Sém. Bourbaki* 355 + Deligne-Serre 1974 *Ann. Sci. ENS* 7 (two-dimensional $\ell$-adic Galois representations attached to weight-$\geq 1$ cusp eigenforms, trace of Frobenius equal to $a_p$); Deligne 1974 *Publ. Math. IHES* 43 (Ramanujan-Petersson conjecture as theorem); Shimura 1971 *Introduction to the Arithmetic Theory of Automorphic Functions* (Princeton); Serre 1973 *A Course in Arithmetic* (GTM 7) Ch. VII §5; Lang 1976 *Introduction to Modular Forms* (Grundlehren 222); Diamond-Shurman 2005 *A First Course in Modular Forms* (GTM 228) Ch. 5; Manin-Panchishkin 2005 *Introduction to Modern Number Theory* (Springer EMS 49, 2nd ed.) Ch. 6
  - intermediate: Diamond-Shurman 2005 Ch. 5; Serre 1973 Ch. VII §5
  - beginner: Diamond-Shurman 2005 §5.1 informal — Hecke operators as sub-lattice averages of index $p$; the eigenform-Fourier-coefficient identity ($T_p f = a_p f$, eigenvalue equals coefficient)
- **notes**: The **Hecke operator** $T_p$ at a prime $p$ acting on $M_k(\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z}))$ is the sub-lattice average $(T_p f)(z) = p^{k - 1} f(p z) + p^{-1} \sum_{b = 0}^{p - 1} f((z + b)/p)$, equivalently the sum over coset representatives $f |_k \delta$ for $\delta$ ranging over $\Gamma \backslash M_2(\mathbb{Z})_{\det = p}$. The operators commute pairwise, satisfy the **multiplicativity** $T_{m n} = T_m T_n$ for $\gcd(m, n) = 1$ and the **prime-power recursion** $T_p T_{p^n} = T_{p^{n + 1}} + p^{k - 1} T_{p^{n - 1}}$, and generate the commutative **Hecke algebra** $\mathbb{T} = \mathbb{C}[T_n : n \geq 1] \subseteq \mathrm{End}_\mathbb{C}(M_k(\Gamma))$. **Signature theorem (Hecke 1936)**: for a normalised Hecke eigenform $f$ with $a_1(f) = 1$, the eigenvalue $\lambda_n$ of $T_n$ equals the $n$-th Fourier coefficient $a_n(f)$; consequently the Dirichlet series $L(f, s) = \sum a_n n^{-s}$ acquires the **Euler product** $\prod_p (1 - a_p p^{-s} + p^{k - 1 - 2 s})^{-1}$, with quadratic local factors whose coefficients are the eigenvalue $a_p$ and the weight-dependent constant $p^{k - 1}$. **Petersson self-adjointness (1939)**: the Hecke operators are self-adjoint with respect to the Petersson inner product $\langle f, g \rangle_{\mathrm{Pet}} = \int_{\Gamma \backslash \mathbb{H}} f \bar g y^{k - 2} dx dy$, yielding an orthonormal basis of simultaneous Hecke eigenforms on $S_k(\Gamma)$. **Atkin-Lehner 1970 multiplicity-one** at higher level $\Gamma_0(N)$: every newform is uniquely determined (up to scalar) by its Hecke eigenvalue sequence $\{a_p(f) : p \nmid N\}$ plus Atkin-Lehner signs. **Deligne-Serre 1974** attaches a $2$-dimensional $\ell$-adic Galois representation $\rho_{f, \ell} : \mathrm{Gal}(\overline{\mathbb{Q}}/\mathbb{Q}) \to \mathrm{GL}_2(\overline{\mathbb{Q}_\ell})$ to every normalised weight-$\geq 1$ cusp newform $f$ with $\mathrm{tr}\,\rho_{f, \ell}(\mathrm{Frob}_p) = a_p(f)$ and $\det \rho_{f, \ell}(\mathrm{Frob}_p) = p^{k - 1}$ for $p \nmid N \ell$. Worked example: the discriminant cusp form $\Delta(z) = q \prod (1 - q^n)^{24}$ of weight $12$ on $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$ is the unique normalised cusp form in a one-dimensional space, automatically a Hecke eigenform with eigenvalues $\tau(n)$ (Ramanujan tau), the multiplicativity $\tau(m n) = \tau(m) \tau(n)$ for coprime $(m, n)$ and the recursion $\tau(p) \tau(p^n) = \tau(p^{n + 1}) + p^{11} \tau(p^{n - 1})$ being direct consequences. Deligne 1974 proved $|\tau(p)| \leq 2 p^{11/2}$ (Ramanujan-Petersson). **Originator chain**: Ramanujan 1916 (multiplicativity conjectures for $\tau$); Mordell 1917 *Proc. Cambridge Phil. Soc.* (direct proof via $L$-function); Hecke 1936-37 *Math. Ann.* 112 + 114 (operators $T_n$, Hecke algebra, Euler product); Petersson 1939 *Math. Ann.* 116 (Petersson inner product, self-adjointness); Eichler 1954 + Shimura 1958 (cohomological realisation); Atkin-Lehner 1970 (new/old decomposition, multiplicity-one); Deligne 1971 + Deligne-Serre 1974 (Galois representations); Deligne 1974 (Ramanujan-Petersson). Forward-connects to [21.04.03] Eichler-Shimura correspondence, [21.05.01] $\ell$-adic Galois representations, [21.06.01] modularity theorem and BSD, [21.03.01] Riemann zeta (sibling Dirichlet series), [21.03.02] Dirichlet $L$-functions (sibling $\mathrm{GL}_1$-case), [21.03.03] Dedekind / Hecke / Artin $L$ (sibling higher-rank generalisations). Lean status: `partial`, module `Codex.NumberTheory.ModularForms.HeckeOperators` declares `def heckeOperator (p : ℕ) [Fact (Nat.Prime p)] : ModularForm k → ModularForm k`, `theorem hecke_multiplicative`, `theorem hecke_prime_power_recursion`, `theorem hecke_eigenform_fourier_coeff_eq_eigenvalue`, and `theorem hecke_petersson_self_adjoint` with `sorry`-stubbed proof bodies pending Mathlib's coset-decomposition API for $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$ acting on determinant-$n$ integer matrices and the integration-on-fundamental-domain machinery for the Petersson inner product.

### foundations.subgroup-coset-quotient

- **title**: Subgroup, coset, and quotient group with the isomorphism theorems
- **prerequisites**: `foundations.group`
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Lang *Algebra* (Springer GTM 211, 2002) Ch. I §2 + Dummit-Foote Ch. 3
  - intermediate: Artin *Algebra* (Pearson, 2nd ed. 2011) Ch. 2 §§4-12
  - beginner: Fraleigh *A First Course in Abstract Algebra* Ch. 2-3
- **notes**: Subgroup as closed-under-operation-and-inverse subset. Left and right cosets; Lagrange's theorem ($|G| = [G : H] \cdot |H|$). Normal subgroup $N \triangleleft G$ as kernel of a homomorphism, equivalently subgroup whose left and right cosets coincide. Quotient $G/N$ with the canonical projection $\pi: G \twoheadrightarrow G/N$. The four isomorphism theorems: first (kernel-image: $G/\ker f \cong \mathrm{im}\, f$), second (diamond: $(HN)/N \cong H/(H \cap N)$), third (lattice modular law: $(G/N)/(M/N) \cong G/M$ when $N \subseteq M \triangleleft G$), fourth (correspondence: subgroups of $G/N$ correspond bijectively to subgroups of $G$ containing $N$). Used everywhere downstream — character theory of finite groups, Galois theory, classifying spaces, deck transformation groups.

### number-theory.eichler-shimura-correspondence

- **title**: Eichler-Shimura correspondence
- **unit**: `21.04.03`
- **prerequisites**: `number-theory.modular-forms-sl2z`, `number-theory.hecke-operators`, `riemann-surfaces.jacobian-variety`, `algebraic-geometry.elliptic-curve`
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Eichler 1954 *Arch. Math.* 5 + Shimura 1958 *J. Math. Soc. Japan* 10 (originator pair); Shimura *Introduction to the Arithmetic Theory of Automorphic Functions* (Princeton 1971) Ch. 7; Deligne 1971 *Sém. Bourbaki* 355; Deligne-Serre 1974; Diamond-Shurman 2005 Ch. 8-9; Manin-Panchishkin Ch. 6
  - intermediate: Diamond-Shurman *A First Course in Modular Forms* (GTM 228, 2005) Ch. 6 §§6.1-6.6 and Ch. 8; Shimura 1971 Ch. 7
  - beginner: Diamond-Shurman 2005 §6.6 informal — weight-$2$ cusp eigenforms attach to two-dimensional Galois representations on the Jacobian of $X_0(N)$
- **notes**: The **Eichler-Shimura correspondence** attaches to each normalised weight-$2$ cusp newform $f \in S_2(\Gamma_0(N))^{\mathrm{new}}$ a continuous two-dimensional $\ell$-adic Galois representation $\rho_{f, \ell} : \mathrm{Gal}(\overline{\mathbb{Q}}/\mathbb{Q}) \to \mathrm{GL}_2(\overline{\mathbb{Q}_\ell})$, realised on the $f$-isotypic component of the rational $\ell$-adic Tate module $V_\ell = T_\ell J_0(N) \otimes \mathbb{Q}_\ell$ of the modular Jacobian $J_0(N) = \mathrm{Jac}(X_0(N))$. The representation is unramified outside $N\ell$ (Néron-Ogg-Shafarevich applied to $J_0(N)$ which has good reduction at $p \nmid N$), and satisfies the trace-of-Frobenius identity $\mathrm{tr}\,\rho_{f, \ell}(\mathrm{Frob}_p) = a_p(f)$ and $\det \rho_{f, \ell}(\mathrm{Frob}_p) = p$ for every $p \nmid N\ell$. **Signature theorem (Eichler-Shimura congruence relation)**: on the special fibre $X_0(N) \otimes \mathbb{F}_p$ for $p \nmid N$, the Hecke correspondence $T_p$ satisfies $T_p \equiv \mathrm{Frob}_p + p \langle p \rangle \mathrm{Frob}_p^{-1} \pmod{\ell}$ on $T_\ell J_0(N)$ for $\ell \neq p$, derived from the moduli-theoretic split of the $p + 1$ order-$p$ subgroups of an elliptic curve over $\overline{\mathbb{F}_p}$ into one Frobenius-kernel subgroup and $p$ Verschiebung-kernel subgroups. **Eigenform decomposition**: $V_\ell \otimes \overline{\mathbb{Q}_\ell} = \bigoplus_{[f]} V_{\ell, f}$ ranges over Galois-conjugacy classes of weight-$2$ cusp newforms (modulo old-form contributions from $\Gamma_0(M)$, $M | N$), each $V_{\ell, f}$ a free rank-$2$ $\overline{\mathbb{Q}_\ell}$-module on which $T_p$ acts by $a_p(f)$. **Shimura's modular abelian variety**: $A_f := J_0(N)/I_f \cdot J_0(N)$ with $I_f = \ker(\lambda_f : \mathbb{T}_N \to K_f)$ is an abelian variety over $\mathbb{Q}$ of dimension $d = [K_f : \mathbb{Q}]$ where $K_f = \mathbb{Q}(a_p(f) : p \nmid N)$ is the eigenvalue field; isogenous to $\prod_\sigma E_{f^\sigma}$ over Galois conjugates of $f$, with $L(A_f, s) = \prod_\sigma L(f^\sigma, s)$. Worked example: at level $N = 11$, $\dim S_2(\Gamma_0(11)) = g(X_0(11)) = 1$, and the unique normalised weight-$2$ cusp newform $f = q - 2q^2 - q^3 + 2q^4 + q^5 + \cdots$ corresponds to the elliptic curve $E : y^2 + y = x^3 - x^2 - 10x - 20$ of conductor $11$, with $a_p(f) = p + 1 - |E(\mathbb{F}_p)|$ matching at every $p \neq 11$. **Higher-weight extension (Deligne 1971)**: for $k \geq 2$, $\rho_{f, \ell}$ is built from the étale cohomology in degree $k - 1$ of the Kuga-Sato variety $\widetilde{\mathcal{E}}^{k-2}_N$ (smooth compactification of the $(k-2)$-fold fibre product of the universal elliptic curve over $Y_0(N)$), giving $\det \rho_{f, \ell}(\mathrm{Frob}_p) = p^{k-1}$. **Weight-1 case (Deligne-Serre 1974)** via congruences with higher-weight forms; finite-image / Artin representations. **Ramanujan-Petersson (Deligne 1974 *Publ. Math. IHES* 43)**: $|a_p(f)| \leq 2 p^{(k-1)/2}$ via Weil II applied to the étale cohomology of the modular / Kuga-Sato varieties — the modular-form Riemann hypothesis. **Originator chain**: Eichler 1954 *Arch. Math.* 5 (Brandt-matrix trace formula on $S_2(\Gamma_0(N))$ identified with Frobenius trace on $H^1(X_0(N) \otimes \mathbb{F}_p)$); Shimura 1958 *J. Math. Soc. Japan* 10 (systematic construction of Galois representations from weight-2 eigenforms via the Jacobian $J_0(N)$); Shimura 1971 *Introduction to the Arithmetic Theory of Automorphic Functions* (canonical book-form treatment); Deligne 1971 (extension to weight $k \geq 2$ via Kuga-Sato); Deligne-Serre 1974 (weight-1 case); Deligne 1974 (Ramanujan-Petersson via Weil II); Carayol 1986 *Ann. Sci. ENS* 19 (local Langlands compatibility at $p | N$); Ribet 1990 *Invent. Math.* 100 (level-lowering / epsilon conjecture); Mazur-Wiles 1984 (Iwasawa Main Conjecture application); Wiles 1995 + BCDT 2001 (modularity theorem application). Forward-connects to [21.05.01] $\ell$-adic Galois representations (sibling, the systematic framework on which Eichler-Shimura is the originating example), [21.06.01] modularity theorem and BSD (every elliptic curve over $\mathbb{Q}$ arises as $A_f$ for some weight-$2$ cusp newform), [21.07.01] Iwasawa $\mathbb{Z}_p$-extensions (Mazur-Wiles Main Conjecture uses Eichler-Shimura Galois representations), [21.03.03] Dedekind / Hecke / Artin $L$ (sibling, $L(f, s) = L(\rho_{f, \ell}, s)$ identification); backward-connects to [21.04.01] modular forms on $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$, [21.04.02] Hecke operators and Hecke algebra, [06.06.03] Jacobian variety, [04.04.03] elliptic curves. Lean status: `partial`, module `Codex.NumberTheory.ModularForms.EichlerShimura` declares `def Jacobian_X0N`, `def galoisRepOfEigenform`, `theorem trace_frob_eq_fourier_coeff`, `theorem eichler_shimura_congruence`, `theorem unramified_outside_N_ell`, `def modularAbelianVariety`, `theorem tate_module_eigenform_decomposition`, `def KugaSatoVariety`, `theorem deligne_higher_weight` with `sorry`-stubbed proof bodies pending Mathlib's modular-curve / Jacobian over $\mathbb{Q}$ / étale cohomology / Néron-Ogg-Shafarevich / moduli-theoretic Hecke correspondence infrastructure.

### number-theory.p-adic-l-functions-main-conjecture

- **title**: $p$-adic $L$-functions and the Iwasawa Main Conjecture
- **unit**: `21.07.02`
- **prerequisites**: `number-theory.riemann-zeta` (21.03.01 — classical $\zeta$ at negative integers is the seed of $p$-adic interpolation via Bernoulli numbers); `number-theory.dirichlet-l-functions` (21.03.02 — Dirichlet $L$-values at negative integers are the interpolation targets)
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Kubota-Leopoldt 1964 *J. reine angew. Math.* 214/215, 328-339 (originator: $p$-adic Dirichlet L-functions via Kummer congruences); Iwasawa 1969 *J. Math. Soc. Japan* 16 + *Ann. Math.* 89 (interpolation point of view, Main Conjecture conjectured); Mazur 1972 *Invent. Math.* 18 (Iwasawa theory of elliptic curves); Mazur-Wiles 1984 *Invent. Math.* 76, 179-330 (Main Conjecture for $\mathbb{Q}$, theorem); Wiles 1990 *Ann. Math.* 131, 493-540 (Main Conjecture for totally real fields); Rubin 1991 *Invent. Math.* 103 (alternative Euler-system proof); Greenberg 1989 *Astérisque* 165 (Iwasawa theory for $p$-adic representations); Coates-Sujatha 2006 *Cyclotomic Fields and Zeta Values* (Springer Monographs); Washington 1997 *Introduction to Cyclotomic Fields* (GTM 83, 2nd ed.); Kato 2004 *Astérisque* 295 (Euler systems for modular forms, BSD-Iwasawa); Skinner-Urban 2014 *Invent. Math.* 195 (Main Conjecture for elliptic curves); Bertolini-Darmon 2005 *Ann. Math.* 162 (anticyclotomic Main Conjecture); Manin-Panchishkin 2005 *Introduction to Modern Number Theory* Ch. 7
  - intermediate: Washington 1997 Ch. 5-7 + Ch. 13; Coates-Sujatha 2006 Ch. 2-4; Iwasawa 1972 *Lectures on $p$-adic L-functions* (Annals Math. Studies 74)
  - beginner: Washington 1997 Ch. 5 informal opening — generalised Bernoulli numbers and Kummer congruences as the seed of $p$-adic interpolation; Coates-Sujatha 2006 §1 informal overview
- **notes**: The **Kubota-Leopoldt $p$-adic L-function** $L_p(s, \chi) : \mathbb{Z}_p \to \mathbb{C}_p$ attached to a Dirichlet character $\chi$ of conductor coprime to the odd prime $p$ is the unique continuous interpolation of the classical Dirichlet L-values at negative integers, $L_p(1 - n, \chi) = (1 - \chi\omega^{-n}(p) p^{n - 1}) L(1 - n, \chi\omega^{-n})$ for positive integers $n$, with the Euler factor at $p$ removed and $\omega$ the Teichmuller character. Existence + uniqueness via Kummer congruences for generalised Bernoulli numbers $B_{n, \chi}$ — the refinement $n \equiv m \pmod{p^{a - 1}(p - 1)}$ implies the corrected ratios are congruent modulo $p^a$, giving uniform continuity on the dense subset $\{1 - n : n \geq 1\}$ of $\mathbb{Z}_p$. Via the **Amice transform** (Amice 1964 *Bull. Soc. Math. France* 92), $L_p$ becomes an element $\xi_\chi$ of the **Iwasawa algebra** $\Lambda = \mathbb{Z}_p[[\Gamma]] \cong \mathbb{Z}_p[[T]]$ where $\Gamma = \mathrm{Gal}(\mathbb{Q}(\mu_{p^\infty})/\mathbb{Q}) \cong \mathbb{Z}_p$. **Iwasawa Main Conjecture (Iwasawa 1969 conjectured; Mazur-Wiles 1984 *Invent. Math.* 76 theorem)**: for an even non-principal Dirichlet character $\chi$ of conductor coprime to $p$, the characteristic ideal of the minus-part $\chi$-component of the cyclotomic Iwasawa module $X_\chi^- = \varprojlim_n A_n^{(\chi), -}$ (projective limit of $p$-Sylows of ideal class groups of $\mathbb{Q}(\mu_{p^{n+1}})$, $\chi$-isotypic minus part) equals the ideal $(\xi_\chi) \subseteq \Lambda$. **Mazur-Wiles strategy**: cuspidal-Eisenstein congruences on $\Gamma_1(N p^r)$ via $\Lambda$-adic modular forms (Hida 1985 *Ann. Sci. ENS* 19), the Eisenstein ideal $I_E \subseteq \mathbb{T}$ with $\mathbb{T}/I_E \cong \Lambda$, the associated $\Lambda$-adic Galois representation $\rho : G_\mathbb{Q} \to \mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{T})$ with reduction to the diagonal Eisenstein representation, off-diagonal Galois cohomology classes lifting to $X_\chi^-$. **Reverse divisibility** via Iwasawa $p$-adic class-number formula + **Ferrero-Washington 1979 *Ann. Math.* 109** (cyclotomic $\mu$-invariant vanishes for abelian fields). **Generalisations**: Wiles 1990 *Ann. Math.* 131 (totally real fields via Hilbert modular forms + Deligne-Ribet $p$-adic L); Rubin 1991 *Invent. Math.* 103 (Euler-system proof via cyclotomic units, imaginary quadratic fields via elliptic units); Greenberg 1989 *Astérisque* 165 (framework for arbitrary $p$-adic representations); Kato 2004 *Astérisque* 295 (Beilinson-Kato Euler system for modular Galois representations); Skinner-Urban 2014 *Invent. Math.* 195 (Main Conjecture for elliptic curves with good ordinary reduction, residually irreducible $\bar\rho_{E, p}$, characteristic ideal of dual Selmer = Mazur-Swinnerton-Dyer $p$-adic L); Bertolini-Darmon 2005 *Ann. Math.* 162 (anticyclotomic Main Conjecture via Heegner-point Euler system). **BSD application**: Skinner-Urban + Kato proves the $p$-part of Birch-Swinnerton-Dyer for elliptic curves of analytic rank zero; combined with Gross-Zagier 1986 *Invent. Math.* 84 and Bertolini-Darmon, addresses rank one over imaginary quadratic fields. **Originator chain**: Kummer 1851 (Bernoulli congruences); von Staudt-Clausen 1840 (Bernoulli denominators); Vandiver 1937 (irregular primes); Iwasawa 1959 *Bull. Amer. Math. Soc.* 65 ($\mathbb{Z}_p$-extensions); Kubota-Leopoldt 1964 *J. reine angew. Math.* 214/215 ($p$-adic L); Iwasawa 1969 (Main Conjecture conjectured); Coates-Wiles 1977 *Invent. Math.* 39 (BSD-Iwasawa for CM elliptic curves); Mazur-Wiles 1984 (Main Conjecture for $\mathbb{Q}$); Wiles 1990 (totally real); Rubin 1991 (Euler systems); Greenberg 1989 (general framework); Kato 2004 (Beilinson-Kato); Bertolini-Darmon 2005 (anticyclotomic); Skinner-Urban 2014 (elliptic). Forward-connects to [21.06.01] modularity + BSD, [21.07.01] $\mathbb{Z}_p$-extensions + Iwasawa theory (sibling), [21.04.02] Hecke operators (Mazur-Wiles uses cuspidal-Eisenstein Hecke congruences), [21.05.01] $\ell$-adic Galois reps (input to Skinner-Urban), [21.03.01] Riemann zeta (Kubota-Leopoldt principal-character case), [21.03.02] Dirichlet L (general-character case).
