A trace on a von Neumann algebra: a normal positive functional with the trace identity $\tau(x^*x)=\tau(xx^*)$ (equivalently $\tau(uxu^*)=\tau(x)$); a finite factor carries a faithful normal tracial state. **Uniqueness** on a II$_1$ factor (the comparison theorem of 39.03.04 forces one normalised trace), and the induced **continuous dimension** $\dim_M(pH)=\tau(p)\in[0,1]$ with surjective range $[0,1]$ (contrast the discrete $\{0,1/n,\dots,1\}$ of I$_n$); $p\sim q\iff\tau(p)=\tau(q)$, $p\preceq q\iff\tau(p)\le\tau(q)$. **Standard II$_1$ factors**: the group von Neumann algebra $L(G)=\lambda(G)''$ of an i.c.c. group with canonical trace $\tau(x)=\langle x\delta_e,\delta_e\rangle$ (factor $\iff$ i.c.c.); the free group factors $L(F_n)$ and the open isomorphism problem $L(F_n)\cong L(F_m)$ (Dykema-Radulescu dichotomy, free probability); the hyperfinite $R=\overline{\bigcup_n M_{2^n}(\mathbb C)}$ (CAR algebra weak closure) unique among AFD II$_1$ factors (Murray-von Neumann 1943); crossed product / group-measure-space $L^\infty(X,\mu)\rtimes G$. **Jones index** $[M:N]=\dim_N L^2(M)$ for a subfactor with the index theorem $[M:N]\in\{4\cos^2(\pi/n):n\ge3\}\cup[4,\infty]$ (Jones 1983); the basic construction $M_1=\langle M,e_N\rangle=(JNJ)'$, the Jones projection $e_N$ onto $L^2(N)$, the Temperley-Lieb relations $e_ie_{i\pm1}e_i=\beta e_i$, and the planar-algebra standard invariant. **Connes' theorem** injective $\iff$ hyperfinite $\iff$ amenable $\iff$ semidiscrete (Connes 1976), uniqueness of the injective II$_1$ factor. Takesaki Ch. V; Jones 1983; Connes 1976; Jones-Sunder. Lean status none: Mathlib lacks the tracial state, continuous dimension, $L(G)$, the hyperfinite factor, the Jones index/basic construction, the Temperley-Lieb relations, and Connes' injective=hyperfinite theorem.

### operator-algebras.six-term-sequence-bott-periodicity

- **title**: The Six-Term Exact Sequence, Bott Periodicity, and AF Classification
- **unit**: `39.02.03`
- **prerequisites**: `39.02.02` (operator K-theory: K_0 and K_1 of C*-algebras), `39.01.04` (the Toeplitz algebra, Cuntz algebras, and extensions)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `39.02.03`. The cyclic six-term exact sequence of operator K-theory attached to a short exact sequence 0 -> J -> A -> A/J -> 0: K_0(J)->K_0(A)->K_0(A/J)->(exp) K_1(J)->K_1(A)->K_1(A/J)->(index) K_0(J). The index map d_1:K_1(A/J)->K_0(J) (unitary lift of diag(u,u*), projection difference) and the exponential map d_0:K_0(A/J)->K_1(J) (self-adjoint lift a of a projection, [exp(2 pi i a)]). Bott periodicity K_0(A)=K_1(SA) for the suspension SA=C_0(R)(x)A, giving 2-periodicity and folding the long exact sequence into the hexagon (Cuntz's Toeplitz-extension proof). Worked example: the Toeplitz extension 0->K->T->C(T)->0 has index map = Fredholm index = -winding, recovering K^1; K_0(O_n)=Z/(n-1) via the Toeplitz-Cuntz extension. Pimsner-Voiculescu sequence for crossed products A rtimes Z computes K_*(A_theta)=Z^2 (39.02.01). Elliott classification of AF algebras by the pointed ordered K_0 (dimension group) completed as a corollary via approximate intertwining; Effros-Handelman-Shen characterise dimension groups (unperforated + Riesz interpolation). Kirchberg-Phillips classification of purely infinite simple nuclear (Kirchberg) C*-algebras in the UCT class by (K_0,[1],K_1), classifying the Cuntz algebras; the Elliott program, Z-stability, Toms-Winter. Davidson Ch. VII-IX; Blackadar Ch. 8-10; Rordam-Larsen-Laustsen; Elliott 1976; Kirchberg-Phillips 2000. Lean status none: Mathlib lacks the suspension, mapping cone, half-exactness, index/exponential maps, Bott periodicity, the six-term sequence, ordered K_0 as a complete invariant, and the Elliott / Kirchberg-Phillips classification theorems.

### operator-algebras.modular-automorphism-group-kms-condition

- **title**: The Modular Automorphism Group and the KMS Condition
- **unit**: `39.04.03`
- **prerequisites**: `39.04.02` (Tomita's theorem: the modular operator and modular conjugation), `39.04.01` (cyclic and separating vectors and the standard form)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `39.04.03`. The modular automorphism group sigma_t^phi = Ad(Delta^{it}) attached to a faithful normal state phi on a von Neumann algebra M, and its characterisation by the KMS (Kubo-Martin-Schwinger) condition at inverse temperature beta=1: for entire-analytic a and b in M the function F(t)=phi(b sigma_t(a)) extends holomorphically to the strip 0<=Im z<=1 with boundary relation F(t+i)=phi(sigma_t(a) b). Uniqueness of the KMS flow; phi is a trace iff sigma^phi = id (Delta = 1). Connes cocycle Radon-Nikodym derivative (D psi : D phi)_t and the canonical outer flow delta: R -> Out(M), with the T-invariant T(M)=ker delta and Connes spectrum S(M)=cap_phi Spec(Delta_phi). Lean status none: Mathlib lacks Tomita-Takesaki theory, modular automorphism groups, the KMS condition, and the Connes cocycle.

### operator-algebras.nuclear-c-star-algebras-cpap

- **title**: Nuclear C*-Algebras and the Completely Positive Approximation Property
- **unit**: `39.05.04`
- **prerequisites**: `39.05.03` (tensor products of C*-algebras: the minimal and maximal norms), `39.05.02` (operator systems, Arveson's extension theorem, and the Choi-Effros theorem)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `39.05.04`. A C*-algebra A is nuclear iff for every B the minimal and maximal C*-norms on A (x) B coincide; the Choi-Effros / Kirchberg theorem identifies this with the completely positive approximation property (CPAP): a net of CPC maps phi_lambda: A -> M_{n_lambda}(C), psi_lambda: M_{n_lambda}(C) -> A with psi_lambda phi_lambda -> id_A point-norm. Finite-dimensional and AF algebras are nuclear; the factorisation-through-matrices proof that CPAP implies a unique C*-norm. Lean status none: Mathlib lacks tensor-product C*-norms, completely positive maps, and the nuclearity/CPAP equivalence.

### operator-algebras.fredholm-modules-index-pairing

- **title**: Fredholm Modules and the K-Theory/K-Homology Index Pairing
- **unit**: `39.06.03`
- **prerequisites**: `39.06.01` (spectral triples and the reconstruction theorem), `39.02.02` (operator K-theory: K_0 and K_1 of C*-algebras)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `39.06.03`. A Fredholm module (H, F, pi) over A: a representation pi with F=F*, F^2=1, and [F, pi(a)] compact for all a, the bounded model of K-homology K^*(A). The index pairing K_*(A) x K^*(A) -> Z, with the odd pairing of a unitary u with a Fredholm module given by the Fredholm index of PuP (P=(1+F)/2) and the even pairing via the graded commutator. Summability, the Chern character into cyclic cohomology, and the Atiyah-Singer index theorem as the commutative model. Lean status none: Mathlib lacks K-homology, Fredholm modules, and the index pairing.

### operator-algebras.cyclic-cohomology-pairing-k-theory

- **title**: Cyclic Cohomology and the Pairing with K-Theory
- **unit**: `39.07.01`
- **prerequisites**: `39.02.02` (operator K-theory: K_0 and K_1), `39.06.01` (spectral triples and the reconstruction theorem)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `39.07.01`. Connes' cyclic cohomology HC^* and periodic cyclic cohomology HP^* of an algebra, the cyclic bicomplex / (b, B) bicomplex, and the SBI exact sequence relating Hochschild, cyclic, and de Rham theories. The pairing HP^*(A) x K_*(A) -> C extending the trace pairing, computed on a dense smooth subalgebra; the Chern-Connes character ch: K_*(A) -> HP_*(A). Cyclic cohomology of the smooth noncommutative torus; obstruction examples (Cuntz algebras with no trace). Lean status none: Mathlib lacks cyclic/periodic cyclic cohomology, the (b,B) bicomplex, and the Chern-Connes character.

### operator-algebras.exact-c-star-algebras-nuclear-embeddability

- **title**: Exact C*-Algebras and Nuclear Embeddability
- **unit**: `39.05.05`
- **prerequisites**: `39.05.04` (nuclear C*-algebras and the completely positive approximation property), `39.05.03` (tensor products of C*-algebras: the minimal and maximal norms)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `39.05.05`. A C*-algebra A is exact iff the functor - (x)_min A preserves short exact sequences 0 -> J -> B -> B/J -> 0. Kirchberg's theorem: exactness equals nuclear embeddability (A is a C*-subalgebra of a nuclear algebra); for separable A, A embeds in the Cuntz algebra O_2 (Kirchberg-Phillips). The hierarchy nuclear => exact is strict: exactness is subalgebra-stable, so C*_r(F_n) is exact (Haagerup-de Canniere) but not nuclear; Gromov monster groups give non-exact reduced group algebras. Local reflexivity (Effros-Haagerup, automatic) and property C. Lean status none: Mathlib lacks the minimal tensor functor, the exactness condition, nuclear embeddability, and local reflexivity.

### operator-algebras.connes-type-iii-classification

- **title**: The Connes Classification of Type III Factors
- **unit**: `39.04.04`
- **prerequisites**: `39.04.03` (the modular automorphism group and the KMS condition), `39.04.02` (Tomita's theorem: the modular operator and modular conjugation)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `39.04.04`. Connes' invariants of a type III factor M: the Connes spectrum S(M)=cap_phi Spec(Delta_phi) (intersection over faithful normal states of the modular operator spectra) and the T-invariant T(M)={t : sigma_t^phi in Inn(M)}=ker delta. S(M) minus {0} is a closed multiplicative subgroup of R^+, forcing the subdivision of type III into III_0 (S(M)={0,1}), III_lambda for 0<lambda<1 (S(M)={0} union lambda^Z), and III_1 (S(M)=[0,infty)). The discrete decomposition M = N rtimes_theta Z of a III_lambda factor by a trace-scaling automorphism of a type II_infinity factor N (tau o theta = lambda tau), and the continuous decomposition M = N rtimes_theta R with tau o theta_s = e^{-s} tau (Takesaki duality). The smooth flow of weights on Z(N) as the complete invariant of III_0. The Powers / Araki-Woods ITPFI factors R_lambda realising each III_lambda as an infinite tensor product with eigenvalue ratio lambda. Lean status none: Mathlib lacks von Neumann algebras, modular automorphism groups, the Connes spectrum and T-invariant, the crossed-product decompositions, and the flow of weights.

### operator-algebras.quasidiagonality

- **title**: Quasidiagonality
- **unit**: `39.05.08`
- **prerequisites**: `39.05.04` (nuclear C*-algebras and the completely positive approximation property), `39.05.06` (amenable groups: invariant means, the Følner condition, paradoxical decompositions)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `39.05.08`. A set S of operators is quasidiagonal if an increasing net of finite-rank projections P_n -> 1 strongly has |[P_n, s]| -> 0 for all s; a C*-algebra is quasidiagonal if it has a faithful QD representation. Voiculescu's homotopy-invariance theorem and the abstract characterisation: A is QD iff there is a sequence of ucp maps A -> M_{k(n)}(C) that is asymptotically multiplicative and asymptotically isometric. A unital algebra with a proper isometry (Toeplitz, O_n) is not QD; AF and RFD algebras are QD; QD passes to subalgebras. Rosenberg: C*_r(G) QD implies G amenable. Tikuisis-White-Winter (2017): faithful-trace separable nuclear UCT algebras are QD, a pillar of the Elliott classification programme. Lean status none: Mathlib lacks finite-rank projections with the strong topology, QD sets, and the ucp characterisation.

### operator-algebras.group-c-star-algebras-amenability-nuclearity

- **title**: Group C*-Algebras: Amenability and Nuclearity
- **unit**: `39.05.07`
- **prerequisites**: `operator-algebras.amenable-groups-folner-invariant-means`, `operator-algebras.nuclear-c-star-algebras-cpap`
- **tier_anchors**:
  - master: Hulanicki 1966 *Studia Math.* 27; Fell 1962 *Canad. J. Math.* 14; Powers 1975 *Duke Math. J.* 42; Haagerup 1979 *Invent. Math.* 50; Brown-Ozawa *C*-Algebras and Finite-Dimensional Approximations* (AMS, 2008) §2.5-2.6, Ch. 5; Dixmier *C*-Algebras* §13
  - intermediate: Brown-Ozawa §2.5-2.6; Pedersen *C*-Algebras and Their Automorphism Groups* §7.1-7.3; Davidson *C*-Algebras by Example* Ch. VII
  - beginner: two ways to build an algebra from a group's symmetries — the freest possible one (universal for all unitary representations) and the one acting on the group's own functions — and the fact that they coincide exactly when the group is amenable
- **notes**: The full group C*-algebra $C^*(G)$ is the completion of the group ring $\mathbb{C}[G]$ in the universal norm $\|x\|_{\mathrm u} = \sup_\pi \|\pi(x)\|$ over all unitary representations; it is universal for unitary representations of $G$. The reduced group C*-algebra $C^*_r(G)$ is generated by the left-regular representation $\lambda(s)\delta_t = \delta_{st}$ on $\ell^2(G)$, with reduced norm $\|x\|_r = \|\lambda(x)\|$. Since $\lambda$ contributes to the universal norm, $\|x\|_r \le \|x\|_{\mathrm u}$, giving the canonical surjection $\lambda_* : C^*(G) \twoheadrightarrow C^*_r(G)$. Hulanicki's theorem: $G$ amenable $\iff$ the trivial representation $1_G$ is weakly contained in $\lambda$ $\iff$ $\lambda_*$ is an isomorphism $\iff$ $C^*_r(G)$ is nuclear $\iff$ $C^*(G)$ is nuclear $\iff$ $L(G)$ injective $\iff$ Kesten's $\|\lambda(\mu)\| = 1$. The amenable-implies-nuclear direction runs through a Følner sequence, whose normalised indicators $\xi_n = |F_n|^{-1/2}\mathbf{1}_{F_n}$ are almost-invariant vectors for $\lambda$ and supply the completely positive approximation property (compressions onto $\ell^2(F_n)$). Fell's absorption principle $\lambda \otimes \pi \cong \lambda \otimes 1$ via $W(\delta_t \otimes \xi) = \delta_t \otimes \pi(t)\xi$ converts $1_G \prec \lambda$ into the collapse $\|\cdot\|_{\mathrm u} = \|\cdot\|_r$. Examples: abelian $G$ gives $C^*(G) = C^*_r(G) = C(\widehat{G})$ by Gelfand-Pontryagin; finite/compact and virtually abelian groups are nuclear; free groups $F_n$ have $C^*_r(F_n)$ NON-nuclear (non-amenable) but exact (amenable action on the Gromov boundary), simple with unique trace (Powers 1975), with the completely bounded approximation property via the Haagerup inequality. The Haagerup property / a-T-menability previews a weak amenability-like flexibility opposite Kazhdan (T). Lean status `none`: Mathlib lacks the universal/regular completions, the canonical surjection, Hulanicki's theorem, Fell absorption, and the non-nuclearity-yet-exactness of $C^*_r(F_n)$.


### operator-algebras.chern-character-k-homology

- **title**: The Chern Character in K-Homology
- **unit**: `39.07.02`
- **prerequisites**: `39.07.01` (cyclic cohomology and the pairing with K-theory), `39.06.03` (Fredholm modules and the K-theory/K-homology index pairing)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `39.07.02`. The Chern character ch*: K^*(A) -> HP^*(A) sending a Fredholm module to a periodic cyclic cocycle. For an n-summable module (H, F, pi) the Connes character cocycle tau_n(a_0,...,a_n) = lambda_n Tr_s(F[F,a_0][F,a_1]...[F,a_n]), its cyclicity and (b,B)-closedness, and the S-periodicity S[tau_n] = [tau_{n+2}]; the even case uses the grading and the supertrace. Compatibility with the index pairing: <ch*[F], ch_*[e]> = index(eF^+e), the cohomological form of the index theorem. The JLO cocycle for theta-summable modules in entire cyclic cohomology, and the commutative Chern-Weil model giving the A-hat genus. Lean status none: Mathlib lacks Fredholm modules, cyclic/periodic cyclic cohomology, and the Chern character into HP.

### operator-algebras.group-approximation-connes-embedding

- **title**: Group Approximation Properties and the Connes Embedding Problem
- **unit**: `39.05.09`
- **prerequisites**: `39.05.06` (amenable groups: invariant means, the Følner condition, paradoxical decompositions), `39.05.04` (nuclear C*-algebras and the completely positive approximation property)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `39.05.09`. The hierarchy of approximation properties above amenability and the Connes embedding problem as its capstone. The Haagerup property (a-T-menability): a proper conditionally-negative-definite function, equivalently a c_0 sequence of normalised positive-definite functions; free groups have it (word length is CND via the tree cocycle), infinite property (T) groups do not. Weak amenability and the Cowling-Haagerup constant Lambda_cb(G); the CBAP for C*_r(G) and L(G). Sofic and hyperlinear groups (approximation by Sym(n) in the Hamming metric / U(n) in the Hilbert-Schmidt metric). The Connes Embedding Problem: does every separable II_1 factor embed into R^omega? Kirchberg's QWEP / unique-C*-norm-on-C*(F)⊗C*(F) reformulation and Tsirelson's problem; the 2020 MIP*=RE theorem (Ji-Natarajan-Vidick-Wright-Yuen) refuting it. Lean status none: Mathlib lacks CND/positive-definite functions, cb multipliers, the Haagerup property, R^omega, QWEP, and soficity.

### operator-algebras.connes-moscovici-local-index-formula

- **title**: The Connes-Moscovici Local Index Formula
- **unit**: `39.06.06`
- **prerequisites**: `39.06.03` (Fredholm modules and the K-theory/K-homology index pairing), `39.06.05` (the Dixmier trace and the noncommutative integral), `39.07.01` (cyclic cohomology and the pairing with K-theory)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `39.06.06`. The Connes-Moscovici local index formula (1995) computing the index pairing <[D],[u]> of a finitely-summable spectral triple as a finite sum of residues of zeta functions zeta_b(z) = Tr(b |D|^{-z}). The dimension spectrum Sd as the poles of these zeta functions, the pseudodifferential calculus and noncommutative (Wodzicki) residue on a regular spectral triple extending the Dixmier trace, and the residue (b,B)-cocycle (phi_n) representing the Chern character ch*[D] in periodic cyclic cohomology. The odd / spectral-flow and semifinite / resolvent-cocycle refinements, the JLO cocycle as the theta-summable bridge, and the commutative reduction to the heat-kernel proof of Atiyah-Singer. Lean status none: Mathlib lacks the pseudodifferential calculus, the zeta functions and their meromorphic continuation, the dimension spectrum, the noncommutative residue, and the (b,B)-bicomplex.


### operator-algebras.exact-groups-amenable-actions-property-a

- **title**: Exact Groups, Amenable Actions, and Property A
- **unit**: `39.05.10`
- **prerequisites**: `39.05.05` (exact C*-algebras and nuclear embeddability), `39.05.07` (group C*-algebras: amenability and nuclearity)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `39.05.10`. The three equivalent faces of exactness for a discrete group (Ozawa): a group Gamma is exact (C*_r(Gamma) is an exact C*-algebra) iff it acts topologically amenably on some compact Hausdorff space (a net m_n: X -> Prob(Gamma) with sup_x ||g.m_n^x - m_n^{gx}||_1 -> 0) iff it has Yu's property A (a Folner-type slow-variation condition for the word metric: finitely-supported xi_x in l^1(Gamma)_{1,+} of uniform support diameter with ||xi_x - xi_y||_1 small for d(x,y) <= R). The reduced crossed product C(X) rtimes_r Gamma is nuclear when the action is amenable, embedding C*_r(Gamma) into a nuclear algebra (nuclear embeddability, cf 39.05.05). The uniform Roe algebra and coarse geometry; property A as a coarse invariant; the boundary action of a hyperbolic group on its Gromov boundary is amenable (Adams), so hyperbolic and linear groups (Guentner-Higson-Weinberger) are exact; Gromov monster groups are non-exact. Property A implies coarse Baum-Connes / Novikov (Yu, Higson-Roe). Lean status none: Mathlib lacks reduced crossed products, topologically amenable actions, the uniform Roe algebra, and the coarse-geometry category.


### combinatorics.basic-counting-twelvefold-way

- **title**: Basic Counting and the Twelvefold Way
- **unit**: `40.01.01`
- **prerequisites**: none (root unit; assumes basic set/function theory)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `40.01.01`. The sum and product rules, bijective counting, and Rota's twelvefold way: functions f: N -> X (|N|=n, |X|=k) counted under (arbitrary / injective / surjective) crossed with (domain and codomain each distinguishable or not), giving the 3x4 table. Cells: ordinary power k^n, falling factorial (k)_n, binomial coefficient C(k,n), multiset coefficient C(k+n-1,n), Stirling numbers of the second kind S(n,k) and surjections k!S(n,k), Stirling numbers of the first kind c(n,k) and permutations by cycle type, integer partitions p_{<=k}(n) and p_{=k}(n). Pascal and Stirling recurrences, Vandermonde, the binomial theorem as a counting statement, Stirling-matrix orthogonality. Elementary/bijective side of integer partitions (analytic theory at 21.16.01). Lean status none: Mathlib has scattered ingredients (Nat.choose, descFactorial, partitions, multisets) but not the twelvefold way as one structure.


### combinatorics.hadamard-matrices-paley-construction

- **title**: Hadamard Matrices and the Paley Construction
- **unit**: `40.06.05`
- **prerequisites**: none (root unit; applies finite fields / quadratic residues from `21.02.01`, `21.01.06` by cross-reference)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `40.06.05`. A Hadamard matrix is an $n \times n$ matrix $H \in \{\pm 1\}^{n\times n}$ with $HH^\top = nI$; equivalently distinct rows are orthogonal and agree in exactly $n/2$ coordinates. Hadamard's 1893 determinant inequality $|\det A| \le n^{n/2}$ for $\pm 1$ matrices, with equality iff $A$ is Hadamard; the order constraint $n \in \{1,2\}$ or $4 \mid n$ via the three-row counting identity. Equivalence under row/column permutation and negation, and normalisation (first row and column all $+1$). The Sylvester/Kronecker doubling $H \mapsto \big(\begin{smallmatrix} H & H \\ H & -H\end{smallmatrix}\big)$ giving orders $2^k$ (Walsh-Hadamard transform, first-order Reed-Muller code). The Paley construction from the quadratic-residue character $\chi$ over $\mathbb{F}_q$: the Jacobsthal matrix $Q = (\chi(a-b))$ with $QQ^\top = qI - J$, $QJ = 0$, $Q^\top = \chi(-1)Q$; type I ($q \equiv 3 \bmod 4$, $Q$ skew) gives order $q+1$, type II ($q \equiv 1 \bmod 4$, $Q$ symmetric, via the Paley conference matrix) gives order $2(q+1)$. The Hadamard conjecture (existence for every multiple of $4$, open). The bijection between Hadamard matrices of order $4m$ and symmetric $2\text{-}(4m-1, 2m-1, m-1)$ designs and the Hadamard $3$-design; applications to the Hadamard code (Plotkin bound, Mariner 9) and the design-coding-graph triangle. Lean status none: Mathlib lacks the Hadamard-matrix definition, the determinant equality case, the order-divisibility theorem, the Jacobsthal/conference-matrix identities, and the symmetric-design equivalence.


### combinatorics.probabilistic-method-first-moment

- **title**: The Probabilistic Method: First-Moment and Counting Arguments
- **unit**: `40.07.01`
- **prerequisites**: none (root unit; applies basic probability/expectation from 37.01.x)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `40.07.01`. The foundational form of the probabilistic method (Alon-Spencer Ch. 1): the basic existence principle (positive probability of a property forces an object with that property; if E[X] < t some point has X < t, and an integer count with mean below one vanishes somewhere) and the first-moment / union-bound method (sum_i Pr(A_i) < 1 yields a bad-event-free outcome, no independence needed). Flagship application: Erdos's Ramsey lower bound R(k,k) > 2^{k/2} from binom(n,k) 2^{1 - binom(k,2)} < 1 (the lower companion to the upper bound and Ramsey's theorem in 40.05.04). Further applications: tournaments with the Schutte property S_k, dominating sets with E|D| <= n(1+ln(delta+1))/(delta+1), max-cut/bipartite subgraph with >= m/2 edges, and hypergraph 2-colourability (property B, m(n) >= 2^{n-1}). Refinements (deletion, second moment, Lovasz Local Lemma) continue in 40.07.02. Lean status none: Mathlib has discrete-probability and expectation infrastructure but no packaged probabilistic-method existence principle or its Ramsey/property-B corollaries.

### combinatorics.polya-redfield-enumeration-cycle-index

- **title**: Pólya-Redfield Enumeration and the Cycle Index
- **unit**: `40.06.10`
- **prerequisites**: none (root unit; cross-references the Burnside/Cauchy-Frobenius orbit-counting lemma and group actions at `01.02.03`, and generating functions at the co-produced `40.01.03`)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `40.06.10`. Counting under a group action (van Lint-Wilson Ch. 35). The Burnside / Cauchy-Frobenius orbit-counting lemma (number of orbits = average number of fixed points, stated, group-theory home at 01.02.03). The cycle index $Z(G; a_1,\ldots,a_n) = |G|^{-1}\sum_g \prod_k a_k^{j_k(g)}$ of a permutation group recording cycle types; the cyclic $Z(C_n)=\tfrac1n\sum_{d\mid n}\phi(d)a_d^{n/d}$, dihedral, and symmetric $Z(S_n)=\sum_{\lambda\vdash n}\prod a_k^{m_k}/(k^{m_k}m_k!)$ indices. Pólya's enumeration theorem: the pattern inventory is the cycle index with $a_k\mapsto\phi(t^k)$ for the figure-counting series $\phi$; proof via the weighted Burnside lemma and the fact that $g$-fixed colourings are constant on $g$'s cycles. Worked classics: necklaces/bracelets ($C_n$, $D_n$), cube face/vertex/edge colourings, counting graphs up to isomorphism via the pair group $S_p^{(2)}$ on edges, chemical isomer counting (Pólya's 1937 motivation). The power-group enumeration theorem (de Bruijn) for symmetry on both domain and range. Redfield 1927 / Pólya 1937 priority; Harary-Palmer graphical enumeration. Cross-ref 01.02.03 (orbit-counting lemma) and 40.01.03 (generating functions). Lean status none: Mathlib has MulAction and the orbit-counting lemma but lacks the cycle-index polynomial, the substitution theorem, the standard group cycle indices, and the power group.


### combinatorics.graphs-basic-invariants-foundational-lemmas

- **title**: Graphs, Basic Invariants, and the Foundational Lemmas
- **unit**: `40.04.01`
- **prerequisites**: (root unit — no in-spine prerequisites)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `40.04.01`. The L0 root of the graph-theory-core chapter (Diestel Ch. 1). Graphs as (V, E) with E a set of unordered pairs; adjacency, incidence, neighbourhood, degree, minimum/maximum/average degree; subgraphs, induced subgraphs, spanning subgraphs; walks, paths, cycles, connectivity, components, distance, diameter, girth. The handshake lemma (sum of degrees = 2|E|) and the even-count of odd-degree vertices. Trees and forests with the four-way characterisation (connected + acyclic iff connected with n-1 edges iff acyclic with n-1 edges iff unique paths); spanning trees; at least two leaves. Bipartite graphs and the no-odd-cycle characterisation. The minimum-degree averaging lemma (average degree d forces a subgraph of minimum degree > d/2) and high-girth-forces-many-vertices (Moore bound). Euler tours (connected iff every degree even). Contraction, minors, topological minors as the ordering for later structure theory; Cayley's n^(n-2) via Prüfer; Erdős-Gallai degree sequences; Kuratowski-Wagner planarity. Structural side owned here; the algorithmic/data-structures view lives at 25.02.01. Lean status none: Mathlib has a SimpleGraph API and the handshake lemma but not the unified tree-characterisation iff-cycle, the averaging lemma, the Euler degree-parity existence theorem, or the minor-order packaging.


### combinatorics.linear-codes-bounds

- **title**: Linear Codes and the Hamming, Singleton, and Gilbert-Varshamov Bounds
- **unit**: `40.06.06`
- **prerequisites**: none (root unit; applies finite fields `21.02.01` and linear algebra `01.01.04`/`01.01.05` in prose without reproving them)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `40.06.06`. A linear code as a k-dimensional subspace C of F_q^n with parameters [n,k,d]; Hamming distance/weight, minimum distance, the error-correcting radius floor((d-1)/2); generator and parity-check matrices, the dual code, and the criterion d = least number of linearly dependent columns of H; syndrome decoding and the standard array. The classical bounds: Singleton (d <= n-k+1) and MDS codes, the Hamming sphere-packing bound and perfect codes (forward to 40.06.07), the Gilbert-Varshamov existence bound, and the Plotkin bound; the weight enumerator and the MacWilliams identity relating W_C and W_{C^perp} via Krawtchouk polynomials. Shannon's channel-coding theorem is a pointer only. Lean status none: Mathlib has Hamming distance but lacks the linear-code structure, the bounds, MDS theory, and the character-sum proof of MacWilliams.


### combinatorics.linearity-expectation-deletion-method

- **title**: Linearity of Expectation and the Deletion Method
- **unit**: `40.07.02`
- **notes**: stub — see unit `40.07.02`. Two elementary pillars of the probabilistic method (Alon-Spencer Ch. 2-3). Linearity of expectation E[sum X_i] = sum E[X_i] with no independence, and the averaging existence principle (some sample meets or beats the mean): Szele's theorem (a tournament with >= n!/2^(n-1) Hamiltonian paths), the max-cut bound (every graph has a cut of size >= |E|/2), the splitting/unbalancing-lights argument. The deletion (alteration) method: build a slightly-too-good random structure then delete the few offending parts — the improved Ramsey lower bound R(k,k) > (1/e)(1+o(1)) k 2^(k/2), independent sets of size >= n^2/(4|E|), and Erdős's graphs of high girth and high chromatic number (delete one vertex per short cycle), with a dependent-random-choice preview. Lean status none: Mathlib has the probability and SimpleGraph layers but not the first-moment existence corollary, the indicator-sum defect counts, or the alteration argument.


### combinatorics.bibd-fisher-inequality

- **title**: Balanced Incomplete Block Designs and Fisher's Inequality
- **unit**: `40.06.01`
- **prerequisites**: none (root unit; applies linear algebra `01.01.05` rank/positive-definiteness and finite fields `21.02.01` in prose without reproving them)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `40.06.01`. A 2-(v,k,lambda) balanced incomplete block design: v points, blocks of size k, every pair of points in exactly lambda blocks; the derived parameters b (blocks) and r (replication) and the identities bk = vr and lambda(v-1) = r(k-1) by double-counting flags. The incidence matrix N (v x b) and the fundamental identity NN^T = (r-lambda)I + lambda J; the eigenvalues rk and (r-lambda)^(v-1), the determinant (r-lambda)^(v-1) rk > 0, and FISHER'S INEQUALITY b >= v as the rank consequence of nonsingular NN^T. Symmetric designs (b = v, r = k) with N^T N = (k-lambda)I + lambda J and any two blocks meeting in lambda points; the Fano plane as 2-(7,3,1) and biplanes (lambda = 2); complement, derived, and residual designs; t-designs and the lambda_s cascade; resolvable designs, the Bose inequality b >= v + r - 1, and Kirkman's schoolgirl problem; Bruck-Ryser-Chowla preview (forward to 40.06.02). Lean status none: Mathlib lacks the design structure, the parameter identities, the matrix identity, the determinant evaluation, and Fisher's inequality.


### combinatorics.planar-graphs-euler-kuratowski-wagner

- **title**: Planar Graphs: Euler's Formula, Kuratowski's and Wagner's Theorems
- **unit**: `40.04.05`
- **prerequisites**: `40.04.01` (graphs, invariants, the handshake lemma, tree characterisation, bipartiteness, and the minor / topological-minor orders)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `40.04.05`. Planarity from Diestel Ch. 4. Plane graphs as crossing-free drawings in the plane / sphere, faces (including the outer face), and the Jordan curve theorem as the single topological black box. Euler's formula v − e + f = 2 for connected plane graphs, proved by induction on edges; corollaries e ≤ 3n − 6, the triangle-free bound e ≤ 2n − 4, the non-planarity of K_5 and K_{3,3}, and a vertex of degree ≤ 5 (hence 6-degeneracy and 6-colourability). Kuratowski's theorem (planar iff no K_5 or K_{3,3} subdivision / topological minor) and the equivalent Wagner's theorem (no K_5 or K_{3,3} minor), with a proof sketch via the 3-connected reduction and Tutte's contraction ladder; the Wagner clique-sum decomposition of K_5-minor-free graphs. Planar duality, the cycle-bond correspondence, abstract duals, and Whitney's algebraic planarity criterion. The structure of 3-connected planar graphs: Whitney's unique-embedding theorem and Steinitz's theorem identifying them with convex-polytope skeletons. Maximal planar graphs / triangulations with exactly 3n − 6 edges. Colouring consequences forward to 40.04.08; graph-minor structure forward to 40.04.10; connectivity machinery from 40.04.04. Lean status none: Mathlib lacks a plane-graph / rotation-system embedding model, Euler's formula, the edge bounds, Kuratowski-Wagner, planar duality, and the unique-embedding / Steinitz package.


### combinatorics.vertex-colouring-brooks-chromatic-polynomial

- **title**: Vertex Colouring: Brooks' Theorem and the Chromatic Polynomial
- **unit**: `40.04.06`
- **prerequisites**: `40.04.01` (graphs, basic invariants, foundational lemmas)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `40.04.06`. Proper k-colourings as partitions into independent sets; the chromatic number chi(G); the clique number omega and independence number alpha with omega <= chi and chi >= n/alpha. Greedy colouring and the bounds chi <= Delta + 1 and chi <= col(G) = degeneracy + 1 via degeneracy orderings; perfect-elimination orderings and chordal graphs (chi = omega). Brooks' theorem (chi <= Delta unless G is complete or an odd cycle) with the block-decomposition / 2-connected DFS-ordering proof. Mycielski's construction (triangle-free graphs of unbounded chi; C_5 -> Grotzsch). The chromatic polynomial P(G,k): deletion-contraction P(G,k) = P(G-e,k) - P(G/e,k), monic integer polynomial of degree n, sign-alternating coefficients; P for trees k(k-1)^(n-1), cycles (k-1)^n + (-1)^n (k-1), complete graphs k^underline(n). Whitney's broken-circuit theorem and the inclusion-exclusion / Mobius expansion (cross-ref 40.02.02); the Tutte polynomial specialisation P(G,k) = (-1)^(n-1) k T(G; 1-k, 0). Perfect graphs and the strong perfect graph theorem (odd holes/antiholes) stated. List/edge colouring preview forward to 40.04.07; map colouring 40.04.08; Turan extremal sibling 40.05.01. Lean status none: Mathlib has Coloring/chromaticNumber and chi <= Delta+1 but not Brooks, degeneracy/colouring-number, the chromatic polynomial with deletion-contraction, the broken-circuit theorem, Mycielski, or the strong perfect graph theorem.


### combinatorics.generating-functions-ordinary-exponential

- **title**: Generating Functions: Ordinary, Exponential, and the Exponential Formula
- **unit**: `40.01.03`
- **prerequisites**: `40.01.01` (basic counting / twelvefold way)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `40.01.03`. The generating-function method (Stanley EC1 Ch. 1, EC2 Ch. 5; Wilf; Flajolet-Sedgewick Ch. I-II). The ring of formal power series K[[x]] with Cauchy product, the unit criterion (invertible iff non-zero constant term), formal convergence as coefficientwise stabilisation. Ordinary generating functions (OGF): the product rule = convolution (ordered pairs / concatenation), the sequence rule 1/(1-C) for c_0 = 0. Solving linear recurrences (Fibonacci x/(1-x-x^2), Catalan from C = 1 + xC^2) by OGF; partial fractions and rational generating functions for linear-recurrence asymptotics a_n ~ c n^{m-1} rho^n. Exponential generating functions (EGF) for labelled structures; the EGF product rule = labelled product / binomial convolution h_n = sum C(n,k) f_k g_{n-k}. The exponential formula EGF(all) = exp(EGF(connected)) for sets of connected components: set partitions (Bell numbers, e^{e^x-1}), permutations by cycles (1/(1-x) from log(1/(1-x))), derangements e^{-x}/(1-x), labelled trees. The compositional formula G(F(x)) and Lagrange inversion (T = x e^T giving Cayley n^{n-1}) as preview of 40.01.07. The OGF/EGF symbolic dictionary (SEQ/SET/CYC to 1/(1-C), exp(C), log(1/(1-C))) is the engine for the rest of the chapter and the analytic combinatorics of 40.08.01; co-produced 40.01.04 (rational GF / transfer matrix). Lean status none: Mathlib has PowerSeries with unit criterion, exp/log on Q-algebras, but not the enumerative bridge (EGF product rule as labelled product, the exponential formula, Lagrange inversion coefficient extraction).


### combinatorics.posets-lattices-birkhoff

- **title**: Posets, Lattices, and Birkhoff's Representation Theorem
- **unit**: `40.02.01`
- **prerequisites**: `40.01.01` (basic counting and the twelvefold way)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `40.02.01`. Finite partially ordered sets and lattices (Stanley EC1 Ch. 3): the cover relation and Hasse diagram, chains and antichains, height and width; Dilworth's theorem (min chain cover = max antichain) and Mirsky's dual (min antichain cover = height), with the König/Hall matching proof cross-referenced to 40.04.02; linear extensions e(P) and the order polynomial; meet/join, bounded and complete lattices; distributive and modular lattices with the M_3/N_5 forbidden-sublattice characterizations; down-sets and the distributive lattice J(P); BIRKHOFF'S REPRESENTATION THEOREM (every finite distributive lattice L is J(P) for the unique poset P = Irr(L) of join-irreducibles, with J(-) and Irr(-) mutually inverse) via the join-prime lemma; complementation and Boolean lattices B_n; geometric/semimodular lattices and the matroid-flat correspondence (preview); products of chains and the structure of B_n. The Mobius-inversion calculus on J(P) is co-produced in 40.02.02. Lean status none: Mathlib has the order-theoretic core (Lattice, DistribLattice, BooleanAlgebra, LowerSet) but not the finite-poset combinatorics - no Birkhoff functorial duality L ≃o LowerSet(JoinIrreducible L), no Dilworth/Mirsky min-max, no M_3/N_5 theorems, no geometric-lattice/matroid correspondence.


### combinatorics.matchings-konig-hall

- **title**: Matchings I: König's Theorem and Hall's Marriage Theorem
- **unit**: `40.04.02`
- **prerequisites**: `40.04.01` (graphs, basic invariants, bipartite vocabulary)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `40.04.02`. Matching theory in bipartite graphs (Diestel §2.1). A matching as a set of pairwise-disjoint edges; saturated/exposed vertices; maximal vs maximum matchings; the matching number nu(G); perfect matchings. M-alternating and M-augmenting paths, and Berge's lemma (a matching is maximum iff there is no M-augmenting path) via the symmetric-difference argument. Hall's marriage theorem (a bipartite graph has a matching saturating A iff |N(S)| >= |S| for all S subset A) with the alternating-tree proof, plus the defect/deficiency form (max saturation = |A| - max_S(|S|-|N(S)|)). König's theorem (max matching = min vertex cover in bipartite graphs), proved from Hall, with the König-Egerváry / Gallai independent-set dual and the total-unimodularity / LP-duality proof. Systems of distinct representatives and transversals; the Hungarian-method viewpoint on weighted assignment. Applications: k-regular bipartite graphs have perfect matchings, Latin-rectangle row extension, and the Birkhoff-von Neumann theorem (doubly stochastic matrices are convex combinations of permutation matrices). Cross-refs to Dilworth `40.02.01`, the non-bipartite Tutte sequel `40.04.03`, and network flows `40.04.09`. Lean status none: Mathlib has SimpleGraph.Matching/IsPerfectMatching and a Finset SDR form of Hall but lacks Berge's augmenting-path criterion, König duality, the defect form, and Birkhoff-von Neumann as packaged theorems.


### combinatorics.inclusion-exclusion-sieve

- **title**: Inclusion-Exclusion and the Sieve
- **unit**: `40.01.02`
- **prerequisites**: `40.01.01` (basic counting / twelvefold way)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `40.01.02`. The principle of inclusion-exclusion (Stanley EC1 §2.1): the union form |A_1 ∪ … ∪ A_n| = Σ|A_i| − Σ|A_i∩A_j| + …, the complementary form counting elements in none of the sets, and the Boolean-lattice Möbius viewpoint (μ(S,T) = (-1)^|T\S|; forward-pointer to poset Möbius 40.02.02). Applications: surjections / Stirling S(n,k) by sieving the codomain, derangements D_n = n!Σ(-1)^k/k! and the 1/e limit, the Euler totient via the prime-divisor sieve, the ménage problem, the principle of restricted positions / rook polynomials and the permanent connection, coprime / integer-solution counts, the Bonferroni partial-sum inequalities, the Sylvester-Whitney 'exactly m' refinement, and the involution principle making the cancellation bijective. Owns the set-theoretic sieve; cross-refs arithmetic Möbius 21.11.01 (divisor lattice) and the analytic sieves 21.14.x (number-theoretic error control). Lean status none: Mathlib has scattered card_biUnion inclusion-exclusion, Nat.totient, and a derangements file, but not the unified Boolean-lattice Möbius-inversion sieve, the Sylvester-Whitney/Bonferroni layer, or the rook-polynomial / permanent formulation of restricted positions.


### combinatorics.connectivity-menger

- **title**: Connectivity and Menger's Theorem
- **unit**: `40.04.04`
- **prerequisites**: `40.04.01` (graphs, basic invariants, foundational lemmas)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `40.04.04`. Graph connectivity (Diestel Ch. 3). Vertex/edge cuts and separators; the connectivity number κ(G) and edge-connectivity λ(G); k-connected and k-edge-connected graphs; Whitney's inequality κ(G) ≤ λ(G) ≤ δ(G). Menger's theorem in all forms: the local vertex form (max internally disjoint u-v paths = min u-v separator) with the induction-on-edges proof, the edge form via vertex-splitting/line-graph reduction, the directed/arc form, and the global form (k-connected iff every pair joined by k independent paths). The fan lemma. Two-connected graphs and the open-ear decomposition; three-connected graphs and Tutte's wheel theorem (reduction to a wheel by 3-connectivity-preserving contractions/deletions) as the structural pinnacle. The block / block-cut tree decomposition. Menger as the integral, unit-capacity case of max-flow-min-cut, with κ and λ computed by network-flow algorithms. Cross-refs the co-produced planarity unit 40.04.05 (which uses 3-connectivity and Whitney's unique-embedding theorem) and the co-produced network-flows unit 40.04.09 (max-flow-min-cut). Lean status none: Mathlib has reachability-sense connectivity (SimpleGraph.Connected, Walk, Path, Reachable) but lacks the κ/λ invariants, Whitney's inequality, Menger in any form, the fan lemma, ear decompositions, Tutte's wheel theorem, and the block-cut tree as defined objects.

### combinatorics.perfect-codes-hamming-golay

- **title**: Perfect Codes: the Hamming and Golay Codes
- **unit**: `40.06.07`
- **notes**: stub — see unit `40.06.07`. Perfect codes attain the sphere-packing bound with equality, their error-balls tiling the space. The q-ary Hamming codes [(q^m-1)/(q-1), (q^m-1)/(q-1)-m, 3] built from the parity-check matrix of one representative per one-dimensional subspace (all nonzero columns over F_2), their perfectness, and the equidistant simplex dual. The binary [23,12,7] and ternary [11,6,5] Golay codes (quadratic-residue / icosahedral constructions), their extensions [24,12,8] and [12,6,6], weight enumerators, Mathieu automorphism groups M_23, M_24, M_11, M_12 (the first sporadic simple groups), and the 5-designs / Steiner systems S(5,8,24), S(5,6,12) they hold (Assmus-Mattson). Lloyd's theorem and the van Lint-Tietaevaeinen classification: the Hamming and Golay codes are the only genuine perfect codes over fields. Lean status none.


### combinatorics.matchings-tutte-tutte-berge

- **title**: Matchings II: Tutte's 1-Factor Theorem and the Tutte-Berge Formula
- **unit**: `40.04.03`
- **prerequisites**: `40.04.02` (bipartite matchings, Berge's lemma, König/Hall)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `40.04.03`. Matching theory in general (non-bipartite) graphs (Diestel §2.2). Perfect matchings / 1-factors, k-factors, and the odd-component invariant o(G-S) with the parity lemma o(G-S) = |V|-|S| (mod 2). TUTTE'S 1-FACTOR THEOREM (G has a perfect matching iff o(G-S) <= |S| for all S subset V) with the edge-maximal / disjoint-cliques proof. The TUTTE-BERGE FORMULA (max-matching deficiency = max_S(o(G-S)-|S|), so nu(G) = (|V| - max_S(o(G-S)-|S|))/2) via padding with universal dummy vertices. Barrier / Tutte sets as optimality certificates. Factor-critical (hypomatchable) graphs and their odd ear-decomposition characterization. The GALLAI-EDMONDS structure theorem (the D = inessential vertices, A = N(D)\D, C = rest decomposition; components of G[D] factor-critical, G[C] perfectly matchable, A the canonical barrier with def(G) = c(D)-|A|). PETERSEN'S THEOREM (bridgeless cubic graphs have a perfect matching; the 2-factor theorem for even-regular graphs via Euler-orientation and regular-bipartite matching). EDMONDS' BLOSSOM ALGORITHM (first polynomial-time maximum-matching algorithm; blossom contraction preserves augmenting paths; origin of polynomial time as the definition of efficient computation) and the MATCHING POLYTOPE (odd-set / blossom inequalities sum_{e in delta(U)} x_e >= 1 for odd U). Cross-refs the bipartite specialization `40.04.02` (König/Hall) and co-produced network flows `40.04.09`. Lean status none: Mathlib has SimpleGraph.Matching/IsPerfectMatching and a bipartite Hall form but no o(G-S) invariant, no Tutte/Tutte-Berge, no factor-critical or Gallai-Edmonds theory, no blossom algorithm, and no general matching polytope.


### combinatorics.permutation-statistics-eulerian-polynomials

- **title**: Permutation Statistics: Descents, the Major Index, and Eulerian Polynomials
- **unit**: `40.01.05`
- **prerequisites**: `40.01.03` (ordinary and exponential generating functions)
- **notes**: stub — see unit `40.01.05`. Statistics of the symmetric group (Stanley EC1 §1.3-1.4): the descent set D(σ), descent number des(σ), major index maj(σ)=Σ_{i∈D(σ)} i, inversion number inv(σ) as the Coxeter length of type A_{n-1}, and reduced words. MacMahon's equidistribution theorem (inv and maj are Mahonian: Σq^{maj}=Σq^{inv}=(n)_q!), proved both non-constructively (inversion table + maximal-letter insertion) and constructively by Foata's fundamental bijection φ with maj=inv∘φ. The Eulerian numbers ⟨n,k⟩=A(n,k), the Eulerian polynomial A_n(t)=Σ t^{des+1}, the recurrence A(n,k)=(k+1)A(n-1,k)+(n-k)A(n-1,k-1), symmetry A(n,k)=A(n,n-1-k), the Worpitzky identity x^n=Σ_k A(n,k)C(x+k,n), the identity Σ_m m^n t^m=A_n(t)/(1-t)^{n+1}, the EGF Σ_n A_n(t)x^n/n!=(t-1)/(t-e^{(t-1)x}), the Eulerian-Stirling expansion A_n(t)=Σ_k k!S(n,k)(t-1)^{n-k} connecting to ordered set partitions / surjections, the q-Eulerian (Carlitz) refinement tracking (des,maj) jointly, and γ-positivity. Cross-refs the co-produced q-analogues unit 40.01.06 (the same (n)_q! governs Gaussian binomials and finite-field subspace counts), the twelvefold-way surjection count 40.01.01, the generating-function calculus 40.01.03, and the Coxeter length / reduced-word rep theory at 07.04.01. Lean status none: Mathlib has Equiv.Perm and the sign map but not des/maj/inv as named statistics, MacMahon equidistribution, Foata's bijection, the Eulerian recurrence, Worpitzky, or the Eulerian EGF.

### combinatorics.edge-list-colouring-vizing-choosability

- **title**: Edge Colouring and List Colouring: Vizing's Theorem and Choosability
- **unit**: `40.04.07`
- **prerequisites**: `40.04.06` (vertex colouring, Brooks' theorem, the chromatic polynomial)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `40.04.07`. Proper edge colourings as partitions of the edge set into matchings; the chromatic index chi'(G) with the lower bound chi' >= Delta. Vizing's theorem (Delta <= chi' <= Delta + 1, the class-1/class-2 dichotomy) via the fan-recolouring / Kempe-chain proof. The line graph L(G) and chi'(G) = chi(L(G)), so edge colouring is vertex colouring of L(G) (cross-ref 40.04.06). Koenig's edge-colouring theorem chi' = Delta for bipartite graphs via Delta-regular embedding + Hall perfect matchings (cross-ref 40.04.02). List colouring / choosability: ch(G) and ch'(G), ch >= chi, the K_{k,k} example with ch -> infinity while chi = 2 (t-subset lists). Galvin's theorem (bipartite ch' = chi' = Delta) by kernel-perfect orientation of L(G) + stable matchings (Bondy-Boppana-Siegel). The list-edge-colouring conjecture ch' = chi' (open; Kahn asymptotic). Thomassen's theorem (planar graphs 5-choosable; sharp by Voigt). The Combinatorial Nullstellensatz (Alon) and the Alon-Tarsi graph-polynomial / orientation method as the algebraic engine. Total colouring preview chi'' and the Vizing-Behzad conjecture chi'' <= Delta + 2. Cross-ref map colouring 40.04.08, Turan extremal 40.05.01. Lean status none: Mathlib has Coloring/chromaticNumber and lineGraph but not chi', Vizing, Koenig edge colouring, choosability/ch', Galvin, Thomassen, or the Combinatorial Nullstellensatz.

### combinatorics.rational-gf-transfer-matrix-p-partitions

- **title**: Rational Generating Functions, the Transfer-Matrix Method, and P-Partitions
- **unit**: `40.01.04`
- **prerequisites**: `40.01.03` (ordinary/exponential generating functions, the sequence rule, partial fractions)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `40.01.04`. Rational generating functions (Stanley EC1 Ch. 4): a sequence satisfies a constant-coefficient linear recurrence of order d iff its OGF is P/Q with deg P < deg Q = d and Q(0) != 0; the partial-fraction reading a_n = sum_i p_i(n) gamma_i^n; quasi-polynomials and the denominator/period (roots of unity) correspondence. The transfer-matrix method: counting weighted walks in a finite digraph via powers of the transfer matrix A, the walk generating function as the (i,j) entry of (I - xA)^{-1}, rationality with denominator det(I - xA), and the transfer-matrix determinant theorem expanding det(I - xA) over vertex-disjoint cycle collections; applications to words avoiding factors (Goulden-Jackson cluster method), tilings of a 2xn / 3xn board, and the Lindstrom-Gessel-Viennot lemma for nonintersecting lattice paths as a determinant via the sign-reversing tail-swap involution. P-partitions: order-reversing maps P -> N, the fundamental theorem decomposing the order cone over linear extensions, the (P,omega)-partition generating function as a sum of Gessel fundamental quasi-symmetric functions, and the order polynomial Omega_P(m) with its reciprocity. Cross-refs Perron-Frobenius `38.02.03` (spectral angle on the same transfer matrix), nonintersecting paths `40.03.04`, order polytopes / Ehrhart `40.03.02`, quasi-symmetric functions `40.08.06`, and regular languages / automata `40.08.04`. Lean status none: Mathlib has PowerSeries, RatFunc, Matrix.det/charpoly, LinearRecurrence, and an emerging linear-extension API, but not the rationality theorem, the transfer-matrix (I-xA)^{-1} identity and determinant theorem, the Lindstrom-Gessel-Viennot lemma, or the fundamental theorem of (P,omega)-partitions and order-polynomial reciprocity as packaged results.


### combinatorics.network-flows-max-flow-min-cut-nowhere-zero

- **title**: Network Flows: Max-Flow Min-Cut and Nowhere-Zero Flows
- **unit**: `40.04.09`
- **prerequisites**: `40.04.04` (connectivity and Menger's theorem)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `40.04.09`. Network flows (Diestel Ch. 6). Networks as capacitated digraphs with source and sink; flows, the value of a flow, cuts and cut capacity; the max-flow-min-cut theorem (Ford-Fulkerson) with the augmenting-path / residual-network proof and the integrality theorem. Menger as the unit-capacity integral case (cross-ref 40.04.04) and bipartite matching / Koenig as an integral flow (cross-ref 40.04.02). The Ford-Fulkerson and Edmonds-Karp algorithms (shortest augmenting path, O(VE^2)). Group-valued and H-flows, the flow number, the flow polynomial F(G,k) as a deletion-contraction invariant and a Tutte-polynomial specialisation T(G;0,1-k), dual to the chromatic polynomial (cross-ref 40.04.06). Nowhere-zero k-flows; the flow-colouring duality on plane graphs (k-face-colourable iff nowhere-zero k-flow); Tutte's 5-flow / 4-flow / 3-flow conjectures and Seymour's 6-flow theorem; the four-colour link (a bridgeless cubic plane graph has a nowhere-zero 4-flow iff it is 3-edge-colourable iff its faces are 4-colourable, cross-ref 40.04.08). Lean status none: Mathlib lacks networks/cuts/residual graphs, max-flow-min-cut and integrality, group-valued and nowhere-zero flows, the flow polynomial as a Tutte specialisation, and the planar flow-colouring duality.


### combinatorics.incidence-algebra-mobius-function-inversion

- **title**: The Incidence Algebra, the Möbius Function of a Poset, and Möbius Inversion
- **unit**: `40.02.02`
- **prerequisites**: `40.02.01` (posets, lattices, Birkhoff), `40.01.02` (inclusion-exclusion / the sieve)
- **notes**: stub — see unit `40.02.02`. Rota's Möbius theory (Stanley EC1 Ch. 3). The incidence algebra I(P) of a locally finite poset (functions on intervals under convolution (f*g)(x,y)=Σ_{x≤z≤y}f(x,z)g(z,y)), the delta identity δ, the zeta function ζ, and the Möbius function μ=ζ⁻¹ via the recursion μ(x,x)=1, μ(x,y)=−Σ_{x≤z<y}μ(x,z). The Möbius inversion theorem g=f*ζ ⟺ f=g*μ as the master generalization of inclusion-exclusion (Boolean lattice → 40.01.02) and arithmetic Möbius inversion (divisor lattice → 21.11.01). Computing μ: the product theorem μ_{P×Q}=μ_P·μ_Q; chains, Boolean (−1)^|T\S|, divisor (μ(b/a)), partition lattice Π_n with μ(0̂,1̂)=(−1)^{n−1}(n−1)!, subspace lattice L_n(q) with (−1)^n q^{C(n,2)} as the q-analogue. The characteristic polynomial χ_L(t)=Σμ(0̂,x)t^{n−r(x)} of a geometric lattice (Whitney numbers), Weisner's and Crapo's complementation theorems, and Philip Hall's order-complex theorem identifying μ(0̂,1̂) with the reduced Euler characteristic of the order complex. Applications to the chromatic polynomial (40.04.06) and combinatorial reciprocity. Co-produced 40.02.03 (Eulerian posets) imposes μ(x,y)=(−1)^{ℓ(x,y)}. Lean status none: Mathlib has IncidenceAlgebra/zeta/mu and the inversion lemmas but not the product theorem, the Boolean/divisor/partition/subspace closed forms, the characteristic polynomial / Whitney machinery, Weisner/Crapo, or the Philip-Hall topological identity.

### combinatorics.trees-cayley-matrix-tree-lagrange-inversion

- **title**: Trees, Cayley's Formula, the Matrix-Tree Theorem, and Lagrange Inversion
- **unit**: `40.01.07`
- **notes**: stub - see unit `40.01.07`. Enumeration of trees (Stanley EC2 Ch. 5). Cayley's formula n^(n-2) for labelled trees by four proofs (Prufer bijection with degree refinement, matrix-tree determinant, Pitman refining-forest double-count, Lagrange/EGF on the tree function T = x e^T counting n^(n-1) rooted trees). The matrix-tree theorem: spanning trees equal any Laplacian L = D - A cofactor, with weighted/all-minors form via Cauchy-Binet on the signed incidence matrix, and the eigenvalue product (1/n) prod mu_i. The directed (arborescence) matrix-tree and the BEST theorem counting Eulerian circuits. Lagrange-Burmann inversion [x^n]H(R) = (1/n)[lambda^(n-1)] H'(lambda) phi(lambda)^n for R = x phi(R), classical and residue forms, with rooted-plane-tree / Catalan and ballot-number applications. Prereq for the analytic tree-asymptotics unit 40.08.05. Lean status none.


### combinatorics.q-analogues-gaussian-binomials-partitions

- **title**: q-Analogues, Gaussian Binomial Coefficients, and the Combinatorics of Partitions
- **unit**: `40.01.06`
- **prerequisites**: `40.01.01` (basic counting / twelvefold way), `40.01.03` (generating functions)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `40.01.06`. q-analogues and the combinatorial theory of integer partitions (Stanley EC1 Ch. 1). The q-integer [n]_q = 1 + q + ... + q^(n-1), q-factorial, and the Gaussian binomial coefficient [n choose k]_q = [n]_q!/([k]_q![n-k]_q!); its polynomiality and non-negativity, the two q-Pascal recurrences, and its reading as the generating function for partitions in a k x (n-k) box. The q-binomial theorem and q-Vandermonde. Ferrers/Young diagrams, conjugation, rank, Durfee squares. Identities by GF and bijection: Euler's distinct-equals-odd, Glaisher's bijection, and Franklin's involution / pentagonal number theorem (stated; analytic asymptotics cross-ref 21.16.01). Jacobi triple product and the Rogers-Ramanujan identities stated combinatorially. Counting k-dimensional subspaces of F_q^n by [n choose k]_q for q a prime power, linking to finite geometry 40.06.03. Cross-refs 40.01.01, 40.01.03, 21.16.01, 40.06.03, and co-produced 40.01.05, 40.03.02. Lean status none: Mathlib lacks the Gaussian binomial as a packaged object with polynomiality/positivity, the q-Pascal recurrences, the box-partition and subspace readings, and the q-binomial theorem / q-Vandermonde.


### combinatorics.symmetric-designs-bruck-ryser-chowla

- **title**: Symmetric Designs and the Bruck-Ryser-Chowla Theorem
- **unit**: `40.06.02`
- **prerequisites**: `40.06.01` (balanced incomplete block designs and Fisher's inequality)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `40.06.02`. Symmetric 2-(v,k,lambda) designs (b=v, equivalently r=k) per van Lint-Wilson Ch. 19-20. The block-intersection number (any two distinct blocks meet in exactly lambda points), the dual design, the identity NN^T = N^T N = (k-lambda)I + lambda J, the order n = k-lambda, and det N = +-k(k-lambda)^((v-1)/2). The Bruck-Ryser-Chowla theorem: for v even, k-lambda is a perfect square; for v odd, z^2 = (k-lambda)x^2 + (-1)^((v-1)/2) lambda y^2 has a nonzero integer solution, proved via the rational congruence nI + lambda J = NN^T of quadratic forms, the Hasse-Minkowski local-global principle, and Lagrange's four-square identity. Flagship consequence: no projective plane of order 6 (recovering Euler's thirty-six officers), and the computer nonexistence of the plane of order 10 (Lam-Thiel-Swiercz 1989) which passes BRC. Biplanes (lambda=2). Cross-refs 40.06.01 (Fisher equality case), co-produced 40.06.03 (projective planes, lambda=1) and 40.06.05 (Hadamard designs), 40.06.06 (linear codes / the order-10 self-dual code), and quadratic forms / Hasse-Minkowski 21.04.03, sums of two squares 21.01.06. Lean status none: Mathlib has Matrix, ZMod, legendreSym, and Nat.sum_four_squares but no rational quadratic-form congruence / Hasse-Minkowski API for the BRC step.

### combinatorics.ramsey-theorem-ramsey-numbers

- **title**: Ramsey's Theorem and Ramsey Numbers
- **unit**: `40.05.04`
- **prerequisites**: `40.04.01` (graphs, basic invariants, foundational lemmas)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `40.05.04`. Ramsey theory (Diestel Ch. 9). Ramsey's theorem in the finite form (for all k, r there is R with every r-colouring of E(K_R) containing a monochromatic K_k) and the infinite form (an infinite monochromatic set in any finite colouring of [N]^p), via the neighbourhood-split / pigeonhole induction. The two-colour Ramsey numbers R(s,t), the Erdos-Szekeres recursion R(s,t) <= R(s-1,t) + R(s,t-1), the binomial upper bound R(s,t) <= binom(s+t-2,s-1) hence R(k,k) <= 4^k, and the probabilistic lower bound R(k,k) > 2^{k/2} (owned by 40.07.01, cross-ref'd not reproved). Small exact values R(3,3)=6, R(4,4)=18, the bracket 43 <= R(5,5) <= 46, and Erdos's aliens anecdote on the difficulty of R(6,6). The Erdos-Rado canonical Ramsey theorem (unbounded palettes; four canonical patterns for pairs). Schur's theorem and Rado's theorem (partition regularity / columns condition), van der Waerden's theorem (monochromatic arithmetic progressions) and the Hales-Jewett combinatorial-lines theorem as the abstract apex; van der Waerden derived from Hales-Jewett. Infinite Ramsey theory and the density turn to Szemeredi's theorem / Green-Tao (stated). Cross-refs 40.07.01 (probabilistic lower bound), 40.04.01 (graph foundations), and co-produced 40.05.01 (extremal Turan) and 40.05.03 (regularity/Szemeredi). Lean status none: Mathlib lacks the unified finite/infinite Ramsey theorem, the ramseyNumber definition with the Erdos-Szekeres recursion and binomial bound, and Schur/van der Waerden/Hales-Jewett/canonical-Ramsey.


### combinatorics.map-colouring-five-colour-four-colour

- **title**: Map Colouring: the Five-Colour Theorem and the Four-Colour Theorem
- **unit**: `40.04.08`
- **prerequisites**: `40.04.05` (planar graphs, Euler's formula, the light vertex), `40.04.06` (vertex colouring, chromatic number, greedy and degeneracy bounds)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `40.04.08`. Colouring planar graphs from Diestel Ch. 5. The map-colouring problem and its reduction to vertex colouring of the dual plane graph (bridgeless maps give loopless duals). The six-colour theorem (immediate from the degree-<=5 light vertex plus greedy induction); the five-colour theorem (Heawood) with the full Kempe-chain proof, including the Jordan-curve separation that makes the (2,4)-swap valid when the (1,3)-chain closes. Kempe's flawed 1879 four-colour proof and the interfering-double-swap error Heawood found in 1890. The four-colour theorem (Appel-Haken 1976; Robertson-Sanders-Seymour-Thomas 1997): unavoidable sets of reducible configurations, the discharging method (charge 6 - d(v), total 12 by Euler), and computer verification. Equivalents: Tait's theorem (4-face-colouring iff 3-edge-colouring of bridgeless cubic plane graphs via the Klein four-group difference labelling), the nowhere-zero-4-flow reformulation on the dual (cross-ref 40.04.09), and Hadwiger's conjecture for t=5 (cross-ref 40.04.10). Colouring on higher-genus surfaces: the Heawood number H(eps) = floor((7 + sqrt(49 - 24 eps))/2), the torus chromatic number 7 (K_7 embeds), and the Ringel-Youngs theorem giving equality for every surface except the Klein bottle. Cross-refs planar 40.04.05, vertex colouring 40.04.06, flows 40.04.09, minors/Hadwiger 40.04.10. Lean status none: Mathlib lacks the plane-graph/dual model, the six-/five-colour Kempe-chain theory, the four-colour discharging/reducibility apparatus (no Lean port of Gonthier's Coq proof), Tait's theorem, and the Heawood/Ringel-Youngs surface results.


### combinatorics.projective-affine-planes-mols

- **title**: Finite Projective and Affine Planes, MOLS, and the 36-Officers Problem
- **unit**: `40.06.03`
- **prerequisites**: `40.06.01` (BIBDs, Fisher's inequality, symmetric 2-designs)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `40.06.03`. Finite geometry (van Lint-Wilson Ch. 19, 22). Finite projective planes by the three axioms (two points on a unique line, two lines meet in a unique point, a quadrangle exists); order n with n^2+n+1 points/lines and n+1 points per line; the equivalence with symmetric 2-(n^2+n+1, n+1, 1) designs (cross-ref 40.06.01/40.06.02). Desarguesian planes PG(2,q) from F_q^3 with subspace count [3 choose 1]_q = q^2+q+1 (cross-ref 40.01.06). Affine planes (n^2 points, n^2+n lines) and the projective completion / decompletion. Latin squares and mutually orthogonal Latin squares (MOLS), the bound N(n) <= n-1, the equivalence between a complete set of n-1 MOLS and a projective/affine plane of order n, the field construction L_a(i,j)=ai+j giving q-1 MOLS for prime powers, and the orthogonal-array / transversal-design reformulations. Euler's 36-officers problem (N(6)=1, Tarry 1900), the Bruck-Ryser obstruction n = square+square for n = 1,2 mod 4, the order-10 computational exclusion (Lam-Thiel-Swiercz 1989), and the Bose-Shrikhande-Parker disproof of Euler's conjecture (MOLS exist for all n != 2, 6). Cross-refs 21.02.01 (finite fields), 40.06.04 (Steiner), 40.06.05 (Hadamard). Lean status none: Mathlib lacks finite incidence geometry as a structure, the plane-design equivalence, the MOLS field construction, the MOLS-plane bijection, and the Bruck-Ryser obstruction.


### combinatorics.turan-erdos-stone-simonovits

- **title**: Extremal Graph Theory: Turán's Theorem and Erdős-Stone-Simonovits
- **unit**: `40.05.01`
- **prerequisites**: `40.04.01` (graphs, basic invariants, foundational lemmas)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `40.05.01`. The extremal number ex(n,H) = max edges in an n-vertex H-free graph (Diestel Ch. 7). Mantel's theorem (triangle-free => <= floor(n^2/4) edges, unique extremiser the balanced K_{floor(n/2),ceil(n/2)}). Turán's theorem (K_{r+1}-free => the Turán graph T_r(n), the balanced complete r-partite graph, is the unique extremum) with multiple proofs: Zykov symmetrisation (non-adjacency becomes an equivalence relation, forcing complete-multipartite structure, then convex balancing), the Motzkin-Straus weight-shifting quadratic-form identity max over the simplex of sum_{uv in E} x_u x_v = (1/2)(1 - 1/omega(G)), and the degree-maximal-vertex / counting induction. The Erdős-Stone-Simonovits theorem ex(n,H) = (1 - 1/(chi(H)-1)) C(n,2) + o(n^2): the chromatic number alone governs the leading-term extremal density; lower bound is the Turán construction (T_r(n) is H-free since chi(T_r(n)) = r < chi(H)), upper bound is Erdős-Stone embedding a thick K_{r+1}(t) into any super-dense graph via the [40.04.01] averaging lemma. The degenerate/bipartite case chi(H)=2 gives only o(n^2) and needs Kővári-Sós-Turán ex(n,K_{s,t}) = O(n^{2-1/s}) via the cherry/common-neighbour count (co-produced 40.05.02). Supersaturation (above threshold, Theta(n^{|V(H)|}) copies of H) and Simonovits stability (near-extremal graphs are within eps*n^2 edits of T_r(n)). Cross-refs vertex colouring 40.04.06 (chi governing density, omega <= chi), the probabilistic-method lower bounds 40.07.01, and Möbius/inclusion-exclusion counting 40.02.02; co-produced 40.05.02 (KST), 40.05.03 (regularity), 40.05.04 (Ramsey). Lean status none: Mathlib has SimpleGraph.turanGraph, CliqueFree, and IsTuranMaximal (Turán's theorem itself) but not Mantel as the r=2 corollary with the floor(n^2/4) bound, the symmetrisation/Motzkin-Straus proofs as reusable lemmas, the Erdős-Stone-Simonovits o(n^2) density formula, the Kővári-Sós-Turán bound, supersaturation, or Simonovits stability.


### combinatorics.graph-minors-robertson-seymour

- **title**: Graph Minors and the Robertson-Seymour Theorem
- **unit**: `40.04.10`
- **prerequisites**: `40.04.05` (planarity, Kuratowski/Wagner)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `40.04.10`. The graph-minors project (Diestel Ch. 12). Minors and topological minors and the minor order; well-quasi-ordering (no infinite descending chain and no infinite antichain) and Kruskal's tree theorem (finite trees are wqo under topological minor, by Nash-Williams' minimal-bad-sequence argument and Higman's lemma) as the warm-up. The Robertson-Seymour theorem: finite graphs are well-quasi-ordered under the minor relation, so every minor-closed class has a finite forbidden-minor set and membership is decidable in O(n^3) via the disjoint-paths algorithm (non-constructive in the obstruction set). Tree-width and tree-decompositions, the tree-width = bramble-number - 1 duality (cops-and-robber game), the excluded-grid theorem (bounded tree-width iff excludes a grid minor), and the structure theorem for graphs excluding a fixed minor (clique-sums of surface-embeddable pieces plus bounded vortices plus apex vertices). Hadwiger's conjecture (no K_t minor implies (t-1)-colourable; t<=4 elementary, t=5 equivalent to four-colour via Wagner, t=6 by Robertson-Seymour-Thomas, t>=7 open) cross-ref 40.04.08; Wagner's theorem as the K_5-minor genus-zero prototype cross-ref 40.04.05. Lean status none: Mathlib lacks the wqo API, Kruskal's theorem, tree-width and its bramble duality, the excluded-grid theorem, and the graph-minor theorem.


### combinatorics.steiner-systems-triple-systems

- **title**: Steiner Systems and the Existence of Steiner Triple Systems
- **unit**: `40.06.04`
- **prerequisites**: `40.06.01` (balanced incomplete block designs and Fisher's inequality)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `40.06.04`. Steiner systems S(t,k,v) as the lambda=1 t-designs (every t-subset in exactly one block; van Lint-Wilson Ch. 19). Steiner triple systems STS(v)=S(2,3,v): the divisibility/admissibility cascade C(k-i,t-i) | C(v-i,t-i) specialising to v = 1 or 3 (mod 6), and Kirkman's existence theorem that these are also sufficient, via the Bose construction (commutative idempotent quasigroup) for v=3 (mod 6) and the Skolem construction (Skolem sequence) for v=1 (mod 6). The number of non-isomorphic STS (1 for v=7,9; 2 for 13; 80 for 15), subsystems (v >= 2w+1), cyclic systems. Kirkman's schoolgirl problem and resolvable / Kirkman triple systems (resolvability iff v=3 (mod 6), Ray-Chaudhuri-Wilson 1971). Recursive constructions v->2v+1 (one-factorisation doubling), 2v+7, 3v. Large Steiner systems S(4,5,11), S(5,6,12), S(5,8,24) and their Mathieu-group automorphisms / Golay-code links (cross-ref 40.06.07). The general existence of designs for all admissible parameters at large v (Keevash 2014; Glock-Kuhn-Lo-Osthus 2016). Steiner quadruple systems S(3,4,v) (v=2,4 mod 6, Hanani). Cross-refs 40.06.01 (BIBD/Fisher), 40.06.03 (projective planes), 40.06.07 (Golay 5-designs). Connections to 1-factorisations and Latin squares. Lean status none: Mathlib lacks Steiner-system structures, the divisibility cascade, and the Bose/Skolem constructions.


### combinatorics.strongly-regular-graphs-design-graph

- **title**: Strongly Regular Graphs and the Design-Graph Dictionary
- **unit**: `40.06.09`
- **prerequisites**: `40.06.01` (BIBDs, incidence matrix, the $NN^\top = (r-\lambda)I + \lambda J$ identity)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `40.06.09`. Strongly regular graphs (van Lint-Wilson Ch. 21). A $k$-regular graph on $v$ vertices with $\lambda$ common neighbours per adjacent pair and $\mu$ per non-adjacent pair, $\mathrm{srg}(v,k,\lambda,\mu)$; the adjacency-matrix equation $A^2 = kI + \lambda A + \mu(J-I-A)$ and the feasibility identity $k(k-\lambda-1)=(v-k-1)\mu$. The three eigenvalues $k, r, s$ (with $r,s$ the roots of $x^2-(\lambda-\mu)x-(k-\mu)=0$) and multiplicities $f,g$; the integrality/rationality condition forcing either integer eigenvalues or the half case $f=g$ (conference graphs $(4t+1,2t,t-1,t)$). The absolute bound $v\le\tfrac12 f(f+3)$ and the Krein conditions (nonnegativity of the dual structure constants). Examples: Petersen, Paley graphs (cross-ref 40.06.05), triangular $T(n)$ and lattice/Hamming graphs, Moore graphs and the Hoffman-Singleton theorem (degree in {2,3,7,57}). The Bose-Mesner algebra / two-class association scheme (apply 07.05.06). SRGs from symmetric 2-designs (block graphs), generalised quadrangles, Latin squares, and regular two-graphs / Seidel switching; the friendship theorem. The design-graph-code triangle, cross-ref 40.06.01, 40.06.05, 40.06.07, 40.06.08, 07.05.06. Lean status none: Mathlib has SimpleGraph and adjMatrix but lacks the SRG definition, the eigenvalue/multiplicity formulas from the matrix quadratic, the rationality dichotomy, the absolute bound and Krein conditions, the Hoffman-Singleton restriction, and the friendship theorem.


### combinatorics.second-moment-method-random-graph-thresholds

- **title**: The Second-Moment Method and Thresholds for Random Graphs
- **unit**: `40.07.03`
- **prerequisites**: `40.07.01` (the first-moment method, shipped)
- **notes**: stub — see unit `40.07.03`. The second-moment method (Alon-Spencer Ch. 4). Chebyshev's inequality and the second-moment bound (if Var[X] = o(E[X]^2) then X > 0 a.a.s. and X ~ E[X]); the Erdős-Rényi random graph G(n,p); threshold functions for monotone properties and their existence (Bollobás-Thomason 1987); the appearance of a fixed subgraph H at p = n^{-1/m(H)} with m(H) = max_{H'⊆H} e(H')/v(H') (balanced vs unbalanced, proved by first + second moment); the connectivity / isolated-vertex threshold p = (ln n)/n with the Poisson double-exponential limit and the coupon-collector heuristic; the giant-component phase transition at p = 1/n (double jump, n^{2/3} critical window, Erdős-Rényi 1960); the clique number and chromatic number of G(n,1/2) concentrating near 2 log_2 n and n/(2 log_2 n) (Bollobás 1988, cross-ref the concentration unit 40.07.05); the first-order zero-one law (Glebskii et al. 1969, Fagin 1976) via the extension axioms. Cross-refs first-moment 40.07.01, Borel-Cantelli 37.02.01, concentration 40.07.05, Janson 40.07.07. Lean status none: Mathlib has variance/Chebyshev and SimpleGraph but lacks the G(n,p) model, the second-moment corollary, the threshold machinery, m(H) and the subgraph-appearance threshold, the connectivity/Poisson limit, the giant-component transition, and the first-order zero-one law.

### combinatorics.eulerian-posets-face-lattices-characteristic-polynomial

- **title**: Eulerian Posets, Face Lattices, and the Characteristic Polynomial
- **unit**: `40.02.03`
- **prerequisites**: `40.02.02` (incidence algebra, Möbius function, Möbius inversion)
- **notes**: stub — see unit `40.02.03`. Graded posets and their enumerative invariants (Stanley EC1 Ch. 3). Graded/ranked posets with rank function r, the rank-generating function and the zeta polynomial Z(P,m). Eulerian posets: μ(x,y)=(−1)^{r(y)−r(x)} in every interval (equivalently the Euler relation Σ_{x≤z≤y}(−1)^{r(z)}=0), with the Boolean lattice B_n and the face lattice of a convex polytope as motivating examples. The f-vector/h-vector of a polytope, the Euler-Poincaré relation Σ(−1)^i f_i, the Dehn-Sommerville equations as palindromy of the flag h-vector (β_P(S)=β_P(S̄), and h_i=h_{n−i}); the flag f-vector α_P(S) and flag h-vector β_P(S) linked by α=Σ_{T⊆S}β. The cd-index Φ_P(c,d) as the basis-free encoding of the flag f-vector modulo Dehn-Sommerville (Fibonacci-many monomials), with Stanley's nonnegativity for Gorenstein* posets (proved by Karu). The characteristic polynomial χ(P,t)=Σ_x μ(0̂,x)t^{n−r(x)} of a graded/geometric lattice, Whitney numbers of the first kind, Whitney's theorem (no-broken-circuit count), the bond lattice and the identification χ(L_G,t) with the chromatic polynomial P(G,t) (cross-ref 40.04.06), and hyperplane arrangements with Zaslavsky's theorem r(A)=(−1)^d χ_A(−1). Order complexes, shellability and Cohen-Macaulay posets (face lattices are shellable, hence Gorenstein*). Matroid/geometric-lattice connection (cross-ref 40.02.01). Builds on the incidence algebra 40.02.02. Lean status none: Mathlib lacks the Eulerian predicate and its Boolean/face-lattice instances, the flag f/h-vector and Dehn-Sommerville symmetry, the cd-index and its nonnegativity, the characteristic polynomial / Whitney machinery, the bond-lattice = chromatic-polynomial identity, and Zaslavsky's region count.


### combinatorics.kovari-sos-turan-bipartite-extremal

- **title**: Bipartite Extremal Problems: the Kővári-Sós-Turán Theorem
- **unit**: `40.05.02`
- **prerequisites**: `40.05.01` (extremal graph theory: Turán and Erdős-Stone-Simonovits)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `40.05.02`. The degenerate (bipartite, chi(H)=2) case of extremal graph theory, where Erdős-Stone-Simonovits gives only o(n^2) and the true order is the central problem (Diestel Ch. 7). The Kővári-Sós-Turán theorem ex(n,K_{s,t}) <= (1/2)(t-1)^{1/s} n^{2-1/s} + O(n) via double-counting s-stars (s-cherries): convexity/Jensen on sum_v C(d(v),s) <= (t-1) C(n,s) since no s-set has t common neighbours. The Zarankiewicz problem z(m,n;s,t) = max ones in an m x n 0/1 matrix with no all-ones s x t submatrix. The exponent is set by s (smaller side), t only the constant. Matching lower bounds: the Erdős-Rényi polarity graph of a finite projective plane giving ex(n,C_4)=ex(n,K_{2,2})=(1/2+o(1)) n^{3/2} (two lines meet in one point = C_4-free certificate, cross-ref 40.06.03; Füredi exact), and the norm-graph construction (Kollár-Rónyai-Szabó / Alon-Rónyai-Szabó) showing Theta(n^{2-1/s}) tightness for K_{s,t} when t > (s-1)!. Applications: unit distances O(n^{3/2}) via K_{2,3}-freeness, Erdős distinct-distances, and the Szemerédi-Trotter incidence preview. The Bondy-Simonovits even-cycle bound ex(n,C_{2k}) = O(n^{1+1/k}) via path-counting / large-girth neighbourhood expansion, and the cage problem (generalised polygons as exact extremisers for C_4,C_6,C_10). Cross-refs Turán/Erdős-Stone 40.05.01 (dense dichotomy), projective planes 40.06.03 (the C_4-free polarity graph), probabilistic deletion lower bounds 40.07.01, Möbius/inclusion-exclusion 40.02.02; co-produced 40.05.03 (regularity), 40.05.04 (Ramsey). Lean status none: Mathlib has SimpleGraph and convexity tooling but lacks the Kővári-Sós-Turán bound, the s-star counting lemma, the Zarankiewicz function, the projective-plane polarity lower bound, the norm-graph construction, and the Bondy-Simonovits even-cycle bound.


### combinatorics.ring-symmetric-functions-bases

- **title**: The Ring of Symmetric Functions and Its Bases
- **unit**: `40.03.01`
- **prerequisites**: `40.01.06` (partitions, Ferrers diagrams, conjugation, dominance order, q-analogues)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `40.03.01`. The ring $\Lambda = \varprojlim_n \mathbb{Z}[x_1,\dots,x_n]^{S_n}$ of symmetric functions as the graded inverse limit of symmetric-polynomial rings (Stanley EC2 Ch. 7; Macdonald Ch. I). The five classical bases indexed by partitions of $n$: monomial $m_\lambda$, elementary $e_\lambda$, complete homogeneous $h_\lambda$, power-sum $p_\lambda$, and (cross-ref 40.03.02) Schur $s_\lambda$. The fundamental theorem of symmetric functions: $\Lambda = \mathbb{Z}[e_1,e_2,\dots] = \mathbb{Z}[h_1,h_2,\dots]$ on algebraically independent generators (unitriangularity of $e_\lambda \to m_\mu$ against dominance). Generating-function identities $E(t)=\prod(1+x_i t)=\sum e_n t^n$, $H(t)=\prod(1-x_i t)^{-1}=\sum h_n t^n$, the relation $H(t)E(-t)=1$, and $P(t)=\sum p_n t^{n-1}=H'(t)/H(t)$. Newton's identities linking power sums to elementary functions; the involution $\omega$ with $\omega(e_n)=h_n$, $\omega(p_n)=(-1)^{n-1}p_n$. Transition matrices among bases (integral except where $p_\lambda$ needs $\mathbb{Q}$-coefficients, obstruction $z_\lambda$). $\Lambda$ as a positive self-dual Hopf algebra (Zelevinsky). Cross-refs 40.01.06 (partition indexing set, q-specialization), 40.03.02 (Schur), 40.03.03 (Hall inner product), 40.03.06 (Frobenius characteristic), 07.05.02 / 07.05.10 (symmetric-group reps, Murnaghan-Nakayama). Lean status none: Mathlib has finite-variable esymm/psum/hsymm and Newton's identities but lacks the inverse-limit ring $\Lambda$, the partition-indexed bases, the fundamental theorem as a free polynomial ring, and the involution $\omega$.

### combinatorics.szemeredi-regularity-removal-lemma

- **title**: The Szemerédi Regularity Lemma and the Triangle Removal Lemma
- **unit**: `40.05.03`
- **prerequisites**: `40.05.01` (extremal graph theory: Turán, Erdős-Stone-Simonovits)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `40.05.03`. The regularity method (Diestel Ch. 7). ε-regular pairs (edge density d(X,Y)=e(X,Y)/(|X||Y|) stays within ε on all large subsets, so the pair looks random-like). The Szemerédi regularity lemma: every graph admits an ε-regular equipartition into a bounded number M(ε) of parts, proved by the energy/index increment q(P)=sum d(Vi,Vj)^2 weighted by pair size — monotone under refinement (defect Cauchy-Schwarz), an irregular pair forces an ε^4 increment, and q in [0,1] forces termination in O(ε^-5) steps, giving a tower-type bound on M(ε); Gowers's matching tower-type lower bound. The regularity/cleaning/counting triple: the counting lemma (a regular tripartite configuration of densities bounded below has (prod d_ij ± o(1)) |V1||V2||V3| triangles). The triangle removal lemma (o(n^3) triangles => deletable to triangle-free with o(n^2) edges) as the flagship application, and its corollaries the Ruzsa-Szemerédi (6,3)-theorem and Roth's theorem on 3-term APs (via the Cartesian-product / diagonal-triangle encoding, cross-ref 40.05.04 van der Waerden). The general graph removal lemma for fixed H; the link to property testing and to Szemerédi's theorem on arithmetic progressions; sparse-regularity (Kohayakawa-Rödl) and hypergraph regularity (Frankl-Rödl, Gowers) as the route to the Green-Tao theorem (stated). The analytic / graph-limit (graphon, cut metric, Lovász-Szegedy) view. Cross-refs extremal 40.05.01 (modern Erdős-Stone proof, supersaturation, stability), Ramsey/Roth 40.05.04 (density turn), probabilistic/quasirandomness 40.07.01.
