The METHOD OF DIAGRAMS (atomic diagram and elementary diagram, embeddings/elementary embeddings as satisfaction facts) previewing `42.02.02`. Builds toward compactness/diagrams `42.02.02` and quantifier elimination `42.02.05`. Lean status none: Mathlib's ModelTheory has Hom/Embedding/ElementaryEmbedding, Substructure/ElementarySubstructure with ≼, and elementarilyEquivalent, but not the named Tarski-Vaught test, the elementary-chain union theorem, back-and-forth systems with the forth/back properties yielding Cantor's theorem, or the Ehrenfeucht-Fraïssé game with the Fraïssé-Hintikka characterisation of ≡.


### mathematical-logic.representability-recursive-functions

- **title**: Representability of Recursive Functions in Arithmetic
- **unit**: `42.01.08`
- **prerequisites**: `42.01.04` (structures and Tarski semantics — the standard model and truth on numerals)
- **tier_anchors**: Enderton 2001 *A Mathematical Introduction to Logic* 2e §3.3-3.5; Boolos-Burgess-Jeffrey 2007 *Computability and Logic* Ch. 16; Hájek-Pudlák 1993 *Metamathematics of First-Order Arithmetic* Ch. I
- **notes**: The bridge from computability to formal arithmetic that powers incompleteness (Enderton §3.3). ROBINSON ARITHMETIC Q (the eight induction-free axioms of (N;0,S,+,·,<)) and PEANO ARITHMETIC PA (Q plus the first-order induction schema); NUMERALS n̲ = Sⁿ0. REPRESENTABILITY of a relation/function in a theory T (T proves the representing formula true on numerals in the relation and false off it, with provable uniqueness for functions). The REPRESENTABILITY THEOREM: every recursive function (recursive defined in computability 42.04.01) is representable in Q, by induction over composition, primitive recursion, and minimisation; GÖDEL'S β-FUNCTION β(c,d,i)=rem(c,1+(i+1)d) coding finite sequences via the Chinese remainder theorem so primitive recursion is arithmetically definable. Δ₀/Σ₁ formulas and the Σ₁-COMPLETENESS of Q (every true Σ₁ sentence is provable; Q decides every closed Δ₀ sentence in accordance with N), with the Σ₁/Π₁ asymmetry as the engine of incompleteness. ARITHMETISATION groundwork: Gödel numbering makes the PROOF PREDICATE Prf_T(p,x) primitive recursive hence representable, so Pr_T(x)=∃p Prf_T(p,x) is Σ₁ — arithmetic can express its own provability (forward-ref 42.01.09). MRDP/Diophantine sharpening of Σ₁ co-produced 42.04.07. Cross-refs Tarski semantics 42.01.04, recursive functions 42.04.01, halting/recursion theorem 42.04.02, incompleteness 42.01.09. Lean status none: Mathlib has the recursive-functions and model-theory layers but not Q as a finite theory, numeral lemmas, representability definitions, the β-function lemma from Nat.chineseRemainder, the closure-of-representable-functions induction, the Δ₀/Σ₁ classes on BoundedFormula, or Σ₁-completeness of Q.


### mathematical-logic.computably-enumerable-sets-creative-simple

- **title**: Computably Enumerable Sets: Creative and Simple Sets
- **unit**: `42.04.03`
- **prerequisites**: `42.04.02` (the halting problem, undecidability, and the recursion theorem)
- **tier_anchors**: Soare 2016 *Turing Computability* Ch. 2-4; Rogers 1967 *Theory of Recursive Functions* Ch. 7-8, 11; Odifreddi 1989 *Classical Recursion Theory I* Ch. II-III, V
- **notes**: The structure theory of c.e. sets (Soare Ch. 2-4). The EQUIVALENT CHARACTERISATIONS of a computably enumerable set (domain of a partial computable function, range of a computable function, projection of a computable relation, $\Sigma_1$-definable; the normal-form theorem); POST'S COMPLEMENTATION THEOREM ($A$ computable iff $A$ and $\overline A$ both c.e.); the canonical enumeration $\{W_e\}$ with stage approximations and its uniformity; s-m-n index manipulation on c.e. sets; the REDUCIBILITY ZOO $\le_1 \Rightarrow \le_m \Rightarrow \le_{tt} \Rightarrow \le_T$ (Turing at 42.04.04). CREATIVE SETS ($K$ is creative, the identity is a productive function for $\overline K$; every creative set is $m$-complete; MYHILL'S THEOREM creative $=$ $1$-complete $=$ recursively isomorphic to $K$); PRODUCTIVE SETS and the link to incompleteness (the provability set of a consistent axiomatised arithmetic is c.e. with productive true-sentence complement, so productiveness is the recursion-theoretic form of Gödel incompleteness, cross-ref 42.01.09); SIMPLE SETS (Post's construction of a c.e. set with infinite immune complement, neither computable nor $m$-complete — the first attack on Post's problem); the HYPERSIMPLE / HYPERHYPERSIMPLE / MAXIMAL hierarchy and the lattice $\mathcal E$ of c.e. sets, Post's program to force an intermediate Turing degree by a structural property, its failure (Dekker: every nonzero c.e. degree contains a simple set), and the resolution by the priority method (42.04.06). Co-produced with Turing reducibility / c.e. degrees (42.04.04) and the priority method (42.04.06). Lean status none: Mathlib has `Nat.Partrec`/`Code.eval`, `RePred`, `ComputablePred` but not the c.e. equivalence bundle, Post's complementation theorem, the $W_e$ enumeration with stages, $\le_1/\le_m$ as relations, productive/creative sets, Myhill's theorem, or the simple-set construction.


### mathematical-logic.deductive-calculus-soundness

- **title**: A Deductive Calculus for First-Order Logic and Soundness
- **unit**: `42.01.05`
- **prerequisites**: `42.01.04` (structures and Tarski's definition of truth — the semantics this calculus is matched against)
- **tier_anchors**: Enderton 2001 *A Mathematical Introduction to Logic* 2e §2.4-2.5; Mendelson 2015 *Introduction to Mathematical Logic* 6e Ch. 2; Shoenfield 1967 *Mathematical Logic* Ch. 2-3; van Dalen 2013 *Logic and Structure* 5e Ch. 2-3; Takeuti 1987 *Proof Theory* 2e Ch. 1
- **notes**: The syntactic engine of first-order logic — a Hilbert-style proof system whose soundness pins the turnstile ⊢ below the semantic ⊨ of `42.01.04`, set up for completeness `42.01.06` to prove it complete. LOGICAL AXIOMS (all generalizations of): tautologies (A1), the quantifier-INSTANTIATION axiom ∀x φ → φ^x_t for t substitutable (A2), the DISTRIBUTION axiom ∀x(φ→ψ)→(∀x φ→∀x ψ) (A3), the VACUOUS-quantifier axiom φ→∀x φ for x not free in φ (A4), and the EQUALITY axioms u=u (A5) and u=v→(φ→φ') (A6); the single rule MODUS PONENS. FORMAL DEDUCTIONS as finite sequences, Γ ⊢ φ. The DEDUCTION THEOREM (no side condition, since modus ponens is the only rule), the GENERALIZATION THEOREM (Γ ⊢ φ and x not free in Γ ⟹ Γ ⊢ ∀x φ — the eigenvariable restriction) and GENERALIZATION-ON-CONSTANTS (c fresh ⟹ Γ ⊢ ∀y φ^c_y), the rule of SUBSTITUTION. The SOUNDNESS THEOREM Γ ⊢ φ ⟹ Γ ⊨ φ proved by induction on deductions (each axiom valid, modus ponens preserves satisfaction). Syntactic CONSISTENCY and its finitariness; the easy half satisfiable ⟹ consistent (converse = completeness `42.01.06`). The EQUALITY axioms and NORMAL MODELS (= as genuine identity; quotient by provable-equality congruence). ALTERNATIVE CALCULI: NATURAL DEDUCTION (eigenvariable conditions), the SEQUENT CALCULUS LK and the CUT rule, cut-elimination cross-ref proof theory `42.05.01`. Co-produced with completeness `42.01.06`; cross-ref compactness `42.01.02`. Lean status none: Mathlib's ModelTheory has the semantic IsSatisfiable/Model machinery but no Hilbert proof system — no inductive ⊢ predicate, no axiom schemata or modus-ponens/generalization closure, hence no formalized deduction theorem, generalization(-on-constants), soundness-by-induction, or the satisfiable⟹consistent direction against the named-variable Tarski semantics of `42.01.04`.


### category-theory.monads-eilenberg-moore-kleisli

- **title**: Monads, Eilenberg-Moore Algebras, and the Kleisli Category
- **unit**: `41.05.01`
- **prerequisites**: `41.03.01` (adjunctions: hom-set and unit-counit definitions)
- **tier_anchors**: Mac Lane 1998 *Categories for the Working Mathematician* 2e Ch. VI; Riehl 2016 *Category Theory in Context* Ch. 5; Eilenberg-Moore 1965 *Adjoint functors and triples*
- **notes**: A MONAD $(T,\eta,\mu)$ on $\mathcal{C}$ (endofunctor $T$ with unit $\eta\colon\mathrm{Id}\Rightarrow T$ and multiplication $\mu\colon T^2\Rightarrow T$ satisfying the associativity and unit laws) — the "monoid in the category of endofunctors". The MONAD OF AN ADJUNCTION (every $F\dashv G$ gives $T=GF$ with $\mu=G\varepsilon F$). T-ALGEBRAS / the EILENBERG-MOORE category $\mathcal{C}^T$ (structure map $a\colon TA\to A$, the algebra laws; algebras for the free-monoid monad = monoids, free-group = groups, powerset = complete sup-semilattices/lattices, distribution = convex sets; list/Maybe/state monads in functional programming). The KLEISLI category $\mathcal{C}_T$ (free algebras, the Kleisli adjunction, programming with effects). The two canonical adjunctions resolving a monad (Kleisli = INITIAL, Eilenberg-Moore = TERMINAL among resolutions); the COMPARISON FUNCTOR $K^T(d)=(Gd,G\varepsilon_d)$ (full Beck monadicity at 41.05.02). COMONADS dually (store/state). Cross-refs adjunctions 41.03.01, monadicity/Lawvere 41.05.02, codensity/Kan 41.06.02. Lean status none: Mathlib has Monad/Comonad, Monad.Algebra, Kleisli, and the comparison functor but not the initial/terminal universal property of the two resolutions assembled in one place, nor the uniform identification of the algebra examples with Lawvere-theory models.


### category-theory.rapl-reflective-adjoint-functor-theorems

- **title**: RAPL, Reflective Subcategories, and the Adjoint Functor Theorems
- **unit**: `41.03.02`
- **prerequisites**: `41.03.01` (adjunctions: hom-set and unit-counit definitions)
- **tier_anchors**: Mac Lane 1998 *Categories for the Working Mathematician* 2e Ch. V §4-6; Riehl 2016 *Category Theory in Context* §4.5-4.6, §5.4; Borceux 1994 *Handbook of Categorical Algebra 1* §3.3, Ch. 5
- **notes**: RAPL (right adjoints preserve limits, left adjoints preserve colimits) with the hom-set/representability proof; REFLECTIVE SUBCATEGORIES (fully faithful inclusion with a reflector left adjoint; abelianization, Cauchy completion, sheafification cross-ref 04.01.03, Stone-Čech) and the isomorphism counit; reflective localizations as IDEMPOTENT MONADS; the GENERAL ADJOINT FUNCTOR THEOREM (complete locally small domain + limit-preservation + SOLUTION-SET CONDITION) proved via initial objects of comma categories; the SPECIAL ADJOINT FUNCTOR THEOREM (well-powered, complete, cogenerating set) with applications to Stone-Čech, free objects, and colimits in algebraic categories; adjoint existence as representability of the hom-functors (cross-ref Yoneda 41.04.02). Co-produced with representability 41.04.02 and monadicity 41.05.02. Lean status none: Mathlib proves rightAdjointPreservesLimits and packages Reflective subcategories but has no GAFT/SAFT with an explicit SolutionSetCondition predicate, no comma-category initial-object construction, and no derivation of free objects / Stone-Čech / algebraic colimits from one SAFT statement.


### category-theory.yoneda-lemma-embedding-density

- **title**: The Yoneda Lemma, the Yoneda Embedding, and Density
- **unit**: `41.04.02`
- **prerequisites**: `category-theory.representable-functors-universal-elements` (`41.04.01`)
- **tier_anchors**: Riehl 2016 *Category Theory in Context* §2.2-2.4, §6.2; Mac Lane 1998 *Categories for the Working Mathematician* 2e Ch. III §2, Ch. X; Mac Lane-Moerdijk 1992 *Sheaves in Geometry and Logic* Ch. I §5; Kelly 1982 *Basic Concepts of Enriched Category Theory* Ch. 2-3
- **notes**: The YONEDA LEMMA Nat(Hom(c,−),F)≅Fc naturally in c and F (full proof via the bijection α↦α_c(id_c), inverse u↦(f↦F(f)u)), with the contravariant/presheaf form Nat(Hom(−,c),F)≅Fc and the morphism corollary Nat(よc,よd)≅C(c,d). The YONEDA EMBEDDING よ:C↪[C^op,Set], c↦Hom(−,c), is FULL AND FAITHFUL (an object is known by its maps; c≅c' iff よc≅よc'), reflecting isomorphisms. CAYLEY'S THEOREM as the one-object case (a monoid/group embeds into the endomorphism/permutation monoid of its underlying set via the regular representation). The DENSITY THEOREM / co-Yoneda: every presheaf is the canonical colimit of representables indexed by its category of elements ∫F, with the NINJA-YONEDA COEND form F≅∫^c F(c)·Hom(−,c) (full calculus in 41.06.01). PRESHEAVES AS THE FREE COCOMPLETION: [C^op,Set] is cocomplete and Lan_よ H is the unique colimit-preserving extension of any H:C→E into a cocomplete E (cross-ref Kan extensions 41.06.02). The 2-CATEGORICAL YONEDA (stated). Applications: uniqueness of adjoints and representing objects, limits computed representably, the philosophy that all of category theory is shadow-boxing with Yoneda. Deepens the survey [01.02.09]; co-produced with [41.06.01] (ends/coends) and [41.06.02] (Kan extensions). Lean status none: Mathlib has yonedaLemma/yonedaEquiv, the Yoneda/Coyoneda embeddings with fullyFaithful, and the category of elements, but not the density theorem in colimit-of-representables form, the free-cocompletion universal property (Lan along よ), the Cayley reading of the one-object case, or the 2-categorical Yoneda as a unified API.


### mathematical-logic.cofinality-cardinal-exponentiation-sch

- **title**: Cofinality, Cardinal Exponentiation, and the Singular Cardinals Hypothesis
- **unit**: `42.03.04`
- **prerequisites**: `42.03.03` (cardinals and the arithmetic of the infinite)
- **tier_anchors**: Kunen 2011 *Set Theory* Ch. I §13; Jech 2003 *Set Theory* 3e Ch. 3 §3, Ch. 5, Ch. 8, Ch. 24; Shelah 1994 *Cardinal Arithmetic* (Oxford)
- **notes**: Cofinality cf(κ) (least order type of an unbounded subset; regular vs singular cardinals; cf(ℵ_ω)=ω; cf is always regular; regularity of successor cardinals under AC). KÖNIG'S THEOREM κ < κ^{cf(κ)}, hence cf(2^κ)>κ — the one substantive ZFC constraint on the continuum function — with proof. Cardinal exponentiation in depth: the GIMEL function ℷ(κ)=κ^{cf(κ)}, the computation of κ^λ by cases (Hausdorff (ℵ_{α+1})^{ℵ_β} = ℵ_{α+1}·ℵ_α^{ℵ_β} and the Bukovský-Hechler recursion reducing all exponentiation to gimel and the continuum function on regulars). EASTON'S THEOREM: on regular cardinals 2^κ is arbitrary subject only to monotonicity + König (stated; full forcing 42.03.08). The SINGULAR CARDINALS HYPOTHESIS (SCH: 2^{cf(κ)}<κ ⟹ κ^{cf(κ)}=κ^+; failure needs large cardinals — Silver's theorem that SCH cannot first fail at a singular of uncountable cofinality; the pcf-theory pointer to Shelah's bound 2^{ℵ_ω}<ℵ_{ω_4}). Inaccessible cardinals as regular limits (forward-ref 42.03.09). Cross-refs cardinals/aleph arithmetic 42.03.03, forcing independence 42.03.08, large cardinals 42.03.09. Lean status none: Mathlib has Ordinal.cof, Cardinal.IsRegular, a König form, but not the gimel function as a named object, the full exponentiation case analysis, SCH as a predicate, Silver's theorem, Easton forcing, or pcf theory.

### mathematical-logic.godel-completeness-henkin

- **title**: Gödel's Completeness Theorem and the Henkin Construction
- **unit**: `42.01.06`
- **prerequisites**: `42.01.05` (the deductive calculus and soundness — the syntactic engine whose converse this theorem proves)
- **tier_anchors**: Enderton 2001 *A Mathematical Introduction to Logic* 2e §2.5-2.6; Henkin 1949 *The completeness of the first-order functional calculus* (JSL 14); Chang and Keisler 1990 *Model Theory* 3e Ch. 1-2; Hodges 1993 *Model Theory* Ch. 6; Shoenfield 1967 *Mathematical Logic* Ch. 4
- **notes**: The central theorem of the chapter — GÖDEL'S COMPLETENESS THEOREM Γ ⊨ φ ⟹ Γ ⊢ φ, equivalently every consistent set of formulas has a model (the MODEL EXISTENCE theorem), proved by the HENKIN CONSTRUCTION. Adjoin WITNESSING CONSTANTS c_(∃x φ) with the HENKIN AXIOMS ∃x φ → φ^x_c (consistency-preserving via generalization-on-constants of `42.01.05`); the LINDENBAUM LEMMA extends to a MAXIMAL CONSISTENT set Δ (Zorn/AC in general, enumeration in the countable case) with the membership-respects-connectives and EXISTENTIAL-WITNESS properties; the CANONICAL / TERM MODEL on the closed terms modulo the PROVABLE-EQUALITY congruence (the equality axioms (A5)-(A6) make = a congruence; the quotient gives a NORMAL MODEL where = is genuine identity); the TRUTH LEMMA A ⊨ σ ⟺ σ ∈ Δ by induction on σ. Combined with SOUNDNESS gives ⊢ = ⊨. EQUIVALENT forms: completeness ⟺ consistent⟹satisfiable ⟺ COMPACTNESS (`42.01.07`); the EXTENDED completeness for theories. CARDINALITY |A| ≤ |L|+ℵ₀ gives downward LÖWENHEIM-SKOLEM; upward LS by adjoining distinct constants + compactness; the SKOLEM PARADOX. CHOICE: countable case needs only weak choice; uncountable case = Boolean prime ideal theorem (sub-AC). Sharp CONTRAST with Gödel INCOMPLETENESS (`42.01.09`): completeness is about FIRST-ORDER provability matching semantic consequence, incompleteness about no r.e. arithmetic theory deciding every sentence — NOT in tension (the unprovable G_T is false in some nonstandard model). Co-produced with compactness/LS `42.01.07`, model theory `42.02.01`; cross-ref `42.01.04`, `42.01.05`, `42.01.09`. Lean status none: Mathlib's ModelTheory proves a compactness-flavoured IsSatisfiable via ultraproduct/Skolemization but has no Hilbert ⊢ predicate, so the Henkin witness expansion, Lindenbaum lemma, term model, congruence-to-normal-model, and truth lemma against a named-variable calculus are all unformalised.


### mathematical-logic.unsolvable-problems-word-problem-mrdp

- **title**: Unsolvable Problems: the Word Problem and Hilbert's Tenth
- **unit**: `42.04.07`
- **prerequisites**: `42.04.02` (the halting problem, undecidability, and the recursion theorem)
- **tier_anchors**: Matiyasevich 1993 *Hilbert's Tenth Problem* (MIT Press) Ch. 1-6, 10; Lyndon-Schupp 2001 *Combinatorial Group Theory* (Springer) Ch. IV; Rotman 1995 *Theory of Groups* 4e Ch. 12; Soare 2016 *Turing Computability* Ch. 9-10
- **notes**: Undecidability projected onto the rest of mathematics via reductions from the halting set $K$ (Soare Ch. 9-10). The WORD PROBLEM for semigroups/monoids (Markov-Post 1947: a finite Thue system with unsolvable word problem, by encoding Turing-machine configurations as words and transitions as reversible rewrites) and for GROUPS (the NOVIKOV-BOONE theorem 1955/1958: a finitely presented group with unsolvable word problem, via towers of HNN extensions controlled by BRITTON'S LEMMA; the HIGMAN EMBEDDING THEOREM internalising a recursively presented group into a finite presentation; the ADIAN-RABIN theorem that every MARKOV PROPERTY of f.p. groups — triviality, finiteness, abelianness, freeness, torsion-freeness, having solvable word problem — is undecidable). HILBERT'S TENTH PROBLEM and the MRDP THEOREM (Matiyasevich-Robinson-Davis-Putnam: the DIOPHANTINE sets $=$ the c.e. sets, so no algorithm decides solvability of integer polynomial equations): the proof arc is the DAVIS NORMAL FORM (one bounded universal quantifier), the DAVIS-PUTNAM-ROBINSON exponential-Diophantine reduction, and MATIYASEVICH'S step that the Pell relation $u^2-(a^2-1)v^2=1$ makes exponentiation Diophantine (cross-ref Pell 21.01.08). Consequences: every c.e. set is a polynomial value-set; PRIME-REPRESENTING POLYNOMIALS; the universal Diophantine equation; the Diophantine form of incompleteness. Other unsolvable problems: the POST CORRESPONDENCE PROBLEM (undecidable by computation-history reduction), the TILING/domino problem, MATRIX MORTALITY, Rice-type undecidability for context-free grammars, and the undecidability of first-order validity (CHURCH'S THEOREM, cross-ref 42.01.10). Frames the abstract halting problem (42.04.02) as casting undecidability into concrete algebra, number theory, and combinatorics; mentions the Diophantine incompleteness (42.01.09). Lean status none: Mathlib has the computability substrate and fragments of Pell theory but no PCP/MRDP/Diophantine apparatus, no Novikov-Boone/Britton/Higman/Adian-Rabin machinery, and no decidability layer over group presentations.

### mathematical-logic.turing-reducibility-oracles-jump

- **title**: Turing Reducibility, Oracles, and the Jump
- **unit**: `42.04.04`
- **prerequisites**: `42.04.03` (computably enumerable sets: creative and simple sets)
- **tier_anchors**: Soare 2016 *Turing Computability* Ch. 3-4; Rogers 1967 *Theory of Recursive Functions* Ch. 9-13; Lerman 1983 *Degrees of Unsolvability* Ch. I-VI
- **notes**: Relative computation and the structure of non-computability (Soare Ch. 3). ORACLE TURING MACHINES computing $\Phi_e^A$ with a query tape and oracle state; the USE PRINCIPLE and use function $\varphi_e^A(x)$ (convergence depends only on $A$ below the use; the use lemma gives permanence under oracles agreeing below it). TURING REDUCIBILITY $A \le_T B$ ($\chi_A = \Phi_e^B$) and $\equiv_T$, generalising/weakening many-one reducibility ($\le_m \Rightarrow \le_T$, converse fails: $\overline K \le_T K$ but $\overline K \not\le_m K$). The TURING DEGREES $\mathcal D = \mathcal P(\omega)/\equiv_T$ as a partial order with least element $\mathbf 0$ (computable degree); the JOIN $A \oplus B$ giving the least upper bound, so $\mathcal D$ is an UPPER SEMILATTICE (not a lattice — exact-pair theorem). The TURING JUMP $A' = \{e : \Phi_e^A(e)\!\downarrow\} = K^A$ and the JUMP THEOREMS ($A <_T A'$; $A \le_T B \iff A' \le_1 B'$; jump monotone/strictly increasing; $A'$ is the $\le_m$-complete $A$-c.e. set; $\emptyset' = K$). ITERATED JUMPS $A^{(n)}$ and the hierarchy $\mathbf 0, \mathbf 0', \mathbf 0''$; POST'S THEOREM linking the jump ladder to the arithmetical hierarchy ($\Sigma_{n+1}^0 =$ $\mathbf 0^{(n)}$-c.e., 42.04.05). RELATIVISATION (every basic theorem holds over any oracle). The GLOBAL STRUCTURE of $\mathcal D$ ($2^{\aleph_0}$ degrees, every countable poset embeds, incomparable degrees by Kleene-Post forcing, minimal degrees by Spector, jump inversion). Co-produced with the arithmetical hierarchy (42.04.05) and the priority method / Post's problem (42.04.06). Lean status none: Mathlib has the unrelativised `Nat.Partrec`/`Code.eval` substrate but no oracle functional $\Phi_e^A$, no use lemma, no $\le_T$/degrees/join, no jump or jump theorems, and none of the structural results.

### mathematical-logic.godel-numbering-incompleteness-theorems

- **title**: Gödel Numbering, the Fixed-Point Lemma, and the Incompleteness Theorems
- **unit**: `42.01.09`
- **prerequisites**: `42.01.08` (representability of recursive functions in arithmetic — the proof predicate and Σ₁-completeness this unit consumes)
- **tier_anchors**: Enderton 2001 *A Mathematical Introduction to Logic* 2e §3.4-3.5; Boolos-Burgess-Jeffrey 2007 *Computability and Logic* Ch. 17-18; Smoryński 1977 'The incompleteness theorems' (Handbook of Mathematical Logic); Hájek-Pudlák 1993 *Metamathematics of First-Order Arithmetic* Ch. I, III; Lindström 1997 *Aspects of Incompleteness*
- **notes**: The climax of the chapter (Enderton §3.4-3.5). GÖDEL NUMBERING / arithmetisation of syntax: an injective effective coding ⌜·⌝ of symbols, formulas, and proofs as numbers (prime-power or β-function sequence coding), making the SUBSTITUTION function sub(⌜φ(x)⌝,n)=⌜φ(n̲)⌝, the DIAGONAL function d(e)=sub(e,e), and the PROOF PREDICATE Prf_T(p,x) primitive recursive hence representable in Q (cross-ref 42.01.08), with the Σ₁ PROVABILITY PREDICATE Pr_T(x)=∃p Prf_T(p,x). THE DIAGONAL (FIXED-POINT) LEMMA: for every ψ(x) a sentence G with T ⊢ G ↔ ψ(⌜G⌝), proved by representing sub/d and self-applying (the computational form is the recursion theorem 42.04.02). GÖDEL'S FIRST INCOMPLETENESS THEOREM: for consistent recursively axiomatised T ⊇ Q, the Gödel sentence G_T ↔ ¬Pr_T(⌜G_T⌝) is a true Π₁ sentence with T ⊬ G_T (consistency) and T ⊬ ¬G_T (ω-consistency), so T is incomplete; ROSSER'S predicate Pr_T^R refines this to plain consistency. TARSKI'S UNDEFINABILITY OF TRUTH: the truth set {⌜σ⌝ : N ⊨ σ} is not arithmetically definable (diagonalise a putative truth predicate to a Liar) — the semantic counterpart, the obstruction being indefinability of truth not of provability. GÖDEL'S SECOND INCOMPLETENESS THEOREM: T ⊬ Con_T for consistent T ⊇ IΣ₁, via the HILBERT-BERNAYS-LÖB derivability conditions (D1)-(D3) and LÖB'S THEOREM (T ⊢ Pr_T⌜σ⌝→σ iff T ⊢ σ); the modal logic GL (Solovay) captures the provability operator; the consequence is the collapse of Hilbert's programme. Generalisations and MISCONCEPTIONS: what incompleteness does NOT show (no absolutely unknowable truths, no inconsistency of math), the Lucas-Penrose argument critique (it presupposes the mind knows its own consistency, which the second theorem forbids), concrete independent statements GOODSTEIN and PARIS-HARRINGTON (cross-ref Ramsey combinatorics 40.05.04). The CONTRAST with completeness 42.01.06 (complete across all models vs incomplete against the standard model N — no tension; G_T fails in a nonstandard model). Co-produced with Church/Entscheidungsproblem 42.01.10. Cross-refs representability 42.01.08, completeness 42.01.06, halting/recursion theorem 42.04.02, combinatorial independence 40.05.04. Lean status none: Mathlib has the model-theory and recursive-function layers but not the Gödel numbering of BoundedFormula, representability of sub, the diagonal lemma, Pr_T with the derivability conditions, the first/second incompleteness theorems, Tarski undefinability, or Löb's theorem as a stable API.

### category-theory.beck-monadicity-lawvere-theories

- **title**: Beck's Monadicity Theorem and Lawvere Theories
- **unit**: `41.05.02`
- **prerequisites**: `41.05.01` (monads, Eilenberg-Moore algebras, and the Kleisli category)
- **tier_anchors**: Mac Lane 1998 *Categories for the Working Mathematician* 2e Ch. VI §6-8; Riehl 2016 *Category Theory in Context* §5.5; Adámek-Rosický-Vitale 2011 *Algebraic Theories* Ch. 1-3, 9-11; Lawvere 1963 *Functorial Semantics of Algebraic Theories*
- **notes**: The recognition theorem for categories of algebras (Mac Lane Ch. VI §6-7). The COMPARISON FUNCTOR $K\colon\mathcal{D}\to\mathcal{C}^T$ and MONADICITY ($K$ an equivalence). SPLIT / ABSOLUTE COEQUALISERS and $U$-SPLIT pairs (preserved by every functor). BECK'S MONADICITY THEOREM: $U$ monadic iff $U$ has a left adjoint, reflects isomorphisms, and creates coequalisers of $U$-split pairs; proof via the BAR PRESENTATION exhibiting every algebra as the canonical $U$-split coequaliser $\mu_A,Ta\colon T^2A\rightrightarrows TA$ of free algebras. The CRUDE / REFLEXIVE monadicity variants (reflexive coequalisers replace $U$-split). Examples: $\mathbf{Grp}$, $\mathbf{Ring}$, $R\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}$, every variety, and $\mathbf{CHaus}$ (Manes: the ULTRAFILTER/codensity monad $\beta$) are monadic over $\mathbf{Set}$; the (non)monadicity of $\mathbf{Top}$ and $\mathbf{Poset}$ (fail to reflect isomorphisms — continuous bijection counterexample). LAWVERE THEORIES: a category with finite products generated by one object, models = finite-product-preserving functors to $\mathbf{Set}$; the equivalence finitary-monad-on-$\mathbf{Set}$ $\simeq$ Lawvere theory (Lawvere 1963, Linton 1966), with operads/PROPs as theories with restricted variable usage. Duality with universal algebra (varieties, Birkhoff's HSP variety theorem as the classical shadow). The category of monads $\mathbf{Mnd}(\mathcal{C})$ and monad maps. Cross-refs monads/Eilenberg-Moore 41.05.01, AFT/reflective 41.03.02, Kan/codensity 41.06.02, endofunctor category 41.01.02, colimits 41.02.01. Lean status none: Mathlib has the comparison functor and a `Monadic` predicate and reflective-as-monadic instance but not Beck's theorem from the split-coequaliser lemma, the crude/reflexive variants, or the Lawvere-theory/finitary-monad equivalence.

### category-theory.ends-coends-calculus

- **title**: Ends, Coends, and the Calculus of (Co)ends
- **unit**: `41.06.01`
- **prerequisites**: `category-theory.yoneda-lemma-embedding-density` (`41.04.02`)
- **tier_anchors**: Mac Lane 1998 *Categories for the Working Mathematician* 2e Ch. IX-X; Loregian 2021 *(Co)end Calculus* Ch. 1-5; Kelly 1982 *Basic Concepts of Enriched Category Theory* Ch. 2-3; Riehl 2016 *Category Theory in Context* §1.4-1.5, §4.1, §6.2
- **notes**: DINATURAL transformations and WEDGES/COWEDGES (the dinaturality square F(c,f)∘ω_c = F(f,c')∘ω_{c'}). The END ∫_c F(c,c) as the universal wedge (a limit) and the COEND ∫^c F(c,c) as the universal cowedge (a colimit) for a bifunctor F: C^op×C → D; the computation as a (co)limit over the TWISTED-ARROW category TwArr(C), equivalently as an equalizer/coequalizer of the two slot actions. The (CO)END CALCULUS: the FUBINI theorem (ends/coends in independent variables commute and equal the joint (co)end, via TwArr(C×B) ≅ TwArr(C)×TwArr(B)); the NINJA-YONEDA / co-Yoneda identities Fc ≅ ∫^d C(d,c)·Fd and the dual end Fc ≅ ∫_d (Fd)^{C(c,d)} (the density theorem 41.04.02 as rewrite rules); the KEYSTONE Nat(F,G) ≅ ∫_c D(Fc,Gc) (natural transformations AS an end — dinaturality unwinds to the naturality square; cross-ref Yoneda 41.04.02). WEIGHTED (co)LIMITS: lim^W F = ∫_c Wc⋔Fc (cotensor/power) and colim^W F = ∫^c Wc·Fc (tensor/copower), with the conical case W=Δ1 recovering ordinary limits/colimits; COPOWER S·d (coproduct) vs POWER d^S (product), TENSOR/COTENSOR in the enriched case. Examples: the FUNCTOR TENSOR PRODUCT G⊗_C F = ∫^c Gc·Fc, the two-sided BAR CONSTRUCTION, the NERVE, and GEOMETRIC REALIZATION |X| = ∫^{[n]} X_n×Δ^n as a coend (a left Kan extension along the Yoneda embedding of the cosimplicial space). Framed as the calculus the Kan-extension formulas (41.06.02) are written in. Co-produced with [41.06.02] (Kan extensions); cross-refs density/Yoneda 41.04.02, limits 41.02.01, monads/bar resolution 41.05.01. Lean status none: Mathlib has fragmentary End/Wedge/Coend machinery for a bifunctor but not the dinaturality-as-cone API with TwArr(C) as the shape, the Fubini rule, Nat(F,G) ≅ ∫_c D(Fc,Gc) in this generality, the (co)Yoneda rewrite identities, weighted (co)limits as (co)ends, or the coend computation of pointwise Kan extensions that 41.06.02 consumes.

### mathematical-logic.constructible-universe-l-gch

- **title**: The Constructible Universe L and the Consistency of GCH
- **unit**: `42.03.06`
- **prerequisites**: `42.03.04` (cofinality, cardinal exponentiation, and the singular cardinals hypothesis)
- **tier_anchors**: Kunen 2011 *Set Theory* Ch. II; Jech 2003 *Set Theory* 3e Ch. 13, 18, 27; Devlin 1984 *Constructibility*
- **notes**: Gödel's constructible universe L (Kunen Ch. II). The DEFINABLE POWER SET Def(X) and the hierarchy L_0=∅, L_{α+1}=Def(L_α), L_λ=⋃, L=⋃_α L_α; RELATIVISATION φ^M and ABSOLUTENESS (Δ_0/Σ_1, the Lévy hierarchy, what is and is not absolute between transitive models — cross-ref FOL 42.01). INNER MODELS and L as the LEAST inner model of ZF; L ⊨ ZF (each axiom verified via Lévy reflection and Def); the CONDENSATION LEMMA (every elementary submodel of L_α collapses to some L_β) giving L ⊨ V=L. The AXIOM OF CONSTRUCTIBILITY V=L and its consequences: L ⊨ AC via the definable global well-ordering <_L, and L ⊨ GCH via condensation (every subset of ω_α appears by stage ω_{α+1}), hence Con(ZF) ⟹ Con(ZFC + GCH) (Gödel 1938). ◊ and □ hold in L (Jensen, stated); 0# and the covering theorem mark the limits of L (L is 'thin'; measurable cardinals contradict V=L). Contrast with forcing (42.03.07/08 makes CH fail; L makes it hold — the two halves of independence). Cross-refs AC 42.03.05, cardinal arithmetic/GCH 42.03.03/04, FOL/absoluteness 42.01, forcing 42.03.07. Lean status none: Mathlib has the model-theory and ZFSet layers but no Def, no L_α hierarchy, no relativisation/Lévy hierarchy with absoluteness, no inner-model least-model theorem, no condensation, and none of L ⊨ V=L / AC / GCH, ◊, □, 0#, or covering.

### mathematical-logic.compactness-lowenheim-skolem

- **title**: Compactness and the Löwenheim-Skolem Theorems for First-Order Logic
- **unit**: `42.01.07`
- **prerequisites**: `42.01.06` (Gödel's completeness theorem and the Henkin construction — compactness is its corollary)
- **tier_anchors**: Enderton 2001 *A Mathematical Introduction to Logic* 2e §2.6; Chang-Keisler 1990 *Model Theory* 3e Ch. 1-2; Marker 2002 *Model Theory: An Introduction* Ch. 2; Hodges 1993 *Model Theory* Ch. 5-6; Lindström 1969 *On extensions of elementary logic* (Theoria 35); Robinson 1966 *Non-standard Analysis* Ch. 1-3
- **notes**: The two metatheorems that make model theory (42.02.01) possible and reveal first-order logic's expressive limits. COMPACTNESS for FOL — a set of sentences has a model iff every finite subset does — as a corollary of completeness 42.01.06 (finiteness of deductions across ⊢=⊨) and via the ultraproduct/Łoś route (calculus-free, equivalent over ZF to the Boolean prime ideal theorem like the propositional case 42.01.02). NON-STANDARD MODELS: adjoin a constant c with c>S^n0 for every numeral to force an infinite element above the standard part, giving a countable non-standard model of true arithmetic elementarily equivalent to 𝔑 but not isomorphic; the hyperreals *ℝ with a positive infinitesimal by the same device (cross-ref Robinson, transfer = Łoś). The LÖWENHEIM-SKOLEM theorems — downward (every infinite structure in a countable language has a countable elementary substructure, via Skolem-witness closure and the Tarski-Vaught test), upward (models of every cardinality ≥|L|+ℵ₀ by adjoining κ distinct constants), the joint Löwenheim-Skolem-Tarski with bound |X|+|L|+ℵ₀. SKOLEM'S PARADOX: a countable model of ZF with internally-"uncountable" sets, resolved by the non-absoluteness of cardinality (the witnessing bijection exists in V but not in the model); cross-ref set theory 42.03.01. The failure of FIRST-ORDER CATEGORICITY: no first-order theory with an infinite model pins it down up to isomorphism (the price of compactness); second-order logic IS categorical for ℕ and ℝ but loses compactness, Löwenheim-Skolem, and a complete recursive proof system — LINDSTRÖM'S theorem that FOL is the maximal logic with both compactness and downward Löwenheim-Skolem. Cross-refs completeness 42.01.06, propositional compactness 42.01.02, model theory 42.02.01 (co-produced), incompleteness 42.01.09 (non-standard models reconcile completeness with G_T), set theory 42.03.01. Lean status none: Mathlib has compactness (isSatisfiable_iff_isFinitelySatisfiable), ultraproducts/Łoś, and Skolem expansions, but not the bundled downward Löwenheim-Skolem-Tarski cardinality theorem with Tarski-Vaught, the upward theorem, the non-standard model of arithmetic / hyperreals with transfer, Skolem's paradox and non-absoluteness of cardinality, the failure of categoricity, or Lindström's maximality theorem.

### mathematical-logic.arithmetical-hierarchy-posts-theorem

- **title**: The Arithmetical Hierarchy and Post's Theorem
- **unit**: `42.04.05`
- **prerequisites**: `42.04.04` (Turing reducibility, oracles, and the jump — the iterated jump that climbs the hierarchy)
- **tier_anchors**: Soare 2016 *Turing Computability* Ch. 4; Rogers 1967 *Theory of Recursive Functions* Ch. 14-16; Hinman 1978 *Recursion-Theoretic Hierarchies* Ch. III-VI
- **notes**: Classifying sets/relations by quantifier-alternation depth over a computable matrix (Soare Ch. 4). The ARITHMETICAL HIERARCHY $\Sigma_n^0, \Pi_n^0, \Delta_n^0$ ($\Sigma_0=\Pi_0=\Delta_0=$ computable; $\Sigma_1=$ c.e., $\Pi_1=$ co-c.e.), prenex normal form and quantifier-contraction via pairing, closure properties, bounded quantifiers not raising the level. The HIERARCHY THEOREM (the hierarchy is proper, $\Sigma_n \ne \Pi_n$, strict inclusions) via the universal $\Sigma_n$ set and diagonalisation. POST'S THEOREM aligning the hierarchy with the jump ladder: $\Sigma_{n+1}^0 = $ (c.e. in $\emptyset^{(n)}$), $\Delta_{n+1}^0 = \{A : A \le_T \emptyset^{(n)}\}$, $\emptyset^{(n)}$ is $\Sigma_n^0$-complete; the LIMIT LEMMA ($\Delta_2^0 = $ limit-computable). ARITHMETICAL DEFINABILITY (arithmetical $=$ first-order definable in $(\mathbb N,+,\cdot)$) and TARSKI'S undefinability of truth ($\mathrm{Th}(\mathbb N)$ not arithmetical, cross-ref 42.01.09). INDEX-SET completeness: $\mathrm{Fin}$ $\Sigma_2$-complete, $\mathrm{Inf}/\mathrm{Tot}$ $\Pi_2$-complete, $\mathrm{Cof}/\mathrm{Rec}$ $\Sigma_3$-complete; Rice and Rice-Shapiro as level-1 shadows. The HYPERARITHMETICAL ($\Delta^1_1$, jump iterated along Kleene's $\mathcal O$ to $\omega_1^{CK}$) and ANALYTICAL ($\Sigma^1_n$, function quantifiers) hierarchies as the continuation. Co-produced with the priority method / Post's problem (42.04.06). Cross-refs the jump (42.04.04), c.e. sets (42.04.03), incompleteness/undefinability (42.01.09). Lean status none: Mathlib has the $\Sigma_1$/$\Delta_1$ base but no stratified $\Sigma_n/\Pi_n$ predicate-class family, no universal-$\Sigma_n$/hierarchy theorem, and no Post's theorem (which depends on the absent relativised jump of 42.04.04).

### mathematical-logic.turing-degrees-priority-method

- **title**: The Turing Degrees and the Priority Method
- **unit**: `42.04.06`
- **prerequisites**: `42.04.04` (turing reducibility, oracles, and the jump)
- **tier_anchors**: Soare 2016 *Turing Computability* Ch. 6-12; Soare 1987 *Recursively Enumerable Sets and Degrees* Ch. VII-XII; Lerman 1983 *Degrees of Unsolvability* Ch. VII-IX
- **notes**: The priority method as computability theory's bespoke construction engine, building sets in stages to meet infinitely many competing requirements (Soare Ch. 6-7). POST'S PROBLEM (is there a c.e. set of intermediate degree, $\mathbf 0 <_T A <_T \mathbf 0'$?) and Post's failed thinness attempts via simple/hypersimple sets. The FRIEDBERG-MUCHNIK THEOREM (incomparable c.e. degrees exist) by the FINITE-INJURY PRIORITY METHOD: requirements $R_e/N_e$, the priority ordering, witnesses and restraint, injury, and the FINITE-INJURY LEMMA (each requirement injured finitely often, hence eventually met). The structure of the c.e. degrees $\mathcal R$: SACKS DENSITY (a c.e. degree strictly between any two), SACKS SPLITTING, MINIMAL PAIRS ($\mathbf a \wedge \mathbf b = \mathbf 0$), non-distributivity, finite-lattice embeddings. INFINITE-INJURY ($\mathbf 0''$/$\mathbf 0'''$ priority, the TREE METHOD, true path) and the SACKS JUMP THEOREM with the low/high hierarchy. The global structure of $\mathcal D$: SPECTOR MINIMAL DEGREES by perfect-tree forcing, the POSNER-ROBINSON join theorem, automorphism/definability questions (arithmetic interpreted into $\mathcal D$), the decidable $\Sigma_2$ theory of $\mathcal R$ vs undecidable richer fragments (Simpson, Lachlan). Frames Post's problem as the method's founding triumph; connects to $\Pi^0_1$ classes and reverse mathematics. Cross-refs the jump/reducibility (42.04.04), c.e./simple sets (42.04.03), the arithmetical hierarchy (42.04.05). Lean status none: Mathlib has the unrelativised computability substrate but no oracle functional, no $\le_T$/degrees/jump, and no priority-method apparatus (no stage approximations, requirement lists, injury sets, or finite-injury lemma), so Friedberg-Muchnik and the structure of $\mathcal R$ are far beyond the library.

### category-theory.kan-extensions-all-concepts

- **title**: Kan Extensions: All Concepts Are Kan Extensions
- **unit**: `41.06.02`
- **prerequisites**: `category-theory.ends-coends-calculus` (`41.06.01`)
- **tier_anchors**: Mac Lane 1998 *Categories for the Working Mathematician* 2e Ch. X; Riehl 2016 *Category Theory in Context* §6.1-6.5; Riehl 2014 *Categorical Homotopy Theory* Ch. 1-2; Loregian 2021 *(Co)end Calculus* Ch. 2-3
- **notes**: LEFT and RIGHT KAN EXTENSIONS Lan_K F and Ran_K F along K: C→D of F: C→E — the 2-categorical universal property (the best approximate extension; Lan_K ⊣ K* ⊣ Ran_K as adjoints to restriction). The POINTWISE FORMULA as a (co)end/weighted (co)limit: Lan_K F(d) ≅ ∫^c D(Kc,d)·Fc (copower, a weighted colimit) and Ran_K F(d) ≅ ∫_c (Fc)^{D(d,Kc)} (power, a weighted limit), equivalently the comma-category (co)limit over K↓d and d↓K; cross-ref ends/coends 41.06.01. EXISTENCE when E is (co)complete; PRESERVATION of pointwise left Kan extensions by cocontinuous functors (and dually). "ALL CONCEPTS ARE KAN EXTENSIONS" (Mac Lane Ch. X): (CO)LIMITS as Kan extension along !: C→1 (constant weight); ADJOINTS — a left adjoint is the absolute Lan_F id (Ran_F id for the right); the DENSITY theorem id ≅ Lan_よ よ and the YONEDA LEMMA as F ≅ Lan_よ F restricted; the DENSITY/REALIZATION-NERVE ADJUNCTION Lan_よ ⊣ N_F with the nerve N_F = E(F-,=), geometric realization |X| = ∫^{[n]} X_n·Δ^n. CODENSITY MONADS Ran_K K (a monad on D; cross-ref monads 41.05.01): the ULTRAFILTER MONAD β as the codensity of FinSet↪Set and DOUBLE-DUALIZATION V↦V** as the codensity of fin-dim vector spaces. HOMOTOPY/DERIVED Kan extensions: derived functors LF, RF as homotopy Kan extensions along a localisation, the gateway to (∞,1)-categories and the derived category 04.03.11. Capstone of the category-theory chapter; cross-refs ends/coends 41.06.01, Yoneda/density 41.04.02, adjunctions 41.03.01, monads 41.05.01, limits 41.02.01, derived categories 04.03.11. Lean status none: Mathlib has Lan/Ran via the universal property and pointwiseLeftKanExtension from comma-category colimits but not the (co)end formula proved equal to the weighted (co)limit, the preservation theorem, or the "all concepts" unifications and codensity-monad construction as stated theorems.

### mathematical-logic.forcing-posets-generics-names

- **title**: Forcing I: Posets, Generic Filters, Names, and the Fundamental Theorem
- **unit**: `42.03.07`
- **prerequisites**: `42.03.06` (the constructible universe L and the consistency of GCH — the dual inner-model technique)
- **tier_anchors**: Kunen 2011 *Set Theory* Ch. IV, VII; Jech 2003 *Set Theory* 3e Ch. 14-15, 21; Cohen 1966 *Set Theory and the Continuum Hypothesis*
- **notes**: Cohen's forcing method (Kunen Ch. IV/VII). FORCING POSETS (P, ≤, 1): conditions, q ≤ p = "stronger", compatibility/incompatibility, antichains, the countable chain condition (ccc), DENSE/open sets; the canonical example Fn(I,J) of finite partial functions, Fn(ω,2)=Add(ω,1) adding a COHEN REAL and Fn(ω×ω,2) adding ω-many. GENERIC FILTERS G over a countable transitive M (meet every dense D ∈ M; existence by Rasiowa-Sikorski; G ∉ M for atomless P). P-NAMES by ∈-recursion (sets of (name, condition) pairs), the interpretation τ_G = val(τ,G), the check name ǎ (ǎ_G = a) embedding M ⊆ M[G], the canonical name Ġ (Ġ_G = G). The GENERIC EXTENSION M[G] transitive, same ordinals, least model of ZF over M ∪ {G}. The FORCING RELATION p ⊩ φ by recursion on names and formulas (definable IN M). The FUNDAMENTAL THEOREM: Definability Lemma (⊩ definable in M) + Truth Lemma (M[G] ⊨ φ iff some p ∈ G forces φ). M[G] ⊨ ZFC (Comprehension/Replacement via Definability, Power Set via the nice-name bound, Choice from a well-order of M^P). Chain-condition/closure preservation (ccc preserves cardinals via the countable-spread bound; <κ-closed adds no <κ-sequences); mixing/maximality lemma; ground-model definability + cover/approximation preview (geology). Dual to L 42.03.06 (L shrinks, forcing grows); CH payoff 42.03.08; Martin's axiom/iterated forcing 42.03.09; AC 42.03.05; FOL/absoluteness 42.01. Lean status none: Mathlib has ZFSet and abstract filters/posets but no forcing poset/generic/Rasiowa-Sikorski, no P-names/interpretation/M[G], no forcing relation, and neither half of the Fundamental Theorem nor the M[G] ⊨ ZFC verification (nor the Boolean-valued V^B route).

### mathematical-logic.entscheidungsproblem-church-decidable-theories

The decision problem for first-order logic and theories: the Entscheidungsproblem, Church's theorem that validity is c.e. (via completeness) but undecidable (via representability and the halting problem), the decidability of complete recursively axiomatised theories, the undecidability of PA/ZFC/group theory by interpreting arithmetic, and the quantifier-elimination decidable islands (DLO, ACF, RCF/Tarski, Presburger) with their complexity.


### mathematical-logic.compactness-method-of-diagrams

- **title**: The Compactness Theorem and the Method of Diagrams
- **unit**: `42.02.02`
- **prerequisites**: `42.02.01` (structures, embeddings, and elementary equivalence — the map hierarchy and the diagram definitions this unit deploys)
- **tier_anchors**: Marker 2002 *Model Theory: An Introduction* Ch. 2; Chang and Keisler 1990 *Model Theory* 3e Ch. 2-5; Hodges 1993 *Model Theory* Ch. 5-6, 8-9; Robinson 1956 *A result on consistency...* (Indag. Math. 18); Craig 1957 *Three uses of the Herbrand-Gentzen theorem* (J. Symbolic Logic 22)
- **notes**: Compactness as the model-building workhorse and the METHOD OF DIAGRAMS as how "consistent" becomes "there is a model embedding what I want" (Marker Ch. 2). The atomic diagram Diag(M) and elementary diagram Diag_el(M); the DIAGRAM LEMMA (models of Diag(M) = structures with an embedding of M, models of Diag_el(M) = structures with an elementary embedding) and the consistency-to-embedding dictionary T ∪ Diag(M) satisfiable iff T has a model embedding M. Building models with prescribed properties by compactness — non-standard models of arithmetic, proper elementary extensions, the upward Löwenheim-Skolem theorem via new constants. The ŁOŚ-VAUGHT TEST (a satisfiable theory with no finite models that is κ-categorical for some κ ≥ |L| is complete; proof by resize-to-κ-and-compare), with the complete/decidable corollaries ACF_p, DLO, the random graph, infinite vector spaces (cross-ref categoricity 42.02.06). ELEMENTARY AMALGAMATION and the joint embedding property over the shared elementary diagram. The ROBINSON joint consistency theorem (alternating elementary-chain amalgamation over the L_0-reduct), CRAIG INTERPOLATION (derived from Robinson consistency) and BETH DEFINABILITY (implicit = explicit, from interpolation). PRESERVATION THEOREMS via diagrams: Łoś-Tarski (substructures iff Π_1-axiomatizable), Chang-Łoś-Suszko (unions of chains iff Π_2). ULTRAPRODUCTS and ŁOŚ'S THEOREM as the calculus-free compactness proof and a construction tool (the elementary ultrapower, ℝ* = ℝ^ℕ/U the hyperreals, transfer = Łoś), KEISLER-SHELAH (M ≡ N iff some ultrapowers are isomorphic). Cross-refs FOL compactness 42.01.07, structures/embeddings 42.02.01, types 42.02.03 (co-produced), QE/model completeness 42.02.05 (co-produced), categoricity 42.02.06. Lean status none: Mathlib has the raw compactness statement, ultraproducts with Łoś, and the embedding/elementary-embedding types, but not the diagram lemma, the Łoś-Vaught test, elementary amalgamation, Robinson consistency, Craig interpolation, Beth definability, the Łoś-Tarski/Chang-Łoś-Suszko preservation theorems, or the Keisler-Shelah ultrapower characterization of ≡.

### mathematical-logic.types-omitting-types

- **title**: Types and the Omitting Types Theorem
- **unit**: `42.02.03`
- **prerequisites**: `42.02.02` (the compactness theorem and the method of diagrams — the term-model technology the omitting construction runs on)
- **tier_anchors**: Marker 2002 *Model Theory: An Introduction* Ch. 4-5; Chang and Keisler 1990 *Model Theory* 3e Ch. 2-5; Hodges 1993 *Model Theory* Ch. 6, 10; Ryll-Nardzewski 1959 (Bull. Acad. Polon. Sci. 7); Vaught 1961 *Denumerable models of complete theories*
- **summary**: n-types over a parameter set, the Stone space S_n(A) of complete types, isolated/principal types and their forced realization, the Omitting Types Theorem via the Henkin construction, atomic and prime models, and the Ryll-Nardzewski and Vaught analyses of countable models.


### mathematical-logic.o-minimality-cell-decomposition

- **title**: O-Minimality and the Cell Decomposition Theorem
- **unit**: `42.02.08`
- **prerequisites**: `42.02.02` (the compactness theorem and the method of diagrams — the model-building substrate over which o-minimality is propagated to every model of the theory)
- **tier_anchors**: van den Dries 1998 *Tame Topology and O-minimal Structures* (Cambridge LMS 248) Ch. 1-10; van den Dries and Miller 1996 *Geometric categories and o-minimal structures* (Duke Math. J. 84); Wilkie 1996 *Model completeness... exponential function* (J. AMS 9); Pila-Wilkie 2006 *The rational points of a definable set* (Duke Math. J. 133)
- **notes**: O-minimality as the model-theoretic axiomatization of TAME GEOMETRY (Grothendieck's "topologie modérée"), by the one-line condition that every definable subset of the line is a finite union of points and open intervals. The motivating model ℝ = (ℝ,+,·,<) and the SEMIALGEBRAIC sets (Tarski-Seidenberg QE, cross-ref RCF in 42.02.05). The MONOTONICITY THEOREM (a definable one-variable function is piecewise monotone/constant and continuous). The CELL DECOMPOSITION THEOREM C(n)+C'(n) by simultaneous induction (cells as graphs and bands, definable functions piecewise continuous). DIMENSION theory (= topological/algebraic dimension, additivity, frontier inequality, no definable space-filling map). FINITENESS: uniform finiteness, definable choice, definable triangulation and trivialization (no fractals/Cantor sets definable). The o-minimal EULER CHARACTERISTIC and definable cohomology. Expansions ℝ_an, ℝ_exp (WILKIE'S THEOREM, model-complete via Khovanskii-Pfaffian finiteness), ℝ_an,exp. The PILA-WILKIE counting theorem (sub-polynomially many rational points off the algebraic part) and its use in the ANDRÉ-OORT / Manin-Mumford diophantine results. Cross-refs QE/RCF 42.02.05 (co-produced, the tameness framework this specializes), structures/embeddings 42.02.01, compactness/diagrams 42.02.02, real algebraic geometry. Lean status none: Mathlib has the ordered-field and FirstOrder.Language cores but no o-minimality layer — no IsOMinimal predicate, monotonicity, Cell/cell decomposition, o-minimal dimension, uniform-finiteness/definable-choice/triangulation/trivialization, Euler characteristic, the ℝ_an/ℝ_exp/ℝ_an,exp expansions, or Pila-Wilkie counting.

### mathematical-logic.forcing-cohen-independence-ch

- **title**: Forcing II: Cohen and the Independence of the Continuum Hypothesis
- **unit**: `42.03.08`
- **prerequisites**: `42.03.07` (forcing posets, generic filters, names, and the Fundamental Theorem)
- **tier_anchors**: Kunen 2011 *Set Theory* Ch. V, VII-VIII; Jech 2003 *Set Theory* 3e Ch. 15, 28-29; Cohen 1966 *Set Theory and the Continuum Hypothesis*; Bartoszyński-Judah 1995 *Set Theory: On the Structure of the Real Line*
- **notes**: Cohen's theorem that CH is independent of ZFC (Kunen Ch. V/VII-VIII). The COUNTABLE CHAIN CONDITION (ccc): every antichain countable. The Δ-SYSTEM (sunflower) LEMMA and the proof that Fn(I,2) is ccc. CARDINAL PRESERVATION: ccc forcing preserves cardinals and cofinalities via the countable-cover / maximal-antichain bound. ADDING κ COHEN REALS via Add(ω,κ)=Fn(κ×ω,2), generic reals c_ξ pairwise distinct, so 2^ℵ₀ ≥ κ; the NICE-NAME COUNT bounding 2^ℵ₀ ≤ |P|^ℵ₀ above. FORCING ¬CH: over M ⊨ GCH, add ℵ₂ Cohen reals → 2^ℵ₀ = ℵ₂, so CH fails; Con(ZFC) ⟹ Con(ZFC+¬CH). FORCING CH/GCH via the countably-closed collapse (adds no reals). With L ⊨ GCH (42.03.06) this gives the INDEPENDENCE of CH, settling Hilbert's first problem. EASTON'S THEOREM on the continuum function over the regulars (cross-ref 42.03.04); independence of the Suslin hypothesis, ◊, the Borel conjecture, and the cardinal invariants (Cichoń diagram). Levy-Solovay: large cardinals do not decide CH. Multiverse vs. new axioms (Woodin, Ω-logic; Hamkins). Cross-refs forcing machinery 42.03.07, L/GCH 42.03.06, cardinal exponentiation/Easton 42.03.04, Martin's axiom / iterated forcing 42.03.09 (co-produced). Lean status none: Mathlib has ZFSet, abstract posets/filters, and Cardinal arithmetic but no Fn(I,2)/ccc, no Δ-system lemma at the uncountable cardinal-arithmetic level, no ccc cardinal-preservation or nice-name bound, and hence neither the Cohen ¬CH model nor the countably-closed CH collapse nor Easton's theorem.
### mathematical-logic.quantifier-elimination-model-completeness

- **title**: Quantifier Elimination and Model-Completeness
- **unit**: `42.02.05`
- **prerequisites**: `42.02.02` (the compactness theorem and the method of diagrams — the diagram/consistency dictionary the QE and model-completeness tests specialize)
- **tier_anchors**: Marker 2002 *Model Theory: An Introduction* Ch. 3; Chang and Keisler 1990 *Model Theory* 3e Ch. 3, 5; Hodges 1993 *Model Theory* Ch. 7-8; Tarski 1948 *A Decision Method for Elementary Algebra and Geometry* (RAND); Marker, Messmer, Pillay 2006 *Model Theory of Fields* 2e
- **notes**: QUANTIFIER ELIMINATION (T has QE iff every formula is T-equivalent to a quantifier-free one; geometrically the definable sets are boolean combinations of atomic ones) and the QE tests — the primitive-formula / one-quantifier reduction and the back-and-forth / substructure-completeness criterion via existentially closed structures. MODEL-COMPLETENESS (every embedding between models is elementary), the Robinson test (T ∪ Diag(M) complete for every model M iff every formula is T-equivalent to an existential one), QE ⟹ model-complete, and model-complete + an embeddable prime structure ⟹ complete. Existentially closed models, model companions, and model completions. The WORKED THEORIES: DLO (QE, ℵ_0-categorical); ALGEBRAICALLY CLOSED FIELDS (Tarski QE → the Tarski-Chevalley projection theorem that constructible images are constructible, the Nullstellensatz cross-ref 04.02.07, ACF_p complete and decidable, strongly minimal); REAL CLOSED FIELDS (Tarski's QE for (ℝ,+,·,<), decidability of elementary algebra and geometry, semialgebraic sets and the Tarski-Seidenberg theorem); Presburger arithmetic (QE after adding divisibility predicates, decidable); (ℤ,+,<), divisible torsion-free abelian groups, atomless boolean algebras. QE as the structural source of tameness and decidability (cross-ref 42.01.10), with o-minimality the order-theoretic refinement (forward-ref 42.02.08). Cross-refs compactness/diagrams 42.02.02, types 42.02.03, categoricity 42.02.06 (ACF strongly minimal), o-minimality 42.02.08, Nullstellensatz 04.02.07, decidability 42.01.10. Lean status none: Mathlib has quantifier-free/prenex formula predicates and the diagram and elementary-embedding apparatus but not the QE predicate with the primitive-formula and back-and-forth tests, IsModelComplete with Robinson's test, model companions/completions, or the four worked QE theories (DLO, ACF with Tarski-Chevalley and strong minimality, RCF with Tarski-Seidenberg, Presburger).

### mathematical-logic.martins-axiom-large-cardinals

- **title**: Martin's Axiom, Iterated Forcing, and Large Cardinals
- **unit**: `42.03.09`
- **prerequisites**: `42.03.08` (Cohen forcing and the independence of CH — the single ccc step this unit iterates)
- **tier_anchors**: Kunen 2011 *Set Theory* Ch. III, VIII, X; Jech 2003 *Set Theory* 3e Ch. 16-17, 20; Shelah 1998 *Proper and Improper Forcing* 2e; Kanamori 2009 *The Higher Infinite* 2e
- **notes**: The capstone of set theory: after forcing showed CH is independent (42.03.08), the two programs that hunt for the RIGHT additional axioms. MARTIN'S AXIOM MA_κ (for every ccc poset and family of <κ dense sets a filter meets them all; MA = MA_{<2^ℵ₀}) read as "the universe is already generic for small ccc forcings". Consequences of MA+¬CH: additivity of measure/category = 2^ℵ₀, Knaster property K and productivity of ccc, the Suslin hypothesis (no Suslin lines), all ℵ₁-dense real order types isomorphic, 𝔭=2^ℵ₀ collapsing the cardinal invariants. ITERATED FORCING: finite-support iteration of ccc stays ccc (Δ-system on supports), two-step P*Q̇, the SOLOVAY-TENNENBAUM theorem Con(MA+¬CH). Countable-support iteration and Shelah's PROPER FORCING (preservation of ℵ₁), the Proper Forcing Axiom PFA (⟹ 2^ℵ₀=ℵ₂, all ℵ₁-Aronszajn trees special, Baumgartner), consistency from a supercompact. The cardinal invariants and the CICHOŃ DIAGRAM (cross-ref 42.03.08). LARGE CARDINALS: inaccessible, Mahlo, weakly compact, measurable (κ-complete ultrafilter ⇔ ultrapower embedding j:V→M, crit κ), Ramsey, Woodin, supercompact; the CONSISTENCY-STRENGTH LADDER (each proves Con of those below; empirical near-linearity). Scott: a measurable refutes V=L. DETERMINACY: AD in L(ℝ), the MARTIN-STEEL theorem (Woodin cardinals ⟹ projective determinacy), the Solovay model (all sets Lebesgue measurable). Large cardinals as new axioms settling what L cannot; Levy-Solovay (small forcing absorbed, so large cardinals don't decide CH); Woodin's Ω-logic / ℙ_max vs. Hamkins's multiverse. Model-theory stability/o-minimal-tameness parallel (Shelah on both rulers; 42.02.05, 42.02.08). Cross-refs forcing/CH 42.03.08, machinery 42.03.07, L/GCH 42.03.06, cofinality/Easton 42.03.04, AC 42.03.05. Lean status none: Mathlib has Cardinal arithmetic and ultrafilters but no MA, no finite/countable-support iteration or Solovay-Tennenbaum, no proper forcing/PFA, no class V or j:V→M elementary embeddings, no consistency-strength ladder, and no Martin-Steel determinacy.


### mathematical-logic.saturation-homogeneity-monster-models

- **title**: Saturation, Homogeneity, and Monster Models
- **unit**: `42.02.04`
- **prerequisites**: `42.02.03` (types and the omitting types theorem — the Stone space of types and the back-and-forth method the saturated-model construction realizes maximally)
- **tier_anchors**: Marker 2002 *Model Theory: An Introduction* Ch. 4, 6; Chang and Keisler 1990 *Model Theory* 3e Ch. 5-6; Hodges 1993 *Model Theory* Ch. 10; Tent and Ziegler 2012 *A Course in Model Theory* Ch. 4-6; Morley and Vaught 1962 *Homogeneous universal models* (Math. Scand. 11)
- **summary**: κ-saturation (every type over <κ parameters realized), κ- and strong κ-homogeneity, the back-and-forth uniqueness of saturated models of equal cardinality, saturated ⟹ universal and homogeneous, existence under κ=κ^{<κ} and via special models, the monster model ℭ as a κ̄-saturated strongly κ̄-homogeneous universal domain with types as Aut(ℭ/A)-orbits, and recursively saturated and resplendent models.

### mathematical-logic.categoricity-morley-baldwin-lachlan

- **title**: Categoricity: Ryll-Nardzewski, Morley, and Baldwin-Lachlan
- **unit**: `42.02.06`
- **prerequisites**: `42.02.05` (quantifier elimination and strong minimality — the finite/cofinite dichotomy and acl-dimension this unit's categoricity reconstruction runs on)
- **tier_anchors**: Marker 2002 *Model Theory: An Introduction* Ch. 4-6; Chang and Keisler 1990 *Model Theory* 3e Ch. 7; Morley 1965 *Categoricity in power* (Trans. AMS 114); Baldwin and Lachlan 1971 *On strongly minimal sets* (J. Symbolic Logic 36); Shelah 1990 *Classification Theory* 2e; Tent and Ziegler 2012 *A Course in Model Theory*
- **notes**: CATEGORICITY in a cardinal (κ-categorical = exactly one model of size κ up to iso). ℵ_0-categoricity and the RYLL-NARDZEWSKI theorem (finite S_n(T) iff every type isolated iff Aut(M) oligomorphic; DLO, the random graph, the pure infinite set). MORLEY's categoricity theorem (Łoś's conjecture: categorical in one uncountable cardinal iff in all), proved via ω-STABILITY/total transcendentality, MORLEY RANK and degree, prime/saturated models, the absence of VAUGHTIAN PAIRS, and the two-cardinal transfer. STRONGLY MINIMAL sets, the exchange property, the acl-pregeometry and DIMENSION. The BALDWIN-LACHLAN theorem (uncountably categorical ⟹ I(T, ℵ_0) ∈ {1, ℵ_0}, refining Vaught). The categoricity SPECTRUM and the launch of CLASSIFICATION THEORY / STABILITY (Shelah's main gap). Examples: ACF_p uncountably categorical via transcendence degree, vector spaces over a fixed field, the failure for (ℝ,+,·) and (ℕ,+,·). Cross-ref QE/strong minimality 42.02.05, types/Ryll-Nardzewski 42.02.03, saturation 42.02.04, stability 42.02.07, cardinal arithmetic 42.03.03.

### mathematical-logic.strongly-minimal-morley-rank-stability

- **title**: Strongly Minimal Sets, Morley Rank, and Stability
- **unit**: `42.02.07`
- **prerequisites**: `42.02.05` (quantifier elimination and model-completeness — the QE that makes ACF strongly minimal, the running rank-1 example this unit's dimension theory abstracts)
- **tier_anchors**: Marker 2002 *Model Theory: An Introduction* Ch. 5-6; Tent and Ziegler 2012 *A Course in Model Theory* (LNL 40) Ch. 5-8; Pillay 1996 *Geometric Stability Theory* (Oxford Logic Guides 32); Shelah 1990 *Classification Theory* 2e; Hrushovski 1996 *The Mordell-Lang conjecture for function fields* (J. AMS 9)
- **notes**: The capstone of model theory: stability theory replaces categoricity with a fine geometry of independence and dimension on definable sets. STRONGLY MINIMAL SETS (every definable subset finite or cofinite — the model-theoretic line; ACF, vector spaces, the pure infinite set). The algebraic-closure PREGEOMETRY/matroid with the Steinitz EXCHANGE property, independence, BASIS and DIMENSION (= transcendence degree for ACF, linear dimension for vector spaces; powers Baldwin-Lachlan, cross-ref 42.02.06). MORLEY RANK (Cantor-Bendixson rank of the type space) and Morley degree; strong minimality = RM 1, degree 1; additivity of rank (Lascar) in ω-stable theories. STABILITY by type-counting (ω-stable/superstable/stable), the ORDER PROPERTY and Shelah's unstable/stable dichotomy ((ℝ,<) and arithmetic unstable; ACF, modules, free groups stable). FORKING and the abstract independence calculus (symmetry, transitivity, local character, nonforking extensions, canonical bases) generalizing algebraic and linear independence. The ZILBER TRICHOTOMY (disintegrated / locally modular / field-like), Zilber's conjecture and the HRUSHOVSKI CONSTRUCTION refutation; Zariski geometries. SHELAH'S MAIN GAP and the dividing lines (stable, superstable, NIP, simple). Applications: Hrushovski's Mordell-Lang for function fields, model theory of differential/difference fields. Cross-refs strong minimality/QE 42.02.05, categoricity/dimension 42.02.06 (co-produced, consumes the dimension theory), types/Stone space 42.02.03, o-minimality as parallel tame world 42.02.08, structures/embeddings 42.02.01. Lean status none: Mathlib has the CompleteType Stone space and FirstOrder.Language definable-set API but no IsStronglyMinimal/exchange pregeometry, Morley rank/degree/additivity, the type-counting stability spectrum and order-property dichotomy, the forking/dividing independence calculus with canonical bases, the Zilber trichotomy/Hrushovski construction, or the main-gap dividing lines — the largest single gap in Mathlib.ModelTheory.

### mathematical-logic.sequent-calculus-cut-elimination-consistency

- **title**: Sequent Calculus, Cut-Elimination, and the Consistency of Arithmetic
- **unit**: `42.05.01`
- **prerequisites**: `42.01.05` (a deductive calculus for FOL and soundness)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `42.05.01`. Gentzen's sequent calculus LK, the cut-elimination Hauptsatz and the subformula property, natural-deduction normalization and the Curry-Howard preview, and the ordinal analysis of PA — Gentzen's consistency proof by transfinite induction to epsilon_0, the proof-theoretic ordinal, and the link to the independence results (Goodstein, Paris-Harrington) of `42.01.09`. Lean status none: Mathlib lacks LK, cut-elimination, and ordinal analysis.

### mathematical-logic.indiscernibles-ehrenfeucht-mostowski

- **title**: Indiscernibles and Ehrenfeucht-Mostowski Models
- **unit**: `42.02.09`
- **prerequisites**: `42.02.04` (saturation, homogeneity, and monster models)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `42.02.09`. Order-indiscernible sequences, extraction of indiscernibles from any infinite sequence via Ramsey's theorem (cross-ref `40.05.04`), the Ehrenfeucht-Mostowski functor building a model on any linear order realizing few types and admitting many automorphisms, and the role of indiscernibles/Morley sequences in stability theory (`42.02.07`). Lean status none: Mathlib lacks indiscernible sequences and the EM construction.

### mathematical-logic.club-stationary-fodor

- **title**: Club Sets, Stationary Sets, and Fodor's Lemma
- **unit**: `42.03.10`
- **prerequisites**: `42.03.04` (cofinality, cardinal exponentiation, and the SCH)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `42.03.10`. The closed-unbounded (club) filter on a regular uncountable kappa (kappa-completeness, normality, closure under diagonal intersection), stationary sets and the non-stationary ideal, Fodor's pressing-down lemma, and Solovay's stationary-splitting theorem — the infinite-combinatorics layer beneath Silver's theorem (`42.03.04`), diamond (`42.03.06`), and the large-cardinal/forcing-axiom material (`42.03.09`). Lean status none: Mathlib lacks the club filter, stationary sets, and Fodor's lemma.

### mathematical-logic.kolmogorov-complexity-algorithmic-randomness

- **title**: Kolmogorov Complexity and Algorithmic Randomness
- **unit**: `42.04.08`
- **prerequisites**: `42.04.04` (Turing reducibility, oracles, and the jump)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `42.04.08`. Plain (C) and prefix-free (K) Kolmogorov complexity, the invariance theorem and incompressibility, Martin-Lof randomness via effective null sets, the Schnorr-Levin incompressibility characterization, Chaitin's Omega as a left-c.e. ML-random real Turing-equivalent to the halting problem, and the randomness-degrees interface (K-low = low-for-K = low-for-random, Nies-Hirschfeldt-Downey). Lean status none: Mathlib lacks Kolmogorov complexity and algorithmic randomness.

### category-theory.monoidal-categories-coherence

- **title**: Monoidal Categories and Mac Lane's Coherence Theorem
- **unit**: `41.07.01`
- **prerequisites**: `41.01.02` (natural transformations, functor categories, and equivalence)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `41.07.01`. Monoidal categories (tensor, unit, associator/unitors, the pentagon and triangle axioms), monoid objects (recovering rings, algebras, and monads as monoids in End), Mac Lane's coherence theorem (every formal diagram commutes / strictification), monoidal functors and natural transformations, and braided/symmetric monoidal categories (hexagon axioms, the braid group / Yang-Baxter, rigid & compact-closed categories) — the foundation under tensor categories, TQFT, and quantum groups. Lean status none: Mathlib has MonoidalCategory but lacks the coherence theorem and strictification.

### numerical-analysis.power-inverse-rayleigh-quotient-iteration

- **title**: Power iteration, inverse iteration, and Rayleigh quotient iteration
- **unit**: `43.06.01`
- **prerequisites**: `linear-algebra.rayleigh-quotient-courant-fischer`, `01.01.08` (eigenvalue/eigenvector)
- **notes**: The first eigenvalue *algorithms* (root unit of chapter 43.06). Power iteration $x_{k+1}=Ax_k/\|Ax_k\|$ converges to the dominant eigenpair at the linear rate $|\lambda_2/\lambda_1|$ (proof via eigenbasis expansion $A^kx_0=\sum_i c_i\lambda_i^k u_i$), with the Rayleigh quotient as the optimal eigenvalue estimate and quadratic eigenvalue-error sharpening in the self-adjoint case; inverse iteration with a shift $\mu$ is power iteration on $(A-\mu I)^{-1}$ and converges to the eigenvalue nearest $\mu$ at rate $|\lambda_J-\mu|/\delta$ (Wielandt 1944; Wilkinson's accuracy-despite-ill-conditioning analysis); Rayleigh quotient iteration feeds the current Rayleigh quotient back as the shift, giving cubic local convergence for symmetric matrices (quadratic in general, Parlett); plus deflation and shifted/polynomial spectral relocation as the bridge to the QR algorithm (43.06.03) and Krylov methods (43.07). Lean status none: Mathlib has the static spectral infrastructure but no iterative eigenvalue algorithms or their convergence-rate theorems.

### numerical-analysis.floating-point-arithmetic-ieee-model

- **title**: Floating-point arithmetic and the IEEE model
- **unit**: `43.01.01`
- **prerequisites**: `00.01.01` (real numbers), `25.03.01` (algorithmic complexity / big-O)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.01.01`. The floating-point number system F(β,p,e_min,e_max) ⊂ ℝ (normalized significand/exponent, IEEE-754 binary32/binary64), unit roundoff u = ½β^(1−p) and machine epsilon, the rounding map fl(x) = x(1+δ) with |δ| ≤ u and the fundamental axiom fl(a op b) = (a op b)(1+δ) for the four arithmetic operations under correct rounding, the IEEE rounding modes, overflow/underflow/subnormals/gradual-underflow and NaN/signed-zero, catastrophic cancellation as a conditioning (not rounding) phenomenon, and the accumulated-rounding theorem for recursive summation (the γ_n = (n−1)u/(1−(n−1)u) backward-error bound), with Sterbenz's lemma, Kahan compensated summation, and the dot-product backward-error bound. The conceptual root of chapter 43.01; the numerical substrate of every later stability theorem. Lean status none: Mathlib lacks the floating-point number system as a reasoned-about subset of ℝ, the correctly-rounded rounding map, the standard model with the proven |δ| ≤ u bound, and the summation/dot-product error theorems (the Flocq-in-Coq layer).

### numerical-analysis.bisection-scalar-root-problem

- **title**: The Bisection Method and the Scalar Root-Finding Problem
- **unit**: `43.02.01`
- **prerequisites**: `02.01.05` (metric space + Banach fixed-point), `02.05.02` (mean value theorem / Taylor-Lagrange remainder)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.02.01`. The scalar root-finding problem $f(x)=0$ for continuous $f$; bracketing via the intermediate value theorem; the bisection algorithm, its guaranteed convergence and the a-priori error bound $(b-a)/2^{n+1}$; the iteration count for a tolerance; linear convergence of order $p=1$ with rate $1/2$; robustness-vs-slowness; worst-case optimality in the sign-evaluation model; the order-of-convergence framing the rest of chapter 43.02 (fixed-point, Newton) builds on; finite-precision accuracy floor (forward cross-ref to Trefethen 43.01). Lean status none: Mathlib has the IVT but not bisection as a verified numerical method with the error bound and iteration-count lemma.

### numerical-analysis.conditioning-condition-numbers

- **title**: Conditioning and condition numbers of problems
- **unit**: `43.01.02`
- **prerequisites**: `01.01.12` (SVD, kappa=sigma_1/sigma_n), `02.05.03` (multivariable chain rule / Jacobian)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.01.02`. A problem as a map $f:X\to Y$; the absolute condition number $\hat\kappa=\|J(x)\|$ and relative condition number $\kappa=\|J(x)\|\,\|x\|/\|f(x)\|$ via the Jacobian; the condition number of matrix-vector multiplication $\|A\|\|x\|/\|Ax\|$ and of the linear system $Ax=b$ with the matrix condition number $\kappa(A)=\|A\|\|A^{-1}\|=\sigma_1/\sigma_n$; reciprocal condition number as relative distance to singularity (Eckart-Young); componentwise / Bauer-Skeel conditioning; conditioning of polynomial roots (Wilkinson's polynomial) and of eigenvalues ($1/|y^*x|$, Bauer-Fike); ill-conditioning vs ill-posedness; conditioning as a property of the problem independent of any algorithm (distinct from stability, 43.01.03). Originators: Turing 1948 (matrix condition number), von Neumann-Goldstine 1947, Wilkinson 1963-65 (conditioning vs stability, Wilkinson polynomial), Higham 2002 (componentwise theory). Lean status none: Mathlib has matrix/operator norms and partial SVD but no conditioning theory (no condition-number definition, no $\kappa(A)=\sigma_1/\sigma_n$ theorem, no linear-system sensitivity bound).

### numerical-analysis.polynomial-interpolation-lagrange

- **title**: Polynomial interpolation: existence, uniqueness, and the Lagrange form
- **unit**: `43.08.01`
- **prerequisites**: `01.01.09` (Gram-Schmidt / finite-dim inner-product space), `02.05.02` (MVT + Taylor-Lagrange remainder)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.08.01`. Root unit for chapter 43.08 (Suli-Mayers Ch. 6). The interpolation problem: match n+1 data (x_i, f_i) at distinct nodes with a degree-<=n polynomial. Existence-and-uniqueness (unisolvence) via the Vandermonde determinant det V = prod_{i<j}(x_j - x_i) and the too-many-roots argument; the evaluation map Pi_n -> F^(n+1) as a linear isomorphism. The Lagrange cardinal basis L_k (L_k(x_i) = delta_ki), the Lagrange interpolation formula, and the cardinal partition of unity sum L_k = 1. The Newton divided-difference form, the recurrence f[x_i..x_{i+k}] = (f[x_{i+1}..] - f[..x_{i+k-1}])/(x_{i+k} - x_i), the leading-coefficient identity [x^n]p_n = f[x_0..x_n], and O(n) incremental add-a-node construction. The barycentric weights w_k = 1/ell'(x_k) and the second (true) barycentric form as the numerically stable representation (Berrut-Trefethen 2004, Higham 2004). Equivalence of the three forms as bases of Pi_n; the node polynomial ell(x) = prod(x - x_i) threading error (43.08.02), splines (43.08.03), Chebyshev nodes (43.08.04), and quadrature (43.09). Lean status none: Mathlib has Lagrange.interpolate and Matrix.det_vandermonde but not the unisolvence-both-ways theorem, the divided-difference recurrence as a verified incremental update, or the equivalence of the Lagrange/Newton/barycentric forms.


### numerical-analysis.one-step-methods-euler-runge-kutta

- **title**: One-step methods: Euler, trapezoidal, Runge-Kutta; consistency and order
- **unit**: `43.10.01`
- **prerequisites**: `02.12.01` (phase space / vector field / IVP, Picard-Lindelöf existence-uniqueness), `02.06.03` (systems of linear ODE, matrix exponential), `02.05.05` (multivariable Taylor)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.10.01`. The initial-value problem y'=f(t,y), y(t_0)=y_0 and the general one-step map u^{n+1}=u^n+h*Phi(t_n,u^n,h); forward (explicit) and backward (implicit) Euler, the trapezoidal and implicit-midpoint rules; the explicit Runge-Kutta family with its Butcher tableau (A,b,c) and the classical RK4. Local truncation error tau^n as the exact-solution residual, consistency (tau->0) and order p via Taylor matching; the convergence theorem for one-step methods (global error O(h^p) from consistency plus a Lipschitz/discrete-Gronwall stability bound), cross-referencing Picard-Lindelöf existence-uniqueness (02.12.01). RK order conditions / Butcher rooted-tree barriers, implicit RK / Gauss collocation (order 2s), global-error expansion and Richardson extrapolation, and the A-stability obstruction for explicit methods at a high level. Root unit for chapter 43.10 (multistep 43.10.02, zero-stability/Dahlquist 43.10.03, absolute stability 43.10.04, stiffness 43.10.05 are downstream). Lean status none: Mathlib has Picard-Lindelöf and Gronwall but not the discretisation layer (one-step map, LTE/order, discrete-Gronwall convergence, RK order conditions).

### numerical-analysis.backward-stability-backward-error-analysis

- **title**: Backward stability and backward-error analysis of algorithms
- **unit**: `43.01.03`
- **prerequisites**: `43.01.01` (floating-point arithmetic / IEEE model), `43.01.02` (conditioning / condition numbers)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.01.03`. Forward error ||tilde-f(x)-f(x)||/||f(x)|| vs backward error eta(x)=inf{||dx||/||x|| : tilde-f(x)=f(x+dx)}; stability (mixed forward-backward: nearly right answer to a nearly right question) vs backward stability (exact answer to a nearby question, eta(x)=O(u)); the slogan "the right answer to a nearby question". The fundamental theorem of backward-error analysis: forward error <= condition number x backward error = O(kappa(x) eps_mach), proved from the first-order conditioning expansion of 43.01.02. Backward stability of correctly-rounded operations (the standard-model factor (1+delta) absorbed into operands), of back substitution (|Delta T| <= gamma_n |T| componentwise, kappa-independent), and of Householder QR (tilde-Q tilde-R = A + Delta A with ||Delta A|| = O(eps ||A||), independent of kappa because orthogonal transformations have condition number 1). Worked backward-error analysis of the floating-point inner product / matrix-vector product (hat-y = (A+Delta A)x, |Delta A| <= gamma_n |A|) and of summation under cancellation. Rigal-Gaches closed form eta = ||r||/(||A|| ||x|| + ||b||) turning backward stability into a computable residual certificate. The Wilkinson paradigm (charge rounding error backward to the data). Originators: Wilkinson 1963-65 (backward error analysis, GE and orthogonal-reduction analysis), Rigal-Gaches 1967 (normwise backward error closed form), Trefethen-Bau 1997 (modern definitions / forward-error theorem), Higham 2002 (componentwise back-substitution and Householder analyses, gamma_n notation). Lean status none: Mathlib has matrix/operator norms and partial SVD but no algorithm-stability layer (no algorithm-as-map tilde-f, no backward-error definition, no fundamental forward<=kappa x backward theorem, no verified inner-product / Householder backward-error bounds).

### numerical-analysis.fixed-point-iteration-contraction-order

- **title**: Fixed-Point Iteration, Contraction Convergence, and Order of Convergence
- **unit**: `43.02.02`
- **prerequisites**: `02.01.05` (metric space + Banach fixed-point), `02.05.02` (mean value theorem / Taylor-Lagrange remainder), `43.02.01` (bisection / scalar root-finding)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.02.02`. Reformulating $f(x)=0$ as $x=g(x)$; the simple (Picard) iteration $x_{n+1}=g(x_n)$; the contraction-mapping convergence theorem on an interval with the a-priori bound $\frac{L^n}{1-L}|x_1-x_0|$ and the a-posteriori bound $\frac{L}{1-L}|x_n-x_{n-1}|$; local convergence under $|g'(x^*)|<1$ with asymptotic linear rate $|g'(x^*)|$, divergence when $>1$; the order-of-convergence definition ($p$, asymptotic error constant) and the theorem that the order equals the index of the first non-vanishing derivative of $g$ at $x^*$ ($e_{n+1}\sim \frac{g^{(p)}(x^*)}{p!}e_n^p$); Aitken/Steffensen acceleration; Banach-space and multivariable ($\rho(g'(x^*))<1$) generalisation; the connection that Newton (43.02.03) is the fixed-point map with $g'(x^*)=0$ and quadratic order. Cross-refs the abstract Banach fixed-point statement in 02.01.05. Lean status none: Mathlib has ContractingWith.fixedPoint with the geometric bounds but not the derivative-criterion local contraction or the order-$p$ Taylor theorem.

### numerical-analysis.hessenberg-tridiagonal-reduction

- **title**: Reduction to Hessenberg/tridiagonal form
- **unit**: `43.06.02`
- **prerequisites**: `01.01.09` (Gram-Schmidt / Householder reflectors / QR), `43.06.01` (power / inverse / Rayleigh-quotient iteration)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.06.02`. The orthogonal-similarity reduction of a general $A$ to upper-Hessenberg form ($h_{ij}=0$ for $i>j+1$), and of a Hermitian $A$ to tridiagonal form, by a finite product of at most $n-2$ Householder reflectors $Q=Q_1\cdots Q_{n-2}$ acting as two-sided similarities $A\mapsto Q_k^*AQ_k$ (each zeroing the subcolumn below row $k+1$ while the right-multiply preserves the earlier zeros). Spectrum preserved by unitary similarity; symmetry preserved so the symmetric output is tridiagonal; operation count $\tfrac{10}{3}n^3$ general, $\tfrac{4}{3}n^3$ symmetric. The Hessenberg band is invariant under one QR sweep ($H=QR\Rightarrow RQ$ Hessenberg), which is why this is the cheap finite first phase that makes the iterative QR algorithm (43.06.03) cost $O(n^2)$ (or $O(n)$ tridiagonal) per sweep instead of $O(n^3)$. Why finite triangularization is impossible (Abel/Galois obstruction forcing the iterative second phase); backward stability $\mathrm{fl}(Q^*AQ)=Q^*(A+E)Q$, $\|E\|=O(\epsilon_{\mathrm{mach}}\|A\|)$; the implicit-Q / unreduced-Hessenberg uniqueness theorem underlying bulge-chasing QR; the same Hessenberg/tridiagonal structure arising as $Q_m^*AQ_m$ from Arnoldi/Lanczos (43.07.02). Originators: Householder 1958 (the reflector and unitary triangularization), Wilkinson 1965 (backward-error analysis), Parlett 1998 (symmetric tridiagonalisation). Lean status none: Mathlib has matrix similarity/charpoly invariance and reflection machinery but no Hessenberg/tridiagonal band predicate, no constructive Householder-reduction lemma, and no $O(n^3)$ count or backward-error bound.


### numerical-analysis.bauer-fike-eigenvalue-conditioning

- **title**: Bauer-Fike and the conditioning of eigenvalues
- **unit**: `43.06.04`
