- **prerequisites**: `01.01.08` (eigenvalue/diagonalization), `01.01.14` (Rayleigh quotient / Weyl-interlacing, Hermitian perturbation), `43.01.02` (conditioning)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.06.04`. Eigenvalue perturbation for non-normal matrices. The Bauer-Fike theorem: for diagonalisable $A = V\Lambda V^{-1}$, every eigenvalue of $A+E$ lies within $\kappa_p(V)\|E\|_p$ of an eigenvalue of $A$ (proof via conjugation by $V$ and the diagonal resolvent norm $1/\min_i|\lambda_i-\mu|$). The condition number of a simple eigenvalue $\kappa(\lambda)=1/|y^*x|=1/\cos\angle(x,y)$ via biorthogonal left/right eigenvectors, and the first-order expansion $\dot\lambda = y^*Ex/(y^*x)$ (Kato/Stewart-Sun). Normal/Hermitian matrices have $\kappa_2(V)=1$, $|y^*x|=1$, perfectly conditioned eigenvalues - recovering the Weyl/interlacing bound of `01.01.14` as the boundary case; non-normal matrices have ill-conditioned eigenvalues. Defective eigenvalue: Puiseux $\epsilon^{1/m}$ splitting. Gershgorin disc localisation. The $\epsilon$-pseudospectrum (three equivalent definitions: reachable spectra, resolvent-norm level set $\|(zI-A)^{-1}\|\ge\epsilon^{-1}$, smallest singular value) as the sharp qualitative picture of non-normal sensitivity; Bauer-Fike as the disc-inflation upper bound $\sigma_\epsilon(A)\subseteq\bigcup_i\{|z-\lambda_i|\le\kappa_2(V)\epsilon\}$. Originators: Gershgorin 1931, Bauer-Fike 1960, Kato 1980, Stewart-Sun 1990, Trefethen-Embree 2005 (pseudospectra). Lean status none: Mathlib has static eigenstructure and operator/matrix norms but no eigenvalue perturbation theory (no Bauer-Fike, no $1/|y^*x|$ condition number, no Gershgorin, no pseudospectrum/resolvent-norm characterisation).


### numerical-analysis.interpolation-error-runge-phenomenon

- **title**: Interpolation error and the Runge phenomenon
- **unit**: `43.08.02`
- **prerequisites**: `43.08.01` (polynomial interpolation, Lagrange/Newton forms, the node polynomial), `02.05.02` (MVT / Rolle + Taylor-Lagrange remainder)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.08.02`. The interpolation error theorem f(x)-p_n(x) = f^{(n+1)}(xi)/(n+1)! prod_k (x-x_k), proved by forming the auxiliary function phi(t)=f(t)-p_n(t)-G*pi(t) and applying Rolle n+1 times; the node (nodal) polynomial pi_{n+1}(x)=prod_k(x-x_k) as the one analyst-controllable factor, and the uniform bound ||f-p_n|| <= M_{n+1}/(n+1)! * ||pi_{n+1}||. The divided-difference remainder f(x)-p_n(x)=f[x_0..x_n,x]*pi_{n+1}(x) and the mean-value identity f[x_0..x_n,x]=f^{(n+1)}(xi)/(n+1)!. The Lebesgue function sum_k |L_k(x)|, the Lebesgue constant Lambda_n as the sup-norm operator norm of the interpolation projection, the near-best bound ||f-I_n f|| <= (1+Lambda_n) E_n(f), and the growth contrast Lambda_n ~ 2^{n+1}/(e n log n) equispaced vs (2/pi) log n Chebyshev. The Runge phenomenon: divergence of equispaced interpolation of 1/(1+25x^2) on [-1,1] despite real-analyticity, explained by the function's complex poles (Bernstein ellipse) and the endpoint swelling of the equispaced node polynomial via the logarithmic potential of the uniform vs arcsine (equilibrium) density; cure by Chebyshev nodes (forward 43.08.04). Lean status none: Mathlib has Rolle/taylor_mean_remainder_lagrange and Lagrange.interpolate but not the interpolation remainder theorem, the divided-difference remainder, or any Lebesgue-constant / near-best theory.


### numerical-analysis.least-squares-orthogonal-polynomials

- **title**: Least-squares approximation and orthogonal polynomials
- **unit**: `43.08.05`
- **prerequisites**: `02.11.07` (inner-product space / orthogonal projection), `01.01.09` (Gram-Schmidt / finite-dim inner-product space), `43.08.01` (polynomial interpolation, the Pi_n backdrop)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.08.05`. Best least-squares (2-norm) polynomial approximation in a weighted L^2 inner product (f,g)_w = int_a^b w f g dx (and the discrete weighted-sum variant) as orthogonal projection onto Pi_n; the best-approximation theorem characterised by the orthogonality / normal condition (f - p_n, q)_w = 0 for all q in Pi_n. The normal equations G a = b with the monomial Gram = moment matrix G_jk = mu_{j+k}, the Hilbert matrix for w=1, and its exponential ill-conditioning (forward cross-ref 43.01.02). Orthogonal polynomial families by Gram-Schmidt on the monomials against the weight (Legendre w=1 on [-1,1], Chebyshev w=(1-x^2)^{-1/2}); the three-term recurrence phi_{k+1} = (x - alpha_k) phi_k - beta_k phi_{k-1} with alpha_k,beta_k inner-product formulas; the symmetric tridiagonal Jacobi matrix J_n; roots of phi_n real, simple, interlacing, in (a,b); Christoffel-Darboux reproducing kernel; diagonalisation of the least-squares coefficients c_k = (f,phi_k)_w/(phi_k,phi_k)_w. Forward link: the roots are the Gauss-quadrature nodes (43.09.03), the Golub-Welsch eigenproblem. Distinct from data-regression least squares (26.06.01) and the linear-algebra QR/normal-equation conditioning (Trefethen 43.04). Lean status none: Mathlib has abstract orthogonalProjection and the named Chebyshev/Hermite families but not the weighted-L^2 polynomial inner product, the recurrence-with-coefficient-formulas, or the general real-simple-interlacing-roots theorem.


### numerical-analysis.linear-multistep-adams-bdf

- **title**: Linear multistep methods: Adams and BDF families, order via the characteristic polynomials
- **unit**: `43.10.02`
- **prerequisites**: `43.10.01` (one-step methods, LTE/order framework), `02.05.05` (multivariable Taylor), `02.12.01` (IVP / Picard-Lindelöf)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.10.02`. The r-step linear multistep method sum alpha_j u^{n+j} = h sum beta_j f^{n+j}; the first and second characteristic polynomials rho(zeta), sigma(zeta); the linear difference operator L and its error constants C_q, with order p iff C_0=...=C_p=0 and the analytic form rho(e^h)-h sigma(e^h)=C_{p+1}h^{p+1}+O(h^{p+2}) (the log condition); consistency iff rho(1)=0 and rho'(1)=sigma(1). Adams-Bashforth (explicit, order r) and Adams-Moulton (implicit, order r+1) by interpolatory quadrature; backward-differentiation formulas (BDF, order r, sigma a single newest-node term, usable BDF1-6); predictor-corrector pairs (PEC/PECE), Milne's device for local error, and the starting-value problem. Sets up but does not develop the root condition / zero-stability / Dahlquist equivalence theorem (43.10.03) and the first Dahlquist barrier. Lean status none: Mathlib has the IVP theory and the polynomial algebra but not the multistep-discretisation layer (the difference recurrence, the operator L, the C_p constants, the order-condition characterisation, or the Adams/BDF coefficient families).


### numerical-analysis.gaussian-elimination-lu-stability

- **title**: Gaussian elimination, LU factorization, and its stability
- **unit**: `43.03.01`
- **prerequisites**: `01.01.06` (systems of linear equations / Gaussian elimination as elementary operations), `01.01.07` (determinant / leading principal minors), `43.01.02` (conditioning / condition numbers), `43.01.03` (backward stability / backward-error analysis)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.03.01`. Gaussian elimination as the LU factorization A = LU (L unit lower triangular recording the multipliers, U upper triangular), realised by the elementary Gauss transforms M_k = I - l_k e_k^T. Existence/uniqueness of unpivoted LU iff every leading principal minor det A_k (k=1..n-1) is nonzero, with the pivot identity u_kk = det A_k / det A_{k-1} (hence det A = prod u_kk, the O(n^3) determinant). Operation count 2n^3/3 for the factorization; n^2 per triangular solve (forward Ly=Pb, back Ux=y), so extra right-hand sides are cheap. Instability of unpivoted GE (tiny pivot -> exploding multiplier, e.g. [[eps,1],[1,1]]) and the rescue by partial pivoting PA = LU forcing |l_ik| <= 1. The growth factor rho_n = max|a_ij^(k)| / max|a_ij|; the backward-error theorem tilde-L tilde-U = P(A+E), ||E|| <= c_n rho_n eps_mach ||A|| (Wilkinson), so GEPP is backward stable iff rho_n is modest. The 2^{n-1} worst case (attained by the Wilkinson matrix W_n with unit diagonal, -1 below, last column 1) vs the benign average case rho_n ~ n^{2/3} for random matrices (Trefethen-Schreiber), and why GEPP is the default despite being only conditionally stable; complete pivoting PAQ=LU with its slow-growing bound at O(n^3) comparison cost. Forward-error estimate ||tilde-x - x||/||x|| = O(kappa(A) rho_n eps_mach) by multiplying conditioning (43.01.02) against this backward error (43.01.03). Originators: Gauss (least-squares elimination), Dwyer 1940s (LU formulation), von Neumann-Goldstine 1947 (pessimistic forward analysis), Wilkinson 1961-65 (backward-error analysis and the 2^{n-1} bound), Trefethen-Schreiber 1990 (average-case smallness), Higham 2002 (componentwise modern treatment). Lean status none: Mathlib has matrices/det/triangular predicates but no constructive LU decomposition, no leading-minor existence theorem, no PA=LU pivoted form, no growth factor, and no GEPP backward-error bound.

### numerical-analysis.newton-secant-superlinear-quadratic

- **title**: Newton's Method and the Secant Method: Superlinear and Quadratic Convergence
- **unit**: `43.02.03`
- **prerequisites**: `02.05.02` (Taylor with Lagrange remainder / MVT), `02.05.04` (inverse/implicit function theorem, Jacobian nonsingularity), `02.05.05` (multivariable Taylor), `43.02.02` (fixed-point iteration / order of convergence)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.02.03`. Newton's iteration $x_{n+1}=x_n-f(x_n)/f'(x_n)$ as the tangent-line/linearisation root and as the fixed-point map $g(x)=x-f/f'$ with $g'(x^*)=0$; the local quadratic-convergence theorem at a simple root ($f'(x^*)\ne0$, $f\in C^2$) via Taylor with Lagrange remainder, $e_{n+1}\to \frac{f''(x^*)}{2f'(x^*)}e_n^2$; failure modes (multiple root $\Rightarrow$ linear with rate $1-1/m$, overshoot, basins/Julia sets); the secant method $x_{n+1}=x_n-f(x_n)(x_n-x_{n-1})/(f(x_n)-f(x_{n-1}))$ with divided-difference error recurrence $e_{n+1}\approx Ce_ne_{n-1}$ giving superlinear order $\varphi=(1+\sqrt5)/2$ (root of $p^2=p+1$); Newton for systems $\mathbf x_{k+1}=\mathbf x_k-J(\mathbf x_k)^{-1}F(\mathbf x_k)$ with the Jacobian, quadratic convergence under $J(\mathbf x^*)$ invertible (inverse-function nonsingularity, cross-ref 02.05.04) and $J$ Lipschitz; the Newton-Kantorovich semilocal/affine-invariant theorem; damped/global Newton and continuation. Distinguishes robustness (bisection 43.02.01) vs speed. Lean status none: Mathlib has taylor_mean_remainder_lagrange, the inverse/implicit function theorems, and ContractingWith but not the Newton iteration map, its quadratic-convergence theorem, the secant golden-ratio order, or the $\mathbb{R}^n$ Jacobian Newton theorem.


### numerical-analysis.hermite-cubic-spline-interpolation

- **title**: Hermite interpolation and piecewise / cubic spline interpolation
- **unit**: `43.08.03`
- **prerequisites**: `43.08.01` (polynomial interpolation, Lagrange/Newton forms, node polynomial), `43.08.02` (interpolation error theorem and the Runge phenomenon)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.08.03`. Hermite (osculatory) interpolation matching values and derivatives at repeated nodes as the confluent limit of Lagrange interpolation, the generalized divided differences f[x_k,...,x_k]=f^{(j)}(x_k)/j!, and the error f(x)-p(x)=f^{(2n+2)}(xi)/(2n+2)! prod_k (x-x_k)^2 (squared node polynomial). Piecewise-polynomial interpolation as the cure for the Runge phenomenon of 43.08.02 by lowering the local degree. Cubic splines: the C^2 piecewise cubic, the tridiagonal moment system mu_i M_{i-1}+2 M_i+lambda_i M_{i+1}=d_i with M_i=s''(x_i), strict diagonal dominance giving existence-uniqueness and an O(n) Thomas solve; natural / clamped (complete) / not-a-knot end conditions. The minimum-curvature (minimum bending energy) variational theorem: the natural cubic spline uniquely minimises int (g'')^2 among C^2 interpolants, via the first-integral orthogonality int s''(g''-s'')=0 (Holladay 1957). Sharp O(h^4) convergence with the best constant 5/384 (Hall-Meyer 1976) and graded derivative orders h^{4-j}; boundedness of the spline projector independent of the mesh (de Boor) as the structural reason splines avoid Runge. Lean status none: Mathlib has Lagrange.interpolate and Rolle/Taylor but no Hermite confluent interpolation, no cubic-spline space, no tridiagonal moment system with diagonal dominance, no minimum-bending-energy theorem, and no O(h^4) bound.


### numerical-analysis.qr-algorithm-shifts

- **title**: The QR algorithm for eigenvalues, with shifts
- **unit**: `43.06.03`
- **prerequisites**: `43.06.02` (Hessenberg/tridiagonal reduction), `43.06.01` (power/inverse/Rayleigh-quotient iteration), `01.01.09` (QR factorisation)
- **notes**: The workhorse eigenvalue algorithm of numerical LA (Francis/Kublanovskaya 1961). The unshifted QR iteration $A_k = Q_k R_k$, $A_{k+1} = R_k Q_k$ is a unitary similarity at each step, and the power identity $A^k = \hat Q_k \hat R_k$ shows it is simultaneous orthogonal power iteration, so under distinct-modulus eigenvalues $A_k$ converges to (quasi-)triangular Schur form with off-diagonal decay rate $|\lambda_i/\lambda_j|^k$ and the eigenvalues sorting onto the diagonal. Run on the Hessenberg/tridiagonal reduction (43.06.02) the band is invariant under each sweep, so a sweep is $O(n^2)$ (tridiagonal $O(n)$). The shifted iteration $A_k - \mu_k I = Q_k R_k$, $A_{k+1} = R_k Q_k + \mu_k I$ preserves the spectrum; a shifted step is one step of inverse iteration on the trailing coordinate, so the Rayleigh-quotient shift $\mu_k = (A_k)_{nn}$ and its globally-convergent sharpening, the Wilkinson shift (eigenvalue of the trailing $2\times2$ block nearest the corner), give cubic local convergence on symmetric tridiagonals, with deflation peeling off converged eigenvalues. The Francis implicit double-shift applies a complex-conjugate shift pair in real arithmetic by computing the first column of $(H-\mu I)(H-\bar\mu I)$ and bulge-chasing back to Hessenberg form, correct by the implicit-Q theorem. Backward stable, $O(\epsilon_{\mathrm{mach}}\|A\|)$. Forward link: the generalised eigenproblem $Ax=\lambda Bx$ via QZ (43.06.10). Lean status none: Mathlib has the static Schur form and QR factorisation but no QR iteration map, no power-identity / simultaneous-power-iteration lemma, no convergence-rate or shift-strategy theorems, and no bulge-chasing implicit-Q equivalence.


### numerical-analysis.newton-cotes-peano-kernel

- **title**: Newton-Cotes rules and their error via the Peano kernel
- **unit**: `43.09.01`
- **prerequisites**: `43.08.01` (polynomial interpolation, Lagrange cardinal polynomials -> the weights), `43.08.02` (interpolation error, the node polynomial -> the error engine), `02.05.02` (MVT / Taylor with integral & Lagrange remainder)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.09.01`. Interpolatory quadrature int_a^b f approx sum_i w_i f(x_i) with weights w_i = int_a^b L_i (integrals of the Lagrange cardinal polynomials), equivalently Q(f)=int_a^b p_n; degree of exactness d (>= n for interpolatory rules, n+1 for symmetric rules with even n). The closed Newton-Cotes family: trapezium h/2(f_0+f_1) error -(b-a)^3/12 f''(eta), Simpson h/3(f_0+4f_1+f_2) error -(b-a)^5/2880 f^{(4)}(eta), Simpson 3/8; open midpoint (b-a)f(m) error +(b-a)^3/24 f''(eta). The Peano kernel theorem: a continuous linear functional E annihilating Pi_{r-1} satisfies E(f)=int_a^b K_r(t) f^{(r)}(t) dt with K_r(t)=E_x[(x-t)_+^{r-1}]/(r-1)!, proved via Taylor-with-integral-remainder + Fubini; sign-definite (one-signed) kernel gives the mean-value form E(f)=f^{(r)}(eta) int K_r with error constant int K_r = E(x^r)/r!. Definiteness of the classical rules (trapezium negative-definite, midpoint positive-definite, Simpson negative-definite). Negative weights / instability of high-order Newton-Cotes: first negative weight at n=8, sum|w_i|->infinity ~ 2^n/(n log n), Polya-Steklov convergence iff sup_n sum|w_i| finite; the quadrature face of the Runge phenomenon (43.08.02). Two repairs: composite rules (43.09.02) and Gauss quadrature (43.09.03). Lean status none: Mathlib has intervalIntegral/FTC and Taylor remainders but no quadrature-rule object, no degree-of-exactness, no Newton-Cotes weights, and no Peano kernel theorem.

### numerical-analysis.minimax-chebyshev-best-approximation
- **title**: Best uniform approximation, minimax, and Chebyshev polynomials
- **unit**: `43.08.04`
- **prerequisites**: `43.08.02` (interpolation error + node polynomial + Runge), `02.11.07` (inner-product space / projection, the L^2 contrast)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.08.04`. Best uniform (minimax / infinity-norm) approximation in C[a,b]: the minimax error E_n(f)=inf_p ||f-p||_inf, existence of a best approximant by compactness on finite-dimensional Pi_n, and the Chebyshev equioscillation (alternation) theorem characterising p* by an (n+2)-point alternating error f-p*=+/- L, with uniqueness via the averaging argument. The Chebyshev polynomials T_n(cos theta)=cos(n theta), recurrence T_{n+1}=2x T_n - T_{n-1}, leading coefficient 2^{n-1}, ||T_n||=1 with extreme points cos(k pi/n) and roots cos((2j+1)pi/2n); the minimal-deviation theorem that monic 2^{1-n}T_n uniquely minimises the sup norm among monic degree-n polynomials (deviation 2^{1-n}), so the node polynomial pi_{n+1} is minimised by Chebyshev-point nodes (closing the Runge loop of 43.08.02). Master layer: Haar/Chebyshev systems and the abstract characterisation, strong uniqueness and Lipschitz dependence (nonlinear minimax operator vs linear L^2 projection of 43.08.05), de la Vallee-Poussin lower bound, near-minimax Chebyshev interpolation (within (1+Lambda_n), Lambda_n~(2/pi)log n), and the Remez exchange algorithm. Weierstrass density (Fejer form in 02.10.01) as the existence backdrop. Lean status none: Mathlib has Polynomial.Chebyshev.T with the recurrence and T_n(cos)=cos(n) identities and abstract best-approximation existence, but not E_n(f), the Haar predicate, the equioscillation theorem, uniqueness, the de la Vallee-Poussin bound, the monic minimal-deviation theorem, or the Remez algorithm.

### numerical-analysis.zero-stability-dahlquist-equivalence

- **title**: Zero-stability, the root condition, and the Dahlquist equivalence theorem
- **unit**: `43.10.03`
- **prerequisites**: `43.10.02` (linear multistep methods, the rho/sigma characteristic polynomials, consistency/order), `43.10.01` (one-step convergence theorem, discrete-Gronwall template), `01.01.08` (eigenvalues / characteristic roots), `02.03.02` (Cauchy/Bolzano-Weierstrass — boundedness criterion)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.10.03`. Zero-stability of an r-step linear multistep method as uniform boundedness of the discrete solution operator as h->0 with Nh fixed; the root condition on the first characteristic polynomial rho(zeta) (all roots in the closed unit disc, those on the unit circle simple), with the principal root zeta=1 carrying the true solution and the spurious/parasitic roots constrained. The companion-matrix reformulation: zero-stability is power-boundedness ||C^n||<=M of the companion matrix of rho, equivalent to the root condition via Jordan structure (companion matrices are non-derogatory). The Dahlquist equivalence theorem: for a consistent LMM, convergence <=> zero-stability <=> root condition, with convergence order equal to consistency order; proof via discrete variation of constants plus discrete Gronwall, and the (1)=>(3) contrapositive exhibiting an unbounded parasitic mode. The first Dahlquist barrier (order <= r+1 for odd r, r+2 for even r, the even cap attained only with all roots on the circle), with the consistent-but-unstable order-3 two-step example diverging on a smooth problem, Simpson's rule as the order-4 barrier method, strong vs weak stability, and the necessity of order-p one-step starters. Linear-multistep analogue of the Lax-Richtmyer equivalence theorem (43.11.05); forward-points absolute stability (43.10.04). Lean status none: Mathlib has polynomial roots, the companion matrix, power-boundedness, and discrete Gronwall but not the numerical zero-stability layer (the discrete solution operator, the root-condition/power-boundedness equivalence, the equivalence theorem, or the first barrier).

### numerical-analysis.least-squares-normal-equations-qr-svd-conditioning

- **title**: Least squares: normal equations vs QR vs SVD conditioning
- **unit**: `43.04.01`
- **prerequisites**: `01.01.09` (QR / Householder / modified Gram-Schmidt), `01.01.12` (SVD / pseudoinverse / normal equations), `43.01.02` (conditioning and condition numbers)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.04.01`. The three algorithms for min_x ||Ax-b||_2 and their numerical comparison (solution theory itself is proved in 01.01.09 / 01.01.12 and only cross-referenced here): normal equations A*Ax=A*b via Cholesky (cheapest, condition number kappa(A*A)=kappa(A)^2 — squares the conditioning, avoid for ill-conditioned A); Householder QR x=R^-1Q*b (backward stable, conditioning kappa(A)); SVD x=V Sigma^+ U* b (most robust, the only clean route for rank-deficient / nearly rank-deficient A and the truncated-SVD / Tikhonov filtering). The conditioning of the least-squares problem itself: the four sensitivity factors kappa(A), the residual angle theta (tan theta = ||r||/||Ax||, sec theta for the fitted-value map), and eta; the Wedin perturbation bound with its small-residual kappa term and large-residual kappa^2 tan theta term — separating the avoidable algorithmic kappa^2 of normal equations from the intrinsic kappa^2 of a large-residual problem. Cost/accuracy tradeoff and the historical replacement of normal equations by orthogonal-factorization methods (Golub 1965). Lean status none: Mathlib has the QR/SVD/pseudoinverse linear-algebra layer but no floating-point model, no kappa(A*A)=kappa(A)^2 numerical-analysis statement, no QR backward-stability theorem, and no least-squares condition number.

### numerical-analysis.perturbation-a-posteriori-error-linear-systems

- **title**: Perturbation theory and a posteriori error for linear systems
- **unit**: `43.03.03`
- **prerequisites**: `43.03.01` (Gaussian elimination / LU stability), `43.01.02` (conditioning / condition numbers), `43.01.03` (backward stability)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.03.03`. Sensitivity of Ax=b to perturbations of A and b: the normwise bound ||dx||/||x|| <= kappa(A)(||dA||/||A|| + ||db||/||b||)/(1 - kappa(A)||dA||/||A||) via the Neumann-series correction; the residual r=b-A x-tilde, the error identity e=A^{-1}r, and the a posteriori bound ||x-x-tilde||/||x|| <= kappa(A) ||r||/||b|| (sharp on both sides, so a small residual is only a small *backward* error, not a small forward error when kappa is large). Backward error: Rigal-Gaches normwise eta=||r||/(||A|| ||x-tilde|| + ||b||) as the minimal data perturbation consistent with x-tilde, Oettli-Prager componentwise omega=max_i |r_i|/(|A||x-tilde|+|b|)_i. Componentwise/Skeel conditioning cond(A,x)=|| |A^{-1}| |A| |x| ||/||x|| (row-scaling invariant, can be << kappa_inf). Condition-number estimation (Hager/LINPACK). Iterative refinement: exact correction lands on x, finite-precision contraction ratio ~kappa(A) eps_mach, limiting accuracy ~cond(A,x) eps_r, Skeel's one-step componentwise stability. Lean status none: Mathlib has matrix/operator norms and the Neumann bound but no linear-system perturbation bound, residual estimate, Rigal-Gaches/Oettli-Prager backward error, Skeel condition number, or iterative-refinement convergence theory.


### numerical-analysis.cholesky-spd-solve

- **title**: Cholesky factorization and the symmetric positive-definite solve
- **unit**: `43.03.02`
- **prerequisites**: `43.03.01` (Gaussian elimination / LU / GEPP stability), `01.01.15` (quadratic forms / positive-definiteness), `01.01.13` (spectral theorem / SPD diagonalisation)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.03.02`. The Cholesky factorization A = R^*R of a Hermitian positive-definite matrix (R upper triangular, positive diagonal), existence/uniqueness from the SPD structure via the Schur-complement recursion (each Schur complement of an SPD matrix is SPD, so every radicand stays positive) and from positivity of all leading principal minors (Sylvester); R = D^(1/2) L^* relating Cholesky to the symmetric LDL^* reduction with pivots d_k = det A_k / det A_{k-1} > 0. The n^3/3 operation count, half of general LU, by exploiting symmetry to compute one triangle. No pivoting is needed: every entry of every active submatrix is bounded by the largest diagonal (|a_ij|^2 <= a_ii a_jj) and the diagonal does not grow, so the growth factor rho_n <= 1 a priori. Hence unconditional backward stability tilde-R^* tilde-R = A + E, ||E|| <= c_n eps_mach ||A|| with no growth-factor dependence, for every SPD matrix (contrast the only conditionally stable GEPP of 43.03.01). Cholesky as a backward-stable test of positive-definiteness (first nonpositive radicand certifies indefiniteness); pivoted rank-revealing Cholesky on the positive-semidefinite boundary; the LDL^* / block Bunch-Kaufman generalisation for symmetric indefinite matrices (forward 43.03.08). Use in the normal-equations least-squares route A^*Ax = A^*b with the squared condition number kappa(A^*A) = kappa(A)^2 (43.04.01), and the shared SPD structure with conjugate gradient and incomplete-Cholesky preconditioning (43.07.04). Originator: A.-L. Cholesky (geodetic least squares, posthumous Benoit 1924); modern stability theory Wilkinson/Higham; symmetric-indefinite extension Bunch-Parlett 1971, Bunch-Kaufman 1977. Lean status none: Mathlib has PosDef/PosSemidef and the Hermitian spectral theorem but no constructive Cholesky decomposition, no Schur-complement existence recursion, no positive-diagonal uniqueness, no rho_n <= 1 entry bound, and no unconditional backward-error theorem.

### numerical-analysis.stationary-iterations-jacobi-gauss-seidel-sor

- **title**: Stationary iterative methods: Jacobi, Gauss-Seidel, SOR
- **unit**: `43.07.01`
- **prerequisites**: `01.01.08` (eigenvalue / eigenvector / spectral radius), `43.03.01` (Gaussian elimination / LU / the direct-solve baseline)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.07.01`. The splitting A = M - N with M nonsingular and cheap to solve, the stationary iteration x_{k+1} = M^{-1}(N x_k + b) with iteration matrix G = M^{-1}N, and the error recursion e_k = G^k e_0. Convergence for every x_0 iff the spectral radius rho(G) < 1 (Jordan-form / Gelfand proof), with asymptotic factor rho(G) and rate -log10 rho(G). The three classical splittings from A = D - L - U: Jacobi (M = D), Gauss-Seidel (M = D - L), and SOR (M = D/omega - L) with relaxation parameter omega. Classical convergence theorems: strict diagonal dominance => Jacobi and Gauss-Seidel converge; SPD A with M + M* - A positive-definite => convergence (Householder-John), giving SOR convergence iff 0 < omega < 2 for SPD A (Kahan necessary bound rho(G_omega) >= |omega-1| from det G_omega = (1-omega)^n). Stein-Rosenberg ranking of Jacobi vs Gauss-Seidel; consistently ordered identity rho(G_GS) = rho(G_J)^2. SOR optimal parameter omega_opt = 2/(1 + sqrt(1 - rho(G_J)^2)) with rho(G_opt) = omega_opt - 1 (Young's functional equation). For the model Poisson problem, Jacobi/Gauss-Seidel have rho = 1 - O(h^2) needing O(N^2) iterations while optimal SOR has rho = 1 - O(h) needing O(N) iterations - the order-of-magnitude speedup. Slowness as rho -> 1 with grid refinement motivates Krylov methods (43.07.02-04). Originators: Jacobi 1845, Gauss/Seidel 1874, Young 1950 (SOR optimal omega), Varga 1962 (matrix iterative analysis), Saad (modern synthesis). Lean status none: Mathlib has spectrum/spectral-radius/Gelfand but no splitting structure, no spectral-radius convergence equivalence, no diagonal-dominance or Householder-John SPD convergence theorem, and no SOR optimal-parameter derivation.

### numerical-analysis.elliptic-bvp-5-point-laplacian-convergence

- **title**: Finite differences for the elliptic BVP: the 5-point Laplacian and its convergence
- **unit**: `43.11.01`
- **prerequisites**: `02.13.02` (continuous Poisson / fundamental solution — the BVP discretised), `02.13.01` (Laplace / maximum principle — model for the discrete maximum principle), `43.03.01` (Gaussian elimination / LU — the sparse linear solve), `01.01.08` (eigenvalues — the discrete-Laplacian spectrum)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.11.01`. The 1-D second-difference operator D^2 = tridiag(-1,2,-1)/h^2 and the 2-D 5-point stencil for -Delta u = f on a square with Dirichlet data; the sparse SPD linear system A_h U = F with A_h = I⊗T + T⊗I; local truncation error O(h^2) from the -h^2/12 u'''' Taylor term; the discrete maximum principle and the M-matrix / monotonicity structure (positive diagonal, nonpositive off-diagonals, irreducible diagonal dominance ⇒ A_h^{-1} >= 0); the stability bound ||A_h^{-1}||_inf <= 1/8 via a quadratic barrier/comparison function; consistency + stability ⇒ ||U-u||_inf = O(h^2) convergence; the discrete Green's function; the explicit eigenvalues lambda_{p,q} = (4/h^2)(sin^2(p*pi*h/2)+sin^2(q*pi*h/2)) and sine-mode eigenvectors, giving kappa_2(A_h) = O(h^{-2}). The finite-DIFFERENCE method, cross-ref'd against the finite-ELEMENT/Galerkin discretisation 24.02.01 (energy-norm Céa vs maximum-norm M-matrix) and the continuous Poisson/maximum-principle theory 02.13.02/02.13.01; forward-points the parabolic method of lines 43.11.02 and the Lax equivalence theorem 43.11.05. Lean status none: Mathlib has SPD matrices, the spectral theorem, Kronecker products, and entrywise order but no finite-difference discretisation layer (no D^2/5-point construction, no M-matrix predicate or inverse-positivity theorem, no discrete maximum principle, no truncation/stability/convergence estimates, no closed-form discrete-Laplacian spectrum).

### numerical-analysis.composite-euler-maclaurin-romberg-adaptive

- **title**: Composite rules, Euler-Maclaurin, and Romberg / adaptive quadrature
- **unit**: `43.09.02`
- **prerequisites**: `43.09.01` (Newton-Cotes rules + Peano-kernel single-interval errors -> summed across a partition)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.09.02`. Composite trapezium T_N(f)=h(f_0/2+f_1+...+f_{N-1}+f_N/2) with global error -(b-a)h^2 f''(eta)/12 = O(h^2), composite Simpson O(h^4), obtained by summing the single-interval Peano errors of 43.09.01 across N equal subintervals (all weights positive). The Euler-Maclaurin summation formula: T_N(f)-int_a^b f = sum_{k=1}^m B_{2k}/(2k)! h^{2k}[f^{(2k-1)}(b)-f^{(2k-1)}(a)] + R_m with Bernoulli-number coefficients and periodic-Bernoulli-function remainder, proved by repeated integration by parts against the Bernoulli polynomials on each panel with the interior endpoint corrections telescoping; the series is asymptotic (B_{2k} ~ (2k)!), only endpoint derivatives contribute. Spectral accuracy of the trapezoidal rule for smooth periodic integrands: all endpoint corrections vanish, error = aliased Fourier tail sum_{N|n,n!=0} hat f_n, geometric decay exp(-2 pi sigma N) for analytic f (Trefethen-Weideman). Romberg integration: Richardson extrapolation of T_{2^j} along h^2, recurrence R_{j,k}=R_{j,k-1}+(R_{j,k-1}-R_{j-1,k-1})/(4^k-1), even-power structure forcing the 4^k-1 denominators; first column = composite Simpson. Adaptive quadrature: local error estimation by comparing a rule with its subdivided refinement (Simpson 1/15 factor), recursive bisection with length-apportioned tolerance. Lean status none: Mathlib has Polynomial.bernoulli, Bernoulli numbers, intervalIntegral/FTC but no composite-rule object, no Euler-Maclaurin formula, no Romberg recurrence, and no periodic spectral-accuracy statement.

### numerical-analysis.absolute-stability-regions-test-equation

- **title**: Absolute stability, stability regions, and the linear test equation
- **unit**: `43.10.04`
- **prerequisites**: `43.10.03` (zero-stability, the root condition, Dahlquist equivalence), `43.10.02` (linear multistep methods, the rho/sigma characteristic polynomials), `01.01.08` (eigenvalues / diagonalisation), `06.01.01` (holomorphic function / the complex plane)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.10.04`. The Dahlquist linear test equation y'=lambda*y and the rescaled variable z=h*lambda in C; the stability function R(z) of a one-step method (forward Euler 1+z, backward Euler 1/(1-z), trapezoidal (1+z/2)/(1-z/2), RK4 the degree-4 Taylor polynomial of e^z), and the stability polynomial pi(zeta;z)=rho(zeta)-z*sigma(zeta) for a linear multistep method. The region of absolute stability S={z:|R(z)|<=1} (or the root-condition form for LMMs); the boundary-locus method tracing the boundary as z(theta)=rho(e^{i theta})/sigma(e^{i theta}). Forward-Euler disc about -1 (interval [-2,0]), backward-Euler exterior of the disc about +1, trapezoidal exactly the left half-plane, RK4 bulb; Adams-Bashforth regions shrinking with order, BDF A(alpha)-stability and the six-step limit. Eigen-decoupling: for U'=AU diagonalisable the step is admissible iff every h*lambda_k in S, so the spectral spread (not accuracy) caps the step on a decaying/oscillatory problem; boundary locus tangent to the imaginary axis at the origin for any consistent method. Forward-points A-stability/L-stability and the Dahlquist second barrier (43.10.05); the parabolic-FD step restriction (43.11.02) applies this to the discrete Laplacian. Lean status none: Mathlib has Complex.exp and its Taylor polynomials, the unit disc, polynomial roots, the companion matrix, and diagonalisation, but not the absolute-stability layer (R(z), pi(zeta;z), the region S, the boundary-locus characterisation, or the eigen-decoupling step restriction).

### numerical-analysis.matrix-functions-matrix-exponential

- **title**: Computing matrix functions: the matrix exponential
- **unit**: `43.06.11`
- **prerequisites**: `43.06.03` (QR algorithm / Schur form), `02.06.03` (matrix exponential / linear ODE systems), `01.01.11` (Jordan canonical form)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.06.11`. The three equivalent definitions of f(A) (Jordan/Hermite-interpolating-polynomial, Cauchy contour integral of the resolvent, interpolating polynomial on the spectrum); the matrix exponential e^A as the numerically-computed object (theory of e^{At} as the ODE flow lives in 02.06.03). Why naive Taylor series (the hump/cancellation for non-normal A) and eigendecomposition (the kappa(V) blow-up toward defective A) fail. Scaling-and-squaring e^A=(e^{A/2^s})^{2^s} with a diagonal Pade approximant, Higham's backward-error analysis fixing (m,s) and the overscaling phenomenon (the modern expm). The Schur-Parlett method for general f(A): the Parlett recurrence on the triangular Schur factor, its breakdown for close eigenvalues, and the blocked/reordered Sylvester-equation form. Conditioning via the Frechet derivative L_f(A,E) and the 2n-by-2n block-triangular formula. The Moler-Van Loan 'nineteen dubious ways' survey. Lean status none: Mathlib has Matrix.exp, Schur/Jordan forms, and holomorphic functional calculus but no equivalence-of-definitions theorem, no scaling-and-squaring algorithm or backward-error bound, no Parlett recurrence, and no Frechet-derivative conditioning layer.

### numerical-analysis.gauss-quadrature-orthogonal-polynomials

- **title**: Gauss quadrature via orthogonal polynomials
- **unit**: `43.09.03`
- **prerequisites**: `43.09.01` (interpolatory quadrature / Newton-Cotes: weights as integrals of cardinal polynomials, degree of exactness, the error functional), `43.08.05` (orthogonal polynomials for a weight, the three-term recurrence, real-simple-interlacing roots, the Jacobi matrix)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.09.03`. Gaussian (Gauss-Christoffel) quadrature: choosing both the n nodes and n weights of Q(f)=sum_i w_i f(x_i) approximating int_a^b w f dx to maximise the degree of exactness; the 2n free parameters cap exactness at 2n-1. The central theorem: the rule is exact on Pi_{2n-1} iff its nodes are the n roots of the degree-n orthogonal polynomial phi_n for the weight w (proved by dividing p=q*phi_n+r with q,r in Pi_{n-1} and using (phi_n,q)_w=0), with the converse forcing the nodes via uniqueness of the monic orthogonal polynomial; no n-point rule beats 2n-1 (the integrand prod(x-x_i)^2 in Pi_{2n} witnesses it). Positivity of the Gauss/Christoffel weights w_i = int w L_i^2 > 0 (L_i^2 in Pi_{2n-2} integrated exactly), summing to int w, hence minimal operator norm and convergence for every continuous f via Polya-Steklov (contrast the divergent high-order Newton-Cotes of 43.09.01). The error term E(f) = (phi_n,phi_n)_w/(k_n^2 (2n)!) f^{(2n)}(eta) from Hermite interpolation at the doubled nodes. The classical families: Gauss-Legendre (w=1 on [-1,1]), Gauss-Chebyshev (w=(1-x^2)^{-1/2}), Gauss-Hermite (w=e^{-x^2} on R), Gauss-Laguerre (w=e^{-x} on [0,inf)); the Gauss-Radau (one fixed endpoint, exact on Pi_{2n-2}, modified weight (x-a)w) and Gauss-Lobatto (both endpoints, exact on Pi_{2n-3}, weight (x-a)(b-x)w) variants. The Golub-Welsch algorithm: nodes are eigenvalues and weights are beta_0 times squared first-eigenvector-components of the symmetric tridiagonal Jacobi matrix J_n of recurrence coefficients (cross-ref the symmetric eigenproblem 43.06.x). Lean status none: Mathlib has orthogonal projection, some classical orthogonal polynomials, the symmetric-matrix spectral theorem, and polynomial division, but no Gauss-rule object, no 2n-1 exactness theorem, no weight-positivity, no f^{(2n)} error term, and no Golub-Welsch eigenvalue characterisation; sits atop the orthogonal-polynomial gap of 43.08.05.

### numerical-analysis.bunch-kaufman-symmetric-indefinite-ldlt

- **title**: Symmetric-indefinite factorisation: the Bunch-Kaufman LDL^T algorithm
- **unit**: `43.03.08`
- **prerequisites**: `43.03.02` (Cholesky / SPD solve, the definite special case), `43.03.01` (Gaussian elimination / LU / GEPP stability)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.03.08`. For a symmetric possibly indefinite A, the block factorization P A P^T = L D L^T with L unit lower triangular and D block-diagonal of 1x1 and 2x2 pivots; the symmetric block-Schur-complement step S = B - C E^{-1} C^T preserving symmetry so one triangle does the work of two (n^3/3 flops and half storage vs unsymmetric LU). The Bunch-Kaufman partial-pivoting strategy: a one-column O(n^2) search with threshold alpha = (1+sqrt 17)/8 ~ 0.6404 choosing a 1x1 or indefinite 2x2 pivot; the 2x2 block has determinant bounded away from zero (|det E| > (1-alpha^2) lambda^2) even when both diagonal entries vanish, rescuing a step no scalar pivot could take. Bounded element growth (per eliminated row <= 1 + alpha^{-1}) hence backward stability P(A+E)P^T = L~ D~ L~^T, ||E|| <= c_n g_n eps_mach ||A|| - the symmetric analogue of GEPP (43.03.01); contrast Cholesky (43.03.02) which fails on indefinite A. Bunch-Parlett complete pivoting (O(n^3), smaller growth, bounded L); the bounded-L defect of Bunch-Kaufman and its rook-pivoting (bounded Bunch-Kaufman) repair (Ashcraft-Grimes-Lewis). Inertia (n_+, n_-, n_0) read off the pivot signs via Sylvester's law of inertia (each 2x2 block indefinite, contributing (1,1,0)) in n^3/3 ops without eigenvalues - spectrum slicing / Sturm counting and second-order optimality certificates. Application: saddle-point / KKT systems [[H B^T],[B 0]] which are symmetric indefinite by construction (inertia (m,p,0) when H pos-def and B full row rank). Originators: Bunch-Parlett 1971, Bunch-Kaufman 1977 (LAPACK routine), stability theory Higham, rook pivoting Ashcraft-Grimes-Lewis 1998. Lean status none: Mathlib has Hermitian/symmetric predicates, the spectral theorem, PosDef/PosSemidef and block/determinant infrastructure but no symmetric-indefinite (1x1/2x2-block) LDL^T decomposition, no Bunch-Kaufman pivot rule, no termination/nonsingular-2x2 proof, no inertia-from-pivot-signs identity, and no element-growth backward-error bound.


### numerical-analysis.stiffness-a-stability-dahlquist-second-barrier

- **title**: Stiff equations, A-stability, and the Dahlquist second barrier
- **unit**: `43.10.05`
- **prerequisites**: `43.10.04` (absolute stability, stability regions, the linear test equation), `43.10.03` (zero-stability, the root condition, the Dahlquist equivalence theorem)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.10.05`. Stiffness as widely separated eigenvalue time-scales (a decayed fast transient that still pins an explicit method's stable step via the bounded absolute-stability region, far below the accuracy-limited step), with the deliberate refusal of a sharp definition (operational symptom: stable step << accurate step). A-stability as the region of absolute stability containing the whole closed left half-plane {Re z <= 0}; A(alpha)-stability as containing the sector |arg(-z)| <= alpha; L-stability as A-stability plus R(infinity)=0. Backward Euler R(z)=1/(1-z) (A- and L-stable, exterior of the disc about +1), trapezoidal rule R(z)=(1+z/2)/(1-z/2) (A-stable, region exactly the left half-plane, R(infinity)=-1, A- but not L-stable, ringing on stiff transients). The second Dahlquist barrier: no A-stable linear multistep method has order > 2, the trapezoidal rule the optimal (smallest error constant) A-stable order-two method = the (1,1) diagonal Pade approximant to e^z; proof via the principal-root logarithm constraint Re(phi(iy)) >= 0 on the imaginary axis / the order-star finger count. BDF1-BDF2 A-stable, BDF3-BDF6 only A(alpha)-stable (alpha shrinking 86deg->18deg), BDF beyond six not zero-stable. Implicit Runge-Kutta evasion (the barrier is multistep-only): Gauss A-stable order 2s, Radau IIA L-stable order 2s-1 (RADAU5). No explicit method is A-stable (polynomial R blows up). Cross-ref absolute stability 43.10.04; forward-points the parabolic method of lines 43.11.02 (Crank-Nicolson trapezoidal A-stability, backward-Euler L-stability for the discrete Laplacian). Lean status none: Mathlib has Complex.exp, Pade/Taylor approximants, the left half-plane and sectors as sets, rational limits at infinity, and the maximum-modulus principle, but no A-stability / A(alpha)-stability / L-stability predicates, no order-star object, no second-Dahlquist-barrier theorem, and no Ehle A-stable-Pade characterisation.

### numerical-analysis.arnoldi-lanczos-iterations

- **title**: Arnoldi and Lanczos iterations
- **unit**: `43.07.02`
- **prerequisites**: `01.01.08` (eigenvalue / eigenvector — Ritz values), `01.01.09` (modified Gram-Schmidt / Householder / QR), `43.06.02` (Hessenberg/tridiagonal reduction — the dense analogue)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.07.02`. The Krylov subspace K_m(A,b) = span{b, Ab, ..., A^(m-1)b} = {p(A)b : deg p < m}, its strictly increasing dimensions up to the grade of b (where K_m becomes A-invariant and the process terminates). The Arnoldi iteration: modified Gram-Schmidt on the Krylov sequence producing orthonormal Q_m and the Arnoldi factorisation A Q_m = Q_{m+1} H-tilde_m = Q_m H_m + h_{m+1,m} q_{m+1} e_m^*, with H_m = Q_m^* A Q_m unreduced upper-Hessenberg (the iterative analogue of the dense Hessenberg reduction 43.06.02). The Lanczos specialisation for Hermitian A: H_m becomes tridiagonal T_m and the orthogonalisation collapses to the three-term recurrence beta_{j+1} q_{j+1} = A q_j - alpha_j q_j - beta_j q_{j-1}, giving O(n) storage and one matvec per step. Ritz values/vectors (theta_i, y_i = Q_m s_i) as the Rayleigh-Ritz approximation, the residual identity ||A y - theta y|| = |h_{m+1,m}| |e_m^* s|, exactness at breakdown, and the monotone/interlacing convergence of extremal Ritz values to lambda_min/lambda_max. Kaniel-Paige-Saad Chebyshev convergence bound governed by the relative spectral gap. Finite-precision loss of orthogonality: Paige's theorem that orthogonality is lost exactly as a Ritz value converges (ghost eigenvalues, not wrong ones), and full / selective / partial reorthogonalisation restoring semi-orthogonality O(sqrt(eps)). CG-Lanczos correspondence (T_m z = ||r_0|| e_1 via incremental LDL^*). The engine under GMRES (43.07.03) and CG (43.07.04). Originators: Krylov 1931, Arnoldi 1951, Lanczos 1950, Paige 1971, Parlett (reorthogonalisation), Saad (modern synthesis). Lean status none: Mathlib has OrthonormalBasis/gramSchmidt, spectrum/eigenvalues, and the self-adjoint predicate but no Krylov-subspace submodule, no Arnoldi factorisation, no tridiagonal Lanczos recurrence, no Ritz/Rayleigh-Ritz residual theory, and none of the finite-precision loss-of-orthogonality analysis.


### numerical-analysis.generalised-eigenproblem-qz-algorithm

- **title**: The generalised eigenvalue problem Ax=lambda Bx and the QZ algorithm
- **unit**: `43.06.10`
- **prerequisites**: `43.06.03` (the QR algorithm for eigenvalues, with shifts), `01.01.08` (eigenvalues/eigenvectors), `01.01.13` (spectral theorem), `01.01.19` (simultaneous diagonalisation of two quadratic forms / theoretical symmetric-definite pencil)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.06.10`. The matrix pencil $A-\lambda B$ and the generalised eigenproblem $Av=\lambda Bv$; regular vs singular pencils, finite and infinite eigenvalues carried as projective pairs $(\alpha:\beta)$, the generalised characteristic polynomial $\det(A-\lambda B)$ and the degree drop counting infinite eigenvalues. The generalised Schur (QZ) decomposition: unitary $Q,Z$ with $Q^H A Z$ and $Q^H B Z$ both upper-triangular, eigenvalues $t_{ii}/s_{ii}$. The QZ algorithm (Moler-Stewart 1973): reduce to Hessenberg-triangular form then apply implicit shifted QR sweeps to $HR^{-1}$ without forming the product, paired left-right rotations keeping the generalised eigenvalues at machine precision, deflation and infinite-eigenvalue extraction; backward stable $O(\epsilon_{mach}(\|A\|+\|B\|))$ and why forming $B^{-1}A$ explicitly is avoided when $B$ is ill-conditioned. Symmetric-definite case: Cholesky $B=LL^H$, $C=L^{-1}AL^{-H}$ Hermitian, generalised eigenvalues = eigenvalues of $C$ (constructive form of 01.01.19). Applications: vibration $Kv=\lambda Mv$, polynomial roots via the companion pencil, the chordal metric on eigenvalue pairs (Stewart-Sun). Generalises the standard QR algorithm 43.06.03 ($B=I$). Lean status none: Mathlib has single-matrix Schur/QR/Cholesky but no matrix pencil, no regular/singular distinction, no generalised eigenvalue pairs, no generalised Schur form, and no QZ iteration.


### numerical-analysis.updating-downdating-factorisations

- **title**: Updating and downdating matrix factorisations
- **unit**: `43.04.08`
- **prerequisites**: `43.04.01` (least squares: normal equations vs QR vs SVD conditioning), `43.03.02` (Cholesky factorization and the SPD solve)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.04.08`. Stable O(n^2) modification of a QR or Cholesky factorization under a rank-one change A + sigma v v^T, a row/column append, or a row/column delete, instead of an O(n^3) refactor from scratch. The QR factor of A is the positive-diagonal Cholesky factor of A^T A, so a row append (rank-one change to A^T A) is retriangularized by n Givens rotations chasing a single spike (3/2 n^2 flops, unconditionally backward stable because each rotation is unitary). The Cholesky rank-one update C + w w^T uses Givens rotations; the downdate C - w w^T uses hyperbolic (J-orthogonal, J = diag(1,-1)) rotations and is well-posed iff ||R^-T w||_2 < 1, with conditioning 1/sqrt(1 - ||R^-T w||_2^2) blowing up at the boundary where positive-definiteness is lost (the structural downdating instability; cosh phi unbounded as the removed row's weight approaches the whole). Mixed orthogonal/hyperbolic (Bojanczyk-Brent-Van Dooren-de Hoog) and corrected-seminormal schemes stabilize it. Applications: recursive least squares (sequential row updates, the information form), the sliding-window problem (append newest + downdate oldest), the square-root Kalman filter (propagate a Cholesky factor to guarantee a positive-definite covariance), and quasi-Newton/BFGS Hessian updating (two-rank update-then-downdate guarded by the curvature condition y^T s > 0). Originators: Gill-Golub-Murray-Saunders 1974, Potter square-root Kalman filter (Apollo), Broyden-Fletcher-Goldfarb-Shanno 1970. Lean status none: Mathlib has Givens/orthogonal maps and the QR/Cholesky static API but no factorization-updating layer - no Givens-chasing retriangularization theorem, no hyperbolic rotation as the J-orthogonal downdate, no ||R^-T w||_2 < 1 positivity-preservation criterion, and no update-vs-downdate backward-error analysis (and no floating-point model).


### numerical-analysis.gmres

- **title**: GMRES
- **unit**: `43.07.03`
- **prerequisites**: `43.07.02` (Arnoldi iteration — orthonormal Krylov basis and Hessenberg factorisation), `43.04.01` (least squares — the per-step minimisation)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.07.03`. GMRES as the Krylov method minimising the residual norm ||b - A x||_2 over x in x_0 + K_m(A, r_0) for a general nonsymmetric A. The Arnoldi-based implementation: the Arnoldi relation A Q_m = Q_{m+1} H-tilde_m turns the minimisation into the small (m+1)-by-m Hessenberg least-squares problem min_y || beta e_1 - H-tilde_m y ||, solved by Givens rotations, with the residual norm read off the last rotated component |g_{m+1}| without forming x_m. Monotone (non-increasing) residual decrease from the nesting K_m subset K_{m+1}, and finite termination at the grade of r_0 (lucky breakdown h_{m+1,m}=0 <=> exact solution; no serious breakdown, unlike two-sided Lanczos). Convergence theory via the residual-polynomial characterisation ||r_m|| = min_{p in P_m, p(0)=1} ||p(A) r_0||: the diagonalisable bound ||r_m||/||r_0|| <= kappa(V) min_p max_{lambda in sigma(A)} |p(lambda)|, the eigenvalue-clustering heuristic, and the non-normal subtlety — for highly non-normal A the spectrum does not control convergence (Greenbaum-Ptak-Strakos any-curve theorem), so the field of values W(A) (Elman bound) and pseudospectra govern instead. Restarting GMRES(k) caps storage O(kn) at the cost of stagnation. Contrast with CG (43.07.04, same wave) for the SPD case where the spectrum does determine the curve; stationary splitting M (43.07.01) returns as a preconditioner. Originators: Saad-Schultz 1986, Greenbaum-Ptak-Strakos 1996, Elman 1982, Trefethen-Embree pseudospectra. Lean status none: Mathlib has OrthonormalBasis/gramSchmidt, QR/least-squares, Givens rotations, and the spectrum but no minimal-residual Krylov solver, no GMRES iterate, no least-squares reduction or monotone/finite-termination theorems, no residual-polynomial identity, and no field-of-values / pseudospectral convergence theory.


### numerical-analysis.total-least-squares-generalised-svd

- **title**: Total least squares and the generalised SVD
- **unit**: `43.05.08`
- **prerequisites**: `43.04.01` (ordinary least squares: normal equations / QR / SVD conditioning), `01.01.12` (singular value decomposition / Eckart-Young / pseudoinverse)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.05.08`. The errors-in-variables / total-least-squares (TLS) problem min ||[E r]||_F s.t. (A+E)x=b+r, i.e. the nearest rank-deficient augmentation of [A|b] in Frobenius norm; its SVD solution from the smallest singular triple of [A|b] (x = -z/gamma from the last right singular vector v_last=(z,gamma), minimal perturbation norm sigma_{n+1}), the genericity condition sigma_n([A|b]) > sigma_{n+1} / sigma_min(A) > sigma_{n+1} for existence+uniqueness, the gamma=0 non-existence and sigma_n=sigma_{n+1} non-uniqueness caveats, and the closed form x = (A^T A - sigma_{n+1}^2 I)^{-1} A^T b (a deregularised normal-equations solve, opposite of Tikhonov's +mu^2 I, hence more noise-sensitive). The geometric orthogonal-distance-regression reading (perpendicular distances / principal-axis fit vs ordinary LS vertical residuals, 43.04.01). The generalised SVD (GSVD) of a matrix pair (A,B): simultaneous diagonalisation A=U Sigma_A X^-1, B=V Sigma_B X^-1 with U,V orthogonal, X nonsingular, alpha_i^2+beta_i^2=1, generalised singular values gamma_i=alpha_i/beta_i; the Paige-Saunders CS-decomposition construction (from an orthonormal basis of the stacked range, no rank assumption on individual blocks, no cross-products), and its diagonalisation of the symmetric-definite pencil (A^T A, B^T B) = constructive form of 01.01.19, specialising the QZ / generalised eigenproblem 43.06.10; application to constrained / weighted / Tikhonov-regularised least squares. Originators: Pearson 1901 (orthogonal-distance fit), Van Loan 1976 (GSVD), Golub-Van Loan 1980 (TLS analysis), Paige-Saunders 1981 (CS-decomposition GSVD), Van Huffel-Vandewalle 1991 (monograph). Lean status none: Mathlib has the SVD/Eckart-Young/Frobenius-norm substrate but no TLS problem statement, no smallest-singular-triple solution theorem, no genericity/non-existence characterisation, no closed form, and no GSVD of a matrix pair (neither the Van-Loan simultaneous-diagonalisation form nor the Paige-Saunders CS construction nor the pencil diagonalisation).

### numerical-analysis.conjugate-gradient-method

- **title**: The conjugate gradient method
- **unit**: `43.07.04`
- **prerequisites**: `43.07.02` (Krylov subspaces, the Lanczos three-term recurrence), `43.01.02` (conditioning and the condition number kappa)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.07.04`. CG for symmetric positive-definite A as the Krylov method minimising the A-norm (energy norm) of the error e_k = x_k - x_* over the affine subspace x_0 + K_k(A, r_0); equivalence of the energy-minimisation property, the Galerkin orthogonal-residual property r_k perp K_k, and the residual-polynomial form ||e_k||_A = min{ ||p(A) e_0||_A : p(0)=1, deg p <= k }. The A-conjugacy p_i^T A p_j = 0 of the search directions and the Euclidean orthogonality r_i^T r_j = 0 of the residuals, the coupled two-term recurrences (alpha_k = r_k^T r_k / p_k^T A p_k, beta_k = r_{k+1}^T r_{k+1} / r_k^T r_k) and the short-recurrence miracle inherited from the symmetric Lanczos tridiagonalisation 43.07.02 via an incremental LDL^* factorisation of T_k (CG-Lanczos correspondence). Finite termination in exact arithmetic in at most grade(r_0) <= n steps, sharpened to the number of distinct eigenvalues. The Chebyshev convergence bound ||e_k||_A <= 2 ((sqrt kappa - 1)/(sqrt kappa + 1))^k ||e_0||_A via the shifted-scaled Chebyshev polynomial T_k solving the min-max problem on [lambda_min, lambda_max] with p(0)=1, giving the O(sqrt kappa) iteration count versus O(kappa) for steepest descent / stationary iterations 43.07.01; clustered-spectrum superlinear convergence (annihilate outliers with polynomial roots, Chebyshev-minimise on the bulk). MINRES / conjugate-residual for symmetric-indefinite A (residual-norm minimisation via Givens QR of the Lanczos tridiagonal when the A-norm fails). Contrast with GMRES 43.07.03 (same Krylov optimality, long Arnoldi recurrence for nonsymmetric A). Originators: Hestenes-Stiefel 1952, Lanczos 1950/1952, Reid 1971 (iterative revival), Paige-Saunders 1975 (MINRES), Greenbaum / Saad (modern convergence theory). Lean status none: Mathlib has Matrix.PosDef / IsSelfAdjoint, quadratic forms, the spectral theorem, and Polynomial.Chebyshev.T but no energy inner product gated on positive-definiteness, no A-conjugate family, no Krylov A-norm optimality of the CG iterate, no finite-termination-at-the-grade theorem, no residual-polynomial reformulation connected to a matrix spectrum, and no MINRES / conjugate-residual construction.


### numerical-analysis.method-of-lines-parabolic-stability

- **title**: The method of lines and stability for parabolic problems
- **unit**: `43.11.02`
- **prerequisites**: `43.11.01` (the 5-point / second-difference spatial discretisation and the discrete-Laplacian spectrum), `43.10.05` (stiffness / A-stability — the semidiscrete system is stiff), `02.13.03` (the continuous heat equation / heat kernel / Duhamel — the problem discretised)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.11.02`. The method of lines: semidiscretise u_t = u_xx in space by the second-difference operator D^2 to get the stiff ODE system U' = A_h U with A_h = (1/h^2) tridiag(1,-2,1), then time-step with an ODE integrator. Eigenvalues mu_p = -(4/h^2) sin^2(p*pi*h/2) spread from -pi^2 (slow) to -4/h^2 (fast), giving stiffness O(h^-2). The theta-method family: forward Euler / FTCS U^(n+1)=(I+kA_h)U^n (explicit, parabolic CFL k <= h^2/2 from feeding k*mu_p into the forward-Euler stability interval [-2,0], cross-ref 43.10.04), backward Euler (I-kA_h)U^(n+1)=U^n (implicit, unconditionally/A-stable, one tridiagonal solve per step), Crank-Nicolson (trapezoidal, theta=1/2, second-order in time, unconditionally stable). Spectral stability ||r(kA_h)||_2 = max_p |r(k mu_p)| via the symmetric A_h and the sine-mode spectral theorem; A-stability of the implicit schemes, L-stability of backward Euler (r(inf)=0) vs Crank-Nicolson r(inf)=-1 (ringing on unresolved high modes, Rannacher startup). Consistency O(k+h^2) for the Euler schemes, O(k^2+h^2) for Crank-Nicolson; convergence by the discrete-Duhamel/Lax synthesis E^n = -k sum r(kA_h)^(n-1-j) tau^j with power-boundedness. Smoothing estimate ||A_h r(kA_h)^n|| <= C/t_n only for L-stable schemes. Cross-ref the discrete Laplacian spectrum 43.11.01 (same matrix, largest vs smallest eigenvalue), stiffness/A-stability 43.10.05, absolute stability 43.10.04, continuous heat 02.13.03; forward-points von Neumann/CFL 43.11.03 and Lax-Richtmyer equivalence 43.11.05. Originators: Rothe 1930 (horizontal lines), Courant-Friedrichs-Lewy 1928 (explicit step limit), Crank-Nicolson 1947, Thomee (modern stability/smoothing theory), Hairer-Wanner (stiff-ODE side). Lean status none: Mathlib has symmetric tridiagonal matrices + spectral theorem, Matrix.exp, resolvents/rational matrix functions, and the second-difference spectrum, but no method-of-lines layer (no U'=A_h U construction from the heat equation, no FTCS/backward-Euler/Crank-Nicolson one-step operators r(kA_h), no eigenvalue-based parabolic-CFL or unconditional-stability theorems, no truncation/convergence orders).

### numerical-analysis.preconditioning

- **title**: Preconditioning of Krylov methods
- **unit**: `43.07.05`
- **prerequisites**: `43.07.03` (GMRES), `43.07.04` (conjugate gradient), `43.07.01` (stationary splittings reused as preconditioners)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.07.05`. Krylov convergence is governed by the spectrum/conditioning of the operator the method runs on, so solve the transformed system M^{-1}Ax = M^{-1}b with a preconditioner M approximating A but cheap to invert. Left (M^{-1}A), right (AM^{-1}), and split (L^{-1}AL^{-*} for M=LL^*) preconditioning are algebraically equivalent; the split/symmetric form preserves SPD structure for preconditioned CG, which is ordinary CG run in the M-inner product, replacing kappa(A) by kappa(M^{-1}A) in the Chebyshev bound. Spectral-clustering goal: a degree-k residual polynomial pinned to p(0)=1 is small exactly when sigma(M^{-1}A) is clustered (ideally near 1). Classical preconditioners: Jacobi/diagonal M=diag(A), Gauss-Seidel/SSOR built on the A=D-L-U stationary splittings (43.07.01), incomplete LU/Cholesky ILU/IC (Meijerink-van der Vorst existence for symmetric M-matrices, ILU(0)/ILU(p)/ILUT fill hierarchy). Modern preconditioners at high level: multigrid as a spectrally optimal preconditioner, additive-Schwarz domain decomposition, sparse approximate inverse (SPAI) minimising ||I-MA||_F. Spectral equivalence c1(Mx,x) <= (Ax,x) <= c2(Mx,x) bounds kappa(M^{-1}A) <= c2/c1 mesh-independently, the defining property of an optimal preconditioner. Central tradeoff: preconditioner cost (construction + per-apply) versus iteration reduction. Capstone of chapter 43.07; cross-ref CG 43.07.04, GMRES 43.07.03, stationary splittings 43.07.01, conditioning 43.01.02. Lean status none: Mathlib has invertible operators, spectrum/condition number, and PosDef but no Krylov solver, no M^{-1}A preconditioned operator, no split/left/right equivalence, no PCG-in-M-inner-product, no spectral-equivalence bound, and no incomplete-factorisation/multigrid/Schwarz machinery.


### numerical-analysis.von-neumann-stability-cfl-condition

- **title**: Von Neumann stability analysis and the CFL condition
- **unit**: `43.11.03`
- **prerequisites**: `43.11.02` (explicit/implicit/Crank-Nicolson heat schemes — the constant-coefficient test cases), `02.10.01` (Fourier series / Riemann-Lebesgue — the diagonalising mode), `02.13.04` (wave equation / characteristics / domains of dependence — the CFL geometry)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.11.03`. Von Neumann (Fourier) stability analysis for constant-coefficient linear one-step difference schemes on a periodic grid: substitute the Fourier mode U^n_j = g^n e^(i xi j h), factor out the common term, read off the amplification factor (symbol) g(xi) = sum c_l e^(i xi l h); l2 stability <=> sup_xi |g(xi)| <= 1 + Ck (strict |g|<=1 when the true solution does not grow). Fourier-diagonalisation theorem: on the periodic grid the discrete Fourier transform is unitary and carries the shift to multiplication by e^(i xi h), so ||C^n||_l2 = sup_xi |g(xi)|^n, identifying von Neumann analysis with l2 Lax-Richtmyer stability. Worked symbols: FTCS heat g = 1 - 4r sin^2(xi h/2) stable iff r=k/h^2 <= 1/2 (sawtooth xi h = pi binds); backward Euler g = 1/(1+4r sin^2) and Crank-Nicolson g = (1-2r sin^2)/(1+2r sin^2) unconditionally stable. Advection u_t + a u_x = 0, Courant number nu = a k/h: upwind, Lax-Friedrichs |g|^2 = 1 - (1-nu^2) sin^2(xi h), Lax-Wendroff |g|^2 = 1 - nu^2(1-nu^2)(1-cos xi h)^2 all stable iff |nu|<=1; centred explicit |g|^2 = 1 + nu^2 sin^2(xi h) > 1 unconditionally unstable. CFL condition nu <= 1 as the necessary domain-of-dependence requirement (numerical domain of dependence must contain the analytic/characteristic one), proved by a perturbation-of-initial-data argument; CFL necessary but NOT sufficient (centred scheme is the witness: passes CFL, fails von Neumann). Systems case: amplification matrix G(xi), uniform power-boundedness, Kreiss matrix theorem (resolvent/numerical-range/Lyapunov/uniform-diagonalisability characterisation). GKS (Gustafsson-Kreiss-Sundstrom) normal-mode theory for non-periodic initial-boundary-value problems. Cross-ref method-of-lines/heat schemes 43.11.02 (same restriction by eigenvalues), elliptic 43.11.01, Fourier series 02.10.01, wave/characteristics 02.13.04; forward-points hyperbolic schemes 43.11.04 and Lax-Richtmyer equivalence 43.11.05. Originators: von Neumann (1940s Los Alamos), von Neumann-Richtmyer 1950 (artificial viscosity), Courant-Friedrichs-Lewy 1928 (CFL/domains of dependence), Richtmyer-Morton (amplification-matrix/spectral stability), Kreiss 1962 (matrix theorem), Gustafsson-Kreiss-Sundstrom 1972 (GKS). Lean status none: Mathlib has the discrete/continuous Fourier transform, Parseval/Plancherel, multiplier picture, and matrix-power/spectral-radius API, but no von Neumann layer (no amplification factor g(xi) of a finite-difference scheme, no Fourier-diagonalisation equivalence with l2 stability, no worked scheme symbols, no amplification matrix G(xi), no Kreiss matrix theorem, no domain-of-dependence CFL-necessity proof, no GKS boundary theory).

### numerical-analysis.lax-equivalence-theorem

- **title**: The Lax-Richtmyer equivalence theorem for finite-difference schemes
- **unit**: `43.11.05`
- **prerequisites**: `43.11.03` (von Neumann / Fourier stability — the computable sufficient test), `43.11.02` (method of lines / one-step PDE schemes — the source of the evolution operators C(k))
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.11.05`. Abstract Lax-Richtmyer framework: a well-posed linear evolution IVP du/dt = Au on a Banach space B with solution semigroup E(t), sup_[0,T] ||E(t)|| < infinity (well-posedness); a one-step finite-difference scheme u^(n+1) = C(k) u^n with C(k) bounded; consistency ||(C(k)-E(k))v||/k -> 0 on a dense subspace (local Taylor comparison); Lax-Richtmyer stability = uniform power-boundedness sup of ||C(k)^n|| over 0<=nk<=T, 0<k<=k0, equal to M < infinity. Lax-Richtmyer equivalence theorem: for a consistent scheme approximating a well-posed linear IVP, stability <=> convergence. Sufficiency via the telescoping identity C^n - E^n = sum_j C^(n-1-j)(C-E)E^j giving ||(C(k)^n - E(nk))v|| <= M*t*eta_k; necessity via the Banach-Steinhaus / uniform boundedness principle (a convergent, hence pointwise-bounded, operator family is uniformly bounded). Order of convergence = order of accuracy under stability. Well-posedness is indispensable (backward heat equation counterexample). Stability made verifiable: Kreiss matrix theorem (resolvent / numerical-range / Lyapunov / uniform-diagonalisability characterisation of uniform power-boundedness of the amplification matrix G(xi)); the scalar von Neumann condition sup_xi |g(xi)| <= 1+Ck is the normal-matrix special case. Explicit parallel to the Dahlquist equivalence theorem 43.10.03 (consistency + zero-stability <=> convergence, companion matrix of rho <-> abstract C(k)) and to the Lax-Milgram / Cea stability-times-approximation story for FEM 24.01.03. Cross-ref von Neumann 43.11.03 (computable engine), method of lines 43.11.02 (the schemes C(k)), elliptic 43.11.01, eigenvalues/operator powers 01.01.08, ODE analogue Dahlquist 43.10.03. Originators: Lax-Richtmyer 1956 (equivalence theorem + Banach-Steinhaus necessity), Richtmyer-Morton 1967 (abstract Banach-space treatment), Kreiss 1962 (matrix theorem), Courant-Friedrichs-Lewy 1928 (CFL geometric shadow). Lean status none: Mathlib has Banach spaces, bounded operators, C0-semigroups (Hille-Yosida), and banach_steinhaus, but no numerical-approximation layer (no consistent family C(k) vs E(t), no Lax-Richtmyer stability, no telescoping/Banach-Steinhaus equivalence proof, no Kreiss matrix theorem).

### numerical-analysis.hyperbolic-fd-upwind-lax-wendroff-diffusion-dispersion

- **title**: Hyperbolic finite differences: upwind, Lax-Friedrichs, Lax-Wendroff; numerical diffusion and dispersion
- **unit**: `43.11.04`
- **prerequisites**: `43.11.03` (von Neumann amplification factor / CFL number — the stability test and source of the scheme symbols), `43.11.02` (method of lines / local truncation error / consistency-plus-stability convergence framework), `02.13.04` (wave equation / characteristics / domains of dependence — continuous transport), `02.18.06` (scalar conservation laws / entropy solutions — the nonlinear context monotonicity serves)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.11.04`. The linear advection (transport) equation u_t + a u_x = 0 as the model hyperbolic problem, exact solution u_0(x-at) carried along characteristics x-at=const. Three schemes with Courant number nu=ak/h: first-order UPWIND U_j^(n+1)=U_j^n-nu(U_j^n-U_(j-1)^n) for a>0 (one-sided, follows the characteristic); LAX-FRIEDRICHS U_j^(n+1)=1/2(U_(j-1)+U_(j+1))-nu/2(U_(j+1)-U_(j-1)) = FTCS plus artificial viscosity 1/2 delta^2; second-order LAX-WENDROFF U_j^(n+1)=U_j^n-nu/2 delta_0 U_j+nu^2/2 delta^2 U_j (centred + curvature correction from the Cauchy-Kowalevski device u_tt=a^2 u_xx). Centred-only FTCS unconditionally unstable (cross-ref 43.11.03; CFL necessary not sufficient). All three stable iff |nu|<=1. MODIFIED-EQUATION (equivalent-PDE) analysis: upwind solves u_t+a u_x = (ah/2)(1-nu) u_xx + O(h^2), an EVEN-order DIFFUSION term (numerical viscosity, dissipative smearing, amplitude decay |g|<1); Lax-Friedrichs diffusion coeff (h^2/2k)(1-nu^2) > upwind's; Lax-Wendroff solves u_t+a u_x = -(ah^2/6)(1-nu^2) u_xxx + O(h^3), an ODD-order DISPERSION term (no amplitude loss, wavenumber-dependent phase speed, spurious trailing oscillations/wiggles). Parity of leading surviving derivative locked to accuracy order; modulus of g(xi)=diffusion, phase of g(xi)=dispersion (dual to von Neumann symbol). GODUNOV order barrier: a linear constant-coefficient monotonicity-preserving scheme (nonneg coefficients) is at most first-order, so no fixed linear scheme is both second-order and oscillation-free (variance-of-displacements argument forcing positive u_xx); resolution is nonlinear high-resolution/TVD/flux-limiter schemes (finite-volume theory). At nu=1 every scheme is exact one-cell transport. Cross-ref von Neumann/CFL 43.11.03, method-of-lines 43.11.02, continuous wave/characteristics 02.13.04, conservation laws/entropy 02.18.06; forward-points Lax-Richtmyer equivalence 43.11.05. Originators: Courant-Friedrichs-Lewy 1928 (CFL/upwind domain of dependence), Lax 1954 (Lax-Friedrichs), Lax-Wendroff 1960, Warming-Hyett 1974 (modified-equation method), Godunov 1959 (order barrier). Lean status none: Mathlib has Taylor expansion, Fourier transform/Parseval, smooth partial derivatives, polynomial algebra, but no hyperbolic finite-difference layer (no upwind/Lax-Friedrichs/Lax-Wendroff operators, no order theorems, no modified-equation construction, no diffusion/dispersion parity classification, no Godunov barrier).

### numerical-analysis.sylvester-lyapunov-bartels-stewart

- **title**: Sylvester and Lyapunov matrix equations: the Bartels-Stewart algorithm
- **unit**: `43.06.12`
- **prerequisites**: `43.06.03` (real Schur form via the QR algorithm), `01.01.08` (eigenvalues/eigenvectors/characteristic polynomial), `02.06.03` (systems of linear ODEs / matrix exponential), `02.12.08` (Lyapunov stability, direct method)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.06.12`. The Sylvester equation $AX+XB=C$ as a linear equation in a matrix unknown; the Sylvester operator $\mathcal S\colon X\mapsto AX+XB$ and its Kronecker/vec form $(I_n\otimes A + B^{\mathsf T}\otimes I_m)\operatorname{vec}X=\operatorname{vec}C$. Solvability: unique solution for every $C$ iff $\sigma(A)\cap\sigma(-B)=\varnothing$, because $\sigma(\mathcal S)=\{\lambda+\mu:\lambda\in\sigma(A),\mu\in\sigma(B)\}$ (operator-spectrum proposition proven in 43.06.11, cross-referenced not reproved). The Bartels-Stewart algorithm (1972): reduce $A,B$ to real Schur form by the QR algorithm 43.06.03, transform $C\mapsto U^{\mathsf T}CV$, solve the quasi-triangular $R_A Y + Y R_B=\tilde C$ column-by-column by back-substitution over $1\times1$/$2\times2$ blocks, transform back; $\mathcal O(m^3+n^3)$, backward stable, the standard dense solver. Conditioning by the separation $\operatorname{sep}(A,-B)=\sigma_{\min}$ of the Kronecker matrix (not the eigenvalue gap, which differs for non-normal data). Lyapunov equation $A^{\mathsf T}X+XA=-Q$ (symmetric special case $B=A^{\mathsf T}$): Hurwitz stability iff every SPD $Q$ yields a unique SPD solution $X=\int_0^\infty e^{A^{\mathsf T}t}Qe^{At}\,dt$ (controllability/observability Gramian), tying the solve to the analytic Lyapunov direct method 02.12.08 and the matrix exponential 02.06.03. Hammarling 1982 variant computes the Cholesky factor of $X$ directly. Applications: control stability certificates, balanced-truncation model reduction. Originators: Sylvester (operator spectrum), Lyapunov 1892 (direct method), Bartels-Stewart 1972, Golub-Nash-Van Loan 1979 (Hessenberg-Schur), Hammarling 1982. Lean status none: Mathlib has Kronecker products, vec-adjacent reshaping, Schur triangulation, matrix exponential and PosDef, but no linear-matrix-equation layer (no Sylvester operator, no Kronecker-form invertibility theorem, no Bartels-Stewart back-substitution, no sep conditioning, no Lyapunov/Gramian/Hurwitz equivalence, no Hammarling factor).

### numerical-analysis.two-point-boundary-value-problems

- **title**: Finite-difference methods for two-point boundary-value problems
- **unit**: `43.10.06`
- **prerequisites**: `43.10.01` (one-step ODE integrators — the IVP march inside shooting), `43.02.03` (Newton/secant root-finding — the shooting root-solve and the nonlinear-BVP Newton step), `43.03.01` (Gaussian elimination/LU — the tridiagonal Thomas solve)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `43.10.06`. The 1-D two-point BVP -u''+q(x)u=f (and variable-coefficient -(kappa u')'+qu=f) on [a,b] with Dirichlet/Neumann/Robin data. Two solution families: the SHOOTING method (recast as an IVP with unknown initial slope s, integrate with a 43.10.01 one-step method, Newton/secant on the residual phi(s)=u(b;s)-beta, exact in one step for linear BVPs by superposition) and the FINITE-DIFFERENCE method (centred second difference D^2 = tridiag(-1,2,-1)/h^2 giving the symmetric tridiagonal M-matrix system A_h U=F solved by Thomas/LU 43.03.01). Local truncation error O(h^2) from the h^2/12 u'''' Taylor term; the 1-D discrete maximum principle and monotonicity (A_h^{-1}>=0); the stability bound ||A_h^{-1}||_inf <= (b-a)^2/8 via the quadratic barrier phi=(x-a)(b-x)/2; consistency+stability implies ||U-u||_inf=O(h^2). Variable-coefficient conservative discretisation with midpoint kappa at j+/-1/2; ghost-point handling of Neumann/Robin BCs and the half-order pitfall of the naive one-sided difference; nonlinear BVPs -u''=g(x,u,u') by Newton on the discrete system with tridiagonal Jacobian. The genuinely 1-D ODE-BVP precursor of the 2-D elliptic 5-point Laplacian 43.11.01 (which uses tridiag(-1,2,-1) only as the Kronecker factor T); cross-refs the IVP integrators 43.10.01 and the continuous linear ODE theory 02.06.02. Lean status none: Mathlib has the continuous IVP theory and SPD/M-matrix linear-algebra pieces but no two-point-BVP numerical layer (no D^2 tridiagonal construction, no 1-D M-matrix/discrete-maximum-principle theorem, no truncation/stability/convergence estimates, no shooting reformulation, no ghost-point Neumann/Robin treatment).


### optimization-control.convex-sets-functions-operations

- **title**: Convex sets, convex functions, and convexity-preserving operations
- **unit**: `44.01.01`
- **prerequisites**: `01.01.13` (spectral theorem / PSD matrices), `01.01.15` (bilinear and quadratic forms), `01.01.18` (hyperplanes and affine subspaces), `02.05.05` (multivariable Taylor and extrema)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.01.01`. Convex sets in R^n (segment-containment definition, convex combinations), hyperplanes/halfspaces/polyhedra, convex hull (Caratheodory), cones and convex cones, dual cone and recession cone. Convexity-preserving set operations: intersection, affine image/preimage, Minkowski sum. Convex functions via the defining chord inequality and the epigraph characterization (epi f convex iff f convex); first-order (supporting-line) and second-order (PSD Hessian) conditions; sublevel sets; Jensen's inequality. Key examples: norms, log-sum-exp, PSD quadratic forms. Quasiconvex (convex sublevel sets, strictly weaker) and log-convex/log-concave functions (closed under products and sums via Holder). Convexity-preserving function operations: nonnegative weighted sums, pointwise supremum, affine precomposition, perspective t*f(x/t), partial minimization (infimal projection), and the monotone-convex composition / vector-composition (disciplined convex programming) rules. Supporting-hyperplane theorem and the support function as the outer dual description; the self-dual PSD cone. The modeling foundation for the whole optimization-control section. Lean status none: Mathlib has scalar ConvexOn/convexHull/one-variable second-derivative test but no R^n PSD-Hessian characterization, no perspective lemma, no partial-minimization preservation, no packaged quasiconvex/log-convex API, and no disciplined-convex-modeling atom library.


### optimization-control.caratheodory-relative-interior

- **title**: Carathéodory's Theorem and the Relative Interior of Convex Sets
- **unit**: `44.01.02`
- **prerequisites**: `01.01.03` (vector space — the ambient finite-dimensional carrier), `01.01.08` (eigenvalue/eigenvector — the linear-algebra/rank anchor for affine-hull dimension), `01.01.18` (linear manifolds, hyperplanes and affine subspaces — the affine-hull machinery)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.01.02`. The finite-dimensional structure theorems of convex analysis (Rockafellar §6, §17). Carathéodory's theorem: every point of conv(S) in R^n is a convex combination of at most n+1 points of S (affine version), proved by removing a point along a linear dependency among the differences; the conic version gives at most n linearly independent points with non-negative coefficients. The affine hull aff(C) and the dimension dim C = dim(aff C); the relative interior ri(C) as the interior relative to aff(C), coinciding with int(C) only in the full-dimensional case. Non-emptiness of ri(C) for non-empty convex C (build a dim-C simplex from affinely independent points; its barycentre is relatively interior) and dim(ri C) = dim C. The line-segment principle (x in ri C, y in cl C implies the half-open segment lies in ri C), the accessibility lemma, and the ri/closure calculus: cl(ri C) = cl C and ri(cl C) = ri C. Operator rules ri(AC) = A(ri C), ri(C+D) = ri C + ri D, and ri(C ∩ D) = ri C ∩ ri D when ri C ∩ ri D ≠ ∅. Supporting-hyperplane characterisation of ri (no proper supporting hyperplane / bidirectional perturbation). Why relative interior (not interior) is the right notion for constraint qualifications and duality: equality constraints make feasible sets flat so int is empty; the CQ ri(dom f) ∩ ri(dom g) ≠ ∅ underwrites Fenchel/Lagrangian strong duality. Fenchel-Bunt sharpening (n points for sets with ≤ n components) and recession-cone interplay ri(0+C) = 0+(ri C). Originators: Carathéodory 1907 (coefficient regions of power series), Steinitz 1913 (relative interior/accessibility), Rockafellar 1970 (systematic account), Fenchel 1951 / Fenchel-Bunt. Lean status none: Mathlib has convexHull and a finrank+1 Carathéodory bound but no relativeInterior operator paired with affineSpan, no line-segment principle, no ri non-emptiness theorem, no ri(cl C)=ri C / cl(ri C)=cl C identities, and no constraint-qualification layer.

### optimization-control.finite-horizon-dp-principle-of-optimality

- **title**: Finite-Horizon Dynamic Programming and the Principle of Optimality
- **unit**: `44.08.01`
- **prerequisites**: `37.01.01` (probability spaces / expectation), `37.05.01` (Markov property / transition matrices — the controlled-Markov disturbance model)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.08.01`. The dynamic-programming method for finite-horizon sequential decision problems (Bertsekas Vol. 1 Ch. 1). Discrete-time controlled system x_{k+1}=f_k(x_k,u_k,w_k) with additive cost g_N(x_N)+sum g_k(x_k,u_k,w_k), the basic problem under perfect state information, admissible Markov policies pi=(mu_0,...,mu_{N-1}). Bellman's principle of optimality. The DP algorithm: backward recursion J_N=g_N, J_k(x)=inf_u E[g_k(x,u,w)+J_{k+1}(f_k(x,u,w))] computing cost-to-go functions and a greedy optimal policy. Optimality theorem proved by downward induction (lower bound J_{pi,k}>=J_k for all policies; attainment J_{pi*,k}=J_k for the greedy policy), giving J*=J_0. Deterministic and stochastic (expected-cost) versions; deterministic finite-state case equals shortest path on a layered acyclic graph and the converse reduction; cost-to-arrive/cost-to-go forward-backward decomposition. State augmentation for control delays and correlated disturbances restoring the controlled-Markov structure. Measurable-selection (Kuratowski-Ryll-Nardzewski) refinement for uncountable spaces. Scalar linear-quadratic example yielding the discrete Riccati recursion. Foundation for infinite-horizon theory (44.08.02) and MDPs (44.08.03); continuous-time dual is the Pontryagin maximum principle (44.07.01), the co-state being the value-function gradient. Originators: Bellman (1957), Bertsekas (modern stochastic treatment), Puterman (MDP measurable-selection). Lean status none: Mathlib has probability kernels, Ionescu-Tulcea trajectories, and ciInf machinery but no finite-horizon DP layer (no controlled-system datum, no cost-to-go backward recursion, no policy-cost functional, no optimality/principle-of-optimality theorem, no measurable-selection attainment).


### optimization-control.farkas-lemma-theorems-of-alternative
