- **title**: Farkas' Lemma and the Theorems of the Alternative
- **unit**: `44.02.04`
- **prerequisites**: `02.11.02` (geometric Hahn-Banach / separating-hyperplane theorem - the separation engine), `01.01.03` (vector space), `01.01.05` (linear transformation / rank-nullity - the column-space picture), `01.01.06` (systems of linear equations / Kronecker-Capelli solvability), `01.01.18` (hyperplanes and affine subspaces)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.02.04`. Farkas' lemma as the foundational theorem of the alternative for finite linear systems over R^n: exactly one of (F1) Ax=b, x>=0 (b in the finitely generated cone cone(A)) or (F2) A^T y <= 0, <b,y> > 0 (a separating direction) holds. Proof route: the new content is finite-dimensional closedness of cone(A) = {Ax : x>=0}, established by Caratheodory reduction (every conic point uses a linearly independent subset of columns, so cone(A) is a finite union of closed images of independent subsets); then the second geometric Hahn-Banach separation theorem 02.11.02 strictly separates the closed convex cone from the external point b, and cone-scaling t*a_j in cone(A) pushes the separating functional to A^T y <= 0. Geometric reading: a vector is in the finitely generated cone OR is strictly separated from it. Corollary family of theorems of the alternative: Gordan (Ax=0,x>=0,x!=0 vs A^T y>0), Stiemke (Ax=0,x>0 vs A^T y>=0,!=0), Motzkin transposition theorem (Bx>0,Cx>=0,Dx=0 vs the dual multiplier system) - all reductions to Farkas via slacks/normalisation. Sharper forms: fundamental theorem of linear inequalities (basis-supported separating hyperplane, rational certificate), conic Caratheodory bound (<= m independent columns), Minkowski-Weyl equivalence (finitely generated <=> polyhedral via bipolar K=K^{circ circ}), Helly (number m+1). Applications previewed: LP weak/strong duality (Farkas on the augmented value-<-gamma system) and KKT multiplier existence under a linear constraint qualification (objective gradient as nonneg combination of active constraint gradients). The linear-algebraic backbone of constrained-optimization duality; conic/SDP generalisation needs a Slater/closedness hypothesis while the polyhedral case is unconditional because finite generation forces closedness. Originators: Gordan 1873, Farkas 1902 (virtual work), Stiemke 1915, Motzkin 1936; systematised by Rockafellar 1970 (sec 19,22) and Schrijver 1986. Lean status none: Mathlib has separating-hyperplane infrastructure but no general Farkas lemma, no closedness theorem for finitely generated cones, no Gordan/Stiemke/Motzkin alternatives, and no Minkowski-Weyl FG<=>polyhedral equivalence or LP-duality bridge.


### optimization-control.lqr-riccati-equations

- **title**: The Linear-Quadratic Regulator: Differential and Algebraic Riccati Equations
- **unit**: `44.07.02`
- **prerequisites**: `02.06.03` (linear ODE systems / matrix exponential — the plant dynamics and cost-flow), `43.06.12` (Sylvester/Lyapunov matrix equations, Bartels-Stewart — the closed-loop Lyapunov solve each Newton-Kleinman step performs), `01.01.13` (spectral theorem / PSD principal axes — the Loewner-cone structure of Q, R, P)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.07.02`. The linear-quadratic regulator. Finite-horizon continuous-time LQR: quadratic cost J = x(t_f)^T S x(t_f) + int (x^T Q x + u^T R u) dt over the linear plant dx/dt = Ax + Bu, derived by dynamic programming / the HJB equation with the quadratic value-function ansatz V(t,x)=x^T P(t) x; the matrix Riccati differential equation -dP/dt = A^T P + P A - P B R^{-1} B^T P + Q, P(t_f)=S, and the optimal time-varying feedback u = -K(t)x with K=R^{-1}B^T P. Infinite-horizon problem and the continuous-time algebraic Riccati equation (CARE) A^T P + P A - P B R^{-1} B^T P + Q = 0; existence/uniqueness of the stabilising maximal PSD solution under (A,B) stabilisable and (A,Q^{1/2}) detectable; closed-loop A_cl = A - B R^{-1} B^T P Hurwitz, via the closed-loop Lyapunov identity A_cl^T P + P A_cl = -(Q + K^T R K) tying LQR to the Lyapunov direct method (02.12.08) and the Lyapunov-equation solver (43.06.12). Discrete-time LQR and the DARE with symplectic pencil. Solving the ARE via the Hamiltonian matrix H = [[A, -BR^{-1}B^T],[-Q,-A^T]] and its stable invariant subspace (Laub's Schur method, cross-ref real Schur 43.06); Hamiltonian eigenvalue symmetry about the imaginary axis; Newton-Kleinman iteration as repeated Lyapunov solves. Forward links: Pontryagin maximum principle (44.07.01), HJB/dynamic programming (44.08), and the dual Kalman filter (estimation Riccati). Originators: Riccati (1720s scalar equation), Bellman 1957 (dynamic programming/HJB), Kalman 1960 (state-space optimal control), Willems 1971, Kleinman 1968, Laub 1979 (Schur solver). Lean status none: Mathlib has matrix exponential, PosDef/PosSemidef, spectral theorem, and (via 43.06.12) the Lyapunov layer, but no optimal-control or Riccati theory (no RDE flow on the symmetric cone, no HJB value-function statement, no CARE/DARE, no stabilisability/detectability predicates, no Hamiltonian stable-invariant-subspace construction, no closed-loop-stability theorem).


### optimization-control.finite-differences-automatic-differentiation

- **title**: Derivative Computation: Finite Differences and Automatic Differentiation
- **unit**: `44.03.08`
- **prerequisites**: `02.05.05` (multivariable Taylor / extrema), `43.01.01` (floating-point arithmetic, IEEE model, unit roundoff)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.03.08`. Computing gradients and Hessians for optimization. FINITE DIFFERENCES: forward $D_h^+f=(f(x+he_i)-f(x))/h$ (truncation $O(h)$) and central $D_h^0=(f(x+he_i)-f(x-he_i))/(2h)$ (truncation $O(h^2)$); the truncation-vs-roundoff error decomposition $|\widetilde D_h^+f-f'|\le\tfrac12 M_2 h + 2\delta/h$ with $\delta=u|f|$, minimised at optimal step $h^\star\sim\sqrt u$ (forward) / $u^{1/3}$ (central) and accuracy floor $\sqrt u$ / $u^{2/3}$; Hessian-vector products by differencing the gradient $\nabla^2 f\,v\approx(\nabla f(x+hv)-\nabla f(x))/h$. AUTOMATIC DIFFERENTIATION on the evaluation procedure (Wengert list / computational graph): FORWARD MODE (dual numbers $v+\dot v\varepsilon$, $\varepsilon^2=0$; Jacobian-vector products $J\dot x$, n sweeps for a gradient) and REVERSE MODE (adjoint / backpropagation, vector-Jacobian products $J^\top w$, full gradient of a scalar at cost a small constant times TIME(f), independent of n -- the cheap-gradient principle). Memory cost of the tape and Revolve/binomial checkpointing (binom(s+t,s) >= L; O(log L) memory for O(log L) time). Contrast with symbolic differentiation (expression swell). Why reverse-mode AD is foundational to large-scale optimization and ML training (backprop = reverse mode at a scalar loss); cross-ref the AI/ML survey 25.09.01 (theorem-level cost/accuracy treatment, not a duplicate). Lean status none: Mathlib has fderiv/iteratedFDeriv, Taylor remainder, and dual numbers as TrivSqZeroExt, but no finite-difference error-decomposition theorem, no Wengert-list/forward-functor/reverse-adjoint layer, no cheap-gradient complexity bound, no checkpointing tradeoff.


### optimization-control.recession-cones-polyhedral-convexity

- **title**: Recession Cones and Polyhedral Convexity (Minkowski-Weyl)
- **unit**: `44.01.03`
- **prerequisites**: `44.01.01` (convex sets, convex functions, convexity-preserving operations), `44.01.02` (Caratheodory's theorem and the relative interior — supplies the conic Caratheodory bound and relative-interior calculus), `01.01.18` (linear manifolds, hyperplanes and affine subspaces — the halfspace/affine-rank machinery)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.01.03`. Recession (asymptotic) cones of closed convex sets and polyhedral convexity. RECESSION CONE 0^+C = {d : x+td in C for all x in C, t>=0}: a closed convex cone; single-basepoint test valid for closed C; for a polyhedron P={x:Ax<=b}, 0^+P={d:Ad<=0}. BOUNDEDNESS criterion: closed convex C bounded iff 0^+C={0}. RECESSION FUNCTION f0^+ (epigraph = recession cone of epi f), coercivity (f0^+)(d)>0 for d!=0, and the EXISTENCE-OF-MINIMISERS theorem: a proper closed convex coercive f attains its inf on a non-empty compact convex minimiser set (all level sets share one recession cone). POLYHEDRAL CONVEXITY: polyhedron = finite halfspace intersection; polytope = bounded polyhedron; polyhedral cone {x:Ax<=0}; finitely generated set conv(points)+cone(directions). MINKOWSKI-WEYL theorem: polyhedral <=> finitely generated, proved by homogenisation to a cone, closedness of finitely generated cones (conic Caratheodory), bipolar/polarity (H-description of P = V-description of polar cone), and Fourier-Motzkin elimination for Weyl's direction. FACES/VERTICES/EXTREME POINTS: faces F_I = active-constraint sets, the finite face lattice, vertices = 0-dim faces (rank-n active normals), facets, minimal faces = lineality translates. MINKOWSKI's extreme-point theorem C=conv(ext C) for compact convex C (induction on dimension via supporting hyperplanes). MOTZKIN decomposition P = conv(vertices) + 0^+P and the lineality split C=(C cap L^perp)+L. Polyhedral functions (epigraph polyhedral = max of finitely many affines + polyhedron indicator) closed under sum/max/inf-convolution/conjugacy => self-duality of LP. WHY IT MATTERS: recession cone certifies existence of optima (no improving escape ray); Minkowski-Weyl + vertex attainment is the structural backbone of linear programming, LP duality, the simplex method, and Farkas/theorems-of-the-alternative (44.02.04). Originators: Minkowski 1896 (extreme points/rays), Weyl 1934 (halfspace=>finitely generated), Fourier 1827 / Motzkin 1936 (elimination, polytope+cone decomposition), systematised by Rockafellar 1970 (secs 8, 17-19, 27) and Schrijver 1986 (secs 7-8). Lean status none: Mathlib has the convex-cone hierarchy but no general recessionCone operator, no IsBounded<=>recessionCone={0} theorem, no recession-function/coercivity attainment calculus, no Minkowski-Weyl equivalence in either direction (no finitely-generated<=>polyhedral, no Fourier-Motzkin elimination), no Polyhedron.vertices/minimal-face decomposition, and no Minkowski extreme-point theorem C=conv(ext C).


### optimization-control.subdifferential-calculus

- **title**: Subdifferential Calculus: Sum Rule, Chain Rule, and Danskin's Theorem
- **unit**: `44.02.05`
- **prerequisites**: `44.01.01` (convex sets and functions, epigraph, supporting hyperplanes), `44.01.02` (Caratheodory and the relative interior - the qualification conditions), `37.07.03` (Legendre-Fenchel transform / convex conjugate - Fenchel-Young equality), `02.11.02` (geometric Hahn-Banach / separating-hyperplane theorem - the separation engine)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.02.05`. The subdifferential of a proper convex function (Rockafellar sec 23-25): subgradient inequality f(y)>=f(x)+<g,y-x>, the subdifferential partial-f(x) as the closed convex set of subgradients (supporting hyperplanes to the epigraph). Nonemptiness, convexity, and compactness of partial-f(x) on relint(dom f); the one-sided directional derivative f'(x;d) and the support-function formula f'(x;d)=max over g in partial-f(x) of <g,d>. The Moreau-Rockafellar sum rule partial(f+g)=partial-f+partial-g under the relative-interior qualification relint(dom f) meets relint(dom g) (with the always-true inclusion direction), the affine chain rule partial(f composed A)=A^* partial-f(Ax), and the max-rule / Danskin's theorem partial(max_i f_i)(x)=conv of union over active i of partial-f_i(x), with the differentiable-unique-maximiser corollary (differentiate through the max). Fermat's rule 0 in partial-f(x^*) characterises a global minimiser (no local/global gap, kink or not); constrained form via the normal cone N_C=partial of indicator, giving 0 in partial-f+N_C and the KKT system. The conjugacy inversion g in partial-f(x) iff x in partial-f^*(g) (partial-f^* = (partial-f)^{-1}), tying the calculus to the Fenchel conjugate of 37.07.03; differentiability iff partial-f singleton; partial-f maximal monotone with the proximal map as resolvent (Rademacher/Alexandrov a.e. differentiability). The engine for subgradient/proximal methods (ch 06) and KKT multiplier existence (44.02.04). Originators: Moreau and Rockafellar (early 1960s), Danskin 1966 (max-min). Lean status none: Mathlib has ConvexOn, fderiv, and lsc but no convex-analytic subdifferential set object, no nonemptiness/compactness-on-relint theorem, no directional-derivative support-function identity, no Moreau-Rockafellar sum rule, no affine chain rule, no Danskin max-rule, no Fermat 0-in-subdiff characterisation, and no conjugacy inversion partial-f^*=(partial-f)^{-1}.


### optimization-control.lagrangian-duality-slater

- **title**: Lagrangian Duality, Weak and Strong Duality, and Slater's Condition
- **unit**: `44.02.01`
- **prerequisites**: `44.01.01` (convex sets/functions, epigraph and supporting-hyperplane calculus), `02.11.02` (geometric Hahn-Banach / separating-hyperplane theorem)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.02.01`. The Lagrangian dual of a constrained program (Boyd ch 5). The Lagrangian L(x,lambda,nu)=f0+sum lambda_i f_i + sum nu_j h_j; the dual function g(lambda,nu)=inf_x L as a pointwise infimum of affine functions, hence concave and upper-semicontinuous regardless of primal convexity; the dual problem d*=sup_{lambda>=0,nu} g, a concave maximization. Weak duality d*<=p* (unconditional) and the duality gap p*-d*. Strong duality for convex problems under Slater's constraint qualification (strict feasibility of nonlinear inequalities, interior feasible point), proved by separating the point (0,p*) from the convex value/perturbation set A={(u,t): exists x, f_i(x)<=u_i, f0(x)<=t} via the finite-dimensional geometric Hahn-Banach theorem 02.11.02; Slater excludes the vertical-supporting-hyperplane (beta=0) case. Geometric reading: the dual is the search for the best non-vertical supporting hyperplane to A at (0,p*); the multiplier lambda* is the supporting slope, equal to the marginal sensitivity -grad p(0). Saddle-point characterization of L over R^n x (R^m_+ x R^p) and the max-min inequality sup inf L <= inf sup L (= p*); saddle point <=> optimal primal-dual pair with zero gap (von Neumann/Sion minimax). Perturbation/conjugate-duality picture: g = -p*-conjugate, weak duality = Fenchel-Young, strong duality = biconjugation p**(0)=p(0) (cross-ref 37.07.03). Bertsekas min-common/max-crossing framework separating dual finiteness, attainment, and zero gap. Examples: LP dual, equality-constrained QP dual, min-norm problem dual featuring the dual norm. Constraint qualifications beyond Slater: unconditional polyhedral/affine case via Farkas 44.02.04, refined Slater, LICQ/MFCQ/Abadie hierarchy. Sets up KKT 44.02.02 and Fenchel duality 44.02.03. Originators: Lagrange 1788 (equality multipliers), von Neumann 1928 (minimax), Karush 1939 / Kuhn-Tucker 1951 (inequality conditions), Slater 1950 (interior CQ), Fenchel 1949 and Rockafellar 1970 (conjugate duality), Bertsekas 2009 (MC/MC). Lean status none: Mathlib has convexity scaffolding and geometric Hahn-Banach separation but no Lagrangian/dual-function object, no weak-duality inequality, no perturbation value function with convexity-under-convex-data, no Slater strong-duality theorem, and no saddle-point/max-min-inequality equivalence.

### optimization-control.infinite-horizon-discounted-dp

- **title**: Infinite-Horizon Discounted Dynamic Programming: Bellman Contraction, Value and Policy Iteration
- **unit**: `44.08.02`
- **prerequisites**: `44.08.01` (finite-horizon DP / principle of optimality — the backward recursion iterated to a fixed point), `02.01.05` (metric space / Banach fixed-point theorem — the completeness + contraction substrate)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.08.02`. Infinite-horizon discounted-cost dynamic programming (Bertsekas Vol. 2 Ch. 1). Stationary controlled system x_{k+1}=f(x_k,u_k,w_k) with bounded stage cost g and discount factor alpha in (0,1); total discounted cost J_pi=lim E[sum alpha^k g], optimal cost J*=inf_pi J_pi attained within stationary policies. The Bellman operator T and policy operator T_mu on the Banach space B(S) of bounded functions under the sup-norm; both monotone and sup-norm contractions of modulus alpha. Discounted Bellman equation J*=TJ*; by the Banach fixed-point theorem (02.01.05) J* exists, is unique, and value iteration J_{k+1}=TJ_k converges geometrically (||J_k-J*||_inf <= alpha^k ||J_0-J*||_inf), with a-posteriori bound alpha/(1-alpha)||J_k-J_{k-1}||. Greedy-optimality criterion: a stationary policy is optimal iff greedy w.r.t. J* (T_mu J*=TJ*). Policy iteration (exact policy evaluation J_mu=T_mu J_mu + greedy improvement) gives monotone improvement J_{mu_{k+1}} <= J_{mu_k} and terminates at an optimal policy in finitely many steps for finite MDPs. Approximate value iteration error bounds (effective horizon 1/(1-alpha)); linear-programming formulation via J <= TJ => J <= J*. Average-cost criterion and the vanishing-discount limit J*_alpha = g*/(1-alpha) + h + o(1) (gain g*, bias h). Sets up MDPs (44.08.03); continuous-time/HJB counterpart (44.07.02); finite-horizon precursor (44.08.01). Originators: Bellman (1950s), Howard 1960 (policy iteration), Blackwell 1965 (rigorous discounted DP, stationary optimality), Bertsekas (operator treatment), Puterman (MDP / average-cost). Lean status none: Mathlib has the Banach fixed-point theorem, BoundedContinuousFunction sup-norm space, and probability kernels but no Bellman/policy operator on B(S), no monotonicity or alpha-contraction lemma, no J*=TJ* identification, no greedy-optimality equivalence, and no policy-iteration finite-termination theorem.

### optimization-control.line-search-wolfe-conditions

- **title**: Line-Search Methods: Wolfe Conditions and Global Convergence
- **unit**: `44.03.01`
- **prerequisites**: `44.01.01` (convex sets and convex functions), `02.05.05` (multivariable Taylor / extrema)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.03.01`. The line-search framework for unconstrained minimization (Nocedal-Wright ch. 3). Generic iteration x_{k+1}=x_k+alpha_k p_k with a descent direction (g_k^T p_k<0) and a step length alpha_k. The Armijo sufficient-decrease condition f(x_k+alpha p_k)<=f_k+c_1 alpha g_k^T p_k; the curvature condition g(x_k+alpha p_k)^T p_k>=c_2 g_k^T p_k with 0<c_1<c_2<1; the weak and strong Wolfe conditions; the Goldstein two-sided bracket. Existence of a Wolfe step-length interval (C^1 function bounded below along a descent ray) via the mean value theorem; backtracking line search needing only Armijo and its finite termination. Zoutendijk's theorem sum_k cos^2(theta_k) ||g_k||^2 < infinity under a Lipschitz gradient and Wolfe steps; global convergence ||grad f_k||->0 to stationary points under the angle condition cos(theta_k)>=delta>0. Linear convergence of steepest descent on a strongly convex quadratic with the condition-number factor ((kappa-1)/(kappa+1))^2 via Kantorovich's inequality. The curvature condition forces s_k^T y_k>0, the secant positivity that keeps BFGS positive-definite. Sets up Newton (44.03.03), quasi-Newton/BFGS (44.03.04), trust-region (44.03.05), and nonlinear CG (44.03.06). Lean status none: Mathlib has fderiv/gradient, the mean value inequality, Taylor remainder, LipschitzWith, and ConvexOn, but no descent/Armijo/Wolfe/Goldstein predicates on the 1-D restriction, no Wolfe-existence or backtracking-termination lemma, no Zoutendijk summability, no angle-condition convergence, and no steepest-descent Kantorovich rate theorem.

### optimization-control.trust-region-cauchy-dogleg

- **title**: Trust-Region Methods: The Cauchy Point and Dogleg Step
- **unit**: `44.03.02`
- **prerequisites**: `44.01.01` (convex sets/functions, PSD-Hessian and quadratic forms), `02.05.05` (multivariable Taylor / extrema)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.03.02`. Trust-region methods for unconstrained minimization (Nocedal-Wright ch 4). The quadratic model $m_k(p)=f_k+g_k^\top p+\tfrac12 p^\top B_k p$ on the ball $\|p\|\le\Delta_k$; the trust-region subproblem $\min_{\|p\|\le\Delta}m_k(p)$; the ratio test $\rho_k=\mathrm{ared}/\mathrm{pred}$ governing radius update (shrink/expand/accept). The Cauchy point $p^C=-\tau(\Delta/\|g\|)g$ and the sufficient-reduction bound $m(0)-m(p^C)\ge\tfrac12\|g\|\min(\Delta,\|g\|/\|B\|)$ that drives global convergence. The dogleg method (path from unconstrained Cauchy step $p^U$ to Newton step $p^B=-B^{-1}g$, $B\succ0$) and two-dimensional subspace minimisation over $\mathrm{span}\{g,B^{-1}g\}$. The exact subproblem solution: global characterisation $(B+\lambda I)p^*=-g$ with $\lambda\ge0$, $B+\lambda I\succeq0$, complementarity $\lambda(\Delta-\|p^*\|)=0$; the secular equation and the hard case ($g\perp$ leftmost eigenspace, $\lambda_1<0$, eigenvector correction); Moré-Sorensen safeguarded Newton. Global convergence to stationary points under bounded model Hessians and a fraction-of-Cauchy-decrease condition; contrast with line search (caps step length, then chooses direction; absorbs indefiniteness via $\lambda$). Steihaug-Toint truncated CG for large-scale subproblems (monotone-norm CG iterates, negative-curvature/boundary truncation, first iterate is the Cauchy point), cross-ref 43.07.04. Lean status none: Mathlib has fderiv/iteratedFDeriv, Taylor remainder, Matrix.PosDef/PosSemidef, and the spectral theorem, but no trust-region subproblem, no Cauchy point or sufficient-reduction lemma, no $(B+\lambda I)p=-g$ characterisation or secular-equation convexity, no dogleg/Steihaug-Toint, and no fraction-of-Cauchy-decrease convergence theorem.


### optimization-control.fenchel-duality-conjugate-modeling

- **title**: Fenchel Duality and the Conjugate as a Modeling Tool
- **unit**: `44.02.03`
- **prerequisites**: `44.02.01` (Lagrangian duality, weak/strong duality, Slater), `37.07.03` (Legendre-Fenchel transform, conjugate, biconjugation)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.02.03`. Fenchel's duality theorem inf_x [f(x)+g(Ax)] = sup_y [-f*(A^T y) - g*(-y)] for proper closed convex f,g (Rockafellar sec 31, Boyd sec 3.3/5). Weak duality unconditional via two Fenchel-Young inequalities; strong duality and dual attainment under the relative-interior constraint qualification (exists x-hat in relint(dom f) with A x-hat in relint(dom g)), proved through the perturbation/value function p(u)=inf_x[f(x)+g(Ax+u)]: p convex, the qualification puts 0 in relint(dom p), so p is subdifferentiable at 0, -y* in partial-p(0), and p*(-y)=f*(A^T y)+g*(-y) identifies the dual. Relation to Lagrangian duality (44.02.01): splitting z=Ax with a multiplier on z=Ax turns the Lagrange dual function into the conjugate-pair form; Slater becomes the relative-interior qualification. Conjugate calculus as a modeling algebra: separability (sum of f_i conjugates coordinatewise), scaling/affine precomposition, the sum / infimal-convolution exchange (f1 box f2)*=f1*+f2* (unconditional) and (f1+f2)*=f1* box f2* under domain overlap (Rockafellar sec 16), the support function sigma_C as conjugate of the indicator iota_C (iota_C*=sigma_C, sigma_C*=iota_C), the dual norm as conjugate of a norm ((||.||)*=indicator of dual unit ball), the self-dual quadratic (1/2)||.||_2^2, and the Moreau envelope/Moreau decomposition prox_{gamma f}+gamma prox_{f*/gamma}=Id linking primal and dual proximal steps (cross-ref 44.02.05 conjugacy inversion partial-f*=(partial-f)^{-1}). Worked duals: LASSO (l1 penalty -> l_infty dual constraint), Tikhonov/ridge (concave QP dual, primal recovery x*=A^T y*/gamma), log-sum-exp / negative-entropy-on-the-simplex conjugate pair (exponential family core). Rockafellar conjugate-duality framework (sec 31): bivariate F(x,u), value function p(u)=inf_x F(x,u), Lagrangian K(x,y)=inf_u(F+<y,u>); Fenchel, Lagrangian, and conic duality are specializations; the gap is p(0)-p**(0). Polyhedral special case: strong duality and attainment with no relative-interior hypothesis (cross-ref Farkas 44.02.04). Feeds proximal/splitting methods (ch 06). Originators: Fenchel 1949/1953 (conjugate, duality theorem), Moreau early 1960s (infimal convolution, envelope, proximal decomposition), Rockafellar 1970 (conjugate-duality unification), Boyd-Vandenberghe (engineering conjugate calculus), Bauschke-Combettes (Hilbert-space gap-vs-attainment). Lean status none: Mathlib has convexity scaffolding and geometric Hahn-Banach but no general LinearMap-paired Fenchel conjugate, no Fenchel-Young/Fenchel-Moreau, no support-function/dual-norm/infimal-convolution conjugate identities, no (f box g)*=f*+g* sum-exchange, no Fenchel duality theorem with the relative-interior qualification, and no Rockafellar perturbation-function conjugate-duality framework.

### optimization-control.kkt-conditions-constraint-qualifications

- **title**: Karush-Kuhn-Tucker Conditions: Constraint Qualifications and Second-Order Conditions
- **unit**: `44.02.02`
- **prerequisites**: `44.02.01` (Lagrangian duality / strong duality / saddle-point characterization), `44.02.04` (Farkas' lemma / theorems of the alternative — the multiplier-existence engine), `44.01.01` (convex sets/functions and supporting hyperplanes), `02.05.05` (multivariable Taylor / extrema)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.02.02`. The KKT optimality conditions for nonlinear programming (Boyd ch 5, Nocedal-Wright ch 12). KKT point: stationarity grad_x L = grad f0 + sum lambda_i grad f_i + sum nu_j grad h_j = 0, primal feasibility, dual feasibility lambda >= 0, complementary slackness lambda_i f_i(x*) = 0; the active set A(x*). Convex case: under Slater (44.02.01) KKT is necessary AND sufficient for a global minimizer, proved from strong duality / the saddle-point characterization (stationarity = x* minimizes the Lagrangian; sufficiency from convexity of L for lambda >= 0). General nonconvex case: the tangent cone T_Omega(x*) and the linearized feasible cone F(x*), with T subset F always; first-order necessary conditions need a constraint qualification forcing T = F. Constraint qualifications: LICQ (linear independence of active gradients => unique multiplier), MFCQ (a Mangasarian-Fromovitz strict feasible direction => bounded compact multiplier polytope, Gauvin), Slater (the global convex CQ), linear-constraints CQ, Abadie (T = F, weakest standard), Guignard (polars equal, genuinely weakest); hierarchy LICQ => MFCQ => Abadie => KKT-necessary. Counterexamples without a CQ (the cusp: minimizer that is not a KKT point because the linearized cone exceeds the tangent cone). Multiplier existence via Farkas' lemma (44.02.04) applied to the objective gradient against active-constraint gradients. Fritz John form (lambda_0 grad f0 + ... = 0 with (lambda_0, lambda, nu) != 0) and a CQ as exactly what excludes the degenerate lambda_0 = 0. Second-order theory on the critical cone C(x*): second-order necessary d^T grad^2_xx L d >= 0 on C, second-order sufficient d^T grad^2_xx L d > 0 on C\{0} => strict local minimizer with quadratic growth; curvature read off the Lagrangian Hessian, not the objective; strict complementarity collapses C to a lineality space and the test to reduced-Hessian Z^T grad^2_xx L Z positive definiteness. Sensitivity: multipliers as value-function gradients -dp*/db_i; IFT on the KKT system has nonsingular Jacobian iff the second-order sufficient condition holds (the SQP / interior-point convergence hypothesis). Examples: closest-point-to-hyperplane, water-filling threshold x_i = max(0, mu - alpha_i), the saddle-point KKT matrix [[P, A^T],[A, 0]] for equality-constrained QP. Sets up constrained Newton (44.03.03) and SQP (44.04.01). Originators: Karush 1939 (unpublished thesis), Kuhn-Tucker 1951, Fritz John 1948, Mangasarian-Fromovitz 1967 (MFCQ), Gauvin (multiplier compactness), Rockafellar (value-function subgradient view), Nocedal-Wright (second-order critical-cone form). Lean status none: Mathlib has fderiv/gradient/Taylor-remainder and ConvexOn plus geometric Hahn-Banach but no KKT predicate, no tangent/linearized-feasible-cone objects with the T subset F inclusion, no LICQ/MFCQ constraint qualifications, no Farkas-based multiplier-existence theorem, no LICQ-uniqueness lemma, and no second-order critical-cone conditions.

### optimization-control.subgradient-methods

- **title**: Subgradient Methods for Nonsmooth Convex Optimization
- **unit**: `44.06.01`
- **prerequisites**: `44.01.01` (convex sets and convex functions), `44.02.05` (subdifferential and the subgradient inequality)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.06.01`. The subgradient method for minimizing a nonsmooth convex function (Beck ch. 8 / Nesterov sec 3.2 / Boyd EE364b). Iteration x_{k+1}=x_k - alpha_k g_k with g_k in partial-f(x_k) (cross-ref 44.02.05). NOT a descent method (the value can increase), so the convergence object is the best-so-far value f_best^k = min_{i<=k} f(x_i). Step-size rules: constant, constant step length, diminishing nonsummable (alpha_k->0, sum alpha_k=infinity), square-summable-nonsummable (iterate convergence), and Polyak's optimal step alpha_k=(f(x_k)-f*)/||g_k||^2 when f* is known. The fundamental inequality ||x_{k+1}-x*||^2 <= ||x_k-x*||^2 - 2 alpha_k (f(x_k)-f*) + alpha_k^2 ||g_k||^2 (from nonexpansiveness of projection + subgradient inequality), the best-iterate bound f_best^k - f* <= (R^2 + G^2 sum alpha_i^2)/(2 sum alpha_i), and the O(1/sqrt(k)) rate for alpha_i = R/(G sqrt(k+1)) under an L-Lipschitz (bounded-subgradient) assumption. Projected subgradient x_{k+1}=P_C(x_k - alpha_k g_k) for constrained problems; fixed points are constrained Fermat points 0 in partial-f+N_C. The Omega(1/sqrt(k)) lower bound for nonsmooth first-order methods (Nemirovski-Yudin resisting-oracle / Nesterov worst-function f=max_i x_i + (mu/2)||x||^2), proving the rate is optimal for the black-box nonsmooth class. Stochastic and incremental variants retain the rate in expectation. Sets up proximal-gradient (44.06.02) and accelerated methods (44.06.03), which beat the lower bound by exploiting smooth structure. Originators: Shor, Polyak, Ermoliev (1960s-70s), Nemirovski-Yudin 1983 (lower bound). Lean status none: Mathlib has ConvexOn, inner-product spaces, and metric projection but no convex-analytic subdifferential set object (inherited from 44.02.05), no subgradient iterate, no fundamental inequality, no best-so-far value, no step-rule convergence theorems, no Polyak step, and no Omega(1/sqrt(k)) information-based lower bound.

### optimization-control.newton-method-optimization

- **title**: Newton's Method for Optimization: Unconstrained and Equality-Constrained
- **unit**: `44.03.03`
- **prerequisites**: `44.03.01` (line search / Wolfe conditions — the globalisation that damps the Newton step), `01.01.13` (spectral theorem / symmetric positive-definite operators — the eigenvalue modification and the strong-minimiser condition), `02.05.05` (multivariable Taylor / extrema — the second-order model and the integral-remainder error analysis)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.03.03`. Newton's method for minimisation (Nocedal-Wright ch. 3,6; Boyd-Vandenberghe ch. 9-10). Distinct from Newton root-finding (43.02.03): here the Newton step solves the linear system $\nabla^2 f\,p=-\nabla f$ (Hessian as Jacobian of the stationarity map $\nabla f=0$). The second-order model $m_x(p)=f+g^\top p+\tfrac12 p^\top Hp$ and its minimiser $p^{\mathrm{nt}}=-H^{-1}g$; the Newton decrement $\lambda(x)^2=g^\top H^{-1}g=-g^\top p^{\mathrm{nt}}$ with $f-\min m_x=\tfrac12\lambda^2$ as the affine-invariant stopping test. Descent property $g^\top p^{\mathrm{nt}}=-\lambda^2$ under $H\succ0$. Local quadratic convergence at a strong minimiser ($\nabla^2 f(x^*)\succ0$, Lipschitz Hessian): $\|e_{k+1}\|\le\gamma L\|e_k\|^2$. Affine invariance of step and decrement. Globalisation: damped/guarded Newton with backtracking line search (cross-ref 44.03.01), two-phase convergence (damped phase + quadratic phase). Modified Newton when $H$ not PD: modified Cholesky (Gill-Murray-Wright) and eigenvalue/spectral flooring $B=Q\max(\Lambda,\delta I)Q^\top$, giving a descent direction with bounded angle. Equality-constrained Newton (Boyd ch. 10): KKT system $[[H,A^\top],[A,0]]$, nonsingular when $A$ full row rank and reduced Hessian $Z^\top HZ\succ0$; feasible-start step equals reduced step $-Z(Z^\top HZ)^{-1}Z^\top g$ and preserves feasibility; infeasible-start variant drives the full primal-dual residual to zero. Self-concordance $|f'''|\le 2(f'')^{3/2}$ as the affine-invariant, constant-free route to interior-point complexity. Sets up quasi-Newton/BFGS (44.03.04), trust region (44.03.05), interior-point (44.04.04, 44.05.04). Lean status none: Mathlib has fderiv/iteratedFDeriv, Taylor remainder, gradient, Hessian bilinear form, PosDef, and the spectral theorem, but no Newton step as solution of $\nabla^2 f\,p=-\nabla f$, no descent/decrement identity, no local quadratic-convergence theorem for minimisation, no modified-Cholesky/eigenvalue-flooring surrogate, no KKT-matrix nonsingularity from null-space positive-definiteness, no feasible/infeasible-start convergence, and no self-concordance complexity bound.


### optimization-control.pontryagin-maximum-principle

- **title**: Pontryagin's Maximum Principle for Constrained Optimal Control
- **unit**: `44.07.01`
- **prerequisites**: `09.02.02` (Euler-Lagrange equations — the unconstrained interior special case the principle generalises), `02.18.04` (direct method of the calculus of variations — existence of minimisers the necessary conditions then characterise)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.07.01`. Pontryagin's maximum (minimum) principle for continuous-time optimal control. The Bolza problem min phi(x(T)) + int L(x,u,t) dt s.t. dx/dt = f(x,u,t), x(t0)=x0, u(t) in U (a constraint set). The control Hamiltonian H = L + p^T f, the costate/adjoint ODE dp/dt = -dH/dx with transversality conditions p(T) = (d phi/dx)^T (free endpoint), terminal-manifold transversality, and the free-time condition H(T)=0. The defining pointwise condition u*(t) = argmin_{u in U} H — minimisation over the constraint set, NOT interior stationarity dH/du=0 — which is why the principle generalises Euler-Lagrange (09.02.02) to bounded controls and produces bang-bang solutions (forward 44.07.03). Constancy of H for autonomous problems (the optimal-control image of energy conservation). Normal/abnormal multiplier dichotomy (H = p0 L + p^T f, p0<=0, nontriviality of (p0,p)). Needle/spike-variation proof: spike control on a vanishing interval, the variational (state-transition) equation, and the costate identity collapsing downstream cost sensitivity to p(tau)^T xi. Sufficiency counterpart is the HJB / dynamic-programming value function with p = dV/dx (forward 44.07.04). Linear-quadratic instance: state-costate Hamiltonian system with matrix H_mat = [[A, -BR^{-1}B^T],[-Q,-A^T]], ansatz p = Px gives the Riccati equation (44.07.02). Originators: Pontryagin-Boltyanskii-Gamkrelidze-Mishchenko 1956-1962 (needle-variation proof, Steklov Institute), Bellman 1957 (HJB sufficiency), Clarke (nonsmooth/abnormal generalisation). Lean status none: Mathlib has ODE existence/uniqueness, Frechet derivatives, and finite-dim convexity but no optimal-control theory — no controlled system with measurable controls in a constraint set, no control Hamiltonian, no adjoint equation, no pointwise minimisation condition, no transversality/constancy results, and no needle-variation machinery or normal/abnormal multiplier dichotomy.
### optimization-control.markov-decision-processes

- **title**: Markov Decision Processes and Stochastic Dynamic Programming
- **unit**: `44.08.03`
- **prerequisites**: `44.08.02` (infinite-horizon discounted DP / abstract Bellman contraction this unit specializes), `37.05.01` (Markov chains / transition matrices — the controlled-Markov kernel P(s'|s,a))
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.08.03`. Markov decision processes as the stochastic-control formalism (Puterman 1994; Bertsekas Vol. 2). The MDP tuple (S, A, {A(s)}, P, r, alpha): states, admissible actions, transition kernel P(.|s,a), bounded reward r, discount alpha in [0,1). Policy classes nested SD <= MD <= MR <= HR (stationary-deterministic, Markov-deterministic, Markov-randomized, history-dependent-randomized). Value function V^pi, action-value Q^mu(s,a)=r+alpha sum P V^mu. Bellman expectation equation V^mu=T_mu V^mu and Bellman optimality equation V*=TV* with optimality operator (TV)(s)=sup_a {r(s,a)+alpha sum_{s'} P(s'|s,a) V(s')}. Specializes the abstract alpha-contraction of 44.08.02 to the MDP operator (does not re-prove it): T monotone sup-norm contraction of modulus alpha on B(S), unique fixed point V*, geometric value iteration. New stochastic-control content: attainment of the supremum + measurable selection (Kuratowski-Ryll-Nardzewski / Berge) giving an optimal stationary deterministic policy over the whole HR class; greedy-optimality criterion T_mu V*=TV* iff mu optimal. Solution methods: value iteration, policy iteration (evaluation V^mu=(I-alpha P_mu)^{-1} r_mu + greedy improvement, Newton step, finite termination for finite MDPs), Q-iteration via the contraction H on B(SxA), and the linear-programming formulation (primal min sum beta V s.t. V>=TV; dual over discounted state-action occupation measures, cross-ref 44.04.01). Bridge to reinforcement learning: TD learning and Q-learning as Robbins-Monro stochastic approximations whose mean fields are the Bellman operators T_mu and H; effective horizon 1/(1-alpha) sets the sample-complexity scale. Originators: Bellman (1950s), Howard 1960 (policy iteration, MDP vocabulary), Blackwell 1965 (rigorous discounted DP, stationary optimality), Bertsekas (operator treatment / approximate DP), Puterman (comprehensive discrete stochastic theory), Watkins-Dayan 1992 (Q-learning convergence). Lean status none: Mathlib has the Banach fixed-point theorem, BoundedContinuousFunction sup-norm space, and probability kernels, but no MDP tuple structure, no policy/optimality operators on B(S), no monotonicity/alpha-contraction lemmas for them, no V*=TV* identification, no measurable-selection attainment of an optimal stationary deterministic policy (no Kuratowski-Ryll-Nardzewski / Berge selection over kernels), and no policy-iteration finite-termination theorem.

### optimization-control.nonlinear-conjugate-gradient

Nonlinear conjugate gradient methods (Fletcher-Reeves, Polak-Ribiere/PR+, Hestenes-Stiefel, Dai-Yuan) for unconstrained minimization: the direction recurrence and beta_k family, strong-Wolfe descent guarantees, restarts, global convergence, n-step quadratic termination, and the low-memory alternative to L-BFGS.

### optimization-control.quasi-newton-bfgs-dfp-sr1

- **title**: Quasi-Newton Methods: BFGS, DFP, and SR1
- **unit**: `44.03.04`
- **prerequisites**: `44.03.03` (Newton's method — the exact-Hessian solve quasi-Newton approximates), `44.03.01` (line search / Wolfe conditions — the curvature condition giving s^T y > 0), `01.01.13` (spectral theorem / symmetric positive-definite operators — square root and eigenvalue analysis)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.03.04`. Quasi-Newton methods for unconstrained minimisation (Nocedal-Wright ch 6). Replace the exact Hessian solve grad^2 f p = -grad f of Newton (44.03.03) by a secant-updated approximation B_k ~ grad^2 f (or its inverse H_k). The secant equation B_{k+1} s_k = y_k with s_k = x_{k+1}-x_k, y_k = grad f_{k+1}-grad f_k (average-Hessian matching by Taylor 02.05.05); the curvature condition s_k^T y_k > 0 required for a positive-definite secant update and guaranteed by the Wolfe line search (44.03.01). The DFP and BFGS rank-two updates and their inverse-Hessian forms via Sherman-Morrison-Woodbury (O(n^2)/step, no linear solve); BFGS and DFP dual under B<->H, s<->y. Hereditary positive-definiteness of BFGS (B_k > 0 and s^T y > 0 => B_{k+1} > 0, Cauchy-Schwarz + spectral square root 01.01.13). The symmetric rank-one (SR1) update, its handling of indefiniteness (suits trust regions 44.03.02), and the skipping safeguard. The Broyden class B^phi = (1-phi)BFGS + phi DFP. Least-change weighted-Frobenius variational characterisation. Local q-superlinear convergence and the Dennis-More condition lim ||(B_k - grad^2 f(x*))s_k||/||s_k|| = 0 as its exact characterisation. Self-correcting property of BFGS (trace/det identities, Powell function tr B - ln det B) and global convergence with Wolfe line search on convex objectives. SR1 converges to the true Hessian under uniform linear independence. Broyden-class conjugate-direction property: quadratic termination in <= n steps, BFGS = linear CG on a quadratic, motivating L-BFGS (44.03.05) and the link to nonlinear CG (44.03.06). Originators: Davidon 1959 (variable metric), Fletcher-Powell 1963 (DFP), Broyden-Fletcher-Goldfarb-Shanno 1970 (BFGS), Dennis-More 1977 (theory), Conn-Gould-Toint 1991 (SR1 trust-region), Nocedal 1980 (L-BFGS). Lean status none: Mathlib has fderiv/gradient, inner-product spaces, PosDef, rank-one operators, the spectral theorem, Taylor remainder, and LipschitzWith, but no secant equation, no DFP/BFGS/SR1 update operators, no Sherman-Morrison-Woodbury inverse-update lemma in this form, no hereditary positive-definiteness theorem, no Dennis-More superlinear characterisation, no bounded-deterioration/self-correcting analysis, and no global-convergence theorem for BFGS with a Wolfe line search.

### optimization-control.active-set-quadratic-programming

- **title**: Active-Set Methods for Quadratic Programming
- **unit**: `44.04.01`
- **prerequisites**: `44.02.02` (KKT conditions, active set, complementary slackness, second-order critical-cone conditions), `44.03.03` (constrained Newton / KKT saddle-point system, null-space and range-space solves, reduced Hessian, inertia)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.04.01`. Active-set methods for quadratic programming (Nocedal-Wright ch. 16; Fletcher ch. 10). The QP min (1/2) x^T G x + c^T x s.t. linear equality/inequality constraints; convex (G PSD), strictly convex (G PD), nonconvex (G indefinite, NP-hard). Equality-constrained QP (EQP) and its KKT saddle-point system [[G, A^T],[A, 0]]; the inertia condition In(K)=(n,m,0) iff A full row rank and reduced Hessian Z^T G Z PD (unique minimizer). Null-space solve p=-Z(Z^T G Z)^{-1} Z^T g and range-space (Schur-complement) solve as two factorizations of the same K^{-1}; symmetric-indefinite LDL^T and inertia-controlling factorizations. Primal active-set method: working set W_k of linearly independent active constraints, the EQP step p_k each iteration, the step length alpha_k = min(1, ratio test) and the blocking constraint added when alpha_k<1, the multiplier-sign drop test (lambda_i<0 => release constraint i, a strict descent move) and the all-nonnegative-multiplier optimality certificate. Finite termination for strictly convex QP via strict objective decrease on every nonzero step plus a working set determining a unique EQP minimizer; degeneracy/cycling and anti-cycling (Bland smallest-index, lexicographic perturbation). Convex KKT-point=global-minimizer via 44.02.02; nonconvex needs the second-order critical-cone test. QP as the inner subproblem of SQP (44.04.03); interior-point QP (44.04.04) as the alternative inner solver sharing the same KKT system; simplex as the G=0 linear specialization. Originators: Dantzig (simplex, 1947), Frank-Wolfe 1956, Wolfe 1959 (simplex method for QP), Lemke 1965 (complementary pivoting), Gill-Murray-Wright (stable factorization updates), Fletcher (working-set/SQP treatment), Bland 1977 (anti-cycling). Lean status none: Mathlib has symmetric/PosDef matrices, the spectral theorem, block matrices, and kernels/ranges but no QP structure, no EQP KKT saddle-point solve, no inertia-condition nonsingularity theorem, no null-space/range-space equivalence, no working-set/step-length/multiplier-drop machinery, and no finite-termination-with-anti-cycling theorem.


### optimization-control.lp-duality-simplex-method

- **title**: Linear-Programming Duality and the Simplex Method
- **unit**: `44.05.02`
- **prerequisites**: `44.02.04` (Farkas' lemma / theorems of the alternative - the strong-duality engine), `44.01.03` (recession cones and polyhedral convexity - vertices, Minkowski-Weyl, vertex-attainment)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.05.02`. Linear programming over R^n (Bertsimas-Tsitsiklis / Boyd). Standard form min <c,x> s.t. Ax=b, x>=0 and canonical inequality form min <c,x> s.t. Ax>=b, interconverted by slacks and free-variable splitting. Basic feasible solutions (choose m independent columns B, x_B = A_B^{-1}b >= 0, x_N=0) are exactly the vertices/extreme points of the feasible polyhedron P={Ax=b,x>=0} (cross-ref 44.01.03): a feasible x is a vertex iff its positive-support columns are independent. The dual LP max <b,y> s.t. A^T y <= c (y free for an equality primal); dual slack z = c - A^T y >= 0 is the reduced-cost / multiplier vector. Weak duality <b,y> <= <c,x> by the one-line pairing; strong duality p*=d* proved via the affine Farkas alternative 44.02.04 applied to the infeasible value-<-gamma system (the lambda=0 case is excluded by primal feasibility, then rescale lambda to 1); complementary slackness x_j (c-A^T y)_j = 0 for all j characterises a primal-dual optimal pair. The simplex method: pivot between adjacent vertices along edges, entering variable with negative reduced cost, ratio test theta = min_{d_i>0} (x_B)_i/d_i selects the leaving variable; optimality test bar c >= 0 (with y = A_B^{-T}c_B dual-feasible). Degeneracy and cycling; Bland's smallest-index rule and lexicographic perturbation give finite termination. Two-phase (minimise sum of artificials; feasible iff phase-I optimum is zero) and big-M initialisation. Klee-Minty cube: 2^n-vertex exponential worst case for Dantzig's rule, vs practical O(m)-O(m log n) pivots explained by Spielman-Teng smoothed analysis; motivates the polynomial-time interior-point alternative 44.05.04 (central path through the relative interior). The dual simplex (maintain dual feasibility, restore primal feasibility) for warm-start re-optimisation after RHS perturbation or cutting planes. Sensitivity analysis: optimal value is piecewise-linear convex in b with subgradient y (shadow prices), basis-stability ranges from A_B^{-1}b >= 0. Goldman-Tucker strict complementarity and the optimal partition. LP is the non-negative-orthant special case of conic programming 44.05.01; strong duality is unconditional precisely because the orthant is a finitely generated (hence closed) cone, while general cones need a Slater hypothesis. Originators: Dantzig 1947/1963 (simplex, duality), von Neumann 1947 (duality<->minimax), Koopmans/Kantorovich (shadow prices, 1975 Nobel), Klee-Minty 1972, Bland 1977, Khachiyan 1979 (ellipsoid, LP in P), Karmarkar 1984 (interior point); Hirsch-conjecture diameter disproved by Santos 2011. Lean status none: Mathlib has no LP theory (no standard-form structure, no BFS<->vertex theorem, no weak/strong duality, no complementary slackness, no simplex pivot / reduced costs / Bland anti-cycling, no dual simplex, two-phase, or sensitivity ranges).

### optimization-control.conic-optimization-lp-socp-sdp

Conic optimization unifying LP, SOCP, and SDP (Boyd & Vandenberghe ch. 4; Ben-Tal & Nemirovski). Generalized inequalities w.r.t. a proper cone K, the standard conic program min cᵀx s.t. Ax=b, x ∈_K 0, over the three self-dual canonical cones (nonnegative orthant, second-order/Lorentz cone, PSD cone) with the strict inclusion LP ⊂ SOCP ⊂ SDP. Conic duality via the dual cone K*, weak/strong duality and the conic Slater condition, the Schur complement as an SDP-modeling primitive, eigenvalue optimization and SDP relaxations (max-cut, Lovász theta), robust LP as SOCP. Sets up interior-point methods for conic programs.


### optimization-control.bang-bang-minimum-time-control

- **title**: Bang-Bang and Minimum-Time Optimal Control
- **unit**: `44.07.03`
- **prerequisites**: `44.07.01` (Pontryagin's maximum principle — the pointwise Hamiltonian minimisation this specialises), `44.07.02` (LQR and the Hamiltonian/costate machinery — the convex counterpart with smooth interior feedback), `02.06.03` (linear ODE systems / matrix exponential — the plant dynamics, the adjoint flow e^{-A^T t}, and the Kalman controllability/normality condition)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.07.03`. Minimum-time and bang-bang control as the canonical consequence of Pontryagin's maximum principle (44.07.01) when the Hamiltonian is linear in a bounded control. The minimum-time problem min T for linear systems dx/dt = Ax + Bu with control cube |u_i| <= 1; Hamiltonian H = 1 + p^T(Ax + Bu), adjoint dp/dt = -A^T p, and the switching function sigma(t) = B^T p(t) (the costate-projected control coefficient). The bang-bang principle: minimising the linear functional sigma^T u over the cube sends the control to a vertex, u_i* = -sign(sigma_i), switching when sigma_i changes sign. Number-of-switches bound: at most n-1 switches per component when A has only real eigenvalues (Polya-Descartes zero bound on the exponential-polynomial sigma); unbounded switching for complex eigenvalues (harmonic oscillator, sinusoidal sigma, switches spaced pi apart). Double-integrator example (affine sigma, single switch, parabolic switching curve x_1 + (1/2) x_2|x_2| = 0) and harmonic-oscillator example. Normality (controllability of (A,b)) gives isolated zeros, no singular arcs, and uniqueness; singular arcs occur where sigma_i vanishes on an interval. Existence via Filippov (compactness/convexity of the attainable set); the deeper bang-bang principle via Lyapunov's theorem on the convex range of a nonatomic vector measure. Synthesis of the time-optimal state feedback (the switching-curve law for the double integrator). Dual to the LQR (44.07.02): linear cost gives vertex/discontinuous feedback vs quadratic cost giving interior/smooth feedback; forward link to singular control and the HJB value function (44.07.04). Originators: Pontryagin-Boltyanskii-Gamkrelidze-Mishchenko 1956-1962, LaSalle/Hermes/Filippov ~1960 (existence and bang-bang via Lyapunov convexity), Athans-Falb 1966 (switching function, normality, synthesis). Lean status none: Mathlib has the matrix exponential, linear-ODE flow, finite-dim convexity and polytope extreme points but no time-optimal/bang-bang control theory — no minimum-time problem, no switching function B^T p, no bang-bang principle, no switch-count bound, no normality/singular-arc dichotomy, no Filippov existence or Lyapunov-convexity bang-bang principle, and no switching-curve feedback synthesis.


### optimization-control.proximal-operator-gradient-method

- **title**: The Proximal Operator, Moreau Envelope, and Proximal-Gradient Method
- **unit**: `44.06.02`
- **prerequisites**: `44.06.01` (subgradient method — the O(1/sqrt(k)) baseline this unit beats), `44.02.05` (subdifferential / subgradient inequality / Fermat rule / subgradient inversion)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.06.02`. The proximal operator prox_{lambda f}(v)=argmin_x{f(x)+(1/(2 lambda))||x-v||^2} and the Moreau envelope M_{lambda f} (Beck ch 6, Parikh-Boyd). First prox theorem (existence/uniqueness from 1/lambda-strong convexity), second prox theorem / resolvent prox_{lambda f}=(I+lambda partial f)^{-1} with u=prox iff (v-u)/lambda in partial f(u), firm nonexpansiveness, Moreau envelope C^1 with grad M=(1/lambda)(x-prox) and 1/lambda-Lipschitz gradient, the Moreau decomposition prox_f + prox_{f*}=I (cross-ref conjugate 44.02.03, subdifferential inversion 44.02.05). Closed-form proxes: soft-thresholding S_lambda for the l1 norm, projection P_C as prox of an indicator, linear shrinkage for a quadratic, separable-sum coordinatewise rule. The composite problem min F=f+g with f L-smooth and g nonsmooth/simple: the proximal-gradient method (forward-backward splitting) x_{k+1}=prox_{t g}(x_k - t grad f(x_k)), fixed-point characterization x*=prox iff 0 in grad f(x*)+partial g(x*), the sufficient-decrease lemma (descent lemma + prox inequality + polarisation), and the O(1/k) rate F(x_k)-F* <= ||x_0-x*||^2/(2 t k) for t <= 1/L; linear rate (1-1/kappa)^k under strong convexity. ISTA for LASSO as the canonical instance (grad f=A^T(Ax-b), L=sigma_max(A)^2, prox=soft-threshold). Sets up acceleration/FISTA (44.06.03, O(1/k^2)) and ADMM (44.06.04). Originators: Moreau 1965 (prox, Moreau envelope, decomposition), Martinet 1970 / Rockafellar 1976 (proximal point algorithm), Lions-Mercier / Passty 1979 (forward-backward splitting), Combettes-Wajs 2005, Beck-Teboulle 2009 (ISTA/FISTA composite rates). Lean status none: Mathlib has ConvexOn, StrongConvexOn, inner-product spaces, and fderiv but no proximal operator, no first/second prox theorems, no resolvent identity, no firm nonexpansiveness, no Moreau envelope/decomposition, no soft-thresholding/projection-as-prox lemmas, and nothing in the composite proximal-gradient layer (iterate, fixed-point equivalence, sufficient-decrease lemma, O(1/k) rate).


### optimization-control.gauss-newton-levenberg-marquardt

- **title**: Gauss-Newton and Levenberg-Marquardt for Nonlinear Least Squares
- **unit**: `44.03.07`
- **prerequisites**: `44.03.03` (Newton's method for optimization), `44.03.02` (trust-region methods, Cauchy point/dogleg), `43.04.01` (linear least squares: normal equations, QR, SVD, conditioning)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.03.07`. Nonlinear least squares $\min\tfrac12\|r(x)\|^2$ (Nocedal-Wright ch 10). Structured derivatives $\nabla f=J^\top r$, $\nabla^2 f=J^\top J+\sum_j r_j\nabla^2 r_j$ (residual-curvature term $S$). The Gauss-Newton method drops $S$: the step solves the normal equations $J^\top J\,p=-J^\top r$, i.e. the linear least-squares subproblem $\min_p\|Jp+r\|^2$, solved stably by QR (cross-ref 43.04.01); descent direction when $J$ full column rank. Local convergence dichotomy: fast (quadratic in the zero-residual case) when $S^*$ is negligible, degrading to linear with rate $\|(J^\top J)^{-1}S^*\|$ and diverging when that rate $\ge1$ (large-residual failure). Levenberg-Marquardt as the trust-region regularisation $(J^\top J+\lambda I)p=-J^\top r$ (cross-ref 44.03.02): the step is the trust-region subproblem of the Gauss-Newton model, $J^\top J\succeq0$ so the hard case never arises, interpolating Gauss-Newton ($\lambda\to0$) and steepest descent ($\lambda\to\infty$); gain-ratio update of $\lambda$; Marquardt's diagonal scaling $(J^\top J+\lambda D)$. Connection to statistical regression (MLE under Gaussian errors, $J^\top J$ as Fisher information). Separable least squares / variable projection (Golub-Pereyra): eliminate linear parameters $x(y)=\Phi(y)^+d$, minimise $\psi(y)=\tfrac12\|\Pi^\perp_{\Phi(y)}d\|^2$. Applications: curve fitting, bundle adjustment. Lean status none: Mathlib has fderiv, EuclideanSpace inner/norm, orthogonalProjection, Gram-Schmidt/QR, Matrix.PosDef and the spectral theorem, but no residual-objective derivative structure, no Gauss-Newton/LM step, no descent/convergence-rate theorems, and no variable-projection reduction.

### optimization-control.hamilton-jacobi-bellman-verification

- **title**: The Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman Equation and the Verification Theorem
- **unit**: `44.07.04`
- **prerequisites**: `44.08.02` (infinite-horizon discounted DP / Bellman contraction — the discrete dynamic-programming principle this unit carries to continuous time), `09.05.02` (the classical Hamilton-Jacobi equation — the calculus-of-variations PDE HJB generalises to controlled minimisation), `02.18.05` (viscosity solutions of Hamilton-Jacobi equations — the weak-solution framework that rescues HJB where V is nonsmooth)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.07.04`. The Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman (HJB) equation as the continuous-time dynamic-programming counterpart to Pontryagin (44.07.01) and the continuous limit of discrete DP (44.08.02). The value function V(x,t) = inf_u {phi(x(T)) + int_t^T L ds}; the dynamic-programming principle V(x,t) = inf_u {int_t^{t+h} L ds + V(x(t+h),t+h)}; the HJB PDE -dV/dt = min_{u in U}[L + (grad_x V)^T f] = H(x, grad_x V, t) with terminal condition V(.,T)=phi, derived from the DP principle by an infinitesimal-step expansion. The VERIFICATION THEOREM: a C^1 solution W of HJB satisfying the terminal condition lower-bounds the cost of every admissible control (chain-rule integration along trajectories), with equality along the feedback minimiser, so W=V and the pointwise minimiser is an optimal FEEDBACK control — a sufficiency statement, contrasting Pontryagin's necessity. Costate-gradient identity p = grad_x V along the optimal trajectory (the bridge to 44.07.01); the adjoint equation is the differentiated HJB equation (envelope theorem). LQR (44.07.02) re-derived as the quadratic-value HJB solution V = (1/2) x^T P(t) x, reducing HJB to the differential Riccati equation, P = value Hessian. Relation to the classical Hamilton-Jacobi equation (09.05.02): no-control special case, H = Legendre transform of L, value function = mechanical action. Viscosity solutions (02.18.05) for the nonsmooth case where V has corners (non-unique optimal trajectories) and no C^1 solution exists: V is the unique viscosity solution, comparison principle gives uniqueness, classical verification is the differentiable special case. Discrete-to-continuous limit: discrete Bellman operator T_h -> HJB generator as h->0, value iteration = explicit time-marching; discount alpha = e^{-rho h} -> coercive zeroth-order term rho V in the stationary infinite-horizon HJB. The curse of dimensionality: grid solution costs O(N^n), exact only for small n or structured cases (LQR). Originators: Bellman 1957 (HJB, principle of optimality), Pontryagin et al. 1962 (necessity dual), Crandall-Lions 1983 (viscosity solutions, Lions Fields Medal 1994), Fleming-Soner and Bardi-Capuzzo-Dolcetta (rigorous viscosity foundation). Lean status none: Mathlib has ODE existence/uniqueness, Frechet/partial derivatives, gradients, and the Banach fixed-point theorem, but no HJB theory — no controlled value function, no dynamic-programming principle, no HJB PDE, no verification theorem, no p = grad_x V identification, and crucially no viscosity-solution theory for first-order Hamilton-Jacobi equations (no sub/supersolution test functions, no comparison principle, no nonsmooth uniqueness), the gap shared with 02.18.05.

### optimization-control.geometric-programming
Geometric programming: monomials and posynomials, the GP standard form, and the log-log change of variables that converts it into a convex log-sum-exp problem; GP duality, generalized/signomial programming, and engineering-design applications.


### optimization-control.l-bfgs-truncated-newton-cg

Large-scale unconstrained optimization (Nocedal-Wright ch. 7). Limited-memory BFGS (L-BFGS): the two-loop recursion computing the search direction -H_k g_k from the last m secant pairs (s,y) in O(mn) without forming a matrix (cross-ref BFGS 44.03.04), the scaling gamma_k = s^T y / y^T y, the compact representation H_k = gamma_k I + low-rank, and R-linear global convergence (Liu-Nocedal 1989). Inexact / truncated Newton: solving grad^2 f p = -grad f approximately by linear CG (cross-ref 43.07.04) terminated by the forcing sequence eta_k with residual test ||r||<=eta_k||grad f||, the Dembo-Eisenstat-Steihaug rate dichotomy (eta_k<1 linear, eta_k->0 superlinear, eta_k=O(||grad f||) quadratic), Hessian-free Hessian-vector products via AD/finite-diff (cross-ref 44.03.08), negative-curvature CG truncation and Steihaug-Toint trust-region CG. Comparison: L-BFGS fixed cheap cost, no Hessian access, R-linear; truncated Newton variable inner cost, superlinear/quadratic when Hessian-vector products are cheap and accurate.


### optimization-control.interior-point-nonlinear-programming

- **title**: Interior-Point Methods for Nonlinear Programming
- **unit**: `44.04.04`
- **prerequisites**: `44.02.02` (KKT conditions, complementary slackness, active set, second-order critical-cone conditions), `44.03.03` (Newton's method, the constrained-Newton KKT system, reduced Hessian, modified Cholesky / Hessian regularization)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.04.04`. Primal-dual interior-point methods for nonlinear programming (Nocedal-Wright ch. 19; Wright, Primal-Dual Interior-Point Methods). Slack variables s>=0 turn inequalities c_i(x)>=0 into c_i(x)-s_i=0 with simple bounds; the logarithmic barrier f(x) - mu sum log s_i and the barrier subproblem. The PERTURBED KKT system: stationarity grad f - A_E^T nu - A_I^T lambda = 0, perturbed complementarity S Lambda e = mu e (relaxing s_i lambda_i = 0 of 44.02.02 to s_i lambda_i = mu), primal feasibility c_E=0, c_I - s = 0, with (s,lambda)>0. The CENTRAL PATH {(x(mu),s(mu),lambda(mu),nu(mu))} as the homotopy mu->0; convex-case existence/uniqueness (strictly convex barrier under Slater), smoothness via the implicit function theorem, and convergence with objective-gap bound f(x(mu)) - p* <= |I| mu. PRIMAL-DUAL NEWTON step F'_mu(w) Delta w = -F_mu(w); the symmetric augmented (LDL^T, inertia-monitored) and condensed (normal-equations) reductions to the constrained-Newton saddle-point system [[W, A_E^T],[A_E, 0]] with W = grad^2_xx L + A_I^T S^{-1} Lambda A_I; the inertia condition (n,|E|,0) inherited from 44.03.03. Fraction-to-the-boundary rule (keep (s,lambda) >= (1-tau)(s,lambda)), duality measure mu_k = s^T lambda / |I|, centering parameter sigma and Mehrotra predictor-corrector. NONCONVEXITY: indefinite Lagrangian Hessian breaks the inertia condition; Hessian regularization W + delta I (inertia correction = modified-Cholesky/eigenvalue-flooring of 44.03.03) restores a descent step. Globalization via barrier merit function + line search or trust-region/filter (Fletcher-Leyffer, IPOPT). Local superlinear/quadratic convergence under LICQ + strict complementarity + second-order sufficient condition. Why primal-dual beats pure SUMT barrier: primal barrier Hessian carries S^{-2}=O(mu^{-2}) ill-conditioning; primal-dual treats lambda independently (diagonal S^{-1}Lambda). Comparison with SQP (44.04.03): active-set combinatorics vs barrier homotopy keeping all inequalities strictly inactive. Conic/SDP interior-point (44.05.04) as the convex specialization: same S Lambda e = mu e but a self-concordant barrier gives O(sqrt(theta) log(1/eps)) polynomial complexity. Originators: Fiacco-McCormick 1968/1990 (SUMT barrier), Karmarkar 1984 (polynomial LP), Nesterov-Nemirovski 1994 (self-concordance), S. Wright 1997 (primal-dual monograph), Forsgren-Gill-Wright 2002 (survey), Waechter-Biegler 2006 (IPOPT filter line search). Lean status none: Mathlib has fderiv/gradient/Taylor, PosDef/symmetric matrices, block matrices, the spectral theorem, and ConvexOn, but no logarithmic-barrier functional, no barrier subproblem, no perturbed-KKT / perturbed-complementarity map with S Lambda e = mu e, no central-path existence/uniqueness/smoothness theorem, no primal-dual Newton step, no fraction-to-the-boundary rule, no inertia-correction operator, and no convergence-as-mu->0 or local-superlinear-rate theorem.


### optimization-control.sequential-quadratic-programming

- **title**: Sequential Quadratic Programming
- **unit**: `44.04.03`
- **prerequisites**: `44.04.01` (active-set QP — the inner QP subproblem), `44.02.02` (KKT conditions / constraint qualifications), `44.03.03` (Newton's method)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.04.03`. Sequential quadratic programming (SQP) for nonlinear constrained optimization (Nocedal-Wright ch 18). SQP as Newton's method applied to the KKT system (cross-ref KKT 44.02.02, Newton 44.03.03): each iteration solves a QP subproblem (cross-ref active-set QP 44.04.01) min 1/2 p^T (Hess_xx L) p + grad f^T p s.t. A p + c = 0/>=0, whose objective is the quadratic model of the Lagrangian and whose constraints are the linearized constraints. Equivalence of the SQP step with the Newton step on the KKT map F=(grad f - A^T lambda, c); local q-quadratic convergence under LICQ + second-order sufficiency + strict complementarity. Quasi-Newton (BFGS) Hessian approximations of the Lagrangian with Powell's damped update (cross-ref 44.03.04), reduced-Hessian SQP. Globalization: the exact ell-1 merit function f+mu||c||_1 and the augmented-Lagrangian merit function (cross-ref 44.04.02); the Maratos effect and second-order corrections; watchdog/nonmonotone strategies; filter methods (Fletcher-Leyffer). Line-search vs trust-region SQP (Byrd-Omojokun, Sell-1-QP) and inconsistent linearized constraints. Inequality constraints via the IQP working set identifying the active set. The premier general-purpose NLP method. Originators: Wilson 1963, Han 1976, Powell 1978 (damped BFGS), Maratos 1978, Boggs-Tolle 1995 survey. Lean status none: Mathlib has fderiv/Jacobians/Hessians, PosDef, the spectral theorem and Newton-Kantorovich, but no NLP/Lagrangian/KKT-map objects, no QP-subproblem, no SQP-equals-Newton-on-KKT equivalence, no local quadratic-convergence theorem, no Powell-damped BFGS, and no merit-function/Maratos/filter globalization theory.


### optimization-control.accelerated-gradient-nesterov-fista

- **title**: Accelerated Gradient Methods: Nesterov Acceleration and FISTA
- **unit**: `44.06.03`
- **prerequisites**: `44.06.02` (proximal-gradient method — the engine FISTA accelerates: the prox step and sufficient-decrease lemma), `44.06.01` (subgradient method and the first-order lower-bound viewpoint)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.06.03`. Nesterov's accelerated gradient method (Nesterov 1983) and FISTA (Beck-Teboulle 2009). The momentum/extrapolation iteration y_k = x_k + ((t_k-1)/t_{k+1})(x_k - x_{k-1}), x_{k+1} = y_k - (1/L) grad f(y_k) (smooth case) and x_{k+1} = prox_{(1/L) g}(y_k - (1/L) grad f(y_k)) (FISTA composite, cross-ref proximal-gradient 44.06.02), with the gradient sampled at the LOOK-AHEAD point y_k. The momentum schedule t_1=1, t_{k+1}=(1+sqrt(1+4 t_k^2))/2 with the identity t_{k+1}^2 - t_{k+1} = t_k^2 and the bound t_k >= (k+1)/2. The O(1/k^2) convergence rate F(x_k)-F* <= 2 L ||x_0-x*||^2/(k+1)^2 for smooth convex minimisation (vs O(1/k) for plain/proximal gradient), matching the Omega(1/k^2) first-order lower bound (Nesterov's tridiagonal 'worst function in the world' on the Krylov-span model) — hence OPTIMAL. Two proof techniques: the potential/energy function E_k = (2 t_k^2/L)(F(x_k)-F*) + ||u_k - x*||^2 shown nonincreasing (Beck-Teboulle distillation) and the estimate-sequence construction (Nesterov: quadratic lower models phi_k with phi_k <= f + (1-lambda_k)(phi_0 - f), lambda_k -> 0). Acceleration under strong convexity: constant momentum beta = (sqrt(kappa)-1)/(sqrt(kappa)+1) giving the linear rate (1 - 1/sqrt(kappa))^k with sqrt(kappa) dependence (vs gradient descent's (1-1/kappa)^k). The ODE/continuous-time interpretation X'' + (3/t) X' + grad f(X) = 0 with vanishing 3/t friction and Lyapunov decay 2 R^2/t^2 (Su-Boyd-Candes). Why acceleration is non-intuitive: not a descent method (F(x_k) non-monotone, oscillation/overshoot), with adaptive restart as the practical fix. The gradient mapping G_L carries the smooth proof to the composite f+g; sets up accelerated splitting (44.06.04). Originators: Nesterov 1983 (optimal method, estimate sequences), Nemirovski-Yudin 1983 (lower bound), Beck-Teboulle 2009 (FISTA, potential proof), Su-Boyd-Candes 2016 (ODE), O'Donoghue-Candes 2015 (restart). Lean status none: Mathlib has ConvexOn, StrongConvexOn, fderiv, but no accelerated iterate, no t_k schedule/identity, no O(1/k^2) rate, no estimate-sequence or potential-function machinery, no prox (inherited from 44.06.02), no sqrt(kappa) linear rate, no Omega(1/k^2) lower bound, and no ODE-limit Lyapunov theory.


### optimization-control.penalty-augmented-lagrangian

- **title**: Penalty and Augmented-Lagrangian Methods
- **unit**: `44.04.02`
- **prerequisites**: `44.02.02` (KKT conditions, active set, complementary slackness, second-order sufficient conditions), `44.02.01` (Lagrangian duality, weak/strong duality, Slater, the dual function)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.04.02`. Penalty and augmented-Lagrangian methods for constrained NLP (Nocedal-Wright ch. 17; Bertsekas). Quadratic penalty Q(x;mu)=f(x)+(mu/2)||c(x)||^2: unconstrained subproblem sequence mu_k -> infinity, convergence to a KKT point under LICQ with mu_k c(x_k) -> lambda*, and the ill-conditioning kappa(grad^2 Q)=Theta(mu) from the mu A^T A term confined to range(A^T) (null-space/range-space split of the KKT system). Nonsmooth exact ell1 penalty phi1(x;mu)=f(x)+mu||c(x)||_1: exactness for mu>||lambda*||_inf, and the Maratos effect (a unit SQP step increases the merit function near x*, repaired by a second-order correction or watchdog). Augmented Lagrangian (Hestenes-Powell) L_A(x,lambda;mu)=f(x)-lambda^T c(x)+(mu/2)||c(x)||^2, the method of multipliers: primal min over x then multiplier update lambda_next = lambda - mu c(x_k); avoids ill-conditioning because a BOUNDED mu>mu_bar suffices (x* strict minimizer once mu exceeds a reduced-Hessian threshold), convergence under second-order sufficiency (44.02.02). Completing the square = a shifted-target penalty L_A=f+(mu/2)||c-lambda/mu||^2. Dual/proximal-point interpretation: multiplier iteration is gradient ascent on the augmented dual / proximal-point on the concave dual g(lambda) of 44.02.01 with proximal weight 1/mu; contraction factor O(1/mu). Inequalities via Rockafellar shifted penalty with projected update lambda <- [lambda - mu c]^+. LANCELOT bound-constrained framework (slacks + simple bounds + inexact inner solves). Relation to ADMM (44.06.04) as the operator-splitting variant; ell1/augmented-Lagrangian merit functions in SQP (44.04.03). Originators: Courant 1943 (quadratic penalty), Hestenes 1969 and Powell 1969 (method of multipliers, independently), Rockafellar 1973/1976 (dual/proximal reading, inequality shifted penalty), Bertsekas 1982 (convergence theory), Maratos 1978 (Maratos effect), Conn-Gould-Toint (LANCELOT), Gabay-Mercier/Glowinski-Marrocco (ADMM). Lean status none: Mathlib has smooth calculus, PosDef/symmetric matrices, and the spectral theorem but no quadratic-penalty/augmented-Lagrangian functions, no penalty-convergence or conditioning-blowup theorem, no ell1 exactness theorem, and no method-of-multipliers local-convergence theorem with bounded penalty parameter.


### optimization-control.admm-douglas-rachford

- **title**: Operator Splitting: ADMM and Douglas-Rachford
- **unit**: `44.06.04`
- **prerequisites**: `44.06.02` (proximal operator / Moreau envelope / forward-backward / resolvent of the subdifferential), `44.02.03` (Fenchel duality and the conjugate as a modeling tool), `44.04.02` (penalty / augmented-Lagrangian and the method of multipliers)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.06.04`. Operator-splitting methods for min f(x)+g(z) s.t. Ax+Bz=c (Boyd-Parikh-Chu-Peleato-Eckstein 2011 monograph; Beck ch 15). The augmented Lagrangian L_rho(x,z,y)=f(x)+g(z)+<y,Ax+Bz-c>+(rho/2)||Ax+Bz-c||^2 (cross-ref method of multipliers 44.04.02). ADMM = alternating x-minimization, z-minimization, scaled dual ascent y<-y+rho(Ax+Bz-c); the scaled form with u=y/rho. Convergence for closed proper convex f,g with a saddle point, for every rho>0. Douglas-Rachford splitting T_DR=(1/2)(I+R_{tB}R_{tA}) with reflected resolvents R=2J-I; the Eckstein-Bertsekas theorem ADMM = DR applied to the Fenchel dual = proximal point on a monotone splitting (cross-ref proximal operators 44.06.02, conjugates 44.02.03). Proximal / firmly-nonexpansive viewpoint: the resolvent J=(I+t∂f)^{-1}=prox is firmly nonexpansive, its reflection nonexpansive, T_DR averaged, so Krasnoselskii-Mann converges. Consensus (min sum f_i(x_i) s.t. x_i=z, z-update = average) and sharing forms for distributed optimization; examples LASSO, consensus. Ergodic O(1/k) rate on the objective gap and primal residual; linear rate under added strong convexity. Stopping criteria via primal residual r=Ax+Bz-c and dual residual s=rho A^T B(z^{k+1}-z^k). Relation to the proximal point method and maximal monotone operators. Lean status none: Mathlib lacks the proximal operator, reflected resolvents, the Douglas-Rachford/averaged-operator convergence theory, and the ADMM iteration with its dual-equivalence theorem.

### optimization-control.stochastic-gradient-mirror-descent

- **title**: Stochastic Gradient and Mirror Descent
- **unit**: `44.06.05`
- **prerequisites**: `44.06.01` (subgradient method — the deterministic O(1/sqrt(k)) baseline), `44.06.02` (proximal operator / proximal-gradient — the Euclidean prox mirror descent generalises), `44.01.01` (convex sets and convex functions), `37.02.02` (strong law of large numbers — the unbiased-estimator foundation)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.06.05`. Stochastic and non-Euclidean first-order methods (Bubeck 2015 sec 4,6; Nemirovski-Yudin 1983; Beck 2017 sec 9). Stochastic gradient descent (SGD): finite-sum/expectation objective f(x)=E[l(x;xi)], unbiased stochastic subgradient oracle E[g_k|F_k] in partial-f(x_k) with E||g_k||^2 <= B^2; the O(BR/sqrt(k)) rate on the averaged iterate for convex f and O(B^2/(alpha k)) for alpha-strongly-convex f with step eta_k=2/(alpha(k+1)); variance and step-size role; mini-batching (variance sigma^2/b); variance reduction SVRG/SAG/SAGA recovering linear (1-c/kappa)^k rates by a snapshot/table correction that drives the variance to zero. Mirror descent (Nemirovski-Yudin): mirror map / distance-generating function Phi (sigma-strongly convex w.r.t. a chosen norm), Bregman divergence D_Phi(x,y)=Phi(x)-Phi(y)-<gradPhi(y),x-y>, the proximal-mirror update x_{k+1}=argmin_{x in C}{eta<g_k,x>+D_Phi(x,x_k)} generalising projected subgradient (44.06.01, Euclidean Phi=||.||^2/2) and the proximal step (44.06.02). The entropic mirror map Phi=sum x_i log x_i on the simplex giving exponentiated-gradient / multiplicative-weights x_{k+1,i} proportional to x_{k,i} exp(-eta g_{k,i}), with KL=Bregman divergence, Pinsker = 1-strong-convexity in L1, and the sqrt(2 log n) (vs sqrt(n)) dimension dependence. Fundamental Bregman recursion + three-point identity D_Phi(x,z)=D_Phi(x,y)+D_Phi(y,z)+<gradPhi(y)-gradPhi(z),x-y>; the regret / online-convex-optimization viewpoint (Reg_T=O(BR sqrt(T/sigma)), Hedge/multiplicative-weights on simplex) and online-to-batch conversion unifying the online O(sqrt(T)) regret with the offline O(1/sqrt(T)) rate. Power large-scale ML training. Originators: Robbins-Monro 1951 (stochastic approximation), Nemirovski-Yudin 1983 (mirror descent), Littlestone-Warmuth/Freund-Schapire (multiplicative weights), Le Roux-Schmidt-Bach/Johnson-Zhang/Defazio (variance reduction). Lean status none: Mathlib has ConvexOn, StrongConvexOn, inner-product spaces, metric projection, fderiv, conditional expectation and LLN, but no convex-analytic subdifferential (inherited from 44.02.05), no stochastic-subgradient iterate or in-expectation fundamental inequality, no rate extraction, and on the non-Euclidean side no mirror map, Bregman divergence/projection, three-point identity, or mirror-descent update with its entropic/Euclidean rates.
### optimization-control.interior-point-barrier-central-path

- **title**: Interior-Point Methods: The Barrier Method, Central Path, and Primal-Dual Algorithms
- **unit**: `44.05.04`
- **prerequisites**: `44.05.01` (conic optimization LP/SOCP/SDP, generalized inequalities, conic duality, the dual cone), `44.03.03` (Newton's method for optimization, the Newton step, self-concordance), `44.02.01` (Lagrangian duality, weak/strong duality, Slater)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.05.04`. Interior-point methods for conic/convex optimization (Boyd-Vandenberghe ch. 11; Nesterov-Nemirovski 1994). The logarithmic barrier for a cone and the (logarithmically-homogeneous) self-concordant barrier abstraction; the three canonical barriers -sum log x_i (orthant, nu=n), -log(t^2-||u||^2) (Lorentz, nu=2), -log det X (PSD, nu=n). The central path x*(t)=argmin{t c^T x + F(x): Ax=b, x in int K} as the barrier-parameter homotopy from the analytic center (t->0) to the optimum (t->inf). The barrier/SUMT method: sequential unconstrained minimization via equality-constrained Newton centering (cross-ref 44.03.03), outer update t<-mu t, stop at nu/t<=eps. Central-path duality bound: the dual slack s*(t)=-(1/t)grad F is in int K* automatically, and the Euler identity <grad F, x>=-nu (log-homogeneity) gives the gap c^T x*(t)-b^T y*(t)=<x,s>=nu/t. Self-concordance => constant-free Newton analysis; short-step path-following with mu=1+gamma/sqrt(nu) gives the O(sqrt(nu) log(1/eps)) iteration bound that makes interior-point methods polynomial. Nesterov-Nemirovski universal-barrier existence theorem (nu=O(n) for any closed convex domain) vs efficiently-evaluable barriers (LP/SOCP/SDP tractable; copositive/nonneg-polynomial cones NP-hard). Primal-dual interior-point methods: Newton on the perturbed KKT system Ax=b, A^T y+s=c, x o s = mu e, (x,s)>0; surrogate gap eta=<x,s>, mu=sigma eta/nu, predictor (sigma=0) / corrector (sigma=1), neighborhoods N_2 and N_-inf, Mehrotra predictor-corrector, superlinear/quadratic local convergence under strict complementarity. Contrast: polynomial-time alternative to the simplex method 44.05.02 (Klee-Minty), and conic specialization of the NLP interior-point method 44.04.04. Originators: Fiacco-McCormick (SUMT, 1960s), Karmarkar 1984, Khachiyan 1979 (ellipsoid), Nesterov-Nemirovski 1994 (self-concordance, complexity), Mehrotra 1992, Renegar, Wright, Gueler (symmetric cones). Lean status none: Mathlib has cones/PosDef/spectral theorem/log det but no self-concordant-barrier structure, no complexity parameter nu, no central-path definition, no nu/t gap identity, no perturbed-KKT system, and no O(sqrt(nu)) path-following complexity theorem.

### optimization-control.quasiconvex-optimization-bisection

- **title**: Quasiconvex Optimization and the Bisection Method
- **unit**: `44.01.04`
- **prerequisites**: `44.01.01` (convex sets/functions/operations: sublevel sets, quasiconvexity, the epigraph and convexity-preserving operations)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.01.04`. Quasiconvex optimization (Boyd-Vandenberghe sec 4.2.5). Quasiconvex functions (convex sublevel sets, strictly weaker than convex) and their representation by a monotone-in-t family of convex inequalities phi_t(x)<=0 with f0(x)<=t iff phi_t(x)<=0. The bisection method: solve a quasiconvex program as a sequence of convex FEASIBILITY problems F(t), bisecting the optimal value t and updating the bracket [l,u] (feasible -> u=t, infeasible -> l=t), with the ceil(log2((u-l)/eps)) iteration count and the bracket invariant l<=p*<=u. Linear-fractional programming (cx+d)/(ex+f) over a polyhedron with ex+f>0 as the canonical quasiconvex (quasilinear) instance, and its exact LP reduction via the Charnes-Cooper change of variables y=x/(ex+f), z=1/(ex+f). Generalized linear-fractional / worst-case fractional max_i (ci x+di)/(ei x+fi) (quasiconvex as a pointwise max), and the Dinkelbach parametric root-finding F(t)=min(p - t q)=0 as the value-based alternative to bisection. First-order and bordered-Hessian characterizations of quasiconvexity; sum-of-ratios is not quasiconvex (NP-hard). Infeasibility certificates from Lagrangian duality (44.02.01) make each step verifiable; conic generalizations (generalized-eigenvalue SDP min t s.t. A(x) <= t B(x)) feed 44.05.01. Cross-ref convexity/quasiconvexity 44.01.01. Lean status none: Mathlib has QuasiconvexOn/QuasiconcaveOn and the sublevel-set definition but no monotone convex family representation, no convex-feasibility subproblem, no bisection algorithm with the log2 complexity certificate, and no linear-fractional objective / Charnes-Cooper transformation / generalized-fractional formulation.

### optimization-control.multicriteria-pareto-scalarization

- **title**: Multicriteria and Vector Optimization: Pareto Optimality and Scalarization
