- **unit**: `44.01.05`
- **prerequisites**: `44.01.01` (convex sets/functions, cones, dual cone, value set, supporting-hyperplane calculus), `44.02.01` (Lagrangian duality/Slater; multipliers as supporting-hyperplane slopes and the tradeoff/sensitivity reading)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.01.05`. Vector/multicriteria optimization (Boyd-Vandenberghe sec 4.7; Ehrgott 2005). Vector objective f0: D -> R^q ordered by a proper cone K (convex, closed, pointed, solid) via x preceq_K y <=> y-x in K; the orthant R^q_+ gives componentwise order. Pareto optimal / efficient point x*: no feasible x with f0(x) preceq_K f0(x*) and f0(x) != f0(x*), i.e. (f0(x*)-K) cap O = {f0(x*)} where O=f0(D) is the achievable/value set; weakly efficient uses int K. The extended achievable set A = O + K, convex when f0 is K-convex; minimal elements of O = minimal elements of A; Pareto frontier as the lower-left boundary of A facing the utopia/ideal point. Scalarization: minimize <lambda, f0(x)> with lambda in K* (dual cone). Sufficiency (unconditional): lambda in int K* => minimizer is Pareto optimal (strict positivity <lambda,d> > 0 on K\{0}); boundary weights give only weak efficiency. Necessity (convex case): every (weakly) efficient point is a weighted-sum minimizer for some lambda in K*\{0}, proved by separating the open convex set a*-int K from the convex A via finite-dimensional geometric Hahn-Banach 02.11.02 (same engine as strong-duality 44.02.01); lambda is the normal to the supporting hyperplane at a*. Weighted-sum vs epsilon-constraint: weighted sums reach only SUPPORTED efficient points (convex-hull of frontier), the unsupported dented points need the epsilon-constraint method (slice with a box {(f0)_i <= eps_i}) or achievement scalarizing (Chebyshev) functions; nonconvex frontier = multicriteria analogue of a duality gap. Tradeoff reading: optimal weight ratio lambda_1/lambda_2 = -phi' = marginal rate of substitution along frontier f_2=phi(f_1), the vector form of the multiplier sensitivity -dp*/du of 44.02.01. Geoffrion proper efficiency: int K* weights cut out exactly the properly efficient (bounded-tradeoff) points; Benson characterization and recession-cone existence; vector optimization over general proper cones (Lorentz, PSD/Lowner order, scalarize by tr(Lambda .)) cross-ref conic 44.05.01. Examples: regularized least squares / Tikhonov ridge x(mu)=(A^T A+mu I)^{-1}A^T b tracing the L-curve; Markowitz risk-return mean-variance efficient frontier. Distinguish from the economics/welfare 'Pareto' (states across agents): here the q coordinates are competing objectives of ONE decision maker. Originators: Pareto 1906 (welfare form), Koopmans 1951 / Kuhn-Tucker 1951 (efficient production, vector maxima), Geoffrion 1968 (proper efficiency <=> positive weights), Markowitz 1952 (mean-variance frontier), Ehrgott/Miettinen (modern scalarization theory). Lean status none: Mathlib has cones/ProperCone/dual cone and geometric Hahn-Banach but no Pareto/efficient predicate under a cone order, no achievable set O+K with K-convexity, no weighted-sum scalarization functional over int K*, and no scalarization correspondence (sufficiency + convex necessity via supporting hyperplane) or proper-efficiency/epsilon-constraint theory.

### optimization-control.broyden-nonlinear-systems

- **title**: Newton and Quasi-Newton Methods for Nonlinear Systems: Broyden's Method
- **unit**: `44.03.09`
- **prerequisites**: `44.03.04` (quasi-Newton BFGS/DFP/SR1 — the symmetric secant-update theory Broyden generalises to nonsymmetric Jacobians), `43.02.03` (Newton/secant for equations — scalar quadratic/superlinear convergence extended to F(x)=0 in R^n)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.03.09`. Solving nonlinear systems F(x)=0, F:R^n->R^n (Nocedal-Wright ch. 11). Newton's method for systems as the Jacobian linear solve J(x_k)p_k=-F(x_k), local quadratic convergence under J(x*) invertible and J Lipschitz (extends the scalar/secant theory 43.02.03). Broyden's method: the least-change rank-one secant update B_{k+1}=B_k+(y_k-B_k s_k)s_k^T/(s_k^T s_k) of a NONSYMMETRIC Jacobian model (contrast the symmetric PD Hessian updates of 44.03.04), unique Frobenius-nearest matrix satisfying the secant equation B_{k+1}s_k=y_k and unchanged on s_k-perp; the Sherman-Morrison inverse update (O(n^2)/step, no factorisation); bounded deterioration ||B_{k+1}-J*||<=||B_k-J*||+O(max ||e||) and local q-superlinear convergence (Dennis-More characterisation ||(B_k-J*)s_k||/||s_k||->0). Globalisation: line search on the merit function (1/2)||F||^2 with grad = J^T F (descent direction, spurious singular-Jacobian stationary points); inexact-Newton (forcing terms eta_k tuning linear/superlinear/quadratic order) and Newton-Krylov (GMRES 43.07.03, matrix-free Jacobian-vector products); continuation/homotopy as global globalisation. Distinction from optimization quasi-Newton (symmetric Hessian + positive-definiteness vs nonsymmetric Jacobian, neither). Originators: Broyden 1965, Dennis-More 1974 (superlinear/bounded deterioration), Dembo-Eisenstat-Steihaug 1982 (inexact Newton), Kelley 1995 (Newton-Krylov), Allgower-Georg 1990 (continuation). Lean status none: Mathlib has fderiv/Jacobian, matrix inverse, LipschitzWith, Taylor remainder, Banach inverse-stability, and rank-one operators, but no system secant equation, no Broyden rank-one update, no Sherman-Morrison Broyden inverse step, no least-change Frobenius characterisation, no bounded-deterioration inequality, no local superlinear theorem, no Dennis-More characterisation for the nonsymmetric case, no inexact-Newton order theorem, and no merit-function-descent lemma.

### optimization-control.derivative-free-optimization

- **title**: Derivative-Free Optimization: Nelder-Mead and Model-Based Trust-Region Methods
- **unit**: `44.03.10`
- **prerequisites**: `44.03.01` (line-search Wolfe conditions, sufficient/curvature decrease, global convergence), `44.03.02` (trust-region methods, Cauchy point, ratio test, radius management)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.03.10`. Derivative-free optimization (Nocedal-Wright ch. 9; Conn-Scheinberg-Vicente 2009) for the black-box / noisy / expensive-gradient setting. The Nelder-Mead simplex method (reflection/expansion/contraction/shrink) and its heuristic nature (McKinnon counterexample, no general convergence guarantee). Directional direct search / generalized pattern search (GPS, MADS): positive spanning sets (some member is a descent direction at any nonstationary point), the mesh/search/poll framework, and convergence to a Clarke-stationary point via the generalized directional derivative (MADS dense refining directions). Model-based trust-region DFO: interpolation/regression models on a sample set, poisedness and Lambda-poisedness via Lagrange polynomials, the fully-linear / fully-quadratic error bounds (||grad f - grad m|| <= kappa_g Delta), the trust-region algorithm with criticality and geometry-improvement steps, and global convergence liminf||grad f||=0. Implicit filtering for noisy functions (shrinking finite-difference stencil, O(sqrt(eps)) noise floor). Contrast with finite-difference/AD gradient methods (44.03.08). Lean status none: Mathlib has fderiv/Taylor/MvPolynomial/Lagrange-interpolation/convexity but no positive spanning set, no Clarke directional derivative/stationarity, no multivariate poisedness or Lambda-poisedness, no fully-linear error bound, and no derivative-free trust-region algorithm or its convergence theorem.


### optimization-control.frank-wolfe-conditional-gradient

- **title**: The Frank-Wolfe (Conditional Gradient) Method
- **unit**: `44.06.06`
- **prerequisites**: `44.06.02` (proximal-gradient / projection-based composite method this projection-free method contrasts with), `44.01.01` (convex sets and functions, compact convex feasible sets, extreme points)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.06.06`. The Frank-Wolfe / conditional-gradient method for min_{x in D} f(x), f convex smooth over a compact convex D (Jaggi 2013; Beck ch 13; Frank-Wolfe 1956). The linear-minimization oracle (LMO) s_k in argmin_{s in D}<s, grad f(x_k)> replacing projection -- "projection-free" -- and why it is cheaper for structured sets: simplex (LMO=single basis vector e_{argmin c}), l1 ball (one signed coordinate), nuclear-norm ball (one top singular pair -tau u_1 v_1^T vs a full SVD for projection). The iteration x_{k+1}=(1-gamma_k)x_k + gamma_k s_k with the open-loop schedule gamma_k=2/(k+2). The affine-invariant curvature constant C_f=sup (2/gamma^2)(f(y)-f(x)-<y-x,grad f(x)>) <= L diam(D)^2; the descent inequality f(x_{k+1})<=f(x_k)-gamma_k g(x_k)+(gamma_k^2/2)C_f, the recursion h_{k+1}<=(1-gamma_k)h_k+(gamma_k^2/2)C_f, and the O(1/k) rate h_k<=2 C_f/(k+2) (full induction). The Frank-Wolfe / duality gap g(x)=max_{s in D}<x-s,grad f(x)>=<x-s_LMO,grad f(x)> >= f(x)-f* as a FREE computable optimality certificate and stopping rule. Affine invariance of method, curvature constant, and rate. Tightness: O(1/k) lower bound (optimum on a face => FW direction orthogonal to -grad, zig-zag). Sparsity/atomic decomposition: x_k a convex combination of at most k+1 vertices/atoms (k-sparse / rank<=k). Variants: away-step and pairwise FW giving linear rate (1-rho)^k for strongly convex f over a polytope, rho set by the pyramidal/vertex width (Lacoste-Julien-Jaggi 2015). Applications: nuclear-norm/matrix completion, structured-sparsity, submodular. Contrast with projected/proximal gradient (44.06.02) and the forgone acceleration O(1/k^2) of 44.06.03. Originators: Frank-Wolfe 1956 (QP via LP linearisation), Levitin-Polyak 1966 / Dunn 1979 (rates), Jaggi 2013 (curvature constant, gap certificate, sparsity revival), Lacoste-Julien-Jaggi 2015 (away/pairwise linear rate). Lean status none: Mathlib has Convex/ConvexOn, compactness, inner-product spaces, fderiv, and Krein-Milman but no linear-minimization oracle, no FW iterate or 2/(k+2) schedule, no set-relative affine-invariant curvature constant, no descent inequality / recursion / O(1/k) theorem, no Frank-Wolfe duality-gap certificate, no atomic-sparsity bound, and no away-step/pairwise linear-rate (pyramidal-width) theory.

### optimization-control.coordinate-descent-methods

- **title**: Coordinate Descent and Block-Coordinate Methods
- **unit**: `44.06.07`
- **prerequisites**: `44.06.02` (proximal operator / proximal-gradient — the coordinate proximal step and the separable-composite model), `44.06.05` (stochastic gradient / mirror descent — randomized CD as a coordinate-sampling sibling of SGD)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.06.07`. Coordinate descent (CD) and block-coordinate methods (Wright 2015 'Coordinate Descent Algorithms'; Nesterov 2012; Beck 2017 sec 11). Update one coordinate (or block) at a time; three selection rules — cyclic (Gauss-Seidel sweep), randomized (sample i with p_i proportional to L_i^alpha), Gauss-Southwell / greedy (argmax_i |grad_i f|). Exact coordinate minimization vs coordinate-gradient step x_i <- x_i - (1/L_i) grad_i f and its proximal version for the separable composite F=f+sum_i g_i(x_i). Coordinate Lipschitz constants L_i; coordinate descent lemma f(x+h e_i) <= f(x)+h grad_i f+(L_i/2)h^2 and per-step decrease f(x)-f(x^+) >= (1/(2 L_i)) (grad_i f)^2. Convergence: smooth + SEPARABLE-nonsmooth composite is exactly the structure that makes CD reach a global minimizer (optimality 0 in grad f+partial g decouples coordinatewise); Powell (1973) three-coordinate nonconvergence counterexample for the nonseparable case. Randomized CD expected O(2 S R^2 / k) rate (S=sum L_i) and linear (1-mu/S)^k under strong convexity (Nesterov 2012); expected per-iteration cost advantage (coordinate gradient touches one column). Block CD = nonlinear Gauss-Seidel; two-block = alternating minimization (EM, NMF); for SPD quadratics equals matrix Gauss-Seidel x_{k+1}=(D-L)^{-1}(U x_k+b). Why CD dominates for LASSO (soft-threshold prox), SVM dual / SMO (clip prox, Platt 1998), large sparse regression. Accelerated coordinate descent O(1/k^2); parallel and asynchronous (Hogwild!) CD under bounded staleness. Cross-ref proximal-gradient 44.06.02 and SGD 44.06.05. Lean status none: Mathlib has ConvexOn, StrongConvexOn, inner-product/EuclideanSpace, metric projection, fderiv, but no coordinate Lipschitz constant, coordinate-gradient step, per-coordinate sufficient-decrease, separable-composite model, decoupled optimality condition, randomized coordinate-sampling expected one-step decrease or rate extraction, and no Powell counterexample analogue.


### optimization-control.nesterov-smoothing

- **title**: Nesterov Smoothing of Structured Nonsmooth Functions
- **unit**: `44.06.08`
- **prerequisites**: `44.06.03` (accelerated gradient / FISTA — the optimal O(1/k^2) smooth-class engine applied to f_mu), `44.02.03` (Fenchel duality / conjugate — the max-type saddle representation f(x)=max_u{<Ax,u>-phi(u)})
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.06.08`. Nesterov's smoothing technique for structured nonsmooth convex optimization (Nesterov 2005 "Smooth minimization of non-smooth functions", Math. Prog. 103). The max-type/saddle model f(x)=max_{u in Q2}{<Ax,u>-phi(u)} (conjugate representation, cross-ref 44.02.03); adding a sigma2-strongly-convex prox-function mu*d2 to the inner max yields a smooth approximation f_mu with grad f_mu(x)=A^T u_mu(x), Lipschitz constant L_mu=||A||^2/(mu sigma2), and uniform two-sided error f_mu <= f <= f_mu + mu D2. Applying the optimal accelerated gradient method (44.06.03) to f_mu and balancing mu ~ epsilon gives O(1/epsilon) complexity f(x_N)-f* <= 4||A|| sqrt(D1 D2/(sigma1 sigma2))/(N+1) — beating the Omega(1/epsilon^2) black-box subgradient lower bound (44.06.01) by EXPLOITING the explicit max-structure (side information, not a contradiction of the black-box bound). Worked smoothings: the max-function via entropy/log-sum-exp (softmax), the l1 and l-infinity norms (Huber). Distinguished from Moreau-Yosida/proximal smoothing (44.06.02) — that is the squared-norm conjugate-smoothing special case; Nesterov smoothing is the general prox-function/saddle smoothing. Excessive-gap technique: a primal-dual scheme keeping f_mu1(x)<=phi_mu2(u) invariant while mu1,mu2 -> 0, giving O(1/epsilon) with a verifiable duality gap. Beck-Teboulle 2012 unified (alpha,beta)-smoothable framework and S-FISTA. Lean status none: Mathlib lacks the Fenchel conjugate, prox-function abstraction, Danskin differentiation for a regularized parametric max, the L_mu gradient bound, and the smoothing-error/FISTA-rate composition giving the O(1/epsilon) complexity.

### optimization-control.pomdp-imperfect-state-information

- **title**: Imperfect State Information and Partially Observed Markov Decision Processes
- **unit**: `44.08.04`
- **prerequisites**: `44.08.03` (Markov decision processes / the fully-observed belief-MDP this reduces to), `44.08.01` (finite-horizon DP / principle of optimality run on the belief state), `37.05.01` (Markov chains / transition matrices and the Bayes filter)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.08.04`. Dynamic programming under imperfect state information / POMDPs (Bertsekas Vol. 1 ch. 4-5; Smallwood-Sondik 1973; Kaelbling-Littman-Cassandra 1998). The controller observes z_t ~ O(.|s',a) instead of the hidden state s_t; its information vector I_t=(z_0..z_t,u_0..u_{t-1}) grows with time. Sufficient-statistic reduction: the belief (conditional distribution) b_t(s)=P(s_t=s|I_t) in the simplex Delta(S) is a sufficient statistic for control, reducing the POMDP to a fully-observed belief-MDP on Delta(S) with belief-state Bellman recursion J_t(b)=min_a {gbar(b,a)+alpha sum_z sigma(b,a,z) J_{t+1}(tau(b,a,z))} (cross-ref MDP theory 44.08.03; inherits the sup-norm alpha-contraction). Belief update (Bayes filter) tau(b,a,z)(s')=O(z|s',a) sum_s P(s'|s,a)b(s) / sigma, with sigma(b,a,z) the predictive observation probability and Bayes normalizer (predict/time update then correct/measurement update). Finite-state finite-observation POMDPs: Smallwood-Sondik result that the finite-horizon value function is piecewise-linear-and-convex (PWLC), J_t(b)=min_{alpha in Gamma_t} <b,alpha>, with exact value iteration over alpha-vectors (backprojection alpha^{a,z}, cross-sum over observations, LP pruning of dominated vectors). Computational hardness: |Gamma_t| <= |A| |Gamma_{t+1}|^{|Z|} doubly-exponential before pruning; exact finite-horizon planning PSPACE-hard, infinite-horizon undecidable; point-based approximations (PBVI/Perseus/HSVI/SARSOP) back up alpha-vectors at sampled beliefs to bound |Gamma|. Perfect-observation special case recovers the MDP (beliefs collapse to simplex vertices). LQG special case: belief is Gaussian, tau is the Kalman filter, separation/certainty-equivalence theorem (LQR gain applied to the estimate, cross-ref 44.07.02). Originators: Astrom 1965 (sufficient statistic / belief reduction), Smallwood-Sondik 1973 + Sondik 1978 (PWLC, exact value iteration), Kalman 1960 (the Gaussian belief update), Kaelbling-Littman-Cassandra 1998 (algorithms / complexity), Bertsekas (control-theoretic treatment). Lean status none: Mathlib has the stdSimplex, finite-dim convexity, the Banach fixed-point theorem, and probability kernels, but no POMDP tuple, no belief-update (Bayes filter) map tau on the simplex, no sufficient-statistic reduction to the belief-MDP, and no Smallwood-Sondik PWLC / alpha-vector backup theorem.


### optimization-control.kalman-filter-lqg-separation

- **title**: The Kalman Filter, Linear-Quadratic-Gaussian Control, and the Separation Principle
- **unit**: `44.08.05`
- **prerequisites**: `44.07.02` (LQR and the algebraic Riccati equation — the control half of LQG and the transpose-dual of the filter covariance Riccati), `26.03.01` (random variables, joint/conditional distributions, covariance, the multivariate normal — the Gaussian-conditioning substrate of the measurement update)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.08.05`. Optimal linear estimation and stochastic control. The discrete-time linear-Gaussian state-space model x_{k+1}=A x_k + B u_k + w_k, y_k = C x_k + v_k with white Gaussian w,v of covariances W,V; the Kalman filter as the recursive conditional-mean (MMSE) estimator derived by Gaussian conditioning / orthogonal projection: the predict (time update) and correct (measurement update) equations, the innovation nu_k = y_k - C xhat_{k|k-1}, the Kalman gain L_k = P_{k|k-1} C^T (C P_{k|k-1} C^T + V)^{-1}, the measurement-update covariance (I - L_k C)P_{k|k-1} as a Schur complement, and the error-covariance Riccati recursion (deterministic, measurement-free). MMSE optimality of the conditional mean (global in the Gaussian case, best linear unbiased in general); innovations are zero-mean white. Steady-state filter and filter algebraic Riccati equation (FARE) under (A,C) detectable and (A,W^{1/2}) stabilisable; the continuous-time Kalman-Bucy filter dP/dt = AP + PA^T - PC^T V^{-1} CP + W with gain K = PC^T V^{-1}. Estimation/control duality: the FARE is the regulator DARE for the transposed data (A^T,C^T,W,V), so 44.07.02's Hamiltonian/Schur and Newton-Kleinman machinery transfers verbatim and one ARE solver serves both. LQG control: combine LQR (44.07.02) with the Kalman filter; the separation principle / certainty equivalence — the optimal output-feedback law is u = -K xhat_{k|k}, the LQR gain on the Kalman estimate, control and estimation designed independently, cost = deterministic LQR cost + tr(Q P_{k|k}) estimation penalty, closed-loop poles = regulator poles ∪ filter poles. Robustness caveat: LQG has no guaranteed gain/phase margins (Doyle 1978), motivating LTR and H-infinity. Originators: Kalman 1960 (filter), Kalman-Bucy 1961, Wonham/Joseph-Tou 1960s (separation), Potter (Apollo square-root filter), Doyle 1978 (non-robustness). Lean status none: Mathlib has PosDef/PosSemidef, Schur complements, condExp as L2 projection, and abstract Gaussian measures but no Gaussian-conditioning formula, no linear-Gaussian state-space / Kalman recursion, no MMSE-projection identification, no error-covariance Riccati or FARE, no Kalman-Bucy flow, no filter/regulator duality, no LQG separation theorem, and no Doyle non-robustness result.


### optimization-control.stochastic-shortest-path-total-cost

- **title**: Stochastic Shortest Path and Total-Cost Dynamic Programming
- **unit**: `44.08.06`
- **prerequisites**: `44.08.02` (infinite-horizon discounted DP — the Bellman contraction, now in a weighted sup-norm, and value/policy iteration), `44.08.03` (Markov decision processes — controlled transition kernel and stationary policies, here with a cost-free absorbing terminal state)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.08.06`. The stochastic shortest path (SSP) / undiscounted total-cost DP model (Bertsekas Vol. 2 Ch. 3; Bertsekas-Tsitsiklis Neuro-Dynamic Programming Ch. 2). Finite-state controlled chain with a cost-free absorbing terminal state t and no discounting (alpha=1); total expected cost J_pi(i)=lim E[sum g(x_k,mu_k)] until absorption. PROPER policies reach t with probability 1 from every state (equivalently the substochastic matrix P_mu over non-terminal states has spectral radius <1, so I-P_mu is invertible and J_mu=(I-P_mu)^{-1}g_mu); IMPROPER policies may wander forever. Assumptions: (3.1) a proper policy exists; (3.2) every improper policy has infinite cost from some state. Under these, a positive weight vector v (minimum expected steps to t) and modulus beta=1-1/max_i v_i < 1 make T and every proper T_mu CONTRACTIONS in the WEIGHTED sup-norm ||J||_v=max_i|J(i)|/v_i (cross-ref the discounted contraction 44.08.02). Hence J*=TJ* has a unique solution vanishing at t, J* is the optimal cost, value iteration converges (beta^k rate), and policy iteration over proper policies improves monotonically and terminates finitely. Dropping (3.2) (e.g. a zero-cost improper self-loop) breaks uniqueness. SSP is the UNIFYING finite-state model: discounted DP = SSP with added termination probability 1-alpha (every policy proper, beta=alpha); deterministic shortest path = deterministic transitions (Bellman-Ford, no negative cycles = assumption 3.2); first-passage = target lumped into t. Gauss-Seidel / asynchronous value iteration and real-time DP (RTDP) converge under the same assumptions. Originators: Bellman (1950s), Bertsekas-Tsitsiklis 1991 (rigorous SSP analysis, weighted contraction), Bertsekas (Vol. 2 textbook), Bertsekas-Tsitsiklis 1996 (neuro-dynamic programming substrate), Barto-Bradtke-Singh 1995 (RTDP). Lean status none: Mathlib has the Banach fixed-point theorem, sup-norm bounded-function spaces, substochastic matrices / spectral radius / Perron-Frobenius, and probability kernels, but no cost-free absorbing terminal state, no proper-policy notion (rho(P_mu)<1), no SSP assumptions, no construction of the weight vector v and modulus beta giving the weighted-sup-norm contraction of T and proper T_mu, no identification of the unique fixed point with J* vanishing at t, and no value/policy/asynchronous iteration convergence theorems.

### optimization-control.chambolle-pock-primal-dual-hybrid-gradient

- **title**: The Chambolle-Pock Primal-Dual Hybrid Gradient Method
- **unit**: `44.06.09`
- **prerequisites**: `44.02.03` (Fenchel duality / conjugate modeling — the saddle representation min f(Kx)+g(x) = min_x max_y <Kx,y>+g(x)-f*(y)), `44.06.02` (proximal operator / resolvent of the subdifferential — the prox steps), `44.06.04` (ADMM / Douglas-Rachford operator splitting — the other dual-splitting solver, contrasted on the linear-solve question)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `44.06.09`. The primal-dual hybrid gradient (PDHG) / Chambolle-Pock method for the saddle-point problem min_x max_y <Kx,y>+g(x)-f*(y), the convex-concave form of the composite primal min_x f(Kx)+g(x) (cross-ref Fenchel/conjugate 44.02.03). The iteration: dual prox-ascent y<-prox_{sigma f*}(y+sigma K bar x), primal prox-descent x<-prox_{tau g}(x-tau K^T y) (cross-ref proximal operators 44.06.02), and the over-relaxation/extrapolation bar x = x + theta(x - x_old) with theta=1. Step condition tau sigma ||K||^2 < 1. O(1/N) ergodic convergence on the partial primal-dual gap (full telescoping proof). Acceleration: O(1/N^2) when g (or f*) is strongly convex via the adaptive (tau_n,sigma_n,theta_n) schedule, and linear rate under bilateral strong convexity. Why PDHG needs NO linear solve / no inner loop (contrast ADMM 44.06.04, whose K^TK-coupled subproblem can require a solve), ideal for imaging/TV problems where K is a difference operator. The Condat-Vu generalization adds a smooth term via a forward gradient step (tau(beta/2+sigma||K||^2)<=1). Worked: total-variation (ROF) denoising. Relation to the proximal point algorithm on the primal-dual monotone operator T in the preconditioned M-metric (He-Yuan), with M>0 iff the step condition holds. Originators: Arrow-Hurwicz-Uzawa 1958, Zhu-Chan 2008, Chambolle-Pock 2011, He-Yuan 2012, Condat 2013 / Vu 2013. Lean status none: Mathlib lacks the Fenchel conjugate, the proximal operator, the saddle/primal-dual functional and gap, the iteration and step condition, the O(1/N), O(1/N^2), and linear rate theorems, and the preconditioned proximal-point identification.

### mathematical-statistics.decision-theory-risk-admissibility

- **title**: Statistical Decision Theory: Loss, Risk, Admissibility, Minimax, and Bayes Rules
- **unit**: `45.01.01`
- **prerequisites**: `37.01.01` (probability spaces — risk as an expectation over the data law), `44.01.01` (convex sets/functions — the convex risk set and supporting-hyperplane geometry)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `45.01.01`. The statistical decision-theoretic framework (Casella-Berger ch 7-10; Berger). Components: parameter space Theta, action space A, loss L(theta,a), decision rule delta, risk R(theta,delta)=E_theta L(theta,delta(X)). Comparing rules: dominance and admissibility/inadmissibility. The minimax principle (minimise sup-risk). Bayes rules: Bayes risk r(pi,delta)=int R d pi and the Bayes rule as its minimiser, characterised pointwise by minimising posterior expected loss (posterior mean under squared error, median under absolute error, mode under 0-1). Relations: unique Bayes rules are admissible; minimax rules as Bayes for least favourable priors (minimax-Bayes saddle/duality, minimax value = sup_pi r(pi), von Neumann minimax). Randomized rules and the convex risk set S (admissible = lower boundary, Bayes = supporting points, minimax = diagonal corner; cross-ref convex sets 44.01.01). Stein's phenomenon: inadmissibility of the sample mean for p>=3, James-Stein dominator, Stein's unbiased risk identity. Complete class theorems (Wald): Bayes and limiting-Bayes rules form a complete class; admissible rules are limits of Bayes rules. Unifying framework for the estimation/testing chapter; cross-ref elementary Bayesian survey 26.07.01. Originators: Wald 1950 (decision functions, complete classes), von Neumann 1928 (minimax), Stein 1956 (inadmissibility), Ferguson 1967, Berger 1985, Lehmann-Casella 1998. Lean status none: Mathlib has expectation/conditional expectation but no decision-rule/loss/risk objects, no dominance order, no Bayes-risk-equals-posterior-expected-loss theorem, no minimax-Bayes duality, no convex risk set, and no complete-class theorems.


### mathematical-statistics.linear-model-gauss-markov-blue

- **title**: Linear Model Theory: Gauss-Markov, the Hat Matrix, and BLUE
- **unit**: `45.06.01`
- **prerequisites**: `01.01.13` (spectral theorem / orthogonal projection onto a subspace), `26.06.01` (elementary correlation and regression survey, cross-ref), `26.03.01` (random variables, expectation, covariance, multivariate normal), `43.04.01` (numerical least squares: normal equations vs QR vs SVD, the computational side)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `45.06.01`. The rigorous theory of the linear model y = X*beta + epsilon (HTF ch. 3; Seber-Lee). The OLS estimator beta-hat = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y via the normal equations and the column-space projection geometry (cross-ref the numerical least-squares treatment 43.04.01; the elementary survey 26.06.01). The hat matrix H = X(X^T X)^{-1} X^T as the symmetric idempotent projection onto col(X), with leverages h_ii in [1/n, 1] and tr(H) = p. The Gauss-Markov theorem: OLS is the best linear unbiased estimator (BLUE) under zero-mean uncorrelated homoscedastic errors, proved by C = A + D with D*X = 0 forcing Cov(C*y) = Cov(beta-hat) + sigma^2 D D^T (positive semidefinite, Loewner order). Distribution theory under normal errors: beta-hat ~ N(beta, sigma^2 (X^T X)^{-1}); RSS/sigma^2 ~ chi^2_{n-p} from the rank-(n-p) idempotent M = I - H; independence of beta-hat and RSS via orthogonality H M = 0 (Cochran's theorem); the t-test t_j ~ t_{n-p} and the F-test for A*beta = c; the coefficient of determination R^2. Generalized least squares (Aitken's theorem) for correlated errors via whitening V = L L^T. Grounds ridge/lasso/selection (45.06.03) and discriminant/GAM methods (45.08.01). Lean status none: Mathlib has orthogonal projection, PosSemidef/Loewner order, and abstract Gaussians but no statistical linear model, no Gauss-Markov/BLUE theorem, no quadratic-form chi-squared distribution theory, no Cochran independence, and no chi-squared/F distribution layer.

### mathematical-statistics.bias-variance-decomposition

- **title**: The Bias-Variance Decomposition and the Prediction-Error Trade-off
- **unit**: `45.06.02`
- **prerequisites**: `26.06.01` (least-squares regression / prediction framework), `26.03.01` (expectation and variance algebra), `37.03.01` (moment functionals, cross-ref)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `45.06.02`. Decomposition of expected squared prediction error into irreducible noise sigma^2 + bias^2 + variance, in pointwise and integrated forms (Hastie-Tibshirani-Friedman ch 2, 7; Geman-Bienenstock-Doursat 1992). The split as the Pythagorean theorem in L^2. In-sample vs out-of-sample/generalization error, training-error optimism = (2/n) sum Cov(y_hat_i, y_i), effective degrees of freedom = tr(S) for a linear smoother (Mallows C_p / AIC / GCV surrogates). Model complexity and the U-shaped test-error curve; the trade-off as the organizing principle for regularization and selection (forward to ridge 45.06.03 and selection 45.06.09). The 0-1 loss subtlety (variance can reduce error, Domingos 2000) and a note on modern double descent (Belkin-Hsu-Ma-Mandal 2019). Cross-ref the learning-theory approximation-estimation trade-off 45.07.02. Lean status none: Mathlib has expectation/variance/conditional-expectation but no training-sample/estimator-as-function-of-data layer, no expected-prediction-error decomposition, no optimism/effective-df identities.


### mathematical-statistics.sufficiency-factorization-exponential-families

- **title**: Sufficiency, the Factorization Theorem, and Exponential Families
- **unit**: `45.01.02`
- **prerequisites**: `37.01.01` (probability spaces), `37.04.01` (conditional expectation as the Radon-Nikodym construction, used to define the replay kernel), `26.03.01` (random variables and expected value)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `45.01.02`. Data reduction by sufficiency (Casella-Berger Ch. 6). Sufficient statistic (conditional distribution of the data given T free of theta); Fisher-Neyman factorization p_theta = g_theta(T) h (stated and proved, discrete + Halmos-Savage dominated form); minimal sufficiency and the Lehmann-Scheffe likelihood-ratio criterion; exponential families in canonical/natural form h(x)exp(<eta,T(x)>-A(eta)), natural parameter space, the natural statistic as sufficient, mean/variance via grad A = E[T] and Hessian A = Cov(T) (log-partition / cumulant function, convex by Holder), curved exponential families and statistical curvature. Foundation for Rao-Blackwell (45.01.06), Basu (45.01.03), MLE in exponential families, and the large-deviation conjugate A* (37.07.03). Lean status none: Mathlib has conditional expectation and rnDeriv but no StatisticalModel/sufficiency/factorization, no exponential-family canonical density, natural parameter space, log-partition function or its moment derivatives, and no minimal sufficiency / Lehmann-Scheffe / curved families.

### mathematical-statistics.method-of-moments-maximum-likelihood

- **title**: Point Estimation: The Method of Moments and Maximum Likelihood
- **unit**: `45.01.04`
- **prerequisites**: `37.02.02` (strong law of large numbers — heuristic consistency of sample moments and the average log-likelihood), `26.05.01` (estimation/inference vocabulary — point estimates as the basis of intervals and tests), `26.03.01` (random variables and expected value — moments and expectation)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `45.01.04`. Finite-sample point estimation (Casella-Berger ch 7; Lehmann-Casella Theory of Point Estimation). The method of moments: equate the first k sample moments m_j=(1/n)sum X_i^j to the population moments mu_j(theta) and solve. Maximum likelihood: the likelihood L(theta|x)=prod f(x_i|theta), the log-likelihood ell=sum log f, the score s(theta)=grad ell, the score/likelihood equations s=0, and the MLE as the global maximiser argmax_theta L (NOT merely a stationary point). Invariance/equivariance: MLE of tau(theta) is tau(theta-hat), plus equivariance under data transformation. Exponential families (cross-ref sufficiency 45.01.02): the likelihood equation reads E_theta[T(X)]=(1/n)sum T(x_i), so the MLE matches the natural sufficient statistic to its model expectation (moment matching), and strict concavity of ell in the natural parameter (Hessian -n Cov(T)) gives existence/uniqueness. Computation when closed form fails: Newton's method and Fisher scoring (cross-ref 44.03.03; observed=expected information for exponential families), EM for latent structure (cross-ref 45.08.07, M-step = moment matching on completed data). Finite-sample properties: bias, MSE=bias^2+variance, heuristic consistency (rigorous asymptotics deferred to 45.04.03). Examples: Bernoulli (p-hat=X-bar), normal ((mu-hat,sigma2-hat)=(X-bar,(1/n)sum(X_i-X-bar)^2), variance MLE biased low E[sigma2-hat]=(n-1)/n sigma^2, Bessel correction), uniform(0,theta) boundary MLE (theta-hat=max X_i, no stationary point, bias -theta/(n+1), MSE O(n^-2) vs moment estimator 2 X-bar O(n^-1)). Originators: Pearson 1894 (method of moments), Fisher 1922/1925 (maximum likelihood, likelihood/sufficiency/information), Koopman-Darmois-Pitman 1935-1936 (exponential-family sufficiency). Lean status none: Mathlib has measures, densities, Radon-Nikodym, expectation/variance, product measures, and the differential-calculus concavity layer, but no estimator type, no method-of-moments or likelihood/log-likelihood functional, no MLE argmax, no equivariance theorem, no exponential-family moment-matching equation E_theta[T]=T-bar from the log-partition gradient, and no finite-sample bias computations (normal variance, uniform endpoint).


### mathematical-statistics.cart-decision-trees

- **title**: Tree-Based Methods: Classification and Regression Trees (CART)
- **unit**: `45.08.04`
- **prerequisites**: `26.06.01` (least-squares/SSE baseline the regression tree specialises), `37.01.01` (probability spaces and the joint law of (X,Y) whose conditional mean a tree estimates)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `45.08.04`. Recursive binary partitioning of the feature space (HTF ch 9; Breiman-Friedman-Olshen-Stone CART). Regression tree as piecewise-constant fit with within-node SSE splitting and leaf-mean values; classification tree with node impurity measures (misclassification error, Gini index, cross-entropy/deviance) and why Gini/entropy are preferred for growing (strict concavity registers every non-degenerate split). Greedy split selection; cost-complexity pruning C_alpha(T)=R(T)+alpha|T| with the weakest-link nested subtree sequence chosen by cross-validation (one-standard-error rule). Categorical predictors via response-ordering, missing values via surrogate splits, instability/high-variance of trees motivating bagging (45.08.05) and boosting (45.08.06). Trees as adaptive nearest-neighbour / partition estimators (Lin-Jeon). Lean status none.

### mathematical-statistics.consistency-modes-of-convergence

- **title**: Consistency of Estimators and the Modes of Stochastic Convergence in Statistics
- **unit**: `45.04.01`
- **prerequisites**: `probability.strong-law-large-numbers`, `probability.characteristic-functions-levy-continuity`
- **notes**: stub - see unit `45.04.01`. The asymptotic-statistics toolkit (van der Vaart Ch. 2). The four modes of stochastic convergence used in statistics - in probability, almost surely, in L^p, and in distribution - with the implication lattice (a.s./L^p => in probability => in distribution; the reverse to a constant), the standard counterexamples, Slutsky's lemma, the continuous-mapping theorem, and the portmanteau theorem (stated; underlying limit theorems cross-referenced to the probability spine 37.02/37.03, not re-derived). The o_P / O_P (Mann-Wald) stochastic-order calculus and its algebraic rules, with O_P(1) = tightness via Prohorov. (Weak/strong) consistency of estimators: definition, the plug-in route via the law of large numbers plus the continuous-mapping theorem, and the M-estimator argmax route via a uniform law of large numbers and a well-separated maximum (Wald, previewed; full theory in 45.04.04). Uniform laws of large numbers and Glivenko-Cantelli previewed (full in 45.05.02). Foundational unit for the asymptotic-statistics chapter. Lean status none.


### mathematical-statistics.bernstein-inequality-sub-exponential

- **title**: Bernstein's Inequality and the Sub-Exponential / Bennett Regime
- **unit**: `45.05.01`
- **prerequisites**: `37.07.02` (Cramer / Legendre-Fenchel rate function — the Chernoff/MGF machinery the proof runs), `40.07.05` (Hoeffding/Azuma/McDiarmid — the variance-blind bounded-difference family this completes), `37.03.01` (moment/characteristic functions — analytic control of the MGF near the origin)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `45.05.01`. The variance-aware concentration inequalities completing the standard toolkit (Boucheron-Lugosi-Massart; Vershynin; Wainwright). Sub-Gaussian (cgf bound on all of R, Orlicz psi_2, Gaussian tail/moment growth) vs sub-exponential / sub-gamma (quadratic cgf bound only on |lambda|<1/b, Orlicz psi_1, exponential tail, linear moment growth |X|_p<=Kp), with equivalence of the four characterisations and the product relation |XY|_psi1<=|X|_psi2|Y|_psi2. The Bernstein condition |E X^k|<=k!/2 sigma^2 b^(k-2) giving the cgf bound psi(lambda)<=sigma^2 lambda^2/(2(1-b lambda)). Bennett's inequality exp(-(v/b^2) h(bt/v)) with h(u)=(1+u)log(1+u)-u, and Bernstein's exp(-t^2/(2(v+bt/3))) as its consequence; the two-regime interpolation — Gaussian e^(-t^2/2v) for small t<=v/b, exponential e^(-t/2b) for large t. Full Chernoff/MGF proof (cross-ref Cramer 37.07.02). Why this beats Hoeffding (40.07.05) when variances are small, and why learning theory / high-dimensional statistics need it (products of sub-Gaussian coords are sub-exponential, not bounded). Application: chi-squared / sample-variance tail (Z^2-1 is sub-gamma with nu^2=2,b=2). Advanced: sub-gamma convolution fixed point, entropy method and self-bounding refinement, matrix Bernstein (Tropp/Ahlswede-Winter), Vershynin min(t^2/nK^2, t/K) form. Originators: Bernstein 1924, Bennett 1962, Hoeffding 1963; modern framing Boucheron-Lugosi-Massart 2013, Vershynin 2018, Wainwright 2019. Lean status none: Mathlib has mgf/cgf and the Chernoff bound but no sub-exponential/sub-gamma predicate, no Orlicz psi_1/psi_2 norms, no Bennett rate h, no Bernstein inequality, and no chi-squared tail derived from these.


### mathematical-statistics.mcmc-metropolis-hastings-gibbs

- **title**: Markov Chain Monte Carlo: Metropolis-Hastings and Gibbs Sampling
- **unit**: `45.03.05`
- **prerequisites**: `37.05.01` (Markov property, transition matrices, Chapman-Kolmogorov), `37.05.07` (the ergodic theorem and detailed balance — reversibility as the design tool, the Metropolis-Hastings acceptance ratio at the Markov-chain-theory level), `26.07.01` (Bayesian prior/posterior — the target distribution)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `45.03.05`. Markov chain Monte Carlo for sampling an (unnormalised) target pi, especially Bayesian posteriors pi(theta) prop L(theta)p(theta) (Robert-Casella Monte Carlo Statistical Methods; Gelman et al. Bayesian Data Analysis 3e). Idea: construct a Markov chain whose stationary distribution is pi, then average along one path (the MCMC ergodic theorem / LLN, cross-ref Markov-chain ergodicity and detailed balance 37.05.07). Detailed balance pi(dx)P(x,dy)=pi(dy)P(y,dx) as the design tool (sufficient, not necessary, for invariance). Metropolis-Hastings: proposal q + acceptance alpha(x,y)=min(1,[pi(y)q(y,x)]/[pi(x)q(x,y)]); proof it leaves pi invariant via detailed balance (two-case computation); the normalising constant Z cancels because only ratios pi(y)/pi(x) enter. Special cases: random-walk Metropolis (symmetric q, alpha=min(1,pi(y)/pi(x))), independence sampler (q(x,y)=g(y), alpha=min(1,w(y)/w(x)) with importance weight w=pi/g). Gibbs sampler: full-conditional updates pi(x_i | x_minus_i) as rejection-free MH (alpha=1); cycle vs random scan = composition vs mixture of pi-invariant kernels. Convergence: consistency under pi-irreducibility + Harris recurrence (Tierney 1994), MCMC CLT with asymptotic variance = integrated autocovariance controlled by the spectral gap, geometric ergodicity / drift conditions licensing burn-in, optimal-scaling 0.234 acceptance rate (Roberts-Gelman-Gilks 1997). Mixing/burn-in, effective sample size, integrated autocorrelation time. Importance-sampling contrast via the independence sampler. Role: makes Bayesian inference (45.03.01-04, 26.07.01) computational by removing the intractable marginal likelihood Z. Originators: Metropolis et al. 1953, Hastings 1970, Geman-Geman 1984 (Gibbs), Gelfand-Smith 1990 (general Bayesian use), Tierney 1994 (general-state-space theory). Lean status none: Mathlib has no general-state-space Markov kernel carrying a prescribed target to itself, no detailed-balance predicate for continuum kernels, no Metropolis-Hastings acceptance constructor or its detailed-balance proof, no Gibbs full-conditional composition invariance, and no Harris-recurrence ergodic theorem / MCMC CLT.

### mathematical-statistics.ancillarity-completeness-basu

- **title**: Ancillarity, Completeness, and Basu's Theorem
- **unit**: `45.01.03`
- **prerequisites**: `37.01.01` (probability spaces — laws and independence), `26.03.01` (random variables and expected value — the integral defining E_theta[g(T)]), `45.01.02` (sufficiency, the factorization theorem, and exponential families)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `45.01.03`. Ancillarity, completeness, and Basu's theorem (Casella-Berger ch 6; Lehmann-Casella). Ancillary statistic (pushforward law A_*P_theta free of theta) and Fisher's conditionality principle / recovery of information by conditioning on an ancillary configuration statistic. Complete and bounded-complete statistics (E_theta[g(T)]=0 for all theta implies g=0 a.s.); completeness of the natural sufficient statistic of a full-rank exponential family (stated and proved via the bilateral Laplace-transform uniqueness theorem on an open eta-set). Basu's theorem: a complete sufficient statistic is independent of every ancillary (stated and proved from the one equation E_theta[psi(T)-p_C]=0 — sufficiency gives the theta-free psi(T), ancillarity the theta-free p_C, completeness the kill); the bounded-complete form. Worked uses: independence of sample mean and sample variance for the normal, the uniform/location and exponential examples, moment factorisation as a computational engine, Studentised statistics and similar-tests/Neyman-structure. Relation to minimal sufficiency: complete sufficient implies minimal sufficient (Bahadur), and the completeness hierarchy complete > bounded complete > minimal. Necessity of completeness (uniform-location counterexample). Cross-ref sufficiency/exponential families 45.01.02; used by Lehmann-Scheffe / UMVUE 45.01.06; decision/testing 45.01.01, 45.02.01. Originators: Fisher 1934 (ancillarity, conditionality), Lehmann-Scheffe 1950 (completeness), Basu 1955 (the theorem), Bahadur 1957 (complete sufficient implies minimal). Lean status none: Mathlib has pushforward laws, IndepFun, and conditional expectation but no statistical-model/ancillarity/completeness predicates, no Basu independence theorem, and no exponential-family completeness via Laplace-transform uniqueness.

### mathematical-statistics.fisher-information-cramer-rao

- **title**: Fisher Information and the Cramér-Rao Lower Bound
- **unit**: `45.01.05`
- **prerequisites**: `mathematical-statistics.method-of-moments-maximum-likelihood`
- **notes**: stub - see unit `45.01.05`. The score function and Fisher information (the two equivalent forms: variance of the score and minus the expected second derivative of the log-likelihood, under regularity), additivity over i.i.d. samples, the reparametrisation rule, and the multiparameter information matrix. The Cramér-Rao lower bound for unbiased estimators (stated and proved via Cauchy-Schwarz on the covariance of the estimator with the score), efficiency and the efficient (CRLB-attaining) estimator, attainment in exponential families (affine-in-the-score; cross-ref 45.01.02). Regularity conditions and the breakdown on non-regular families (uniform endpoint). The multiparameter Loewner-order matrix bound Cov >= I^{-1} and nuisance-parameter information loss via the Schur complement. Sets the bar that asymptotic efficiency (45.04.03) attains. Lean status none.


### mathematical-statistics.neyman-pearson-most-powerful-tests

- **title**: The Neyman-Pearson Lemma and Most-Powerful Tests
- **unit**: `45.02.01`
- **prerequisites**: `37.01.01` (probability spaces — tests as measurable functions, size/power as integrals against P_theta), `45.01.01` (statistical decision theory — testing as a two-action 0-1-loss decision problem; Bayes/admissibility framing), `45.01.02` (sufficiency and the likelihood / likelihood ratio — the object the NP test thresholds)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `45.02.01`. The theory of optimal hypothesis tests (Casella-Berger ch 8; Lehmann-Romano Testing Statistical Hypotheses). Framework: simple/composite hypotheses, (possibly randomized) test functions phi:X->[0,1], size sup_Theta0 beta_phi, level alpha, power function beta_phi(theta)=E_theta phi(X), type I/II errors. The Neyman-Pearson lemma for simple-vs-simple: the likelihood-ratio test phi=1 if p1>k p0, gamma on the boundary, 0 if p1<k p0 is most powerful at its level. Three parts: existence (boundary randomization gamma achieves exact size alpha even with atoms), sufficiency (any size-alpha LR test is MP), necessity (any MP test equals an LR test off the boundary), all via the variational identity (phi-psi)(p1-k p0)>=0. The p-value and its uniform distribution under a continuous null (probability integral transform); conservative/randomized correction with atoms. Worked normal-mean and exponential examples; binomial discrete example with explicit gamma. Generalized NP lemma (multi-constraint, Lagrange multipliers). Monotone likelihood ratio as the bridge to one-sided composite UMP (Karlin-Rubin, next unit 45.02.02). Testing as decision: NP test = Bayes rule with k = prior-times-loss odds; size-power frontier = lower boundary of convex risk set (cross-ref 45.01.01). Originators: Neyman-Pearson 1933, Wald 1950 (decision-theoretic synthesis), Lehmann-Romano. Lean status none: Mathlib lacks a TestFunction type, size/level/power definitions, a MostPowerful predicate, the NP likelihood-ratio-test constructor with boundary randomization, the three-part fundamental lemma, the uniform null distribution of the p-value, and the MLR one-sided generalization.

### mathematical-statistics.delta-method

- **title**: The Delta Method and the Second-Order Delta Method
- **unit**: `45.04.02`
- **prerequisites**: `37.03.02` (Lindeberg-Feller CLT — the asymptotic-normality input), `45.04.01` (modes of stochastic convergence; Slutsky, continuous mapping, o_P/O_P calculus — the proof machinery)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `45.04.02`. The delta method (van der Vaart Ch. 3). Given sqrt(n)(T_n - theta) => N(0, Sigma) and g differentiable at theta, the first-order delta method gives sqrt(n)(g(T_n) - g(theta)) => N(0, grad g . Sigma . grad g^T) via a stochastic Taylor expansion (linearisation error upgraded to o_P by O_P-boundedness of the input) plus Slutsky (cross-ref 45.04.01); scalar case g'(theta)^2 sigma^2. Multivariate form with the Jacobian and the sandwich covariance. Second-order delta method when grad g(theta) = 0: rate jumps to n and the limit is the quadratic form (1/2) S^T H S in the Gaussian (Hessian H), a scaled chi-squared / quadratic-form-in-a-Gaussian, c chi^2_k when H = c Sigma^{-1} (origin of Wald/score/LRT null laws). Variance-stabilizing transformations as the ODE g' = 1/sigma(theta): arcsine (binomial), square-root (Poisson, Bartlett), Fisher z = arctanh (correlation), log (constant CV). Worked examples: sample variance/SD, ratio estimator (with the covariance cross-term), odds/log-odds (logit). Functional delta method via Hadamard differentiability previewed (full empirical-process treatment in 45.05.02). The workhorse for standard errors of plug-in estimators. Lean status none.

### mathematical-statistics.ridge-regression-shrinkage

- **title**: Ridge Regression and Shrinkage Estimation
- **unit**: `45.06.03`
- **prerequisites**: `45.06.01` (linear model theory: OLS, the hat matrix, Gauss-Markov BLUE), `45.06.02` (bias-variance decomposition and the prediction-error trade-off), `01.01.12` (singular value decomposition, the principal-direction shrinkage view)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `45.06.03`. Ridge regression / Tikhonov regularization (HTF ch 3; Hoerl-Kennard 1970). The penalized least-squares problem min ||y - X beta||^2 + lambda ||beta||^2 and its closed-form solution beta-hat(lambda) = (X^T X + lambda I)^{-1} X^T y, which exists and is unique even when X^T X is singular (p > n) because lambda > 0 makes the regularized Gram matrix positive-definite. The SVD view X = U D V^T: ridge shrinks each principal coordinate by the factor d_j^2/(d_j^2 + lambda) (cross-ref SVD 01.01.12). The ridge hat matrix H_lambda = X(X^T X + lambda I)^{-1} X^T (symmetric, not idempotent) and effective degrees of freedom df(lambda) = tr(H_lambda) = sum d_j^2/(d_j^2 + lambda). Bias-variance trade-off in lambda (cross-ref 45.06.02): ridge is biased but the Hoerl-Kennard existence theorem proves some lambda > 0 strictly lowers total MSE below OLS (proved via SVD diagonalization, g'(0) < 0). The Bayesian/Gaussian-prior interpretation: ridge = posterior mean and MAP under y|beta ~ N(X beta, sigma^2 I), beta ~ N(0, tau^2 I), with lambda = sigma^2/tau^2 (forward to Bayesian 45.03.02). Choosing lambda by cross-validation (LOOCV shortcut, GCV, one-standard-error rule). Kernel ridge regression via the push-through identity (forward to RKHS 45.08.01). Sets up the LASSO ell_1 contrast (forward to 45.06.06): smooth contraction (no exact zeros) vs cornered thresholding (sparsity). Lean status none: Mathlib has SVD, PosDef/invertibility, trace, and Gaussian measures but no penalized-least-squares functional, no closed-form/SVD-shrinkage identity, no MSE existence theorem, and no conjugate-normal posterior-mean computation.

### mathematical-statistics.empirical-distribution-glivenko-cantelli

- **title**: The Empirical Distribution and Glivenko-Cantelli Classes
- **unit**: `45.05.02`
- **prerequisites**: `probability.strong-law-large-numbers`, `mathematical-statistics.consistency-modes-of-convergence`
- **notes**: stub - see unit `45.05.02`. Empirical-process foundations (van der Vaart Ch. 19; van der Vaart-Wellner). The empirical measure P_n = n^-1 sum delta_{X_k} and the empirical distribution function F_n(t) = n^-1 sum 1{X_k <= t}; the classical Glivenko-Cantelli theorem (uniform a.s. convergence sup_t |F_n - F| -> 0, proved via monotonicity and a finite grid). The abstract Glivenko-Cantelli CLASS: ||P_n - P||_F = sup_{f in F} |P_n f - Pf| -> 0 a.s. Bracketing numbers N_[](eps, F, L^1(P)) and the bracketing Glivenko-Cantelli theorem; the Vapnik-Chervonenkis (VC) Glivenko-Cantelli theorem (finite VC dimension => GC, distribution-free) via Sauer's lemma and symmetrisation - same VC notion as learning theory 45.07.03. The DKW inequality P(sup_t |F_n - F| > eps) <= 2 exp(-2 n eps^2) with Massart's sharp constant; relation to the elementary EDF / Kolmogorov-Smirnov test. Grounds Donsker's empirical-process limit (37.03.03) and uniform consistency of M-estimators (45.04.04). Lean status none.


### mathematical-statistics.u-statistics

- **title**: U-Statistics and Their Asymptotics
- **unit**: `45.05.05`
- **prerequisites**: `45.04.01` (consistency, modes of stochastic convergence, Slutsky, o_P/O_P calculus), `37.04.01` (conditional expectation, tower property, reverse martingales), `37.03.02` (Lindeberg-Feller central limit theorem)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `45.05.05`. U-statistics (Hoeffding 1948; van der Vaart Ch. 12; Lee, U-Statistics). Definition: the unbiased estimator U_n = binom(n,m)^{-1} sum over m-subsets of a symmetric kernel h of degree m, estimating theta = E h(X_1,...,X_m). Examples: sample variance (kernel (x-y)^2/2), Gini's mean difference, Wilcoxon/Mann-Whitney (1{x<=y}), Kendall's tau (concordance minus discordance). The Hoeffding (H-) decomposition: orthogonal expansion of U_n - theta into completely-degenerate interaction U-statistics of pure order c, with projection kernels h_c (last m-c arguments integrated out) and variance components zeta_c. The variance formula Var(U_n) = binom(n,m)^{-1} sum_c binom(m,c) binom(n-m,m-c) zeta_c. Asymptotic normality of non-degenerate (zeta_1>0) U-statistics via the Hajek projection onto a sum of iid terms hat U_n = theta + (m/n) sum_i (h_1(X_i)-theta) plus an o_P remainder (E(U_n - hat U_n)^2 = O(n^{-2})), giving sqrt(n)(U_n - theta) -> N(0, m^2 zeta_1) by the CLT (37.03.02) and Slutsky. Degenerate (zeta_1=0) case: rate n not sqrt(n), non-normal limit n(U_n-theta) -> sum_j lambda_j (chi^2_{1,j} - 1) via the spectrum of the kernel operator. Reverse-martingale strong law U_n -> theta a.s. over decreasing exchangeable sigma-fields (uses 37.04.01). The projection / Hajek-projection method as a general device. Lean status none.


### mathematical-statistics.pac-learning-erm

- **title**: PAC Learning, Empirical Risk Minimization, and the Finite-Class Bound
- **unit**: `45.07.01`
- **prerequisites**: `40.07.05` (Hoeffding/bounded-differences concentration — the per-hypothesis deviation engine), `45.05.01` (Bernstein/sub-exponential tails — the variance-aware fast-rate refinement), `37.02.02` (strong law of large numbers — empirical risk converges to true risk), `37.03.01` (characteristic/moment generating functions — analytic tail control)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `45.07.01`. The formal framework of statistical learning theory (Shalev-Shwartz & Ben-David Understanding Machine Learning ch 2-4; Mohri-Rostamizadeh-Talwalkar Foundations of Machine Learning ch 2). Instance space X, label space Y, unknown distribution D over X x Y, hypothesis class H, loss ell in [0,1]; true/population risk L_D(h)=E_z ell(h,z) and empirical risk L_S(h)=(1/m) sum ell(h,z_i). Empirical risk minimization ERM_H(S) in argmin L_S(h) and overfitting; inductive bias = fixing H before seeing data. Realizable case: there is h* in H with L_D(h*)=0; PAC learnability with sample complexity m_H(eps,delta); the realizable finite-class bound m >= log(|H|/delta)/eps proved via union bound over eps-bad consistent hypotheses ((1-eps)^m <= e^(-eps m)). Agnostic case: agnostic PAC learnability (compete with min_h L_D(h)+eps); the agnostic finite-class bound via Hoeffding per hypothesis + union bound giving uniform convergence sup_h|L_S(h)-L_D(h)| <= sqrt(log(2|H|/delta)/(2m)) and ERM excess risk 2t, sample complexity 2 log(2|H|/delta)/eps^2. Realizable 1/eps vs agnostic 1/eps^2 rate. Advanced: error decomposition + bias-complexity tradeoff (approximation vs estimation error), No-Free-Lunch theorem (no distribution-free learning without bias), uniform convergence characterizes agnostic learnability (fundamental theorem of statistical learning: agnostic PAC <=> uniform convergence <=> finite VC dim), Bernstein/optimistic interpolation between fast 1/m and slow 1/sqrt(m) rates via best-in-class risk beta (cross-ref 45.05.01). Sets up uniform convergence for infinite classes (45.07.02) and VC theory (45.07.03) where log|H| is replaced by the growth function / VC dimension. Originators: Valiant 1984 (PAC), Vapnik-Chervonenkis 1971 (uniform convergence / VC). Lean status none: Mathlib has the measure/i.i.d./LLN/Hoeffding substrate but no PAC model, risk functionals, ERM predicate, PAC-learnability definition, or the two finite-class theorems.


### mathematical-statistics.em-algorithm-gaussian-mixtures

- **title**: The EM Algorithm and Gaussian-Mixture / Latent-Variable Models
- **unit**: `45.08.07`
- **prerequisites**: `45.01.04` (maximum likelihood, the MLE as global maximiser, exponential-family likelihood equation), `37.04.01` (conditional expectation, the tower property, conditional Jensen / KL non-negativity)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `45.08.07`. The Expectation-Maximization algorithm for maximum-likelihood estimation with latent variables (Dempster-Laird-Rubin 1977; HTF ch 8; McLachlan-Krishnan). Incomplete-data log-likelihood ell(theta)=log p(y|theta)=log integral p(y,z|theta)dz vs complete-data log-likelihood; the E-step forms the Q-function Q(theta|theta')=E_{Z|y,theta'}[log p(y,Z|theta)] (expected complete-data log-likelihood at current params), the M-step maximises Q in theta. Monotonicity theorem: EM never decreases the observed-data likelihood, proved via the decomposition ell=Q-H and the H-function inequality H(theta|theta')<=H(theta'|theta') from conditional Jensen / KL non-negativity (Gibbs); equivalently the ELBO identity ell(theta)=L(q,theta)+KL(q||p(z|y,theta)) making EM exact coordinate ascent on the evidence lower bound, and the minorize-maximize (MM) view. Gaussian mixtures as the canonical instance: responsibilities gamma_ik as posterior class probabilities (E-step), closed-form responsibility-weighted M-step updates (pi_k=N_k/n, mu_k, Sigma_k); soft k-means = EM for spherical-Gaussian mixture, hard k-means as the sigma^2->0 zero-temperature limit (cross-ref 45.08.08). Convergence: Wu 1983 corrected theory — limit points are stationary (not necessarily global) points of ell; multimodality, unbounded mixture likelihood, initialization sensitivity, multiple restarts. Linear/geometric rate = largest fraction of missing information (DM(theta*)=I_com^{-1}I_mis). Contrast with direct gradient/Newton MLE (cross-ref 45.01.04, 44.03.03). Other instances: missing data, HMM Baum-Welch via forward-backward E-step. Lean status none: Mathlib has measures/densities/condExp/klDiv/Gaussian but no incomplete-data likelihood, Q-function, EM operator, monotonicity theorem, ELBO decomposition, Gaussian-mixture updates, or Wu convergence theory.

### mathematical-statistics.rao-blackwell-lehmann-scheffe

- **title**: Rao-Blackwell and Lehmann-Scheffé: UMVU Estimation
- **unit**: `45.01.06`
- **prerequisites**: `45.01.02` (sufficiency, the factorization theorem, and exponential families), `45.01.03` (ancillarity, completeness, and Basu's theorem)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `45.01.06`. Optimal unbiased estimation (Casella-Berger ch 7; Lehmann-Casella). The Rao-Blackwell theorem: conditioning an unbiased estimator on a sufficient statistic yields an unbiased estimator of no larger variance (proved via the conditional-variance / total-variance identity, generalised to any convex loss via conditional Jensen; cross-ref sufficiency 45.01.02). The Lehmann-Scheffé theorem: a complete sufficient statistic yields a unique uniformly minimum-variance unbiased (UMVU) estimator (proved by completeness annihilating the difference of two unbiased functions of T; cross-ref completeness 45.01.03). Finding UMVUEs by Rao-Blackwellising any unbiased estimator or solving the unbiasedness equation in the complete sufficient statistic; the orthogonal-projection characterisation against unbiased estimators of zero. Worked examples: normal mean and variance, Poisson rate and e^{-lambda}, uniform endpoint. Basu used to simplify (independence of mean and variance). Relation to Cramér-Rao (UMVU need not attain the bound; cross-ref 45.01.05). Limitations: non-existence of unbiased estimators, absurd UMVUEs ((-1)^X), and inadmissibility under squared-error loss (Stein's phenomenon, James-Stein shrinkage for dimension >= 3). Originators: Rao 1945 / Blackwell 1947 (the conditioning theorem), Lehmann-Scheffé 1950 (completeness and uniqueness), Stein 1956 / James-Stein 1961 (inadmissibility). Lean status none: Mathlib has condexp, conditional Jensen, and the conditional-variance decomposition but no statistical-model / sufficiency / unbiasedness / completeness predicates, so neither the Rao-Blackwell nor the Lehmann-Scheffé theorem has a Mathlib statement.


### mathematical-statistics.mlr-ump-tests

- **title**: Monotone Likelihood Ratio and Uniformly Most Powerful Tests
- **unit**: `45.02.02`
- **prerequisites**: `45.02.01` (the Neyman-Pearson lemma — the simple-vs-simple optimum that MLR lifts to a one-sided family), `45.01.02` (sufficiency and exponential families — MLR holds in the natural sufficient statistic)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `45.02.02`. Uniformly most powerful (UMP) tests for composite hypotheses (Casella-Berger ch 8; Lehmann-Romano). UMP definition: most powerful at level alpha against EVERY alternative simultaneously (quantifier over Theta_1), versus Neyman-Pearson most-powerful-at-a-point. Monotone likelihood ratio (MLR): p_{theta'}/p_theta nondecreasing in a statistic T for every theta'>theta; one-parameter exponential families with increasing natural parameter have MLR in the natural statistic. The Karlin-Rubin theorem (stated and proved building on 45.02.01): under MLR the threshold test 1{T>c} with boundary randomization is UMP level alpha for H_0:theta<=theta_0 against H_1:theta>theta_0; the power function is monotone so size is attained at theta_0. Worked one-sided normal-mean, binomial, uniform(0,theta) (max), and normal-variance (chi-square) examples. Why UMP FAILS two-sided (the upper- and lower-tail Neyman-Pearson regions disagree; nonexistence proof via NP uniqueness), motivating unbiasedness (beta_phi(theta)>=alpha on the alternative; smooth => beta'(theta_0)=0) and UMP-unbiased (UMPU) tests via the generalized Neyman-Pearson lemma with two side constraints; equal-tailed two-sided UMPU for exponential families. Tests with nuisance parameters: similar tests (beta=alpha on the boundary), Neyman structure (E[phi|V]=alpha), and the completeness theorem (a complete sufficient V for the nuisance forces every similar test to have Neyman structure — cross-ref 45.01.03), reducing the composite problem to conditional Karlin-Rubin slices; origin of the t-, F-, and Fisher-exact tests. Test-inversion duality to uniformly most accurate confidence bounds. Sets up the likelihood-ratio test (45.02.03) where no UMP/UMPU exists. Originators: Neyman-Pearson 1936 (similar regions), Karlin-Rubin 1956 (MLR/UMP), Lehmann-Scheffe 1950 (completeness/Neyman structure), Lehmann-Romano. Lean status none: Mathlib lacks an MLR predicate, a UMP/UMPU predicate over composite alternatives, the Karlin-Rubin theorem, threshold-test power-function monotonicity, two-sided UMP nonexistence, unbiasedness/similarity, and the Neyman-structure completeness reduction.


### mathematical-statistics.confidence-sets-pivots-duality

- **title**: Confidence Sets, Pivotal Quantities, and Test-Interval Duality
- **unit**: `45.02.04`
- **prerequisites**: `45.02.01` (the Neyman-Pearson lemma and most-powerful tests — the level-alpha tests that are inverted into confidence sets)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `45.02.04`. Interval/set estimation (Casella-Berger ch 9; Lehmann-Romano Testing Statistical Hypotheses). Confidence set C:X->2^Theta, coverage probability cov_C(theta)=P_theta(theta in C(X)), confidence coefficient = inf_theta cov_C(theta) (frequentist meaning: theta fixed, the set C(X) random, the level is the procedure's long-run hit rate, not the probability the parameter lies in a realized interval). Test-inversion duality: a family of level-alpha acceptance regions A(theta0)={x:phi_{theta0}(x)=0} inverts to a 1-alpha set C(x)={theta0:x in A(theta0)} and conversely, via the membership identity {theta in C(X)}={X in A(theta)}; the two constructions are mutually inverse (stated and proved). Pivotal quantities Q(X,theta) (law free of theta) and the pivot method: location-scale pivots, the normal Z pivot, the t pivot sqrt(n)(Xbar-mu)/S ~ t_{n-1}, the chi-squared pivot (n-1)S^2/sigma^2 ~ chi^2_{n-1}. Optimal sets: uniformly most accurate (UMA) sets from UMP tests (false coverage = 1 - dual power; cross-ref 45.02.02), unbiased sets from unbiased tests, UMA-unbiased for two-sided normal, shortest-length intervals (phi(a)=phi(b) stationarity; equal-tail chi^2 not shortest). Pratt expected-length identity E[U-L]=integral of false-coverage probability over wrong theta' (Fubini), identifying short intervals with powerful dual tests. CRLB-to-width: inverse Fisher information floors both estimator variance and, via Pratt, expected interval length (cross-ref 45.01.05). Bayesian credible set (posterior mass over theta given x) vs frequentist confidence set (coverage over X given theta) - distinct probabilities on distinct spaces, numerically tangent only in conjugate flat-prior cases. Large-sample Wald/score/likelihood-ratio intervals as inverted asymptotic tests (forward-ref 45.04.09). Worked normal-mean (Z and t) and normal-variance (chi^2) intervals. Originators: Neyman 1937 (confidence interval, confidence coefficient), Lehmann (UMA/unbiased duality), Pratt 1961 (length identity); contrast with Fisher fiducial. Lean status none: Mathlib lacks a ConfidenceSet type, the coverage functional, the inversion bijection between level-alpha test families and 1-alpha set families, a Pivot predicate, and UMA/UMA-unbiased optimality transport.

### mathematical-statistics.likelihood-ratio-wald-score-tests

- **title**: Likelihood-Ratio, Wald, and Score Tests
- **unit**: `45.02.03`
- **prerequisites**: `45.01.04` (the MLE, log-likelihood, score equations, exponential-family likelihood — the fit each statistic is built on), `45.02.01` (the Neyman-Pearson lemma and the simple-vs-simple likelihood-ratio optimum — the optimality benchmark the GLRT generalizes)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `45.02.03`. The three general-purpose test constructions, the "holy trinity" (Casella-Berger ch 8,10; Lehmann-Romano Testing Statistical Hypotheses). The generalized likelihood-ratio test (GLRT): the statistic lambda(x) = sup_{Theta_0} L / sup_{Theta} L (ratio of constrained to unrestricted likelihood suprema), the log form -2 log lambda = 2(ell(theta_hat) - ell(theta_hat_0)), and finite-sample EXACT null distributions in special cases (normal mean reduces to the two-sided t-test; normal variance reduces to a two-tailed chi-squared_{n-1} pivot via Cochran). The Wald test W = (theta_hat - theta_0)^T I(theta_hat)(theta_hat - theta_0), built from the MLE and its standard error (one fit, at the alternative; reparametrization-DEPENDENT). The score / Rao / Lagrange-multiplier test R = s(theta_0)^T I(theta_0)^{-1} s(theta_0), built from the score at the null (needs only the null fit; the mean-zero/information-covariance score identity makes it pivotal). Geometry: drop in height (LR) vs squared distance to the maximum (Wald) vs squared slope at the null (score), all standardized by the Fisher-information metric; identical for a quadratic log-likelihood. Worked finite-sample examples: normal variance GLRT, Bernoulli/Poisson score and Wald, multinomial goodness-of-fit with the deviance G^2 = 2 sum O log(O/E) (LRT) versus Pearson X^2 = sum (O-E)^2/E (score), agreeing to second order. NOTE: the common chi-squared asymptotic null distribution (Wilks) and the first-order equivalence of the three are the ASYMPTOTIC unit 45.04.09 — stated here only as a forward pointer, NOT proved (this unit is finite-sample/constructional). Originators: Neyman-Pearson 1928 (GLR principle), Wilks 1938 (asymptotic chi-squared), Wald 1943 (Wald test), Rao 1948 (score/LM test); Pearson's 1900 chi-squared is the multinomial score statistic. Lean status none: Mathlib lacks GLRT/Wald/score constructors, the normal-mean/variance exact reductions, the multinomial Pearson-vs-deviance pair, and Wilks' theorem.


### mathematical-statistics.bayes-estimation-under-loss

- **title**: Bayes Estimation Under Loss: Posterior Mean, Median, and Mode
- **unit**: `45.03.01`
- **prerequisites**: `45.01.01` (decision theory: loss, risk, Bayes risk, admissibility — the framework specialised here), `26.07.01` (elementary Bayesian survey: prior, posterior, conjugacy — recap/cross-ref), `37.04.01` (conditional expectation and the tower property — the substrate of posterior expected loss)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `45.03.01`. Bayesian point estimation as a decision problem (Casella-Berger ch 7; Berger; Robert The Bayesian Choice). Posterior recap (cross-ref 26.07.01). The Bayes estimator minimises the posterior expected loss rho(pi,a|x)=int L(theta,a) d pi(theta|x); reduction theorem: Bayes risk r(pi,delta)=int rho(pi,delta(x)|x) dm(x) so global optimality reduces to pointwise minimisation. Three canonical losses: squared-error -> posterior mean (=conditional expectation E[theta|x], unique by strict convexity, minimal loss = posterior variance); absolute-error -> posterior median (minimiser set a closed interval); 0-1 / 0-K -> posterior mode/MAP (mode not parametrisation-invariant). Bayes risk and Bayes rule as its minimiser (cross-ref decision theory 45.01.01). Conjugate examples: normal-normal posterior mean as precision-weighted/shrinkage average, beta-binomial (alpha+x)/(alpha+beta+n), gamma-Poisson (alpha+S)/(beta+n) — each a convex combination of prior centre and data summary. Weighted-squared-error -> tilted posterior mean; LINEX -> -1/c log E[e^{-c theta}|x]. Admissibility of proper Bayes estimators (unique Bayes => admissible; full-support continuous-risk version without uniqueness); generalized Bayes and improper priors (flat prior -> sample mean; Jeffreys; improper-posterior and marginalisation hazards). Bayesian vs frequentist estimation contrast; Bernstein-von Mises asymptotic reconciliation. Shrinkage connection to ridge (45.06.03, lambda=sigma^2/tau^2) and James-Stein (45.06.04). Conjugacy theory in full is 45.03.02. Originators: Laplace, Wald 1950, Blackwell-Girshick 1954, Savage 1954, Raiffa-Schlaifer 1961, Berger 1985, Robert 1994/2007, Lehmann-Casella 1998. Lean status none: Mathlib has condExp/disintegration/integral but no posterior object, no posterior-expected-loss functional, none of the mean/median/mode minimiser theorems, no conjugate closed forms, no proper-Bayes admissibility, and no generalized-Bayes construction.


### mathematical-statistics.subset-selection-curse-of-dimensionality

- **title**: Subset Selection and the Curse of Dimensionality
- **unit**: `45.06.05`
- **prerequisites**: `45.06.02` (bias-variance decomposition, the prediction-error trade-off), `45.05.01` (Bernstein/sub-exponential concentration), `40.07.05` (Azuma/bounded-differences concentration)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `45.06.05`. Variable/subset selection and high-dimensional geometry (HTF ch 3, 18). Best-subset selection as the combinatorial 2^p / binom(p,k) problem (leaps-and-bounds); forward/backward stepwise and forward stagewise greedy heuristics and their failure to recover the best subset (joint effects). Why selection is hard: selection-induced optimism / post-selection inference (the selected max of p noise statistics has mean ~sqrt(2 ln p), so naive intervals undercover; PoSI restores coverage), and the discreteness/instability of selection (the y -> S map is piecewise constant, discontinuous, high-variance) versus the continuity of shrinkage (ridge 45.06.03). The curse of dimensionality: median nearest-neighbour distance (1 - 2^(-1/n))^(1/d), the t^d volume law and thin-shell concentration, Gaussian annulus (chi^2_d norm), distance concentration (max/min pairwise distance ratio -> 1) breaking k-NN/kernel density, near-orthogonality of random vectors (E<u,v>^2 = 1/d, sub-Gaussian). The blessing of dimensionality (Johnson-Lindenstrauss random projection, measure-concentration regularity). Motivates regularized methods (lasso 45.06.06) over explicit selection. Lean status none: Mathlib has the measure/volume/Gaussian/concentration substrate but no median-NN-distance, distance-concentration, near-orthogonality, thin-shell, best-subset objective, greedy operators, or post-selection optimism.


### mathematical-statistics.mle-asymptotic-normality-efficiency

- **title**: Asymptotic Normality and Efficiency of the Maximum-Likelihood Estimator
- **unit**: `45.04.03`
- **prerequisites**: `45.04.01` (consistency and the modes of stochastic convergence, the o_P/O_P calculus, Slutsky, continuous mapping), `45.04.02` (the delta method), `45.01.05` (Fisher information and the Cramér-Rao bound)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `45.04.03`. The large-sample theory of the MLE (van der Vaart Ch. 5; Casella-Berger Ch. 10). Consistency of the MLE via Wald's conditions (identifiability + uniform LLN on the log-likelihood with a well-separated KL-projection maximiser, cross-ref 45.04.01). Asymptotic normality: sqrt(n)(thetaHat - theta0) => N(0, I(theta0)^{-1}) under Cramér regularity, proved by a stochastic Taylor expansion of the score about theta0 + an LLN on the observed information -(1/n)ell'' -> I(theta0) + a CLT on the score (1/sqrt n)ell'(theta0) => N(0,I) + Slutsky. Asymptotic efficiency: the MLE attains the Cramér-Rao bound asymptotically (cross-ref 45.01.05); the convolution / local-asymptotic-minimax characterisation of optimality among regular estimators; superefficiency and Hodges' counterexample on a Lebesgue-null set (Le Cam). Plug-in asymptotic variance and Wald confidence intervals; observed vs expected information; the multiparameter Fisher-information-matrix version; functions of the MLE via the delta method (cross-ref 45.04.02). One-step / Newton-Fisher-scoring estimators from a sqrt(n)-consistent start, asymptotically equivalent to the MLE. Z-estimator sandwich J^{-1}Sigma J^{-1} and the misspecified Huber sandwich (full M-estimator theory in 45.04.04). Lean status none.

### mathematical-statistics.uniform-convergence-no-free-lunch

- **title**: Uniform Convergence, the Bias-Complexity Tradeoff, and the No-Free-Lunch Theorem
- **unit**: `45.07.02`
- **description**: Uniform convergence of empirical to true risk as a sufficient condition for agnostic-PAC learnability via ERM; the approximation-estimation (bias-complexity) decomposition; the No-Free-Lunch theorem and the necessity of inductive bias.


### mathematical-statistics.donsker-classes-empirical-process

- **title**: Donsker Classes and the Empirical-Process Weak Limit
- **unit**: `45.05.03`
- **prerequisites**: `mathematical-statistics.empirical-distribution-glivenko-cantelli`, `probability.donsker-invariance-principle-functional-clt`
- **notes**: stub - see unit `45.05.03`. The abstract empirical central limit theorem (van der Vaart Ch. 18-19; van der Vaart-Wellner). The empirical process G_n = sqrt(n)(P_n - P) indexed by a function class F, viewed as a random element of ell^infinity(F). A class F is P-DONSKER if G_n converges weakly (in ell^infinity(F), Hoffmann-Jorgensen outer-expectation sense) to a tight Gaussian process, the P-Brownian bridge with covariance Pfg - Pf Pg in the intrinsic L^2(P) variance semimetric rho_P. The classical EDF Donsker theorem (Brownian-bridge limit on [0,1]) is the half-line-indicator special case, built on the 1-D invariance principle 37.03.03 (not re-proved). The abstract empirical CLT: F is Donsker iff G_n is asymptotically rho_P-equicontinuous (the function-class analogue of the modulus-of-continuity tightness criterion), given total boundedness; proof = finite-dimensional (multivariate CLT) convergence + asymptotic tightness via a finite net. Sufficient conditions: bracketing entropy integral int sqrt(log N_[]) and uniform entropy integral int sup_Q sqrt(log N) finite (full detail 45.05.04); finite-VC + square-integrable envelope => Donsker for every P. Preservation calculus (sums, Lipschitz images, min/max, bounded products). Applications: functional delta method for smooth functionals of P_n (45.05.06), multiplier/bootstrap CLT, uniform inference / Kolmogorov-Smirnov bands. Donsker is to Glivenko-Cantelli (45.05.02) as CLT is to LLN. Lean status none.

### mathematical-statistics.asymptotic-relative-efficiency

- **title**: Asymptotic Relative Efficiency
- **unit**: `45.04.05`
- **prerequisites**: `mathematical-statistics.mle-asymptotic-normality-efficiency` (45.04.03 — the MLE variance floor I(theta)^{-1} that benchmarks every ARE; ARE against the MLE <= 1), `mathematical-statistics.delta-method` (45.04.02 — the asymptotic variances and efficacy slopes the ARE compares)
- **notes**: stub - see unit `45.04.05`. Asymptotic relative efficiency (van der Vaart Ch. 8, 14; Lehmann Elements of Large-Sample Theory Ch. 3, 5). ARE of two sqrt(n)-asymptotically-normal estimators = ratio of asymptotic variances v_T/v_S, read as the limiting sample-size ratio n_S/n_T = 1/ARE for equal precision. Median-vs-mean ARE = 4 sigma^2 f(theta)^2 (2/pi at the normal, 2 at the Laplace, >1 for heavy tails); trimmed means as the robust compromise; influence-function form E[psi_T^2]/E[psi_S^2]. ARE(., MLE) <= 1 via the convolution theorem (cross-ref 45.04.03, 45.04.08). Pitman ARE of tests = squared efficacy ratio (c_1/c_2)^2, where efficacy c = mu'(theta_0)/(sqrt(n) sigma(theta_0)) gives local power Phi(ch - z_{1-alpha}) along contiguous alternatives theta_0 + h/sqrt(n) (Noether's theorem). Sign-test efficacy 2 f(theta) reproduces 2/pi; Wilcoxon-to-t ARE = 12 sigma^2 (int f^2)^2 = 3/pi at the normal, 1.5 at Laplace, with the Hodges-Lehmann universal lower bound 108/125 = 0.864 (Epanechnikov density) and no finite upper bound. Why ARE motivates robust/nonparametric methods: bounded efficiency loss at the Gaussian, unbounded gain under tail-heaviness. Lean status none.


### mathematical-statistics.m-estimators-z-estimators

- **title**: M-Estimators and Z-Estimators: The Argmax Theorem and the Master Theorem
- **unit**: `45.04.04`
- **prerequisites**: `mathematical-statistics.mle-asymptotic-normality-efficiency`, `mathematical-statistics.consistency-modes-of-convergence`
- **notes**: stub - see unit `45.04.04`. The unifying asymptotic theory of estimators defined by optimization or estimating equations (van der Vaart Ch. 5). M-estimators (maximise an averaged criterion M_n(theta) = P_n m_theta) and Z-estimators (solve an estimating equation P_n psi_theta = 0), unified by the first-order condition psi = grad m; the MLE, robust location (Huber), the median, quantile regression, and GMM as instances. Consistency via the argmax/argmin theorem (uniform LLN plus a well-separated maximum, cross-ref 45.04.01 and Glivenko-Cantelli 45.05.02). Asymptotic normality via the Z-estimator master theorem: sqrt(n)(thetaHat - theta0) => N(0, A^-1 B A^-T), the sandwich covariance with A = -E grad psi (sensitivity) and B = Cov psi (variability), proved by the one-term Taylor expansion / stochastic equicontinuity (Donsker 45.05.03).
